questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In Williamson synthesis if tertiary alkyl halide is used than
options: ether is obtained in good yield
ether is obtained in poor yield
alkene is the only reaction product
a mixture of alkene as a major product and ether as a minor product forms.
questions.solution:
If a tertiary alkyl halide is used, an alkene is the only reaction product and no ether is formed. For example, the reaction of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{ONa}$ with $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Br}$ gives exclusively 2-methylpropene.

It is because alkoxides are not only nucleophiles but strong bases as well. They react with alkyl halides leading to elimination reactions.

questions.q: A compound is soluble in conc. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$. It does not decolourise bromine in carbon tetrachloride but is oxidised by chromic anhydride in aqueous sulphuric acid within two seconds, turning orange solution to blue, green and then opaque. The original compound is
questions.solution: Solubility of the compound in conc. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ indicates that it can be an alkene, alcohol or an ether. The inability to discharge bromine colour indicates absence of an alkene. Hence the compound is an alcohol which should be primary because it is readily (within 2 seconds) oxidised by $\mathrm{CrO}_{3}$ in sulphuric acid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $p$-toluidine and benzyl amine can be distinguished by
questions.solution:
$p$-toluidine contains amino group attached directly to benzene ring, thus undergo diazotisation reaction and gives red dye. In benzyl amine, the amino group is not directly attached to benzene ring. Hence, it will not undergo diazotisation reaction.
Thus, these two can be distinguished by dye test.

questions.q: A mixture of benzaldehyde and formaldehyde on heating with aqueous $\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution gives
questions.solution: Benzaldyde and formaldehyde, both do not have $\alpha$ - hydrogen atom, so both will undergo Cannizzaro reaction; here formaldehyde will always be oxidised to formate while the other aldehyde $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{CHO}\right.$ or any other aldehyde not having $\alpha-\mathrm{H}$, viz- $\left.\mathrm{Me}_{3} \mathrm{CCHO}\right)$ will always be reduced to corresponding alcohol (crossed Cannizzaro reaction)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The major product M formed in the following reaction is
questions.solution:
Concept:-
Reductions with diimide:
- Isolated carbon-carbon double bonds can be reduced with hydrazine \(\left(\mathrm{NH}_2 \mathrm{NH}_2\right)\) in the presence of oxygen or an oxidizing agent.
- The actual reducing agent in these reactions is the highly active species diimide, \(\mathrm{HN}=\mathrm{NH}\), formed in situ by oxidation of hydrazine.
- This compound is a highly selective reducing agent which in many cases offers a useful alternative to catalytic hydrogenation for the reduction of carbon-carbon multiple bonds.
- The reagent diimide is prepared by oxidation of hydrazine, by decomposition of tosylhydrazide, or from azodicarboxylic acid.
- It is a suitable reducing agent for symmetrical double bonds such as \(\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}\), and \(\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}\), but unsymmetrical, more polar bonds such as \(\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O}\), and \(\mathrm{S}=\mathrm{O}\) are not reduced. It is particularly suitable for reduction in the presence of other reactive functional groups, or in cases that are unsuccessful with catalytic hydrogenation.

- On reaction with diimide, only the less-conjugated alkene in the substrate is reduced by diimide without affecting the other double bonds or the epoxide ring, whereas catalytic hydrogenation promotes epoxide opening to give the phenol.

- The reactions are highly stereoselective, taking place by cis-addition of hydrogen in all cases. Transfer of the hydrogen atoms is thought to take place simultaneously through a cyclic six-membered transition state. This mechanism explains the stereospecificity of the reaction, and couples the driving force of nitrogen formation with the addition reaction. Concerted cis-transfer of hydrogen is a symmetry allowed process.

Explanation:-
- The reaction pathway is shown below:

- In the first step of the reaction, 1 eq. of hydrazine \(\left(\mathrm{NH}_2 \mathrm{NH}_2\right)\) undergoes in situ by oxidation in the presence of oxygen and \(\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{II})\) which act as an oxidizing agent to give 1 eq. of diimide ( \(\mathrm{NH}=\mathrm{NH}\)).
- The diimide reduces only the less-conjugated alkene in a substrate.
- But in the above substrate both the \(\mathbf{C = C}\) double bond is disubstituted, one of the \(\mathbf{C}=\mathrm{C}\) double bond is in \(\mathbf{Z}\) conformation while the other one is in \(\mathbf{E}\) conformation.
- 1 eq. of diimide \((\mathrm{NH}=\mathrm{NH})\) reagent will the reduce the \(\mathbf{E}\)-alkene stereospecifically to give the final product.
Conclusion:-
- Hence, the major product \(M\) formed in the following reaction is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The sequence of basic strength of alky1 amines follows the order
questions.solution:
It is expected that the basic nature of amines should be in order tertiary > secondary $>$ primary but the observed order in the case of lower members is found to be as secondary > Primary $>$ tetriary. This anomalous behaviour of tetriary amines is due to steric factors i.e crowding of alkyl groups cover nitrogen atom from all sides thus makes the approach and bonding by a proton relatively difficult which results the maximum steric strain in tetiary amines. The electrons are there but the path is blocked, resulting the reduction in basicity. Thus the correct order is
$\mathrm{R}_{2} \mathrm{NH}>\mathrm{RNH}_{2}>\mathrm{R}_{3} \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Chemistry

Due to the presence of electron withdrawing group like Ph group decreases the electron density of nitrogen and hence, the lone pair of nitrogen are not available, for donation.
So, $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)_{3} \mathrm{~N}$ is least basic due to the presence of three electron withdrawing $\mathrm{Ph}\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}\right)$ groups.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following has maximum dipole moment?
questions.solution:
Electronegativity difference between $\mathrm{N}(3.0)$ and $\mathrm{Cl}(3.0)$ is zero and hence, $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bonds are non-polar. As a result, the overall dipole moment of $\mathrm{NCl}_3$ molecule and its direction is just the dipole moment of the lone pair of electrons.
On the other hand, $\mathrm{N}-\operatorname{Br}(3.0-2.8)$, $\mathrm{N}-1(3.0-2.5)$ and $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H}(3.0-2.1)$ are polar and hence, contribute towards the overall dipole moment of the respective molecules. Since the EN difference is highest in case of $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds, therefore, $\mathrm{NH}_3$ has the highest dipole moment.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following which one has the highest cation of anion size ratio?
questions.solution:
The size of cation is in order of$\mathrm{Li}^{+} < \mathrm{Na}^{+} < \mathrm{Cs}^{+}$ and the size of anions in the order of$\mathrm{I}^{-}>\mathrm{F}^{-}$
Thus, when the cation is largest and anion is smallest, the ratio of their sizes is maximum.
Hence, cation to anion sizeratio is maximum for CsF.

questions.q: Among the following, the molecules /ions having same bond order are
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{O}_2^{2-}\left(18 e^{-}\right): K K \sigma(2 s)^2 \sigma^*(2 s)^2 \sigma\left(2 p_z\right)^2 \pi \\ & \quad\left(2 p_x\right)^2 \pi^*\left(2 p_y\right)^2 \pi^*\left(2 p_x\right)^2 \pi^*\left(2 p_y\right)^2\end{aligned}$
$\begin{gathered}\text { Bond order }(\mathrm{B} . \mathrm{O})=\frac{8-6}{2}=1 \\ \mathrm{~B}_2\left(10 e^{-}\right): K K \sigma(2 s)^2 \sigma^*(2 s)^2 \pi\left(2 p_x\right)^1 \pi\left(2 p_y\right)^1 \\ \text { B.O }=\frac{4-2}{2}=1\end{gathered}$
Therefore, $\mathrm{O}_2^{2-}$ and $\mathrm{B}_2$ have identical bond order.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Some of the properties of the two species, $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$are described below. Which one of them is correct?
options: Dissimilar in hybridisation for the central atom with different structures
Isostructural with same hybridisation for the central atom
Isostructural with different hybridisation for the central atom
Similar in hybridisation for the central atom with different structures
questions.solution:
In $\mathrm{NO}_{3}$, $$ H=\frac{1}{2}[5+0-0+1]=3 . \mathrm{So}, \mathrm{sp}^{3} $$ hybridization. Thus, it has trigonal planar geometry.
$\mathrm{In} \mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$ $H=\frac{1}{2}[6+3-1+0]=4 ;$ So, $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridization and it has pyramidal geometry due to the presence of one lone pair of electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is correct comparison of the stability of the molecules?
questions.solution:
Higher the bond order higher will be the stability.
When the bond order of two molecules are same, the molecule with least number of antibonding electrons is more stable. Molecular orbital configuration of $\mathrm{H}_{2}^{+}$is $\mathrm{H}_{2}^{+}(1)=\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^{1}$
$$
\mathrm{BO}=\frac{1-0}{2}=0.5
$$
Molecular orbital configuration of $\mathrm{He}_{2}^{+}$is
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{He}_{2}^{+}(3)=\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, \stackrel{*}{\sigma} \mathrm{s}^{1} \\
\mathrm{BO}=\frac{2-1}{2}=0.5
\end{array}
$$
Hence, stability of $\mathrm{H}_{2}^{+}>$stability of $\mathrm{He}_{2}^{+}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the reaction
$$
\begin{array}{c}
2 \mathrm{NO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \\
\left(K_{c}=1.8 \times 10^{-6} \text { at } 184^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)(\mathrm{R}=0.0831 \mathrm{~kJ} /(\mathrm{mol} . \mathrm{K})
\end{array}
$$
When $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ are compared at $184^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, it is found that
options: Whether $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ is greater than, less than or equal to $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ depends upon the total gas pressure
questions.solution:
For the reaction:-
$$
2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$
Given $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=1.8 \times 10^{-6}$ at $184^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ $\mathrm{R}=0.00831 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} . \mathrm{K}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}} \\
\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=3-2=1 \\
\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{p}}=1.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.00831 \times 457 \\
=6.836 \times 10^{-6}
\end{array}
$$
Hence it is clear that $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}>\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The volume of a closed reaction vessel in which the following equilibrium reaction occurs is halved :
$2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
As a result,
options: The rates of forward and backward reactions will remain the same.
The equilibrium will shift to the right.
The rate of forward reaction will become double that of reverse reaction and the equilibrium will shift to the right.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}$
If volume is reduced to half, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$ will decrease to half. Thus, to maintain the equilibrium, the reaction should shift in the forward direction, i.e. towards right. Also, to attain equilibrium back, the rate of forward direction will become double the rate of backward direction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which reaction is not affected by change in pressure?
questions.solution:
A reaction in which \(\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}\) is zero is not affected by change in pressure, considering various given reactions
(a) \(\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=2-2=0\)
(b) \(\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=2-(3+1)=-2\)
(c) \(\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=2-1=+1\)
[Consider only gaseous substances]
(d) \(\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=2-(2+1)=-1\)
Thus, (a) is the correct option.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the water gas equilibrium reaction, $\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}(g)+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
Which of the following statements is true at equilibrium?
options: If the amount of $\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})$ is increased, less water would be formed
If the amount of $\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})$ is increased, more $\mathrm{CO}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ would be formed
If the pressure on the system is increased by halving the volume, more water would be formed
If the pressure on the system is increased by halving the volume, more $\mathrm{CO}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ would be formed
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{K}=\frac{[\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})\right]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})\right]}$
Concentration will increase, on halving the volume. There are two terms in numerator. So to keep $K$ constant, concentration of $\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]$ should increase much more.

questions.q: A vessel at $1000 \mathrm{~K}$ contains $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ with a pressure of $0.5 \mathrm{~atm} .$ Some of the $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is converted into $\mathrm{CO}$ on the addition of graphite. The value of $\mathrm{K}$ if the total pressure at equilibrium is $0.8 \mathrm{~atm}$, is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})$
Initial $\quad 0.5 \mathrm{~atm}$
pressure
At equi $(0.5-\mathrm{x})$
$\therefore$ Total no. of moles at equilibrium
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad & x=0.3 \mathrm{~atm} \\
\therefore \quad & \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}=0.5-0.3=0.2 \\
& \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}}=2 x=2 \times 0.3=0.6 \mathrm{~atm} \\
& \mathrm{~K}=\frac{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}}^{2}}{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}=\frac{(0.6)^{2}}{0.2}=1.8 \mathrm{~atm}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the reaction,
$2 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{B}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{AB}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
the equilibrium constant, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ is $16.0 .$ The value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ for $\mathrm{AB}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g})+1 / 2 \mathrm{~B}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is
questions.solution:
For the given reaction,
$$
2 A(g)+B_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 A B_{2}(g)
$$
the equilibrium constant,
$$
K_{p}=\frac{p_{A B_{2}}^{2}}{p_{A}^{2} \cdot p_{B_{2}}}=16 ...(i)
$$
For the other given reaction,
$$
A B_{2}(g) \rightleftharpoons A(g)+\frac{1}{2} B_{2}(g)
$$
The equilibrium constant,
$$
K_{p}^{\prime}=\frac{p_{A} \cdot p_{B_{2}}^{1 / 2}}{p_{A B_{2}}}...(ii)
$$
On squaring Eq. (ii), we obtain,
$$
\left(K^{\prime}{ }_{p}\right)^{2}=\frac{p_{A}^{2} \cdot p_{B_{2}}}{p_{A B_{2}}^{2}}...(iii)
$$
Now, from Eq. (i) and (iii), we obtain,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
K_{p} \cdot\left(K_{p}^{\prime}\right)^{2}=1 \Rightarrow 16 \cdot\left(K_{p}^{\prime}\right)^{2}=1 \\
\quad\left(\because K_{p}=16.0\right) \\
\Rightarrow\left(K_{p}^{\prime}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{16}\right)^{1 / 2} \Rightarrow K_{p}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{4}=0.25
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The equilibrium constant for the given reaction is 100 .
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
What is the equilibrium constant for the reaction given below?
$\mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
 \mathrm{N}_{2}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2} \\
K_{1}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{2}} \\
\text { or } 100=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{2}}...(i)
\end{array}
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { Again, }\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right] \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \\
K_{2}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]} \\
\text { or } K_{2}^{2}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}}...(ii)
\end{array}
$$
Eqs. (i) $\times($ ii $)$, we get $100 \times K_{2}^{2}=1$
$$
\Rightarrow K_{2}^{2}=\frac{1}{100} \text { or } K_{2}=\frac{1}{10}=0.1
$$

questions.q:
Given the equilibrium system:

\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{s}) \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\

What change will shift the equilibrium to the right?
options: Dissolving $\mathrm{NaCl}$ crystals in the equilibrium mixture
Dissolving $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ crystals in the equilibrium mixture
questions.solution: Endothermic reactions are favoured at high temperature. Therefore, increasing the temperature will shift equilibrium to the right.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction, $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{I}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \Leftrightarrow 2 \mathrm{HI}(\mathrm{g})$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\mathrm{An}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{n}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{p}(\mathrm{g})}-\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{r}(\mathrm{g})}=2-2=0$
$\therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The increase of pressure on ice $\Leftrightarrow$ water at a constant temperature will cause
questions.solution:
Ice $\rightleftharpoons$ Water
The volume of ice is more than water. Therefore when pressure is increased the equilibrium shifts in forward direction. It favoures melting of ice.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The exothermic formation of $\mathrm{CIF}_{3}$ is represented by the equation :
$$
\mathrm{CI}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{~F}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{ClF}_{3(\mathrm{~g})} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-329 \mathrm{~kJ}
$$
Which of the following will increase the quantity of $\mathrm{CIF}_{3}$ in an equilibrium mixture of $\mathrm{CI}_{2}, \mathrm{~F}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{CIF}_{3} ?$
options: Increasing the volume of the container
questions.solution: The reaction given is an exothermic reaction thus according to Le chatalier's principle lowering of temperature, addition of $\mathrm{F}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ favour the forward direction and hence the production of $\mathrm{ClF}_{3}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In gaseous equilibrium the correct relation between \(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}\) and \(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}\) is
questions.solution: Relation is \(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}(\mathrm{RT})^{\Delta \mathrm{n}}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The value of $K_{c}$ for the reaction : $\mathrm{A}+3 \mathrm{~B} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{C}$ at $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $0.5$. Calculate the value of $K_{P}$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}} &=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}[\mathrm{RT}]_{\mathrm{g}}^{\Delta \mathrm{n}} \quad \Delta \mathrm{n}=2-4=-2 \\ \mathrm{~T} &=673 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{c}}=0.5 \\ \mathrm{R} &=0.082 \mathrm{~L} \cdot \mathrm{atm} \cdot \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \\ \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{p}} &=0.5 \times(0.082 \times 673)^{-2} \\ &=1.64 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~atm} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a closed system consisting of a reaction $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ (g) $\rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$, the pressure
questions.solution: As the system is closed, hence the reaction will be reversible, hence according to Le-chatelier principle pressure decreases since the volume is increasing.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Equivalent amounts of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{I}_{2}$ are heated in a closed vessel till equilibrium is obtained. If $80 \%$ of the hydrogen can be converted to $\mathrm{HI}$, the $\mathrm{Kc}$ at this temperature is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}=\frac{(2 \mathrm{x})^{2}}{(1-\mathrm{x})^{2}}$ Given $\mathrm{x}=80 \%=0.80$ $\therefore \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{(2 \times 0.80)^{2}}{(1-0.80)^{2}}=64$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The reaction $2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is of first order. If volume of reaction vessel is reduced to $1 / 3$, the rate of reaction would be
questions.solution:
For following reaction, $2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ $\rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
When the volume of vessel is reduced to $\frac{1}{3}$ then concentration of reactant becomes three times.
The rate of reaction for first order reaction is proportional to the concentration. So rate of reaction will increase three times.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A substance 'A' decomposes by a first order reaction starting initially with \([\mathrm{A}]=2.00 \mathrm{~m}\) and after \(200 \mathrm{~min},[\mathrm{~A}]\) becomes \(0.15 \mathrm{~m}\). For this reaction \(t_{1 / 2}\) is
questions.solution: Given initial concentration (a) \(=2.00 \mathrm{~m}\); Time taken \((t)=200 \mathrm{~min}\) and final concentration \((\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x})=0.15 \mathrm{~m}\). For a first order reation rate constant,

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the reaction $\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
if $\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{l}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, then value of $\frac{-\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}$ would be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} & \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \\ \frac{-\Delta\left[\mathrm{N}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}} &=-\frac{1}{3} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}} \\ \therefore \frac{-\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}} &=\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{3}{2} \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \\ &=3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~S}^{-1} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The activation energy for a reaction is $9.0 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{cal} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. The increase in the rate constant when its temperature is increased from 298 K to 308 K is
questions.solution:
According to the Arrhenius equation
$\log K=\log A-\frac{E_a}{2.303 R T}$....(i)
$\frac{\log K_{30 B}}{\log K_{29 B}}=\frac{E_a}{2.303 R}\left[\frac{T_2-T_1}{T_2}\right]\ldots(ii)$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\log K_{308}}{\log K_{298}}=\frac{9.0}{2.303 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}\left[\frac{308-298}{308 \times 298}\right] \\ & or \quad \frac{\log K_{308}}{\log K_{298}}=0.2129\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} K_{308} & =1.632 K_{298} \ldots(iii)\\ \% \text { increase } & =\frac{K_{308}-K_{298}}{K_{298}} \times 100 \\ & \cong 63 \%\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction \(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}\). The variation of the concentration of the products is given by the curve
questions.solution: Order of reaction is independent of given factors.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following statements:
I. Increase in concentration of reactant increases the rate of a zero order reaction.
II. Rate constant $\mathrm{k}$ is equal to collision frequency $\mathrm{A}$ if $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=0$.
III. Rate constant $\mathrm{k}$ is equal to collision frequency $\mathrm{A}$ if $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=\infty$.
IV. In $\mathrm{k}$ vs $\mathrm{T}$ is a straight line.
$\mathrm{V}$. In $\mathrm{k}$ vs $1 / \mathrm{T}$ is a straight line.
Correct statements are
questions.solution: According to Arrhenius equation, $\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{Ae}^{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}} / \mathrm{RT}}$ $\therefore$ when $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}=0, \mathrm{k}=\mathrm{A}$ Also $\ln \mathrm{k}$ vs $1 / \mathrm{T}$ is a straight line with slope $=-$ $\mathrm{E}_2 / \mathrm{R}$. $\therefore$ Statements (ii) and (v) are correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The order of a reaction, with respect to one of the reacting component \(Y\), is zero. It implies that:
options: the reaction is going on at a constant rate
the rate of reaction does not vary with temperature
the reaction rate is independent of the concentration of \(Y\)
the rate of formation of the activated complex is zero
questions.solution:
Let us consider a reaction,
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{xX}+\mathrm{yY} \longrightarrow \mathrm{a} A+\mathrm{bB} \\
& \text {rate }=[\mathrm{X}]^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{Y}]^{\mathrm{y}}
\end{aligned}\)
It is given that order of reaction w.r.t. component \(\mathrm{Y}\) is zero.
Hence, rate \(=[\mathrm{X}]^{\mathrm{x}}\)
i.e., rate becomes independent of the concentration of Y.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Select the rate law that corresponds to data shown for the following reaction
\(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \longrightarrow\) products
\(\begin{array}{cccc}\text { Exp. } & {[\mathrm{A}]} & {[\mathrm{B}]} & \text { Initial rate } \\ 1 & 0.012 & 0.035 & 0.1 \\ 2 & 0.024 & 0.070 & 0.8 \\ 3 & 0.024 & 0.035 & 0.1 \\ 4 & 0.012 & 0.070 & 0.8\end{array}\)
questions.solution:
From data 1 and 3 , it is clear that keeping
(B) const, [A] is doubled, rate remains unaffected. Hence rate is independent of [A]. from 1 and 4 , keeping \([\mathrm{A}]\) constant, \([\mathrm{B}]\) is doubled, rate become 8 times. Hence rate \(\propto[\mathrm{B}]^3\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction $2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$, rate and rate constant are $1.02 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{lit}^{-1} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$ and $3.4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$ respectively then concentration of $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ at that time will be
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$ from the unit of rate constant it is clear that the reaction follow first order kinetics. Hence by rate law equation, $\mathrm{r}=k\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]$
where $\mathrm{r}=1.02 \times 10^{-4}, k=3.4 \times 10^{-5}$ $1.02 \times 10^{-4}=3.4 \times 10^{-5}\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]$ $\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]=3 \mathrm{M}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is order with respect to A, B, C, respectively
$\begin{array}{llll}{[\mathrm{A}]} & {[\mathrm{B}]} & {[\mathrm{C}]} & \text { rate }(\mathrm{M} / \mathrm{sec} .) \\ 0.2 & 0.1 & 0.02 & 8.08 \times 10^{-3} \\ 0.1 & 0.2 & 0.02 & 2.01 \times 10^{-3} \\ 0.1 & 1.8 & 0.18 & 6.03 \times 10^{-3} \\ 0.2 & 0.1 & 0.08 & 6.464 \times 10^{-2}\end{array}$
questions.solution:
If rate $=\mathrm{k}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{y}}[\mathrm{C}]^{\mathrm{z}}$
From first two given data
$8.08 \times 10^{-3}=\mathrm{k}[0.2]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.1]^{\mathrm{y}}[0.02]^{\mathrm{z}} \ldots(1)$
$2.01 \times 10^{-3}=\mathrm{k}[0.1]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.2]^{\mathrm{y}}[0.02]^{\mathrm{z}} \ldots .(2)$
Divide $(1)$ by $(2)$ we get, $4=2^{x}(1 / 2)^{y}$
Similarly, from second and third data
$(9)^{\mathrm{y}}(9)^{\mathrm{z}}=3$
$2 y+2 z=1$
From first and fourth data $4^{\mathrm{z}}=8=2^{3}$
$2 z=3 .$ So $z=3 / 2, y=-1, x=1$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a first order reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{P}$, the temperature (T) dependent rate constant $(k)$ was found to follow the equation $\log k=-(2000) \frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}+6.0$. The pre-exponential factor A and the activation energy $\mathrm{E}_{a}$, respectively, are
questions.solution:
$\log k=\log A-\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R T}......(1)$
Also given $\log k=6.0-(2000) \frac{1}{T}......(2)$
On comparing equations, (1) and (2)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \log A=6.0 \Rightarrow A=10^{6} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\
\text { and } & \frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}=2000 \\
\Rightarrow \quad & E_{a}=2000 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \\
=& 38.29 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The following data were obtained for a given reaction at $300 K$.
$\begin{array}{llc} & \text { Reaction } & \begin{array}{c}\text { Energy of activation } \\ \left(\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)\end{array} \\ \text { (i) } & \text { uncatalysed } & 76 \\ \text { (ii) } & \text { catalysed } & 57\end{array}$
The factor by which rate of catalysed reaction is increased, is
questions.solution:
Using Arrhenius equation,
$\begin{array}{l}
K=A \cdot e^{-\frac{E_{a}}{R t}}, \text { we get } \\
\log k=\log A-\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R T} \quad \therefore
\end{array}$
$\log k_{1}=\log A-\frac{E_{a_{(1)}}}{2.303 R T}$
and $\log k_{2}=\log A-\frac{E_{a_{(2)}}}{2.303 R T}$
or $\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{1}{2.303 R T}\left[E_{a(1)}-E_{a(2)}\right]$
(from (i) and (ii))
$=\frac{1}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300}(76000-57000)$
or $\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{19000}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 300}$ $=\frac{190}{6.9 \times 8.314}$
or $\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=2000[$ taking antilog $]$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The half life of the first order reaction
$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} . \mathrm{CHO}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})
$$
If initial pressure of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}$ (g) is $80 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$ and the total pressure at the end of 20 minutes is $120 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{l} \therefore \mathrm{p}^{0}-\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{p}=120 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg} \\ \text { or, } \mathrm{p}^{0}+\mathrm{p}=120 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg} ; \\ \mathrm{p}=120-80=40 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg} \\ \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \ln \frac{\mathrm{p}^{0}}{\mathrm{p}^{0}-\mathrm{p}}=\frac{1}{20} \ln \frac{80}{80-40}=\frac{1}{20} \ln 2 \end{array}$ Again, $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\frac{\ell \mathrm{n} 2}{\mathrm{k}}$ $\therefore \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\frac{\ln 2}{\ln 2} \times 20=20 \mathrm{~min}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The concentrations of the reactant $A$ in the reacntion $A \rightarrow B$ at different times are given below
$\begin{array}{ll}\text { Concentration }(\boldsymbol{M}) & \text { Time (Minutes) } \\ 0.069 & 0 \\ 0.052 & 17 \\ 0.035 & 34 \\ 0.018 & 51\end{array}$
The rate constant of the reaction according to the correct order of reaction is
questions.solution:
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
\hline Interval & Conc.change & Rate \\
\hline 0-17 \mathrm{~min} & 0.069-0.052=0.017 \mathrm{M} & \frac{0.017}{17}=0.001 \\
\hline 17-34 \mathrm{~min} & 0.052-0.035=0.017 \mathrm{M} & \frac{0.017}{17}=0.001 \\
\hline 34-51 \mathrm{~min} & 0.035-0.018=0.017 \mathrm{M} & \frac{0.017}{17}=0.001 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$\because$ Rate remains constant. So, it is independent of concentration, the reaction is of zero order.
According to rate law
Rate $=\mathrm{K}(\text { conc. })^{0}=0.001 \mathrm{M} / \mathrm{min}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Acid hydrolysis of ester is first order reaction and rate constant is given by $k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{V_{\infty}-V_{0}}{V_{\infty}-V_{t}}$ where, $V_{0}, V_{t}$ and $V_{\infty}$ are the volumle of standard $\mathrm{NaOH}$ required to neutralise acid present at a given time, if ester is $50 \%$ neutralised then
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{RCOOR}^{\prime}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \stackrel{\mathrm{H}^{+}}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{RCOOH}+\mathrm{R}^{\prime} \mathrm{OH}$
$\begin{array}{lccc}\text { At } \mathrm{t}=0, & \text { a } & 0 & 0 \\ \text { At timet, } & \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x} & \mathrm{x} & \mathrm{x} \\ \text { At time } \infty, & \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{a} & \mathrm{a} & \mathrm{a} \\ \text { At } \mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{~V}_{0}=\text { volume } & \mathrm{Va}_{\mathrm{t}}=\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{V}_{0} & & \text { (catalyst) }\end{array}$
$\mathrm{V}_{\infty}=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{V}_{0}$
If ester is $50 \%$ hydrolysed then, $x=\frac{a}{2}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\text { or } \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}+\mathrm{V}_{0} \\
\text { or } \mathrm{a}=2 \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{t}}-2 \mathrm{~V}_{0} \\
\therefore \mathrm{V}_{\infty}=2 \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{t}}-2 \mathrm{~V}_{0}+\mathrm{V}_{0} \\
=2 \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{t}}-\mathrm{V}_{0}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The unit of rate constant for a zero order reaction is
questions.solution:
For zero order reaction,
Rate $=k[\text { Reactants }]^{\circ}$
$\therefore$ Rate $=k$
and unit of $k=\operatorname{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
In the reversible reaction,
$2 \mathrm{NO}_{2} \frac{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{k}_{2}} \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$
the rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
2 \mathrm{NO}_{2} \underset{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\stackrel{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}} \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \\
\text { Rate }=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=k_{1}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}-k_{2}\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right]
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$ Rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ i.e.,
$$
-\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 k_{1}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2}-k_{2}\left[\mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right]
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A chemical reaction was carried out at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ and $280 \mathrm{~K}$. The rate constants were found to be $\mathrm{k}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{k}_{2}$ respectively. Then

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The rate constant of a reaction at temperature $200 \mathrm{~K}$ is 10 times less than the rate constant at $400 \mathrm{~K}$. What is the activation energy of the reaction?

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a reaction of type $\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B} \rightarrow$ products, it is observed that doubling concentration of $\mathrm{A}$ causes the reaction rate to be four times as great, but doubling amount of $\mathrm{B}$ does not affect the rate. The unit of rate constant is
questions.solution:
Let the initial rate be $R$ and order with respect to $A$ be $x$ and $B$ be $y$. Thus, rate lawcan be written as, rate, $R=[A]^{x}[B]^{y}$...(i)
After doubling the concentration of $A$, rate becomes $4 R$
$$
4 R=[2 A]^{x}[B]^{y}...(ii)
$$
After doubling the concentration of $B$, rate remains $R$,
$R=[A]^{x}[2 B\}$...(iii)
From Eq. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
\frac{R}{4 R}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{x} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{x}
$$
So, $x=2$
From Eq. (i) and (iii), we get
$$
\frac{R}{R}=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^{y} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{1}{1}\right)^{0}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{y}
$$
So, $Y=0$
Hence, the rate lawis, rate $\mathrm{R}=[A]^{2}[B]^{0}$ This clearly shows that the order of this reaction is 2 and for second order reaction units of rate constant are $\mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{Ls}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The activation energy for a simple chemical reaction \(\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}\) is \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}\) in forward direction. The activation energy for reverse reaction
options: Can be less than or more than \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The rate constant is doubled when temperature increases from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Activation energy in $\mathrm{kJ}$ is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A radioactive element $X$ emits $3 \alpha, 1 \beta$ and $1 \gamma-$ particles and forms $_{76} \mathrm{Y}^{235}$. Element $\mathrm{X}$ is
questions.solution:
The complete nuclear reaction is
$=X^{A} \longrightarrow 76 Y^{235}+\underset{\alpha-\text { particle }}{3_{2} \text { He }^{4}}+\underset{\beta-\text { partide }}{\longrightarrow} e^{0}$
$+\underset{\gamma \text {-particle }}{\gamma}$
On observing the reaction, mass number of $' X^{\prime}$ is $A=235+12+0=247$
On observing the reaction, atomic number of $^{\prime} X^{\prime}$ is
$$
Z=76+6-1=81
$$
Hence, element $^{\prime} X^{n}$ is $_{81} X^{247}$,

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The activity of an old piece of wood is just $25 \%$ of the fresh piece of wood. If $t_{1 / 2}$ of $-14$ is 6000 yr, the age of piece of wood is
questions.solution:
As we know that,
$$
\begin{aligned}
k &=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{6000}=1.155 \times 10^{-4} \\
t &=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{N_{0}}{N}=\frac{2.303}{1.155 \times 10^{-4}} \log \frac{100}{25} \\
&=12000 \mathrm{yr}(\text { age of piece of wood })
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a first order reaction at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the ratio of time required for $75 \%$ completion to $25 \%$ completion of reaction is
questions.solution:
For a first order reaction,
$$
t=\frac{2.303}{\lambda} \log _{10} \frac{a}{a-x}
$$
Let initial amount of reactant is 100 .
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{t_{1}}{t_{2}} &=\frac{\log \frac{100}{100-75}}{\log \frac{100}{100-25}}[\because \lambda \text { remains constant }] \\
&=\frac{\log \frac{100}{25}}{\log \frac{100}{75}}=\frac{\log 4}{\log 4 / 3} \\
&=\frac{\log 4}{\log 4-\log 3} \\
&=\frac{2 \times 0.3010}{2 \times 0.3010-0.4771} \\
&=\frac{0.6020}{0.1249}=4.81
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If a plot of $\log _{10} \mathrm{C}$ versus $\mathrm{t}$ gives a straight line for a given reaction, then the reaction is
questions.solution:
Any first order reaction follows the equation
$\log [\mathrm{A}]=\frac{-\mathrm{k}}{2.303} \mathrm{t}+\log [\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{o}}$
$\because$ it resembles equation of straight line
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}+\mathrm{C} \\
\mathrm{y}=\log [\mathrm{A}] \text { i.e. } \log _{10} \mathrm{C} \\
\mathrm{m}=\frac{-\mathrm{k}}{2.303} \text { if } \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{t} \& \mathrm{C}=\log [\mathrm{A}]_{0}
\end{array}$
hence the plot is for a $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The half life period of a first order reaction is 1 $\min 40$ secs. Calculate its rate constant.
questions.solution:
For $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction.
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2} \text { (halflife time) }=\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{~K}}$
Hence $\mathrm{K}=\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{t}^{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{(60+40) \mathrm{sec}}=0.693 \times$ $10^{-2} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}=6.93 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A nuclear reaction of ${ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}$ with a neutron produces ${ }_{36}^{90} \mathrm{Kr}$ and two neutrons. Other element produced in this reaction is
questions.solution:
${ }_{92}^{235} U+{ }_{0}^{1} n \longrightarrow{ }_{56}^{144} \mathrm{Ba}+{ }_{36}^{92} \mathrm{Kr}+2{ }_{0}^{1} \mathrm{n}$
Sum of atomic number of reactants $=$ sum of atomic masses of products.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: According to Arrhenius equation, the rate constant $(\mathrm{k})$ is related to temperature $(\mathrm{T})$ as
questions.solution: $\ln \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}\right]$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{I}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HI}(\mathrm{g})$, the equilibrium constant $\mathrm{Kp}$ changes with
questions.solution:
The equilibrium constant $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}$ will change with temperature for the reaction
$\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{I}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}_{(\mathrm{g})}$
Catalyst does not alter the state of equilibrium.
Equilibrium constant depends only upon temperature. The relation of $k$ with temp can be shown as
$\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}\right]$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The order of the reaction
$\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}(\mathrm{~g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \mathrm{is}$
questions.solution: It is a first order reaction because rate of reaction $\propto\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
A reaction : $A_{2}+B \rightarrow$ Products, involves the following mechanism :
$A_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 A$ (fast)
( $\bar{A}$ being the intermediate)
$A+B \longrightarrow_{k_{2}} \rightarrow$ Products (slow). The rate law consistent to this mechanism is :
questions.solution:
From slow step :
rate $=k_{2}[A][B]$
From fast step :
$K_{e}=\frac{[A]^{2}}{\left[A_{2}\right]} \text { or }[A]=K_{e}^{1 / 2}[A]^{1 / 2}$
From (i) and (ii)
$\text { rate }=k_{2} k_{e}^{1 / 2}\left[A_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}[B]=k\left[A_{2}\right]^{1 / 2}[B]$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The reactions with low activation energy are always
questions.solution: The reactions with low activation energies are always fast whereas the reactions with high activation energy are always slow.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The half-life of two samples are $0.1$ and $0.8 \mathrm{~s}$. Their respective concentration are 400 and 50 respectively. The order of the reaction is
questions.solution:
It is known,
$\frac{\left(\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right)_{1}}{\left(\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right)_{2}}=\left[\frac{\mathrm{a}_{2}}{\mathrm{a}_{1}}\right]^{(\mathrm{n}-1)}$
Here, $\mathrm{n}=$ order of the reaction
Given, $\left(\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right)_{1}=0.1 \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{a}_{1}=400$ $\left(\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}\right)_{2}=0.8 \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{a}_{2}=50$
On putting the values,
$\frac{0.1}{0.8}=\left[\frac{50}{400}\right]^{(\mathrm{n}-1)}$
Taking log on both sides
$\begin{array}{l}
\log \frac{0.1}{0.8}=(\mathrm{n}-1) \log \frac{50}{400} \\
\log \frac{1}{8}=(\mathrm{n}-1) \log \frac{1}{8} \\
\mathrm{n}-1=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=2
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reaction,
The reaction is of first order in each diagram, with an equilibrium constant of $10^{4}$. For the conversion of chair form to boat form $\mathrm{e}^{-E a / R T}=$ $4.35 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~m}$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ with pre-exponential factor of $10^{12} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. Apparent rate constant $(=k A / k B)$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{Ae}^{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}} / \mathrm{RT}} \\
&=10^{12} \times 4.35 \times 10^{-8} \\
&=4.35 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$
Also equilibrium constant, 
$\mathrm{k}=\frac{\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}}}=10^{4}$ 
$\therefore \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}} \times 10^{4}=4.35 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A chemical reaction wascarried out at $320 \mathrm{~K}$ and $300 \mathrm{~K}$. The rate constants were found to be $\mathrm{k}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{k}_{2}$ respectively. Then
questions.solution: As we know that for every $10^{\circ}$ rise in temperature, rate constant, $k$ becomes doubled. Hence, on rising the temperature $20^{\circ}$, the rate constant will be four times, i.e., $k_{1}=4 k_{2} \Rightarrow k_{2}=\frac{1}{4} k_{1}=0.25 k_{1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $A(g) \longrightarrow B(g)$ is a first order reaction. The initial concentration of $A$ is $0.2 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$. After 10 minutes the concentration of $B$ is found to be $0.18 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$. The rate constant (in $\min ^{-1}$ ) for the reaction is
questions.solution:
$A(g) \longrightarrow B(g)$
Given, concentration of $A,[A]_0=0.2 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
Concentration of $B$ after 10 minute
$=0.18 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
$[B]$ formed $=[A]$ reacted
Hence, concentration of $A$ after 10 min
$=0.20-0.18=0.02 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
Rate constant is given by formula.
$\begin{aligned} k & =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{[A]_0}{[A]}=\frac{2.303}{10} \log \frac{0.20}{0.02} \\ & =\frac{2.303}{10} \log 100 \\ k & =\frac{2.303}{10} \log 10^{10} \\ k & =2.303 \mathrm{~min}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The half-life of a reaction is inversely proportional to the square of the initial concentration of the reactant. Then the order of the reaction is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{a}^2}$
We know that $t_{1 / 2} \propto \frac{1}{a^{n-1}}$ i.e. $\mathrm{n}=3$
Thus reaction is of $3^{\text {rd }}$ order.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q:
Consider the following changes
$\begin{array}{l}
M(s) \longrightarrow M(g) \quad \ldots(1) \\
M(g) \longrightarrow M^{2+}(g)+2 e^{-} \quad \ldots(2) \\
M(g) \longrightarrow M^{+}(g)+e^{-} \quad \ldots(3) \\
M^{+}(g) \longrightarrow M^{2+}(g)+e^{-} \ldots(4) \\
M(g) \longrightarrow M^{2+}(g)+2 e^{-} \ldots(5)
\end{array}$
The second ionisation energy of $M$ could be determined from the energy values associated with
questions.solution:
The amount of energy required to take out an electron from the monopositive cation is called second ionisation energy
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{M}^{2+}(\mathrm{g})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} ...(v) \\ 
\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{M}^{+}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{e}^{-} ...(iii)
\end{array}$
On subtracting eq(iii) form eq. (v) we get,
$\mathrm{M} \longrightarrow \mathrm{M}^{2+}+\mathrm{e}^{-}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Arrange in decreasing order, the energy of $2 s$ orbital in the following atoms $\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{Li}, \mathrm{Na}$ and K .
options: $E_{2 s}(\mathrm{H}) \gt E_{2 s}(\mathrm{Li}) \gt E_{2s}(\mathrm{Na}) \gt E_{2 s}(\mathrm{~K})$
$E_{2 s}(\mathrm{H}) \gt E_{2 s}(\mathrm{Na}) \gt E_{2 s}(\mathrm{Li}) \gt E_{2 s}(\mathrm{~K})$
$E_{2 s}(\mathrm{H}) \gt E_{2 s}(\mathrm{Na})=E_{2 s}(\mathrm{~K}) \gt E_{2 s}(\mathrm{Li})$
$E_{2 s}(\mathrm{~K}) \gt E_{2 s}(\mathrm{Na}) \gt E_{2 s}(\mathrm{Li}) \gt E_{2 s}(\mathrm{H})$
questions.solution:
The correct order (decreasing) of energy of $2 s$ orbital for given atom is
$E_{2 s}(\mathrm{H}) \gt E_{2 s}(\mathrm{Li}) \gt E_{2 s}(\mathrm{Na}) \gt E_{2 s}(\mathrm{~K})$
The reason is that as atomic number (nuclear charge) increases, all the orbitals are pulled closer to the nucleus. Closes in an orbital to the nucleus, less is its energy.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Among the elements Ca, Mg, P and Cl, the order of increasing atomic radii is
questions.solution:
Atomic radii increases, as the number of shells increases. Thus, on moving down a group atomic radii increases.
The electronic configuration of the given element is
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{Mg}_{12}=[\mathrm{Ne}] 3 s^{2} \\
\mathrm{Ca}_{20}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 s^{2} \\
\mathrm{p}_{15}=[\mathrm{Ne}] 3 s^{2} 3 p^{3} \\
\mathrm{Cl}_{17}=[\mathrm{Ne}] 3 s^{2} 3 p^{5}
\end{array}
$$
On the other hand, on increasing the number of electron in the same shell, the atomic radii decreases because effective nuclear charge is increases. In $\mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{C} 1$, the number of electrons are increasing in the same shell, thus the order of their atomic radii is $\mathrm{C} 1 < \mathrm{P} < \mathrm{Mg}$
In case of Ca, the electron is entering in higher shell. So, its atomic radii is highest. Thus, the order of radii is
$$
\mathrm{Cl} < \mathrm{P} < \mathrm{Mg} < \mathrm{Ca}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which of the following order in respect to ionisation energy is correct?
questions.solution:
lonisation energy is the energy required to remove an electron from outer shell of an atom. Since, on moving from left to right in a period, size decreases, thus larger amount of energy is required to remove an electron, i.e. ionisation energy increases.
Although the size of O -atom is smaller as compared to N but the ionisation energy of N is higher.
This is because in case of N, the electron is to be removed from stable half filled $2 p^3$ orbital. Hence, the correct order is $N \gt O \lt F$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Calculate the energy needed to convert three moles of sodium atoms in the gaseous state to sodium ions. The ionization energy of sodium is $495 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}-$
questions.solution:
Energy needed to convert 1 mole of sodium $(\mathrm{g})$ to sodium ions $=495 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\therefore$ Energy needed to convert 3 moles of $\mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{g})$ to $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions $=495 \times 3=1485 \mathrm{~kJ}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: If the ionisation energy and electron affinity of an element are 275 and $86 \mathrm{kcal} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ respectively, then the electronegativity of the element on the Mulliken scale is
questions.solution:
According to Mulliken, electronegativity of an atom is average of ionization energy and electron affinity (in $\mathrm{eV}$ ).
$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{IE}+\mathrm{EA}}{2}$
If ionization energy and electron affinity are in kcalmol $^{-1}$,
$\mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{IE}+\mathrm{EA}}{125}=\frac{275+86}{125}=288$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which of the following species has highest nucleophilicity?
questions.solution: Lower the electronegativity, higher is the tendency of the atom/group to donate its pair of electrons and hence higher is nucleophilicity. Now among $F(4.0), O(3.5), N(3.0)$ and $C(2.5), C$ has the lowest electronegativity and hence, $\mathrm{CH}_3^{-}$ has highest nucleophilicity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which one of the following order is correct for the first ionisation energies of the elements?
questions.solution:
Along a period first ionisation energy increases. Thus, the first IE of the elements of the second period should follow the order $\mathrm{Be} < \mathrm{B} < \mathrm{N} < \mathrm{O}$
But in practice. The first IE of these elements follows the order $\mathrm{B} < \mathrm{Be} < \mathrm{O} < \mathrm{N}$ The lower IE of B than that of Be is because in $\mathrm{B}\left(1 s^{2}, 2 s^{2} 2 p^{1}\right)$, electron is to be removed from $2 p$ which is easy while in $\operatorname{Be}\left(1 s^{2}, 2 s^{2}\right)$, electron is to be removed from $2 s$ which is difficult. The low IE of $\mathrm{O}$ than that of $\mathrm{N}$ is because of the half-filled $2 p$ or bitals in $\mathrm{N}$ $\left(1 s^{2}, 2 s^{2} 2 p^{3}\right)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The radius of $\mathrm{La}^{3+}$ (Atomic number of $\mathrm{La}=57$ ) is $1.06 Å$. Which one of the following given values will be closest to the radius of $\mathrm{Lu}^{3+}$ (Atomic number of $\mathrm{Lu}=71$ ) ?
questions.solution:
Ionic radii $\propto \frac{1}{z}$
Thus, $\frac{z_{2}}{z_{1}} \Rightarrow \frac{1.06}{\left(\text { Ionic radii of } \mathrm{Lu}^{3+}\right)}=\frac{71}{57}$
$\Rightarrow$ Ionic radii of $\mathrm{Lu}^{3+}=0.85 Å$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q:
Match List-I with List-II.
$\begin{array}{l|l|ll}\hline & \text{List I (Atoms) } & & \text{List II (Properties) } \\ \hline \text{A.} & \text{He} & & \text{I.  High electron affinity} \\ \hline \text{B.} & \text{Cl} & & \text{II.  Most electropositive element} \\ \hline \text{C.} & \text{Ca} & & \text{III.  Strong reducing agent} \\ \hline \text{D.} & \text{Li} & & \text{IV.  Highest ionisation energy} \\ \hline\end{array}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
questions.solution:
The correct match is A-IV, B-I, C-II, D-III
- He has highest ionisation energy due to inert gas configuration.
- Cl has highest electron affinity among given atoms.
- Ca is the most electropositive element among given atoms.
- Li is strongest reducing agent among given atoms.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Group 15 elements have more electron gain enthalpy than group 16-elements. The correct reason for this is
questions.solution: The atoms having half-filled and fully filled orbitals are comparatively more stable, hence more energy is required to remove the electron from such atoms. Therefore group 15 have more I.E. than gp. 16 elements.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the silver plating of copper, \(\mathrm{K}\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]\) is used instead of \(\mathrm{AgNO}_3\). The reason is
options: A thin layer of \(\mathrm{Ag}\) is formed on \(\mathrm{Cu}\)
Less availability of \(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\)ions, as Cu cannot displace \(\mathrm{Ag}\) from \(\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]^{-}\)ion
questions.solution:
In the silver plating of copper, \(\mathrm{K}\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]\) is used instead of \(\mathrm{AgNO}_3\). Copper being more electropositive readily precipitate silver from their salt solution
\(\mathrm{Cu}+2 \mathrm{AgNO}_3 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)_2+\mathrm{Ag}\)
whereas in \(\mathrm{K}\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]\) solution a complex anion \(\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]^{-}\)is formed and hence \(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\)are less available in the solution and therefore copper cannot displace \(\mathrm{Ag}\) from its complex ion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: \(\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)_2\right] \mathrm{Cl}\) exhibits
options: linkage isomerism, ionization isomerism and geometrical isomerism
ionization isomerism, geometrical isomerism and optical isomerism
linkage isomerism, geometrical isomerism and optical isomerism
linkage isomerism, ionization isomerism and optical isomerism
questions.solution:
The given compound may have linkage isomerism due to presence of \(\mathrm{NO}_2\) group which may be in the form \(-\mathrm{NO}_2\) or \(-\mathrm{ONO}\). It may have ionisation isomerism due to presence of two ionisable group \(-\mathrm{NO}_2 \&-\mathrm{Cl}\). It may have geometrical isomerism in the form of cis-trans form as follows : \(\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4 \mathrm{Cl}\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)\right] \mathrm{NO}_2 \&\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)_2\right] \mathrm{Cl}-\) - ionisation isomers. \(\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)_2\right] \mathrm{Cl} \&\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5(\mathrm{ONO})_2 \mathrm{Cl}\right.\) -Linkage isomers
Geometrical isomers

questions.q:
Choose the complex which is paramagnetic
(i) $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}$
(ii) $\mathrm{K}_{3}\left[\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]$
(iii) $\mathrm{K}_{3}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]$
(iv) $\mathrm{K}_{2}\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]$
questions.solution:
(i) $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}$
(ii) $\left[\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]^{3-}$
(iii) $\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]^{3-}$
(iv) $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}$
Thus complexes (i), (ii) and (iii) are paramagnetic species.

questions.solution: \(\mathrm{Ti}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6^{3+}\) has one unpaired electron in its d-subshell which gives rise to d-d transition to impart colour.

questions.q: In the silver plating of copper, $\mathrm{K}\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}\right]$ is used instead of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3} .$ The reason is
options: a thin layer of $\mathrm{Ag}$ is formed on $\mathrm{Cu}$
less availability of $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ions, as Cu cannot displace Ag from $\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}\right]^{-}$ion
questions.solution:
In the silver plating of copper, $\mathrm{K}\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}\right]$ is used instead of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$. Copper being more electropositive readily precipitate silver from their salt solution
$$
\mathrm{Cu}+2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+\mathrm{Ag}
$$
whereas in $\mathrm{K}\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}\right]$ solution a complex anion $\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}\right]^{-}$is formed and hence $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ are less available in the solution and therefore copper cannot displace $\mathrm{Ag}$ from its complex ion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Point out incorrect stability order
questions.solution:
Increasing order of stability
(a) $\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4}\right]^{2+} < \left[\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{en})_{2}\right]^{2+}$
$ < \left[\mathrm{Cu}(\text { trein })^{2+}\right.$
Their formation of entropy increases in the same order. Ligand denticity is increased.
(b) $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+} < \left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)_{6}\right]^{3-}$
$ < \left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$
$\because \quad \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is weaker ligand than $\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{-}$.
$\therefore \quad$ The correct stability order is
$\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)^{3+} < \left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+} < \left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)_{6}\right]^{3-}\right.$
(c) $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+} < \left[\mathrm{Rh}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$
$\mathrm{Z}_{\text {of }}$ value increases from $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ to $\mathrm{Ir}^{3+}$,
(d) $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{1+} < \left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}$
O.S of Cr atom increases from $+1$ to $+3$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The total number of possible isomers for squareplanar $\left[\mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{Cl})\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)(\mathrm{SCN})\right]^{2-}$ is:
questions.solution: The square planar complex of the type [Mabcd] $^{n \pm}$, where all four ligands are different, has 3 geometrical isomers. But if one of the ligands is ambidentate, then $2 \times 3=6$ geometrical isomers are possible. But if two ligands are ambidentate, then $4 \times 3=12$ geometrical isomers are possible. In the given example, $\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}$and $\mathrm{SCN}^{-}$are ambidentate ligands.

questions.solution:
For writing IUPAC name of a co-ordination compound we first write the name of $(+)$ ive complex here. $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right]^{2+}$
The names of ligands will come first in alphabatical order, followed by metal ion with its oxidation state written in bracket or parentheis in Roman number i.e. Co (III) here. IUPAC name for cationic complex $\rightarrow$ Pentaamine chloro cobalt (III).
This will follow the name of anion with a gap. i.e. Pentaamine chloro cobalt (III) chloride.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of unpaired electrons calculated in $\left\{\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right\}^{3+}$ and $\left\{\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{F}_{6}\right)\right\}^{3-}$ are
questions.solution:
In $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$ and $\left\{\mathrm{CoF}_{6}\right\}^{3-}$ both the oxidation state of cobalt ion $\mathrm{is}+3$. In first case $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is the neutral ligand which is a strong field ligand. hence the electrons in Co (+III) i.e. $4 \mathrm{~s}^{0} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{6}$ get paired to form inner orbital complex. Hence no unpaired electron.
On the other hand $\mathrm{F}^{-}$is a weak field ligand hence it forms an outer orbital complex with 4 unpaired electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following sets correctly represents the increase in the paramagnetic property of the ions?
questions.solution:
Paramagnetic nature depends upon the number of unpaired electrons. Higher the number of unpaired electrons, higher the paramagnetic property will be.
$\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^{9}$, no. of unpairedelectrons $=1$
$\mathrm{V}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^{3}$, no. ofunpaired electrons $=3$
$\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^{4}$, no. of unpaired electrons $=4$
$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 Å^{5}$, no. of unpairedelectrons $=5$
Hence, correct order is
$$
\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} < \mathrm{V}^{2+} < \mathrm{Cr}^{2+} < \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not a characteristic of transition elements?
questions.solution: Transition elements or d-block elements have variable oxidation states, they form coloured compounds because of partially filled dorbitals and also because of small size they form interstitial compounds. They are stable elements and does not show radio activity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The purple colour of $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ is due to the transition
questions.solution:
The permanganate ion has an intense purple colour. Mn (+ VII) has a d $^{0}$ configuration. So the colour arises from charge transfer and not from d-d spectra. In $\mathrm{MnO}_{4}{ }^{-}$an electron is momentarily changing $\mathrm{O}^{-}$- to $\mathrm{O}^{-}$and reducing the oxidation state of the metal from $\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{VII})$ to Mn (VI). Charge transfer requires that the energy levels on the two different atoms are fairly close.
$\mathrm{O}=(8)=2,6$
$\quad\quad\quad\quad$ K L
$\mathrm{Mn}(25)=2,8,15$
$\quad\quad\quad\quad$ K L M
hence the charge transfer occurs from $\mathrm{L} \rightarrow \mathrm{M}$

questions.solution:
(d) $\operatorname{In} \mathrm{K}_{4}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cu}$ is in $+1$ oxidation state hence has no unpaired electron hence colourless and diamagnetic.
(b) $\mathrm{In}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2}\left[\mathrm{TiCl}_{6}\right] \mathrm{Ti}$ is in $+4$ oxidation state. hence has no unpaired electron hence colourless and diamagnetic.
(c) $\operatorname{In} \operatorname{VOSO}_{4}, V$ is in $+4$ oxidation state hence has one unpaired electron, thus it is coloured and paramagnetic.
(a) $\operatorname{In} \mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$, Cr is in $+6$ oxidation. hence has no unpaired electron and thus it is diamagnetic. Though $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ has no unpaired electron but it is coloured. This is due to charge transfer spectrum.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The magnetic moment of lanthanide ions is determined from which one of the following relation?
questions.solution:
In case of lanthanoids, 4 f orbitals lie too deep and hence the magnetic effect of the motion of the electron in its orbital is not quenched out. Here spin contribution $\mathrm{S}$ and orbital contribution $\mathrm{L}$ couple together to give a new quantum number $\mathrm{J}$.
Thus magnetic moment of lanthanoids is given by, $\mu=g \sqrt{J(J+1)}$
where $\mathrm{J}=\mathrm{L}-\mathrm{S}$ when the shell is less than half fill $\mathrm{J}=\mathrm{L}+\mathrm{S}$ when the shell is more than half fill and 
$\mathrm{g}=1 \frac{1}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{S}+1)-\mathrm{L}(\mathrm{L}+1)}{2 \mathrm{~J}(\mathrm{~J}+1)}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following has maximumnumber of unpaired electrons?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}=1 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{p}^{6}$ (No unpaired electrons) 
$\mathrm{Ti}^{3+}=1 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{p}^{6}, 3 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 3 \mathrm{p}^{6}, 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}, 3 \mathrm{~d}^{1}$ (One unpaired electrons) 
$\mathrm{V}^{3+}=1 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{p}^{6}, 3 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 3 \mathrm{p}^{6}, 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}, 3 \mathrm{~d}^{2}$ (Two unpaired electrons)
$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}=1 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{p}^{6}, 3 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 3 \mathrm{p}^{6}, 4 \mathrm{~s}^{0}, 3 \mathrm{~d}^{6}$ (Four unpaired electrons)
$\therefore \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ has highest number of unpaired electrons.

$\mathrm{Sc}^{3+}(\mathrm{Z}=18): 1 s^2, 2 s^2 p^6, 3 s^2 p^6 d^0, 4 s^0$; no unpaired electron.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How many moles of potassium dichromate oxidises 1 mole of ferrous oxalate in acidic medium?
questions.solution:
Potassium dichromate oxidises ferrous oxalate in acidic medium as follow,
$2 \mathrm{FeC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 +\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+4 \mathrm{CO}_2+7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
Since, 2 moles of $\mathrm{FeC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ are oxidised by 1 mole of $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}$ therefore, 1 mole of $\mathrm{FeC}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ will be oxidised by 0.5 mole of $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: \(\mathrm{Zn}\) amalgam is prepared by electrolysis of aqueous \(\mathrm{ZnCl}_2\) using \(\mathrm{Hg}\) cathode ( \(9 \mathrm{gm}\) ). How much current is to be passed through \(\mathrm{ZnCl}_2\) solution for 1000 seconds to prepare a \(\mathrm{Zn}\) Amalgam with \(25 \% \mathrm{Zn}\) by wt. \((\mathrm{Zn}=65.4\) )
questions.solution:
Let \(\mathrm{x}\) gm of \(\mathrm{Zn}\) deposit on \(9 \mathrm{gm}\) of \(\mathrm{Hg}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \% \text { of } \mathrm{Zn} \text { in Amalgam }=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{9+\mathrm{x}} \times 100=25 \\
& \therefore \mathrm{x}=3 \mathrm{gm}
\end{aligned}\)
Eq. of \(\mathrm{Zn}=\frac{3 \times 2}{65.4}\)
Current \(=\frac{6}{65.4} \times \frac{96500}{1000}=8.85 \mathrm{amp}\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
4.5 g of aluminium (Atomic mass 27 amu ) is deposited at cathode from $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ solution by a certain quantity of electric charge.
The volume of hydrogen produced at STP from $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions in the solution by the same quantity of electric charge will be
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Al}^{3+}+\underset{3 \mathrm{~F}}{3 \mathrm{e}^{-}} \longrightarrow \underset{1 \mathrm{~mole}}{\mathrm{Al}}$
1 mole of Al , i.e. 27 g of Al are deposited by charge $=3 \mathrm{~F}$
$\therefore 4.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of Al will be deposited by charge
$=\frac{3}{27} \times 4.5 \mathrm{~F}=0.5 \mathrm{~F}$
$2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\underset{2 \mathrm{~F}}{2 \mathrm{e}^{-}} \longrightarrow \underset{1 \mathrm{~mole}}{\mathrm{H}_2}$
2 F of charge liberate $\mathrm{H}_2=1 \mathrm{~mol}=22.4$ litre at STP
$\therefore 0.5 \mathrm{~F}$ of charge will liberate $\mathrm{H}_2=\frac{22.4}{2} \times 0.5$
$=5.6 \mathrm{~L}$ at STP

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The resistance of \(1 \mathrm{~N}\) solution of acetic acid is \(250 \mathrm{ohm}\), when measured in a cell of cell constant \(1.15 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\). The equivalent conductance (in ohm \({ }^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2\) equiv-1) of \(1 \mathrm{~N}\) acetic acid will be
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{k}=\frac{\text { Cell constant }}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{1.15}{250} \\
& \Lambda_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{1.15}{250} \times \frac{1000}{1}=4.6
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The standard e.m.f. of a galvanic cell involving cell reaction with \(n=2\) is found to be \(0.295 \mathrm{~V}\) at \(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\). The equilibrium constant of the reaction would be (Given \(\mathrm{F}=96500 \mathrm{C} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\); \(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\))
questions.solution:
\(\mathrm{E}^{\circ}=\frac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log _{10} \mathrm{~K}\)
Here, \(\mathrm{n}=2, \mathrm{E}^{\circ}=0.295\)
\(\therefore \log _{10} \mathrm{~K}=\frac{2 \times 0.295}{0.0591}=10 \text { or } \mathrm{K}=10^{10}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: To deposit $0.634 \mathrm{~g}$ of copper by electrolysis of aqueous cupric sulphate solution, the amount of electricity required (in coulombs) is
questions.solution:
In the electrolysis of cupric sulphate, the reaction that occurs at cathode is
$$
\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}
$$
Thus $2 \mathrm{~F}$ or $2 \times 96500 \mathrm{C}$ of electricity is required to deposit
$=1 \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{Cu}=63.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Cu}$
It means that to deposit $63.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Cu}$, the amount of electricity required $=2 \times 96500 \mathrm{C}$
So, to deposit $0.634 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Cu}$, the amount of
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { electricity required }=\frac{2 \times 96500}{63.5} \times 0.634 \\
& \approx 1930 \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The resistance of $0.01 \mathrm{~N}$ solution of an electrolyte was found to be $220 \mathrm{ohm}$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ using a conductivity cell with a cell constant of $0.88 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$. The value of equivalent conductance of solution is $-$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \Lambda_{\mathrm{eq}} &=\kappa \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{~N}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}} \times \frac{l}{\mathrm{a}} \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{~N}} \\ &=\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}} \times \text { cell constant } \times \frac{1000}{\mathrm{~N}} \\ &=\frac{1}{220} \times 0.88 \times \frac{1000}{0.01} \\ &=400 \mathrm{mhocm}^{2} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{eq}^{-1} \end{aligned}$

questions.q: The emf of a particular voltaic cell with the cell reaction $\mathrm{Hg}_{2}^{2+}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{Hg}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}$ is $0.65 \mathrm{~V}$. The maximum electrical work of this cell when $0.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ is consumed.
questions.solution:
$W_{\max }=-n . F E$;
$\mathrm{W}_{\max }=-2 \times 96500 \times 0.65=-1.25 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~J}$
$0.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{H}_{2}=0.25 \mathrm{~mole}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell, the cell reaction is
$2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\ell)$
If $\Delta \mathrm{G}_{f}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)=-237.2 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, then emf of this cell is
questions.solution:
We know $\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad E^{\circ} &=-\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{n F} \\
&=+\frac{237.2 \times 1000 \mathrm{~J}}{2 \times 96500}=1.23 \mathrm{~V} \quad[\because n=2]
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.q: The equivalent conductivity of a solution containing $2.54 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ per $\mathrm{L}$ is $91.0 \mathrm{~W}^{-1}$ $\mathrm{cm}^{2} \mathrm{eq}^{-1}$. Its conductivity would be
questions.solution:
We know that,
$\begin{array}{l}
\kappa=\Lambda_{\mathrm{eq}} \cdot \mathrm{C} \\
\text { Given, } \Lambda \mathrm{eq}=91.0 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{eq}^{-1} \\
\kappa=\left(91 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{eq}^{-1}\right) \\
=\left(\frac{2.54}{159 / 2 \times 1000} \mathrm{eq} \mathrm{cm}^{-3}\right) \\
=2.9 \times 10^{-3} \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}
\end{array}$

questions.q: A constant current of 30 A is passed through an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ for a time of $1.00 \mathrm{~h}$. What is the volume of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ gas at STP produced?
questions.solution:
Due to electrolysis
$\mathrm{OH}^{-}$formed $=\mathrm{NaOH}$ formed $=\mathrm{Zit}$ $=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{96500} \times \mathrm{i} \times \mathrm{t}$ $=\frac{40}{96500} \times 30 \times 1 \times 60 \times 60=44.77 \mathrm{~g}$ $=\frac{44.77}{40}=1.12 \mathrm{~mol}$ $\mathrm{a}_{2}$ formed $=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ $=\frac{1.12}{2}=0.56 \mathrm{~mol}$ $=0.56 \times 22.4 \mathrm{~L}$ at $\mathrm{STP}=12.54 \mathrm{~L}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
Chemistry
questions.q: Equivalent conductances of \(\mathrm{NaCl}, \mathrm{HCl}\) and \(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COONa}\) at infinitedilution are \(126.45,426.16 \mathrm{and}\) \(91 \mathrm{dhm}^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2\) respectively. Theequivalent conductance of \(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}\) at infinite dilution would be
questions.solution:
By Kohlraush's law, \(\wedge_{\text {eq }}^0 \mathrm{NaCl}=126.45\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \lambda_{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}^0+\lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^0=126.45 \\
& \lambda_{\mathrm{H}^{+}}^0+\lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^0=426.16 . \\
& \lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OO}^{-}}^0+\lambda_{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}^0=91
\end{aligned}\)
on adding (2) and (3) then subtract (1) from it
\(\begin{aligned}
& \lambda_{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}}^0+\lambda_{\mathrm{Na}^{+}}^0=517.16-126.45 \\
& \wedge_{\left(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}\right)}^0=390.71 \mathrm{ohm}^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How long should water be electrolysed by passing through 100 amperes current so that oxygen released can completely burn 27.66 g of diborane? (Weight of $\mathrm{B}=10.8 \mathrm{u}$ )
questions.solution:
The complete electrolysis reaction is as follow,
$\mathrm{B}_2 \mathrm{H}_6+3 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{~B}_2 \mathrm{O}_3+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
27.66 g of $\mathrm{B}_2 \mathrm{H}_6=1 \mathrm{~mole}$ of $\mathrm{B}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$
It requires 3 moles of $\mathrm{O}_2$ for complete burning
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2$
$\frac{1}{2}$ mole of $\mathrm{O}_2$ requires 2 Faraday.
$\therefore 3 \mathrm{~mole}_2$ will require $=12$ Faraday
$\begin{aligned} 12 \times 96500 & =i \times t \\ 12 \times 96500 & =100 \times t \\ t & =3.2 \mathrm{~h}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If \(0.5 \mathrm{amp}\) current is passed through acidified silver nitrate solution for 100 minutes. The mass of silver deposited on cathode, is (eq.wt.of silver nitrate \(=108\))
questions.solution:
Given current (i) \(=0.5 \mathrm{amp}\);
Time \((t)=100\) minutes \(\times 60=6000 \mathrm{sec}\)
Equivalent weight of silver nitrate \((\mathrm{E})=108\).
According to Faraday's first law of electrolysis
\(\mathrm{W}=\frac{\text { Eit }}{96500}=\frac{108 \times 0.5 \times 6000}{96500}=3.3575 \mathrm{~g} .\)

questions.q: The molar conductivities of $\mathrm{KCl}$, $\mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{KNO}_{3}$ are 152,128 and $111 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. What is the molar conductivity of $\mathrm{NaNO}_{3} ?$
questions.solution:
Since $\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}$ is formed by the reaction
$\mathrm{Nacl}+\mathrm{KNO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaNO}_{3}+\mathrm{KCl}$
hence, using Kohlrausch's law
$\begin{aligned}
\stackrel{o}{\wedge} \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}} &=\stackrel{o}{\wedge}_{\mathrm{NaCl}}+\stackrel{o}{\wedge}_{\mathrm{KNO}_{3}}-\stackrel{\circ}{\wedge}_{\mathrm{KCl}} \\
&=128+111-152=87 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The electrode potentials for
$\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})$
and $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s})$ are $+0.15 \mathrm{~V}$ and $+0.50 \mathrm{~V}$, respectively. The value of $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}}^{\circ} / \mathrm{Cu}$ will be:
questions.solution:
$\left.\quad \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+1 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{+} \quad ; \Delta \mathrm{G}_{1}^{0}=-\mathrm{n}_{1} \mathrm{E}_{1}^{0} \mathrm{~F}\right]$
$\frac{\mathrm{Cu}^{+}+1 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu} \quad ; \Delta \mathrm{G}_{2}^{0}=-\mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{E}_{2}^{0} \mathrm{~F}}{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu} \quad \Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=\Delta \mathrm{G}_{1}^{0}+\Delta \mathrm{G}_{2}^{0}}$
$-\mathrm{n} \mathrm{E}^{\circ} \mathrm{F}=-1 \mathrm{n}_{1} \quad \mathrm{E}_{1}^{0} \mathrm{~F}+(-1) \mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{E}_{2}^{0} \mathrm{~F}$
$-\mathrm{n} \mathrm{E}^{\circ} \mathrm{F}=-\mathrm{F}\left(\mathrm{n}_{1} \mathrm{E}_{1}^{0}+\mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{E}_{2}^{0} \mathrm{~F}\right)$
$\mathrm{E}^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{1} \mathrm{E}_{1}^{0}+\mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{E}_{2}^{0}}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{0.15 \times 1+0.50 \times 1}{2}=0.325$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Standard cell voltage for the cell $\mathrm{Pb}\left|\mathrm{Pb}^{2+} \| \mathrm{Sn}^{2+}\right| \mathrm{Sn}$ is $-0.01 \mathrm{~V}$. If the cell is to exhibit $E_{\text {cell }}=0$, the value of $\left[\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}\right] /\left[\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}\right]$ should be antilog of $-$
questions.solution:
Apply Nernst equation to the reaction $\mathrm{Pb}+\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} \rightarrow \mathrm{Pb}^{2+}+\mathrm{Sn}$
or $\quad E^{\circ}+\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}\right]}=E_{\text {cell }}$
or $\log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}\right]}=\frac{0.01 \times 2}{0.059}=0.3\left(\because E_{\text {cell }}=0\right)$
or $\frac{\left[\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}\right]}=$ antilog $(0.3)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The electrochemical cell stops working after sometime because:
options: electrode potential of both the electrodes becomes zero
electrode potential of both the electrodes becomes equal
one of the electrodes is eaten away
questions.solution:
As we know that
$\Delta \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cathode }}-\mathrm{E}_{\text {anode }}$
when $\mathrm{E}_{\text {cathode }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {anode }}$ $\Delta \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=0$
If $\Delta \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=0$ no net reaction occurs. The reactants and products are at equilibrium and no current will flow.
Note that it is only possible to obtain electrical work from a system that is not at equilibrium. In order for current to flow, there must be a net reaction occurring. As the oxidation- reduction reaction proceeds toward equilibrium, and the concentrations of the reacting species approach their equilibrium values, the EMF of the cell decreases to zero. When the system is at equilibrium, the cell potential is zero and we have a dead battery.

questions.q: The following reactions take place in the blast furnace in the preparation of impure iron. Identify the reaction pertaining to the formation of the slag.
questions.solution:
A slag is an easily fusible material which is formed when gangue still present in the roasted or the calcined ore combines with the flux. For example, in the metallurgy of iron, $\mathrm{CaO}$ (flux) combines with silica gangue to form easily fusible calcium silicate, $\left(\mathrm{CaSiO}_{3}\right)$ slag.
$$
\mathrm{CaO}+\mathrm{SiO}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CaSiO}_{3} \text { (slag) }
$$
