questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The product $\mathrm{P}$ for the above given reaction will be
questions.solution:
Nitric acid not only nitrates, but also oxidizes the highly reactive ring as well, with loss of much material as dark-coloured tar. Furthermore, in the strongly acidic nitration medium, the amine is converted into anilinium ion $\left(-\mathrm{NH}_{3}{ }^{+}\right)$; substitution is thus controlled not by the $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group but by the $-\mathrm{NH}_{3}^{+}$group which, because of its positive charge, directs the entering group to the meta- position instead of ortho, and para.
However, all these difficulties are overcome by protecting the amino group by acetylation, with either acetyl chloride or acetic anhydride. Acetylation $\left(-\mathrm{NH}_{2} \longrightarrow\right.$ $\left.\mathrm{NHCOCH}_{3}\right)$ converts $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group to acetamido $\left(-\mathrm{NHCOCH}_{3}\right)$ group which is $o$, $p$-directing but lesser activating toward electrophilic aromatic substitution than the parent $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct order of electrophilic substitution for the compounds given above will be
questions.solution:
An electron releasing group stablilises the carbocation by dispersing its positive charge and thus activates the ring while an electron-withdrawing group destabilises the carbocation by intensifying its positive charge and thus deactivates the ring. $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ being electron releasing group releases electron and thus tend to neutralise positive charge of the ring and itself becomes somewhat positive. The dispersal of positive charge of the ring stabilises carbocation and hence facilitates its formation which ultimately results an increased rate of reaction. Now since such factor is not present in benzene, its carbocation is less stable than (A). Hence aniline undergoes electrophilic substitution at a faster rate than benzene.
On the other hand in $(\mathrm{C})$ the $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group (an electron withdrawing group) intensifies positive charge on the ring and thus destabilises the carbocation and hence its formation becomes difficult which ultimately results a slower reaction. Now since substituent is not present in benzene, its carbocation (B) is relatively more stable than (C). Hence nitrobenzene undergoes electrophilic substitution at a slower rate than benzene.

(iii) Heavy water is more effective solvent than ordinary water

questions.solution: In nuclear reactors heavy water is used as a moderator. It has higher boiling point as compared to the ordinary water. Thus, it is more associated as compared to ordinary water. questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a hydrogen - oxygen fuel cell, combustion of hydrogen occurs to:
options: Create potential difference between the two electrodes
Remove adsorbed oxygen from electrode surfaces
questions.solution:
In hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell, following reactions take place to create potential difference between the two electrodes.
$$
\begin{array}{c}
2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \\
\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \\
\hline \text { Overall reaction }=2 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})
\end{array}
$$
the net reaction is the same as burning (combustion) of hydrogen to form water.

$30 \%$ solution of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ is known as perhydrol
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ decomposes as
$$
2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_{2}
$$
Volume strength of $30 \% \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ solution is 100 i.e., $1 \mathrm{~mL}$ of this solution on decomposition gives $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ oxygen.
$\mathrm{SO}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{3}$ $1 \mathrm{~L} \quad \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~L}$
2L $\quad 1 \mathrm{~L} \quad 2 \mathrm{~L}$
Since, $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of oxygen is obtained by
$$
=1 \mathrm{~mL} \text { of } \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}
$$
$\therefore 1000 \mathrm{~mL}$ of oxygen will be obtained by
$$
=\frac{1}{100} \times 1000 \mathrm{~mL} \text { of } \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}=10 \mathrm{mLof} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}
$$

$\mathrm{~S}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{Sp}}}{4}\right)^{1 / 3} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{32 \times 10^{-12}}{4}\right)^{1 / 3}$
$=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Calculate $\mathrm{pH}$ of a buffer prepared by adding 10 $\mathrm{mL}$ of $0.10 \mathrm{M}$ acetic acid to $20 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ sodium acetate $\left[\mathrm{p} K_{a}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right)=4.74\right]$
questions.solution:
$\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right]=\text { millimoles of } \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$
$=0.1 \times 10=1.0$
$\begin{array}{l}
{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}\right]=\text { millimoles of } \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}} \\
=0.1 \times 20=2.0
\end{array}$
From, Henderson Hasselbalch equation,
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{\text { [conjugate base] }}{[\text { acid }]} \\
=4.74+\log \frac{2}{1}=4.74+0.30=5.04
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
At $298 K$, the conductivity of a saturated solution of AgCl in water is $2.6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{Scm}^{-1}$. Its solubility product at $298 K$.
Given : $\lambda^{\infty}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right)=63.0 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$,
$\lambda^{\infty}\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)=67.0 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
questions.solution:
Solubility $S=\frac{1000 k}{\lambda_{\mathrm{AgCl}}^{\circ}}=\frac{1000 \times 2.6 \times 10^{-6}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+}}^{\circ}+\lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}}$
$$
=\frac{2.6 \times 10^{-3}}{63+67}=2 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} ; K_{\mathrm{sp}}=S^{2}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A saturated solution of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ in $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCl}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ contains $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ ion concentration of $10^{-23} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$. The solubility product of some sulphides are $\mathrm{CuS}=10^{-44}, \mathrm{FeS}=10^{-14}, \mathrm{MnS}=10^{-15}, \mathrm{CdS}=$ $10^{-25}$. If $0.01 \mathrm{M}$ solution of these salts in $1 \mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{HCl}$ are saturated with $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$, which of these will be precipitated?
questions.solution:
$\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]=10^{-23} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
$\left[\mathrm{M}^{2+}\right]=10^{-2} \mathrm{M}$
Ionic product, $\mathrm{K}_{1 \mathrm{P}}=\left[\mathrm{M}^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right]=10^{-25}$
$\therefore \quad$ ionic product is greater than $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}$ of CuS and $\mathrm{CdS}$,
Therefore, all except $\mathrm{MnS}$ and $\mathrm{FeS}$ are precipitated.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{pH}$ of a buffer solution decreases by $0.02$ units when $0.12 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetic acid is added to $250 \mathrm{~mL}$ of a buffer solution of acetic acid and potassium acetate at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The buffer capacity of the solution is
questions.solution:
Buffer capacity, $\beta=\frac{d C_{\mathrm{HA}}}{d_{\mathrm{pH}}}$,
where, $d C_{\mathrm{HA}}=$ no. of moles of acid added per litre
$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\mathrm{pH}} &=\text { change in } \mathrm{pH} \\
d C_{\mathrm{HA}} &=\frac{\text { moles of acetic acid }}{\text { volume }} \\
&=\frac{0.12 / 60}{250 / 1000}=\frac{1}{125} \\
\therefore \beta &=\frac{1 / 125}{0.02}=\frac{1}{2.5}=0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
When copper is treated with a certain concentration of nitric acid, nitric oxide and nitrogen dioxide are liberated in equal volumes according to the equation,
$x \mathrm{Cu}+y \mathrm{HNO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+\mathrm{NO}$ $\quad+\mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ 
The coefficients of $x$ and $y$ are respectively
questions.solution:
Balanced equations are
$3 \mathrm{Cu}+8 \mathrm{HNO}_{3} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+2 \mathrm{NO}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ ...(i)
$\mathrm{Cu}+4 \mathrm{HNO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ ...(ii)
Here $\mathrm{NO}$ and $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ are evolved in equal volumes, hence, on adding Eqs. (i) and (ii)
$\begin{aligned}
4 \mathrm{Cu}+12 \mathrm{HNO}_{3} & \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2} \\
&+2 \mathrm{NO}+2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}$
or $2 \mathrm{Cu}+6 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}$
$\longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+\mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{NO}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
Hence, coefficients $x$ and $y$ of $C u$ and $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ are 2 and 6 respectively.

In a reaction the alkyl part of grignard reagent acts as a nucleophile as carbon is more electronegative than magnesium. Hence the alkyl part will get attached to the electron deficient species.
In $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$, chlorine is more electronegative than phosphorous.
Hence the $\mathrm{Ph}^{-}$will attack the phosphorous in $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$ to form organic phosphine with formula $\mathrm{Ph}_{3} \mathrm{P}$
$3 \mathrm{PhMgBr}+\mathrm{PCl}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ph}_{3} \mathrm{P}+3 \mathrm{MgBrCl}$
$\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad$ Triphenylphosphine

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the given redox reaction, what will be the sum of $x, y, m$ and $n$ ?
$\begin{aligned} x \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+y \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4+z \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow & m \mathrm{Mn}^{2+} +n \mathrm{CO}_2+p \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$
questions.solution:
The half reaction are
(i) $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+}+5 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
(Reduction)
(ii) $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$(Oxidation)
To get balance net reaction, multiply equation (i) by 2 and equation (ii) by 5, then on adding, we get
$2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4+6 \mathrm{H}^{+} \xrightarrow[2 \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}]{}+10 \mathrm{CO}_2+8 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
Thus, $x=2, y=5, m=2, n=10$
$x+y+m+n=2+5+2+10$
$\therefore$ Sum of $x, y, m$ and $n$ is 19.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The oxidation number of oxygen in $\mathrm{KO}_{3}, \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ is
questions.solution:
To find the oxidation number of a given compound we have to equate the charge on the overall compound with the charge on individual atom of which the compound is made of.
In $\mathrm{KO}_{3} . \mathrm{K}$ is an alkali metal. hence its oxidation number is $+1$.
$(+1)+3 \times(\mathrm{x})=0$ or $\quad \mathrm{x}=\frac{-1}{3} \cong-0.33$ 
hence oxidation number of oxygen i.e. $x=-0.33$
In $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}$, again $\mathrm{Na}$ is an alkali metal.
Hence $2 \times(+1)+2 \times x=0$
$x=\frac{-2}{2}=-1$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The equivalent weight of potassium permanganate when it acts as oxidising agent in ferrous ion estimation is
questions.solution:
The oxidation of ferrous ion by $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ takes place in acidic medium as per following reaction
$\begin{array}{l}
2 \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}+8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+10 \mathrm{FeSO}_{4} \\
\rightarrow \mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{MnSO}_{4}+8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+5 \mathrm{Fe}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)_{3} \\
\therefore \text { Eq. mass of } \mathrm{KMnO}_{4} \\
\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\text { changein oxidation number }} \\
=\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{5}=\frac{158}{5}=31.6
\end{array}$

Assertion (A) : $\mathrm{K}$. Rb and Cs form superoxides. 
Reason $(\mathbf{R}):$ The stability of the superoxides increases from ' $\mathrm{K}$ ' to ' $\mathrm{Cs}$ ' due to decrease in lattice energy.
The correct answer is
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is not the correct explanation of $(A)$
(A) is true but (R) is not true
(A) is not true but (R) is true
questions.solution: The species having an $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O}$ bond and $\mathrm{O}$ in an oxidation state of $-\frac{1}{2}$ are super oxides and is represented as $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}$. Usually these are formed by active metals such as $\mathrm{KO}_{2}$, $\mathrm{RbO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{CsO}_{2} .$ For the sal ts of larger anions (like $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}$), lattice energy increases in a group. Since, lattice energy is the driving force for the formation of an ionic compound and its stability, the stability o the superoxides from 'K' to 'Cs' als increases.

questions.q: Excess of NaOH reacts with $\mathrm{Zn}$ to form
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Zn}+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{ZnO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}$
$\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad$ Sod. zincate

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Aluminium reacts with $\mathrm{NaOH}$ and forms compound ' $X$ '. If the coordination number of aluminium in ' $X$ ' is 6 , the correct formula of $X$ is
questions.solution: $2 \mathrm{Al}+2 \mathrm{NaOH}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\begin{array}{c}\text { sodum meta } \\ \text { aluminata }\end{array}}{\mathrm{NaAlO}_{2}}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}$ Sodiummetaaluminate, thus formed, is soluble in water and changes into the complex $\left[\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{OH})_{4}\right]^{-}$, in which coordination number of $\mathrm{Al}$ is 6 .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the solid state, \(\mathrm{MgO}\) has the same structure as that of sodium chloride. The number of oxygens surrounding each magnesium in \(\mathrm{MgO}\) is
questions.solution: Since \(\mathrm{MgO}\) has a rock salt structure. In this structure each cation is surrounded by six anions and vice versa.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The edge length of unit cell of a metal having molecular weight $75 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$ is $5 Å$ which crystallizes in cubic lattice. If the density is $2 \mathrm{~g}$ / cc then find the radius of metal atom. $\left(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=6 \times 10^{23}\right)$. Give the answer in $\mathrm{pm}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An element occuring in the bec structure has $12.08 \times 10^{23}$ unit cells. The total number of atoms of the element in these cells will be
questions.solution: There are two atoms in a $b c c$ unit cell. So, number of atoms in $12.08 \times 10^{23}$ unit cells $=2 \times 12.08 \times 10^{23}=24.16 \times 10^{23}$ atoms.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For orthorhombic system axial ratios are \(a \neq b \neq c\) and the axial angles are
questions.solution: For orthorhombic system \(\alpha=\beta=\gamma=90^{\circ}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A compound $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{q}}$ has cubic close packing (ccp) arrangement of $X$. Its unit cell structure is shown below. The empirical formula of the compound is
questions.solution:
No. of M atoms $=\frac{1}{4} \times 4+1=1+1=2$
No. of $X$ atoms $=\frac{1}{2} \times 6+\frac{1}{8} \times 8=3+1=4$
So, formula $=\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{X}_{4}=\mathrm{MX}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Iron crystallizes in several modifications. At about $911^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the bec ' $\alpha$ ' form undergoes a trasition to fcc ' $\gamma$ ' form. If the distance between the two nearest neighbours is the same in the two forms at the transition temperature, the ratio of the density of iron in fcc form $\left(\rho_{2}\right)$ to that of iron of bec form $\left(\rho_{1}\right)$ at the transition temperature
questions.solution:
In $\alpha-$ form distance between nearest neighbour atom is $\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}_{1}}{2}$.
In $\gamma$ form distance between nearest neighbour atom is $\frac{a_{2}}{\sqrt{2}}$.
$\therefore \quad \frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{a}_{1}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{a}_{2}}{\sqrt{2}}$
(given)
or $\quad \frac{\mathrm{a}_{2}}{\mathrm{a}_{1}}=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}$
$\frac{\rho_{1}}{\rho_{2}}=\frac{z_{1}}{z_{2}}\left(\frac{a_{2}}{a_{1}}\right)^{3}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}\right)^{3}=0.918$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An ionic compound has a unit cell consisting of A ions at the corners of a cube and B ions on the centres of the faces of the cube. The empirical formula for this compound would be
questions.solution:
Number of A ions in the unit cell
$=\frac{1}{8} \times 8=1$
Number of $\mathrm{B}$ ions in the unit cell
$=\frac{1}{2} \times 6=3$
Hence empirical formula of the compound $=\mathrm{AB}_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An alloy of copper, silver and gold is found to have copper atom constituting the ccp lattice. If silver atom occupy the edge centres and gold atom is present at body centred, the alloy has a formula
questions.solution:
In the unit cell number of $\mathrm{Cu}$ atoms $(f c c / c c p)$
$=8 \times \frac{1}{6}+6 \times \frac{1}{2}=4$
As Ag atoms occupying edge centred
$=12 \times \frac{1}{4}=3$
and $\mathrm{Au}$ atoms are presents at the body centred $=1$
$\therefore \quad$ formula, $\mathrm{Cu}_{4} \mathrm{Ag}_{3} \mathrm{Au} .$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A metal has bcc structure and the edge length of its unit cell is $3.04 Å$. The volume of the unit cell in $\mathrm{cm}^{3}$ will be,

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The density of solid argon is $1.65 \mathrm{~g}$ per $\mathrm{cc}$ at $233^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the argon atom is assumed to be a sphere of radius $1.54 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}$, what per cent of solid argon is apparently empty space? $(\mathrm{Ar}=40)$
questions.solution:
Volume of one molecule
$\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^{3}=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(1.54 \times 10^{-8}\right)^{3} \mathrm{~cm}^{3} \\
=1.53 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~cm}^{3}
\end{array}$
Volume of molecules in $1.65 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Ar}$
$=\frac{1.65}{40} \times \mathrm{N}_{0} \times 1.53 \times 10^{-23}=0.380 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$
Volume of solid containing $1.65 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Ar}=1 \mathrm{~cm}^{3}$
$\therefore$ Empty space $=1-0.380=0.620$
$\therefore$ Per cent of empty space $=62 \%$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A solid compound $\mathrm{XY}$ has $\mathrm{NaCl}$ structure. If the radius of the cation is $100 \mathrm{pm}$, the radius of the anion $\left(\mathrm{Y}^{-}\right)$will be
questions.solution:
Radius ratio of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ like crystal $=\frac{r^{+}}{r^{-}}$
$=0.414$ or $r^{-}=\frac{100}{0.414}=241.5 \mathrm{pm}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Tetragonal crystal system has the following unit cell dimensions
questions.solution:
Tetragonal crystal system has the unit cell dimensions
$\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{b} \neq \mathrm{c}, \alpha=\beta=\gamma=90^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A crystalline solid
options: changes rapidly from solid to liquid
undergoes deformation of its geometry easily
questions.solution: A crystalline solid undergoes deformation of its geometry by application of pressure or heat.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Frenkel defect is general ly observed in
questions.solution: When the size of cation is much smaller than the anion then frenkel defect is observed. Hence, $\mathrm{AgBr}, \mathrm{AgI}$ and $\mathrm{ZnS}$ all exhibit Frenkel defect.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Most crystals show good cleavage because their atoms, ions or molecules are
questions.solution: Crystals show good cleavage when, the constitutents are arranged in orderly pattern, i.e., in planes,

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Bragg's law is given by the which of the following equation?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{n} \lambda=2 \mathrm{~d} \sin \theta$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Schottky defect in crystals is observed when
options: unequal number of cations and anions are missing from the lattice
equal number of cations and anions are missing from the lattice
an ion leaves its normal site and occupies an interstitial site
density of the crystal is increased
questions.solution: It is stoichiometric defect and it is observed when equal number of cations and anions are missing from the lattice site.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A solid with high electrical and thermal conductivity from the following is
questions.solution: Out of the given substances, only Li has high electrical and thermal conductivity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The cubic unit cell of a metal (molar mass $=63.55$ $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) has and edge length of $362 \mathrm{pm}$. Its density is $8.92 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}-3$. The type of unit cell is
questions.solution:
Density, $d=\frac{M Z}{N_{0} a^{3}}$
where, $Z=$ number of atoms in unit cell
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore Z &=\frac{d N_{0} a^{3}}{M} \\
&=\frac{8.92 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \times\left(362 \times 10^{-10}\right)^{3}}{63.55} \\
&=4.0
\end{aligned}
$$
Thus, metal has face centred unit cell.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The radius of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$is $95 \mathrm{pm}$ and that of $\mathrm{Cl}$ - ion is $181 \mathrm{pm}$. Hence, the coordination number of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ will be
questions.solution:
$\frac{\text { Radius of cation, } r^{+}}{\text {Radius of anion, } r^{-}}=\frac{95}{181}=0.525$
As this value lies in between $0.414-0.732$, thus, the coordination number of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ion will be $6 .$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of formula units of calcium flouride $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ present in $146.4 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ are (molar mass of $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ is $78.08 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$ )

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Schottky defects occurs mainly in electrovalent compounds where
options: positive ions and negative ions are of different size
positive ions and negative ions are of same size
positive ions are small and negative ions are big
positive ions are big and negative ions are small
questions.solution:
Schottky defect is generally shown by compounds which have ionic nature and small difference in the size of cations \& anions.
In this defect equal no. of cations \& anions is found missing from their lattice sites.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If ' $a$ ' stands for the edge length of the cubic systems : simple cubic, body centred cubic and face centred cubic, then the ratio of radii of the spheres in these systems will be respectively,
questions.solution:
Following generalization can be easily derived for various types of lattice arrangements in cubic cells between the edge length $(a)$ of the cell and $r$ the radius of the sphere.
For simple cubic: $a=2 r$ or $r=\frac{a}{2}$
For body centred cubic :
$$
a=\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}} r \text { or } r=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a
$$
For face centred cubic :
$$
a=2 \sqrt{2} r \text { or } r=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \mathrm{a}
$$
Thus the ratio of radii of spheres for these will be
simple $: \mathrm{bcc}: \mathrm{fcc}$
$=\frac{a}{2}: \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} a: \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} a$ i.e.
option (a) is correct answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The $8: 8$ type of packing is present in
questions.solution: $8: 8$ type of packing is present in $\mathrm{CsCl}$. $6: 6$ type of packing is present in $\mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{KCl}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How many lattice points belong to a face centered cubic unit cell?
questions.solution:
In face centred cubic lattice, the atoms are present at eight corners of faces and one each at 6 faces.
$\therefore$ Lattice points belonging to face centred cubic unit cell $=8 \times \frac{1}{8}+6 \times \frac{1}{2}=4$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Schottky defect in solids is due to
options: a pair of cation and anion vacancies
occupation of interstitial site by a pair of cation and anion
occupation of interstitial site by a cation
occupation of interstitial site by an anion
questions.solution: Schottky defect is caused when equal number of cations and anions are missing from their lattice sites.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following the incorrect statement is
options: Density of crystals remains unaffected due to Frenkel defect.
In BCC unit cell the void space is $32 \%$.
Density of crystals decreases due to Schottky defect.
Electrical conductivity of semiconductors and metals increases with increase in temperature.
questions.solution: The electrical resistance of metals depends upon temperature. Electrical resistance decreases with decrease in temperature and becomes zero near the absolute temperature. Material in this state is said to possess super conductivity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The metal that crystallises in simple cubic system is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: On an X-ray diffraction photograph the intensity of the spots depends on
questions.solution: On an X-ray diffraction photograph the intensity of the spots depends on electron density of atoms/ions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An ion leaves its regular site occupy a position in the space between the lattice sites is called
questions.solution: In Frankels defect an ion leaves its regular site and occupy a position in the space between the lattice sites.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a compound, atoms of element $Y$ form $c c p$ lattice and those of element $X$ occupy $2 / 3^{\text {rd }}$ of tetrahedral voids. The formula of the compound will be
questions.solution:
From the given data, we have
Number of $Y$ atoms in a unit cell $=4$
Number of $\mathrm{X}$ atoms in a unit cell
$=8 \times \frac{2}{3}=\frac{16}{3}$
From the above we get the formula of the compound as $X_{16 / 3} Y_{4}$ or $X_{4} Y_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Deep sea divers used to respirate a mixture of
questions.solution: An oxygen- helium mixture is used for artificial respiration in deep sea diving instead of air because nitrogen present in air dissolves in blood under high pressure when sea diver goes into deep sea. When he comes to the surface nitrogen bubbles out of the blood due to decrease in pressure, causing pain. This disease is called "bends".

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A $0.1$ molal aqueous solution of a weak acid is $30 \%$ ionised. If $K_{f}$ for water is $1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}$, the freezing point of the solution will be
questions.solution:
Freezing point depression $(\Delta T)=i K_{f} m$ $\mathrm{H} \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \quad \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{A}-$
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{HA} \rightarrow & \mathrm{H}^{+} \\
1-\alpha & \alpha \\
1-0.3 & 0.3 \\
i=1-0.3+0.3+0.3 & \mathrm{~A}^{-} \\
i=1.3 & 0.3 \\
\therefore \Delta T_{f}=1.3 \times 1.86 \times 0.1=0.2418^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\
T_{f}=0-0.2418^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\
=-0.2418^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The density of 3 M solution of NaCl is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$. What is the molality of solution?
questions.solution:
Molality of solution $=\frac{\text { Mass of solute }}{\text { Mass of solvent }(\mathrm{kg})}$
3 M NaCl solution means 3 mole of NaCl are present in 1 L of the solution.
Mass of 1 L of the solution $=$ Volume $\times$ Density
$=1000 \times 1.25 \mathrm{~g}=1250 \mathrm{~g}$
Mass of 3 mole of NaCl (solute)
$\begin{aligned} & \qquad=3 \times 58.5=175.5 \mathrm{~g} \\ & \therefore \text { Mass of solvent }=1250-175.5=1074.5 \mathrm{~kg} \\ & \therefore 1074.5 \mathrm{~g}=1.0745 \mathrm{~kg} \\ & \therefore \text { Molality }=\frac{3 \mathrm{~mol}}{1.0745 \mathrm{~kg}}=2.7 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The osmotic pressure of solution prepared by dissolving 25 mg of $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ in 2 litre of water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is (Assuming that it is completely dissociated)
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ dissolved $=25 \mathrm{mg}=0.025 \mathrm{~g}$
Volume of solution $=2 \mathrm{~L}$ Temperature, $T=25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=298 \mathrm{~K}$
Molar mass of $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4=2 \times 39+32+4 \times 16$ $=174 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
As, $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ dissolve completely
$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ i.e. ions produced $\left(1^{\circ}\right)=3$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad i & =3 \\ \pi & =i C R T=i \frac{n}{V} R T=\frac{i}{M}+\frac{1}{V} \times R T \\ & =3 \times \frac{0.025}{174} \times \frac{1}{2} \times 0.0821 \times 298 \\ \pi & =5.27 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~atm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The boiling points of 0.01 M aqueous solution of sucrose, NaCl and $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ would be
questions.solution:
Sucrose $\longrightarrow$ No dissociation
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{NaCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\ & \mathrm{CaCl}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}\end{aligned}$
Elevation in boiling point is directly proportional to the number of particles and $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ solution contains more number of particles. Hence, $\Delta T_b$ will be maximum for $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ and so will be the boiling point.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: During the depression in freezing point experiment, an equilibrium is established between the molecules of
questions.solution: Freezing point of a substance is the temperature at which the solid and the liquid forms of the substance are in equilibrium.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If the elevation in boiling point of a solution of $10 \mathrm{~g}$ of solute $(\mathrm{mol} . \mathrm{wt}=100)$ in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is $\Delta T_{b}$, the ebullioscopic constant of water is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The freezing point of a solution composed of $10.0$ gof $\mathrm{KCl}$ in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of water is $4.5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate the van't Hoff factor, i for this solution.
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
 \Delta \mathrm{T}_{f}=i K_{f} m \\
m=\frac{10 \times 1000}{74.55 \times 100}=1.34 \mathrm{~m} \\
{[\mathrm{Molecular} \mathrm{Mass} \text { of } \mathrm{KCl}=74.55]} \\
\mathrm{i}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{T}_{f}}{\mathrm{~K}_{f} \mathrm{~m}}=\frac{4.5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}{\left(1.86^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{m}\right)(1.34 \mathrm{~m})}=1.8
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $2.76 \mathrm{~g}$ of silver carbonate on being strongly heated yields a residue weighing
questions.solution:
Silver carbonate on being strongly heated decomposes as
$\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} & 2 \mathrm{Ag}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \\
276 \mathrm{~g} & 216 \mathrm{~g}
\end{array}$
As $276 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ gives $=216 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Ag}$ Hence, $2.76 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ will give
$=\frac{2.76 \times 216}{276}=2.16 \mathrm{~g}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
One per cent composition of an organic compound A is, carbon : $85.71 \%$ and hydrogen $14.29 \%$. Its vapour density is 14 . Consider the following reaction sequence
Identify C.
questions.solution: $\mathrm{C}=85.71 \%=\frac{85.71}{12}=7.14 ; \frac{7.14}{7.14}=1$ $\mathrm{H}=14.29 \%=\frac{14.29}{1}=14.29, \frac{14.29}{7.14}=2$ $\therefore$ Empirical formula $=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ and, empirical formulaweight $=12+2=14$ Nowsincemolecular formula weight $=2 \times$ vapour density $$ =2 \times 14=28 $$ $\therefore n=\frac{28}{14}=2$ $\therefore$ Molecular formula $=\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2}\right)_{2}=\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which has the maximum number of molecules among the following ?
questions.solution:
$44 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CO}_{2}=1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{CO}_{2}=\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}$ molecules of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
$48 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{O}_{3}=1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{O}_{3}=N_{A}$ molecules of $\mathrm{O}_{3}$
$8 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{H}_{2}=4 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{H}_{2}=4 \times N_{A}$ molecules of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$
$64 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{SO}_{2}=1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{SO}_{2}=N_{A}$ molecules of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=6.023 \times 10^{23}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $19.85 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{NaOH}$ reacts with $20 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\mathrm{HCl}$ solution for complete neutralisation. The molarity of $\mathrm{HCl}$ solution is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\text { Molarity of base }=\frac{\text { normality }}{\text { Acidity }}=\frac{0.1}{1}=0.1 \\
\mathrm{M}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}=\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2} \\
0.1 \times 19.85=\mathrm{M}_{2} \times 20 \\
\mathrm{M}_{2}=0.09925 \approx 0.099
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Gaseous benzene reacts with hydrogen gas in presence of a nickel catalyst to form gaseous cyclohexane according to the reaction, $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(g) \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12}(g)$
A mixture of $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}$ and excess $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ has a pressure of $60 \mathrm{~mm}$ of $\mathrm{Hg}$ in an unknown volume. After the gas had been passed over a nickel catalyst and all the benzene converted to cyclohexane, the pressure of the gas was $30 \mathrm{~mm}$ of $\mathrm{Hg}$ in the same volume at the same temperature. The fraction of $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}$ (by volume) present in the original volume is
questions.solution:
Let initially, pressure of $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}$ (g) is $\mathrm{p}_{1}$ $\mathrm{mm}$ and for $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is $\mathrm{p}_{2} \mathrm{~mm}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{p}_{1}+\mathrm{p}_{2}=60 \mathrm{~mm}$
After the reaction pressure of
$\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}(\mathrm{~g})=0$ (as all $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}$ has reacted)
$\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})=\mathrm{p}_{2}-3 \mathrm{p}_{1}$
So, total pressure $=\mathrm{p}_{2}-3 \mathrm{p}_{1}+\mathrm{p}_{1}=30$ $\mathrm{mm}$ $\mathrm{p}_{2}-2 \mathrm{p}_{1}=30 \mathrm{~mm}$
\ldots..(ii)
On solving equation (i) and (ii)
$\mathrm{p}_{1}=10 \mathrm{~mm}, \mathrm{p}_{2}=50 \mathrm{~mm}$
Fraction of $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{6}$ by volume $=$ moles
fraction fraction of pressure $=\frac{10}{60}=\frac{1}{6}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The molarity of $\mathrm{NO}_{3}{ }^{-}$in the solution after $2 \mathrm{~L}$ of $3 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ is mixed with $3 \mathrm{~L}$ of $1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ is
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{~L}$ of $3 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ will contains 6 moles of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$
$3 \mathrm{~L}$ of $1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ will contain 3 moles of $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$
$2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}+\mathrm{BaCl}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{AgCl}$
$+\mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}$
So, 6 moles of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ will react with 3 moles on $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ it means, two solution will react completely to form 3 moles of $\mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2} \equiv 6$ moles of $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$ions in $2+$ $3=5 \mathrm{~L}$ solution
Hence, molarity of $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}=\frac{6}{5}=1.2 \mathrm{M}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Caffeine has a molecular weight of $194 \mathrm{u}$. If it contains $28.9 \%$ by mass of nitrogen, number of atom of nitrogen in one molecule of caffeine is
questions.solution:
M.wt. of caffeine $=194 \mathrm{u}$ $\%$ of $\mathrm{N}$ present in one molecular of caffeine is $28.9 \%$ of
$194 u=\frac{28.9}{100} \times 194=56 u$
Mass of one $\mathrm{N}$ atom $=14 \mathrm{~m}$
$=1 \mathrm{~N}$ atom $\quad(\because 14 \mathrm{~m}=14 \mathrm{u})$
$\therefore \quad 56 \mathrm{u}=\frac{56}{14} \mathrm{~N}$ atom $=4 \mathrm{~N}$ atom

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $1.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CdCl}_{2}$ was found to contain $0.9 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Cd}$. Calculate the atomic weight of Cd.
questions.solution:
Masspercent of $\mathrm{Cdin} \mathrm{CdCl}_{2}$
$$
=\frac{0.9}{1.5} \times 100=60 \%
$$
$\therefore$ Mass percent of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ in $\mathrm{CdCl}_{2}$ $=100-60=40 \%$
$\because 40 \%$ part $\left(\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right)$ has atomic weight $=2 \times 35.5=71.0$
$\therefore 60 \%$ part (Cd) has atomic weight
$=\frac{71.0 \times 60}{40}=106.5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of hydrogen atoms present in $25.6$ $\mathrm{g}$ of sucrose $\left(\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}\right)$ which has a molar mass of $342.3 \mathrm{~g}$ is
questions.solution:
No. of moles of a compound
$=\frac{\text { given mass }(\mathrm{gm})}{\text { Molar mass }(\mathrm{gm})}$
i.e. $\frac{25.6}{342.3}=0.0748$ moles.
1 mole of sucrose $\left(\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}\right)$ contains $6.022$ $\times 10^{23}$ molecules of it.
Hence $0.0748$ moles contains
$\begin{array}{l}
=6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.0748 \\
=0.4504 \times 10^{23} \text { molecules. }
\end{array}$
1 molecule of sucrose by formula is having 22 atoms of hydrogen.
$\therefore 0.4504 \times 10^{23} \times 10^{23} \times 22=9.91 \times 10^{23}$ atoms of hydrogen.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An organic compound having carbon, hydrogen and sulphur contains $4 \%$ of sulphur. The minimum molecular weight of the compound is
questions.solution:
The minimum $\mathrm{m}$. wt. must contain at least one S atom.
$\begin{array}{rl}
\therefore \quad \% & \mathrm{~S}=\frac{\text { weight of one } \mathrm{S}-\text { atom }}{\text { minimum } \mathrm{m} . \mathrm{wt}} \times 100 \\
& 4=\frac{32}{\text { minimum } \mathrm{m} . \mathrm{wt} .} \times 100 \\
& \text { minimum } \mathrm{m} . \text { wt. }=\frac{32}{4} \times 100=800
\end{array}$

Volume will become greater by a factor of $2.9$
questions.solution:
(a) $\because \frac{p_{1} V_{1}}{T_{1}}=\frac{p_{2} V_{2}}{T_{2}}$ (By ideal gas equation) or $\frac{1.5 \times \mathrm{V}_{1}}{288}=\frac{1 \times \mathrm{V}_{2}}{298}$ $\therefore \mathrm{V}_{2}=1.55 \mathrm{~V}_{1}$
i.e, volume of bubble will bealmost $1.6$ times to initial volume of bubble.

questions.solution:

questions.q: The position of both, an electron and a helium atom is known within $1.0 \mathrm{~nm}$. Further the momentum of the electron is known within 5.0 $\times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The minimum uncertainty in the measurement of the momentum of the helium atom is
questions.solution:
By Heisenberg uncertainty Principle
$\Delta x \times \Delta p=\frac{h}{4 \pi}$ (which is constant)
As $\Delta \mathrm{x}$ for electron and helium atom is same thus momentum of electron and helium will also be same therefore the momentum of helium atom is equal to $5 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg}$. m. $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The ratio of slopes of $K_{\max }$ vs $V$ and $V_{0}$ vs $v$ curves in the photoelectric effects gives $\left(v=\right.$ frequency,$K_{\max }=$ maximum kinetic energy, $v_{0}=$ stopping potential $)$
options: the ratio of Planck's constant of electronic charge
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{hv}=\mathrm{hv}_{0}+\mathrm{ev}_{0} \\
\mathrm{v}_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{v}-\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{v}_{0}
\end{array}$
On comparing this equation with the straight line equation, i.e $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}+\mathrm{c}$ The slope of $\mathrm{v}_{0}$ vs $\mathrm{v}$ is ( $v_{0}$ is stopping potential)
$(\text { slope })_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{e}}$
Likewise,
$\mathrm{hv}=\mathrm{hv}_{0}+\mathrm{K}_{\max }$
$\text { or } \quad \mathrm{K}_{\max }=\mathrm{h} \mathrm{v}-\mathrm{h} \mathrm{v}_{0}$
Thus, slope of $K_{\max }$ vs $v$ is
$(\text { slope })_{2}=\mathrm{h} \therefore \frac{(\text { slope })_{2}}{(\text { slope })_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{h} / \mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{e}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: According to Bohr's theory, the angular momentum for an electron of $3 \mathrm{rd}$ orbit is
questions.solution:
Angular momentum, mvr
$\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}=\frac{3 \times \mathrm{h}}{2 \pi}=\frac{1.5 \mathrm{~h}}{\pi} \\
=3 \mathrm{~h}\left[\because \mathrm{h}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \pi}\right]
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A certain metal when irradiated by light $(r=3.2 \times$ $10^{16} \mathrm{~Hz}$ ) emits photoelectrons with twice kinetic energy as did photoelectrons when the same metal is irradiated by light $\left(r=2.0 \times 10^{16} \mathrm{~Hz}\right)$. The $v_{0}$ of metal is
questions.solution:
$(\mathrm{KE})_{1}=\mathrm{hv}_{1}-\mathrm{hv}_{0}$
$(\mathrm{KE})_{2}=\mathrm{hv}_{2}-\mathrm{hv}_{0}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\text { As, } & (\mathrm{KE})_{1}^{2}=2 \times(\mathrm{KE})_{2} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{hv}_{1}-\mathrm{hv}_{0}=2\left(\mathrm{hv}_{2}-\mathrm{hv}_{0}\right) \\ \text { or, } & \mathrm{hv}_{0}=2 \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{v}_{2}-\mathrm{hv}_{1} \\ \text { or, } & \mathrm{v}_{0}=2 \mathrm{v}_{2}-\mathrm{v}_{1} \\ & =2 \times\left(2 \times 10^{16}\right)-\left(3.2 \times 10^{16}\right) \\ & =0.8 \times 10^{16} \mathrm{~Hz}=8 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{~Hz}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A near UV photon of $300 \mathrm{~nm}$ is absorbed by a gas and then re-emitted as two photons. One photon is red with wavelength of the second photon is
questions.solution:
Energy values are additives.
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E}_{1}+\mathrm{E}_{2} \\
\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{1}}+\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{2}} \\
\frac{1}{300}=\frac{1}{760}+\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}} \\
\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}=\frac{1}{300}-\frac{1}{760} \\
\lambda=495.6 \mathrm{~nm}=496 \mathrm{~nm}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What transition in the hydrogen spectrum would have the same wavelength as the Balmer transition, $n=4$ to $n=2$ of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$spectrum?
questions.solution:
We have to compare wavelength of transition in the $\mathrm{H}$-spectrum with the Balmer transition $\mathrm{n}=4$ to $\mathrm{n}=2$ of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$spectrum.
$\begin{array}{l}
\because \lambda_{\mathrm{H}}=\lambda_{\mathrm{He}^{+}} \\
\therefore \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}} Z_{\mathrm{H}}^{2}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{2}^{2}}\right]=\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}} Z_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}^{2}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right] \\
1 \times\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{2}^{2}}\right]=4 \times\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}\right) \\
{\left[\begin{array}{l}
\left.\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{2}^{2}}\right]=4 \times \frac{4-1}{16} \\
{\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{2}^{2}}\right]=\frac{3}{4}}
\end{array}\right.}
\end{array}$
If $\mathrm{n}_{1}=1$, then $\mathrm{n}_{2}=2,3, \ldots$
For first line $\mathrm{n}_{2}=2, \mathrm{n}_{1}=1$
$\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{2^{2}}\right]=\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{3}{4}$
Hence, trnasition $n_{2}=2$ to $n_{1}=1$ will give spectrum of the same wavelength as that of Balmer transition, $\mathrm{n}_{2}=4$ to $\mathrm{n}_{1}=2$ in $\mathrm{He}^{+}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the degeneracy of the level of H-atom that has energy $\left(-\frac{R_{H}}{9}\right)$ ?
questions.solution:
Energy of the electron in the nth orbit in terms of $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}$ is
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{n}}=-\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}} \mathrm{Z}^{2}}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}$
where, $Z=$ atomic number, $n^{2}=$ degeneracy
For $\mathrm{H}$-atom, $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{n}}=-\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}(1)^{2}}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}$
$-\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}}{9}=-\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{n}^{2}=9$

questions.solution:
Magnetic moment of a salt depends upon the number of unpaired d-electrons. In $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ salt configuration of cation is $4 \mathrm{~s}^{0} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{10}$. Hence total no. of unpaired electron, $\mathrm{n}$ is zero. So magnetic moment i.e.
$\text { B.M. }=\sqrt{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+2)}=0$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The bond dissociation energies for $\mathrm{Cl}_2, \mathrm{I}_2$ and ICl are $242.3,151.0$ and $211.3 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ respectively. The enthalpy of sublimation of iodine is $62.8 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. What is the standard enthalpy of formation of $\mathrm{ICl}(\mathrm{g})$ nearly equal to?
questions.solution:
The given reaction is as follows,
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_2(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2(g) \longrightarrow \mathrm{ICl}(g)$
We are given,
(i) $\mathrm{Cl}_2(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{g}), \Delta \mathrm{H}=242.3 \mathrm{~kJ}$
(ii) $\mathrm{I}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}(\mathrm{g}), \Delta H=151 \mathrm{~kJ}$
(iii) $\mathrm{ICl}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{g}), \Delta H=211.3 \mathrm{~kJ}$
(iv) $\mathrm{I}_2(g) \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}_2(g), \Delta H=62.8 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\frac{1}{2}$ (ii) $+\frac{1}{2}$ (i) - (iii) $+\frac{1}{2}$
(iv) gives the required equation.
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \Delta H & =\frac{1}{2}(151)+\frac{1}{2}(242.3)-211.3+\frac{1}{2}(62.8) \\ \Delta H & =16.8 \mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The value of $\log _{10} K$ for a reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}$ is
(Given : $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}_{298 \mathrm{O}}^{\circ}=-54.07 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$,
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{S}_{298 \mathrm{~K}}^{\circ}=10 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}$
$\mathrm{mol}^{-1}$;
$2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298=5705)$
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text {  } \mathrm{A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B} \\
& \Delta G^{\circ}=\Delta H^{\circ}-\mathrm{T} \Delta S^{\circ} \\
& \Delta G^{\circ}=-2.303 \mathrm{RT} \log _{10} \mathrm{~K} \\
& -2.303 \mathrm{RT} \log _{10} \mathrm{~K}=\Delta H^{\circ}-T \Delta S^{\circ} \\
& 2.303 \mathrm{RT} \log _{10} \mathrm{~K}=T \Delta S^{\circ}-\Delta H^{\circ} \\
& \log _{10} \mathrm{~K}=\frac{T \Delta S^{\circ}-\Delta H^{\circ}}{2.303 R T} \\
& =\frac{298 \times 10+54.07 \times 1000}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298} \\
& =9.998 \approx 10 \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.solution: For a spontaneous process, \(\Delta \mathrm{S}_{\text {total }}\) is always positive

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: According to the third law of thermodynamics which one of the following quantities for a perfectly crystalline solid is zero at absolute zero?
questions.solution: Entropy states the randomness or disorderness of the system. At absolute zero, the movement of molecules of the system or randomenss of the system is zero, hence entropy is also zero.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The decomposition of limestone
\(\mathrm{CaCO}_3(\mathrm{~s})=\mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})\)
is non-spontaneous at \(298 \mathrm{~K}\). The \(\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\) and \(\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}\) value for the reaction are \(176.0 \mathrm{~kJ}\) and \(160 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}\) respectively. At what temperature the decomposition becomes spontaneous?
questions.solution:
Using the relation, \(\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}\), we get
For a reaction to be spontaneous the value of \(\Delta \mathrm{G}\) should be negative. When \(\Delta \mathrm{G}=0\), the equilibrium exists.
Substituting the given values, we get
\(\begin{array}{ll} 
& \Delta \mathrm{G}=176.0 \times 10^3-\mathrm{T} \times 160 \\
\text { or } & 0=176.0 \times 10^3-\mathrm{T} \times 160 \text{ 
(at equilibrium)}\\
\text { or } & 160 \mathrm{~T}=176.0 \times 10^3 \\
\text { or } & \mathrm{T}=\frac{176.0 \times 10^3}{160}=1100 \mathrm{~K} .
\end{array}\)
The reaction will be spontaneous above \(1100 \mathrm{~K}\) at which it is in equilibrium.
Since, \(1100 \mathrm{~K}=(1100-273)^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=827^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\).
So, the reaction will be spontaneous above \(827^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\).

questions.solution: Since, liquid is passing into gaseous phase so entropy will increase and at $373 \mathrm{~K}$ during the phase transformation it remains at equilibrium. So, $\Delta \mathrm{G}=0$.

$$

\Delta H_{\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{S}}+2 \Delta H_{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{S}}=239 \\
2 \Delta H_{\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{S}}=175
\end{array}
$$
Hence,
$\Delta H_{\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{S}}=239-175=64 \mathrm{kcal} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
Then, $\Delta H$ for $8 \mathrm{~S}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}_{8(\mathrm{~g})}$ is $8 \times(-64)=-512 \mathrm{kcal}$

questions.solution: $\Delta \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{6025 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{273 \mathrm{~K}}=22.1 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}$ $\mathrm{mol}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Consider the reaction : $\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ carried out at constant temperature and pressure. If $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{U}$ are the enthalpy and internal energy changes for the reaction, which of the following expressions is true?
questions.solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{U}+\Delta \mathrm{nRT}$ for
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{N}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \\
\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=2-4=-2
\end{array}$
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{U}-2 \mathrm{RT}$ or $\Delta \mathrm{U}=\Delta \mathrm{H}+2 \mathrm{R} \mathrm{T} \therefore \Delta \mathrm{U}>\Delta \mathrm{H}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A certain reaction is non spontaneous at $298 \mathrm{~K}$. The entropy change during the reaction is 121 $\mathrm{J} \mathrm{K}^{-1}$. Is the reaction is endothermic or exothermic ? The minimum value of $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ for the reaction is
questions.solution:
For non spontaneous reaction
$\Delta \mathrm{G}=+\mathrm{ve}$ $\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}=121 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ For $\Delta \mathrm{G}=+\mathrm{ve}$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}$ has to be positive. Hence the reaction is endothermic.
The minimum value of $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ can be obtained by putting $\Delta \mathrm{G}=0$
$\begin{aligned}
\Delta \mathrm{H} &=\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}=298 \times 121 \mathrm{~J} \\
&=36.06 \mathrm{~kJ}
\end{aligned}$

Then $\Delta \mathrm{S}$ (cal $\mathrm{deg}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) for this process is : $\left(\mathrm{R}=2\right.$ cal $\left.\mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=20 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ the activation energy of the forward reaction is $85 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. The activation energy of the backward reaction will be
questions.solution:
Energy of activation for forward reaction $\left(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}\right)=85 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
$\therefore \quad$ Energy of activation for backward reaction $=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}-\Delta \mathrm{H}=85-20=65 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Standard entropy of $X_{2}, Y_{2}$ and $X Y_{3}$ are 60,40 and $50 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, respectively. For the reaction, $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{X}_{2}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{Y}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{XY}_{3}, \Delta \mathrm{H}=-30 \mathrm{~kJ}$, to be at equilibrium, the temperature will be
questions.solution:
For a reaction to be at equilibrium $\Delta \mathrm{G}=0$. Since $\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$ so at equilibrium $\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}=0$
or $\Delta \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$
For the reaction
$$
\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{X}_{2}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{Y}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{XY}_{3} ; \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}=-30 \mathrm{~kJ}
$$
(given)
Calculating $\Delta \mathrm{S}$ for the above reaction, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Delta S=50-\left[\frac{1}{2} \times 60+\frac{3}{2} \times 40\right] \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \\
=50-(30+60) \mathrm{JK}^{-1}=-40 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \\
\text { At equilibrium, } \mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}=\Delta \mathrm{H}
\end{array}
$$
$[\because \Delta \mathrm{G}=0]$
$\therefore \mathrm{T} \times(-40)=-30 \times 1000[\because 1 \mathrm{~kJ}=1000 \mathrm{~J}]$
or $\quad \mathrm{T}=\frac{-30 \times 1000}{-40}$ or $750 \mathrm{~K}$

questions.subject: Chemistry

options: can be negative, but numerically greater than $\frac{x}{298} \mathrm{Jk}^{-1}$
can be negative, but numerically smaller $\operatorname{than} \frac{x}{298} \mathrm{Jk}^{-1}$
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the given reaction,
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{H}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(a q) \\
\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\mathrm{a}}=-262.4 \mathrm{~kJ}
\end{array}$
The value of free energy of formation
$\left(\Delta \mathrm{G}_{f}^{\mathrm{o}}\right)$ for the ion $\mathrm{Cl}^{-1}(\mathrm{aq})$, therefore will be
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}\left(\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}\right) \text { reaction }=\Delta \mathrm{G}_{f}^{0} & \text { (Products) } \\ &-\Delta \mathrm{G}_{f}^{0} \text { (reactants) } \end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{ll}
\therefore & 264.4=\left[2 \Delta \mathrm{G}_{f}^{0}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)+2 \Delta \mathrm{G}_{f}^{0}\left(\mathrm{C}^{\mathrm{l}}\right)\right] \\
\text { or } & 264.4=-\left[\Delta \mathrm{G}_{f}^{0}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2}\right)+\Delta \mathrm{G}_{f}^{0}\left(\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right)\right] \\
& =\left[0+2 \Delta \mathrm{G}_{f}^{0}\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)\right]+[0+0]
\end{array}$
or, $\quad-262.4=2 \Delta \mathrm{G}_{f}^{0}\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)$
or, $\Delta \mathrm{G}_{f}^{0}\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)=-131.2 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The standard molar heat of formation of ethane, $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and water (1) are respectively $-21.1,-94.1$ and $-68.3 \mathrm{kcal}$. The standard molar heat of combustion of ethane will be
questions.solution:
Given,
(a) $2 \mathrm{C}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-21.1 \mathrm{kcal}$
(b) $\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-94.1 \mathrm{kcal}$
(c) $\mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-68.3 \mathrm{kcal}$
Now, eqs. $2 \times(\mathrm{b})+3 \times(\mathrm{c})-(\mathrm{a})$
$\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
Heat of combustion of Ethane $\Delta \mathrm{x}=2(-94.1)$
$\begin{array}{l}
+3(-68.3)-(-21.1) \\
=(-188.2)+(-204.9)-21.1 \\
=-372 \mathrm{kcal}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If for the cell reaction, $\mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ Entropy change $\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}$ is $96.5 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$, then temperature coefficient of the emf of a cell is
questions.solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{nFT}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dE}}{\mathrm{dT}}\right)_{\mathrm{P}}$ 
and $\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{T}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$
$\therefore \frac{\Delta \mathrm{S}}{\mathrm{nF}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{dE}}{\mathrm{dT}}\right)_{\mathrm{P}}$ 
$\therefore \frac{96.5}{2 \times 96500}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{dE}}{\mathrm{dT}}\right)_{\mathrm{P}}$ 
$\therefore\left(\frac{\mathrm{dE}_{\mathrm{cell}}}{\mathrm{dT}}\right)_{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{2}=5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{VK}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Using the Gibbs free energy change, $\Delta G^{\circ}=+633 \mathrm{~kJ}$. For the reaction,
$\mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q)+\mathrm{CO}_3^{2-}(a q)$
The $K_{\text {sp }}$ of $\mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3(s)$ in water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3(\mathrm{~s}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{CO}_3^{2-}(\mathrm{aq})$
$K_{\text {sp }}=\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^2\left[\mathrm{CO}_3^{2-}\right]=K$ for the reaction
$\left[\because \mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3\right.$ is solid $]$
$\begin{aligned} \Delta G & =-2.303 R T \log K=-2.303 R T \log K_{\text {sp }} \\ 63300 & =-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times \log K_{\text {sp }} \\ K_{\text {sp }} & =8 \times 10^{-12}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The work done in ergs for the reversible expansion of one mole of an ideal gas from a volume of 10 litres to 20 litres at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{W} &=2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log \frac{\mathrm{V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{1}} \\
&=2.303 \times 1 \times 8.314 \times 10^{7} \times 298 \log \frac{20}{10} \\
&=298 \times 10^{7} \times 8.31 \times 2.303 \log 2
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the correct statement for change of Gibbs energy for a system \(\left(\Delta \mathrm{G}_{\text {system }}\right)\) at constant temperature and pressure :
options: If \(\Delta \mathrm{G}_{\text {system }}=0\), the system has attained equilibrium
If \(\Delta \mathrm{G}_{\text {system }}=0\), the system is still moving in a particular direction
If \(\Delta \mathrm{G}_{\text {system }}  < 0\), the process is not spontaneous
If \(\Delta \mathrm{G}_{\text {system }} > 0\), the process is not spontaneous
questions.solution:
If \(\Delta \mathrm{G}_{\text {system }}=0\) the system has attained equilibrium is right choice.
In it alternative (d) is most confusing as when \(\Delta \mathrm{G} > 0\), the process may be spontaneous when it is coupled with a reaction which has \(\Delta \mathrm{G}  < 0\) and total \(\Delta \mathrm{G}\) is negative, so right answer is (a).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following processes
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Delta \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mol}) \\
\quad \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~A} \longrightarrow \mathrm{B}+150 \\
3 \mathrm{~B} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{D}-125 \\
\mathrm{E}+\mathrm{A} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{D}+350
\end{array}
$$
For $\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{D} \longrightarrow \mathrm{E}+2 \mathrm{C}, \Delta \mathrm{H}$ will be
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{2} A \rightarrow B ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=150 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}...(i) \\
3 B \rightarrow 2 C+D ; \Delta H=-125 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}...(ii) \\
E+A \rightarrow 2 D ; \Delta H=+350 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}...(iii) \\
\text { By }[2 \times(\mathrm{i})+(\text { ii })]-(\text { iii }), \text { we have } \\
B+D \rightarrow E+2 C \\
\therefore \Delta H=150 \times 2+(-125)-350 \\
=-175 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Match List I (Equations) with List II (Types of process) and select the correct option.
Codes: $\begin{array}{llll}\mathrm{A} & \mathrm{B} & \mathrm{C} & \mathrm{D} \\ \end{array}$
questions.solution:
(A) If $\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{p}}>\mathrm{Q}$ and goes in forward direction than reaction is spontaneous
(B) Given, $\Delta G^{\circ} < R T \ln Q$, thus, $\Delta G^{\circ}=+$ ve and hence, the reaction is nonspontaneous.
(C) At equilibrium, $K_{p}=Q$
(D) $T>\frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}$
or $T \Delta S=\Delta H$
This is valid condition for spontaneous endothermic reactions (as $\Delta G \geq \Delta H-T \Delta S$ )

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Standard entropies of $\mathrm{X}_{2}, \mathrm{Y}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{XY}_{3}$ are 60 , 30 and $50 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. For the reaction $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{X}_{2}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{Y}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{XY}_{3}, \Delta H=-30 \mathrm{~kJ}$ to be at equilibrium, the temperature should be:
questions.solution:
$\Delta S$ for the reaction $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{X}_{2}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{Y}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{XY}_{3}$
$\Delta S=50-(30+60)=-40 \mathrm{~J}$
For equilibrium $\Delta G=0=\Delta H-T \Delta S$
$T=\frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}=\frac{-30000}{-40}=750 \mathrm{~K}$

questions.solution: When solid melts $\mathrm{S}$ increases. because when solid changes into liquid randomness increases.

questions.solution:
The driving forces which are responsible for a process to be spontaneous are :
i) Tendency for minimizing energy
ii) Tendency for maximum randomness. i.e. maximum entropy

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Enthalpy is equal to
questions.solution:
Gibbs Helmholtz Equation
$\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$ ....(1)
differentiate this equation w.r.t. temperature at constant pressure
$\begin{array}{l}
\left(\frac{\partial \Delta \mathrm{G}}{\partial \mathrm{T}}\right)_{\mathrm{P}}=\left(\frac{\partial \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{y}}}{\partial \mathrm{T}}\right)_{\mathrm{P}}-\left(\frac{\partial \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{x}}}{\partial \mathrm{T}}\right)_{\mathrm{P}} ...(2) \\
=-\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{y}}-\left(-\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{x}}\right) \\
=-\left(\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{y}}-\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)=-\Delta \mathrm{S} ...(3)
\end{array}$
where $\Delta \mathrm{S}$ change in entropy on combining equation (1) \& (3) we get
$\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{H}+\mathrm{T}\left(\frac{\partial(\Delta \mathrm{G})}{\partial \mathrm{T}}\right)_{\mathrm{P}}$ ...(4)
Equation (4) is on alternative form of Gibbs Helmholtz equation
Dividing equ. (4) by $\mathrm{T}^{2}$, we get
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{T}^{2}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{T}^{2}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}\left[\frac{\partial(\Delta \mathrm{G})}{\partial \mathrm{T}}\right]_{\mathrm{P}}$
on rearrangement, we get
$\begin{array}{l}
{\left[\frac{\partial(\Delta \mathrm{G})}{\partial \mathrm{T}}\right]_{\mathrm{P}}=-\frac{\Delta \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{T}^{2}}} \\
\therefore \Delta \mathrm{H}=-\mathrm{T}^{2}\left[\frac{\partial(\Delta \mathrm{G})}{\partial \mathrm{T}}\right]_{\mathrm{P}}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Condition for spontaneity in an isothermal process is
questions.solution:
Since $\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{P} . \Delta \mathrm{V}$
For a spontaneous process $\Delta \mathrm{G}$ should be negative which is possible only if 
$\Delta \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{P} . \Delta \mathrm{V}$ or $\Delta \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{W} < 0$

questions.solution:

During expansion in vaccum $\mathrm{P}_{\text {ext }}=0$ 
$\therefore$ work done $=0$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
We have to find
$2 \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \quad \Delta \mathrm{H}=?$
This is the equation for formation of acelytene Given
$2 \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-787 \mathrm{~kJ}$ ...(1)
$\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\ell)} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-286 \mathrm{~kJ}$ ...(2)
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} & \\ & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\ell)} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{H}=-1310 \mathrm{~kJ} ...(3) \end{aligned}$ 
Addeq.(1) and (2)
$\begin{array}{l}
2 \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \\
+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{(\ell)} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=-1073 \mathrm{~kJ} ...(4)
\end{array}$
Subtract eq. (3) form eq. (4) we get
$\begin{array}{l}
2 \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{s})}+\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \\
\Delta \mathrm{H}=+237 \mathrm{~kJ}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 6 moles of an ideal gas expand isothermally and reversibly from a volume of 1 litre to a volume of 10 litres at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the maximum work done?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{W}=-2.303 \mathrm{nRT} \log \frac{\mathrm{V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{1}}$
Given $\mathrm{n}=6, \mathrm{~T}=27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=273+27=300 \mathrm{~K}$
$\mathrm{~V}_{1}=1 \mathrm{~L}, \mathrm{~V}_{2}=10 \mathrm{~L}$
$\therefore \mathrm{W}=-2.303 \times 6 \times 8.314 \times 300 \log \frac{10}{1}$
$\quad=34.465 \mathrm{~kJ}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Enthalpy of a compound is equal to its
questions.solution: Heat of formation is equal to enthalpy of a compound.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the following cell reaction,
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{Ag}\left|\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right| \mathrm{AgCl}\left|\mathrm{Cl}^{\ominus}\right| \mathrm{Cl}_{2}, \mathrm{Pt} \\
\Delta \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}(\mathrm{AgCl})=-109 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\
\Delta \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)=-129 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\
\Delta \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right)=78 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{array}$
$\mathrm{E}^{\circ}$ of the cell is
questions.solution:
For the given cell, $\mathrm{Ag}\left|\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right| \mathrm{AgCl}\left|\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right| \mathrm{Cl}_{2}, \mathrm{Pt}$
The cell reactions are as follows
At anode:
$$
\mathrm{Ag} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+e^{-}
$$
At cathode: $\mathrm{AgCl}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}(s)+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
Net cell reaction : $\mathrm{AgCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \Delta \mathrm{G}_{\text {reaction }}^{\mathrm{o}} &=\Sigma \Delta \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{p}}^{\mathrm{o}}-\Sigma \Delta \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{R}}^{\mathrm{o}} \\
&=(78-129)-(-109) \\
&=+58 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\
\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ} & \\
58 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~J}=&-1 \times 96500 \times E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ} \\
\Rightarrow E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=& \frac{-58 \times 1000}{96500}=-0.6 \mathrm{~V}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For which one of the following reactions will there be a positive $\Delta S ?$
questions.solution:
$\Delta S$ (entropy change) is the measure of randomness and thus in solid, liquid and gas, the order of entropy is gas $>$ liquid $>$ solid
Thus, $\Delta S$ is positive for the reaction given in option (c) because solid $\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ is forming gaseous $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If an endothermic reaction occurs spontaneously at constant temperature $T$ and $P$, then which of the following is true ?
questions.solution:
For a reaction to take place spontaneously the value of $\Delta \mathrm{G}$ must be negative i.e. $\Delta \mathrm{G} < 0$. Now, $\Delta \mathrm{G}=\Delta \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$.
As the reaction is endothermic, so value of $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ must be positive, i.e. $\Delta \mathrm{H}>0$.
Hence to have a negative $\Delta \mathrm{G}$.
$\Delta \mathrm{H} < \mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$. As $\mathrm{T} \& \mathrm{P}$ are constant. 
$\mathrm{T} \Delta \mathrm{S}$ must be positive to give the total value a negative sign.
Hence $\Delta \mathrm{S}>0$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: IfC(s) $+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{R}$ and $\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{S}$, then heat of formation of $\mathrm{CO}$ is:
questions.solution: Let, $\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{R}$ and $\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{S}$ (ii) Subtrācting ęu. (i) from èu (ii), wê get $\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}) ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{S}$ Hence, heat of formation of $\mathrm{CO}=\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{S}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\tan 65^{\circ}, \tan 40^{\circ}+\tan 25^{\circ}$ and $\tan 25^{\circ}$ are in
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { } \because \tan 65^{\circ}=\tan \left(40^{\circ}+25^{\circ}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \tan 65^{\circ}=\frac{\tan 40^{\circ}+\tan 25^{\circ}}{1-\tan 25^{\circ} \cdot \tan 40^{\circ}} \\
& \Rightarrow \tan 65^{\circ}-\tan 65^{\circ} \tan 25^{\circ} \tan 40^{\circ} \\
& =\tan 40^{\circ}+\tan 25^{\circ} \\
& \left\{\because \tan 65^{\circ} \tan 25^{\circ}=1\right\} \\
& \Rightarrow \tan 65^{\circ}-\tan 40^{\circ}=\tan 40^{\circ}+\tan 25^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$
Adding $\tan 25^{\circ}$ on both sides, we get
$\tan 65^{\circ}+\tan 25^{\circ}=2\left(\tan 40^{\circ}+\tan 25^{\circ}\right) \Rightarrow A P$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For the curve \(\mathrm{y}^{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{n}-1} \mathrm{x}\) if the subnormal at any point is a constant then \(n\) is equal to
questions.solution:
For the curve \(y^n=a^{n-1} x\) On putting \(\mathrm{n}=2\), we get \(\mathrm{y}^2=\mathrm{ax}\) Which is curve of the form of parabola, where the subnormal at any point is a constant.
\(\therefore \mathrm{n}=2 \text {. }\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The two curves \(x^3-3 x y^2+2=0\) and \(3 x^2 y-y^3-2=0\)
questions.solution:
We have \(x^3-3 x y^2+2=0\) ...(1)
and \(3 x^2 y-y^3-2=0\) ...(2)
Diff. (1) and (2) w.r.t. \(x\), we obtain
\(\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{C_1} & =\frac{x^2-y^2}{2 x y} \text { and } \\
\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{C_2} & =\frac{-2 x y}{x^2-y^2}
\end{aligned}\)
Since \(\mathrm{m}_1 \times \mathrm{m}_2=-1\), therefore the two curves cut at right angles.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If tangent to the curve $f(x)=x^3-\alpha x^2-x+\beta$ at point $(1,3)$ on the curve, cut equals non zero intercepts on co-ordinate axes, then
questions.solution:
$\because(1,3)$ lies on the curve.
Then,$f(1)=3$
$\Rightarrow 1-\alpha-1+\beta=3 \Rightarrow \beta-\alpha=3$
$\because$ Tangent cuts equal intercepts that means, slope $=-1$
$\begin{array}{lr}
\Rightarrow & f^{\prime}(1)=-1 \\
\Rightarrow & 3(1)^2-2(1) \alpha-1=-1 \\
\Rightarrow & -2 \alpha=-3 \\
\Rightarrow & 2 \alpha=3 \\
\Rightarrow & \alpha=\frac{3}{2}
\end{array}$
Then, $\beta=3+\frac{3}{2}=\frac{9}{2}$
Therefore, $\alpha+\beta=\frac{3}{2}+\frac{9}{2}=6$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Difference between the maximum and minimum values of $f(x)=-\sin ^3 x+3 \sin ^2 x+5$ in $x \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$ is
questions.solution:
$f^{\prime}(x)=-3 \sin ^2 x \cos x+6 \sin x \cos x$
$\begin{aligned}
& \forall x \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right] \\
& =-3 \sin x \cos x(\sin x-2)
\end{aligned}$
that means, $f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0$
$\therefore f(x)$ is strictly increasing.
Therefore, $f(x)_{\min }=f(0)=5$
and
$f\left(x_{\text {max }}=f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=-1+3(1)+5=7\right.$
Hence, the difference $=7-5=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The radius of a right circular cylinder increases at the rate of $0.1 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{min}$, and the height decreases at the rate of $0.2 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{min}$. The rate of change of the volume of the cylinder, in $\mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{min}$, when the radius is $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and the height is $3 \mathrm{~cm}$ is
questions.solution:
Given $V=\pi r^{2} h$.
Differentiating both sides, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{d V}{d t}=\pi\left(r^{2} \frac{d h}{d t}+2 r \frac{d r}{d t} h\right)=\pi r\left(r \frac{d h}{d t}+2 h \frac{d r}{d t}\right) \\
\frac{d r}{d t}=\frac{1}{10} \text { and } \frac{d h}{d t}=-\frac{2}{10}
\end{array}
$$
$$
\frac{d V}{d t}=\pi r\left(r\left(-\frac{2}{10}\right)+2 h\left(\frac{1}{10}\right)\right)=\frac{\pi r}{5}(-r+h)
$$
Thus, when $r=2$ and $h=3$,
$$
\frac{d V}{d t}=\frac{\pi(2)}{5}(-2+3)=\frac{2 \pi}{5}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The angle of intersection to the curve $y=x^2$, $6 y=7-x^3$ at $(1,1)$ is :
questions.solution:
Let $m_1$ and $m_2$ be slope of curve $y=x^2$ and $6 y=7-x^3$ respectively.
Now, $y=x^2 \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=2 x$
$$
\Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)_{(1,1)}=2 \text { i.e. } \mathrm{m}_1=2
$$
and $6 y=7-x^3 \Rightarrow 6 \frac{d y}{d x}=-3 x^2$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\frac{3}{6} \mathrm{x}^2=-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{x}^2$
$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)_{(1,1)}=-\frac{1}{2}(1)^2=-\frac{1}{2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{m}_2=-\frac{1}{2} \therefore \mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2=2 .-\frac{1}{2}=-1$
$\therefore \quad$ Angle of intersection is $90^{\circ}$ i.e. $\frac{\pi}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d$ and $0 < b^{2} < c$, then in $(-\infty, \infty)$
questions.solution:
$f(x)=x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d, 0 < b^{2} < c$ $\therefore \quad f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^{2}+2 b x+c$ Discriminant $=4 b^{2}-12 c=4\left(b^{2}-3 c\right) < 0$ $\therefore f^{\prime}(x)>0 \forall x \in R$
Thus, $f(x)$ is strictly increasing $\forall x \in R$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A wire $34 \mathrm{~cm}$ long is to be bent in the form of a quadrilateral of which each angle is $90^{\circ}$. What is the maximum area which can be enclosed inside the quadrilateral?
questions.solution:
Let one side of quadrilateral be $x$ and another side be $y$ so, $2(x+y)=34$ 
or, $(x+y)=17 ......(i)$
We know from the basic principle that for a given perimeter square has the maximum area, so, $x=y$ and putting this value in equation (i)
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{y}=\frac{17}{2} \\
\text { Area }=\mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{y}=\frac{17}{2} \times \frac{17}{2}=\frac{289}{4}=72.25
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A value of $\mathrm{c}$ for which conclusion of Mean Value Theorem holds for the function $f(x)=\log _{e} x$ on the interval $[1,3]$ is
questions.solution:
Using Lagrange's Mean Value Theorem
Let $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ be a function defined on $[\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}]$
then, $f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}$
$\mathrm{c} \in[\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}]$
$\therefore \quad$ Given $f(x)=\log _{e} x \quad \therefore f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{x}$
$\therefore \quad$ equation (i) become
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{c}=\frac{f(3)-f(1)}{3-1} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{c}=\frac{\log _{e} 3-\log _{e} 1}{2}=\frac{\log _{\mathrm{e}} 3}{2} \Rightarrow c=\frac{2}{\log _{\mathrm{e}} 3} \\
\Rightarrow & c=2 \log _{3} e
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The two curves $x^{3}-3 x y^{2}+2=0$ and $3 x^{2} y-y^{3}-2=0$ intersect at an angle of
questions.solution:
$x^{3}-3 x y^{2}+2=0$ differentiating w.r.t. $x$ :
$$
\begin{array}{l}
3 x^{2}-3 x(2 y) \frac{d y}{d x}-3 y^{2}=0 \\
\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3 x^{2}-3 y^{2}}{6 x y} \text { and } 3 x^{2} y-y^{3}-2=0
\end{array}
$$
differentiating w.r.t. $\mathrm{x}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow 3 x^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}+6 x y-3 y^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\
\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\left(\frac{6 x y}{3 x^{2}-3 y^{2}}\right)
\end{array}
$$
Now, product of slope
$$
=\frac{3 x^{2}-3 y^{2}}{6 x y} \times-\left(\frac{6 x y}{3 x^{2}-3 y^{2}}\right)=-1
$$
$\therefore$ they are perpendicular.
Hence, angle
$=\pi / 2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $f(x)=\sin x-k x-c$, where $k$ and $c$ are constants, decreases always when
questions.solution:
Let $f(x)=\sin x-k x-c$ where $k$ and $c$ are constants
$f^{\prime}(x)=\cos x-k$ $\therefore f$ decreases if $\cos x \leq k$
Thus, $f(x)=\sin x-k x-c$ decrease always when $k \geq 1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The difference between greatest and least value of $f(x)=2 \sin x+\sin 2 x, x \in\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right]$ is $-$
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
f(x)=2 \sin x+\sin 2 x \\
f^{\prime}(x)=2 \cos x+2 \cos 2 x=2(\cos x+\cos 2 x) \\
\therefore f^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow 2 \cos ^{2} x+\cos x-1=0 \\
\cos x=\frac{-1 \pm 3}{4}=-1, \frac{1}{2} \quad \therefore x=\pi, \frac{\pi}{3}
\end{array}
$$
Now, $\mathrm{f}(0)=0, \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}\right)=-2$
$$
\mathrm{f}(\pi)=0, \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=2 \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}
$$
$\therefore$ difference between greatest value and least value
$$
=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}+2
$$
