questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The value of $x$, for which the matrix
$A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\frac{2}{x} & -1 & 2 \\ 1 & x & 2 x^{2} \\ 1 & \frac{1}{x} & 2\end{array}\right]$ is singular, is
questions.solution:
We know that, A is singular if $|\mathrm{A}|=0$
$|\mathrm{A}|=\left|\begin{array}{llc}\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}} & -1 & 2 \\ 1 & \mathrm{x} & 2 \mathrm{x}^{2} \\ 1 & \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} & 2\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow|\mathrm{A}|=\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}[2 \mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{x}]+1\left[2-2 \mathrm{x}^{2}\right]+2\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}-\mathrm{x}\right]=0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}[0]+\left[2-2 \mathrm{x}^{2}\right]+\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}-2 \mathrm{x}=0$
$\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{x}-2 \mathrm{x}^{3}+2-2 \mathrm{x}^{2}=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{3}+\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{x}-1=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{2}(\mathrm{x}+1)-1(\mathrm{x}+1)=0$
$\Rightarrow(\mathrm{x}+1)\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}-1\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\pm 1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A manufacturer of cotter pins knows that $5 \%$ of his product is defective. He sells pins in boxes of 100 and guarantees that not more than one pin will be defective in a box. In order to find the probability that a box will fail to meet the guaranteed quality, the probability distribution one has to employ is
questions.solution:
Poisson distribution is a probability distribution which is obtained when the probability (p) of the happening of an event is same in all the trials and there are only two events in each trials generally says successes and failures probability (p) of the happening of the event in trial is very less but number of trials $(\mathrm{n})$ is very large.
Here, $\mathrm{p}=5 \%=\frac{5}{100}=\frac{1}{20}$ is very less and $\mathrm{n}=100$, is very large. Hence, one has to employ the Poisson distribution in the given question.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of real roots of the equation $\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{3}+x+\frac{1}{x}=0$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{3}+\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)=0 \\
\Rightarrow\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)+\left[\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^{2}+1\right]=0 \\
\Rightarrow x+\frac{1}{x}=0 \Rightarrow x^{2}+1=0 \Rightarrow x=\pm i
\end{array}$
Thus, the given equation has no real roots.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the roots of the equation $a x^{2}+b x+c=0$, then the value of $\alpha^{3}+\beta^{3}$ is
questions.solution:
Given : $\alpha \& \beta$ are roots of equation $a x^{2}+b x+c=0$
$\therefore \alpha+\beta=-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}} \& \alpha \beta=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{a}}$
Now, $\alpha^{3}+\beta^{3}=(\alpha+\beta)^{3}-3 \alpha \beta(\alpha+\beta)$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \alpha^{3}+\beta^{3}=\left(-\frac{b}{a}\right)^{3}-3 \frac{c}{a} \cdot\left(-\frac{b}{a}\right) \\
\Rightarrow \alpha^{3}+\beta^{3}=-\frac{b^{3}}{a^{3}}+\frac{3 b c}{a^{2}} \\
\Rightarrow \alpha^{3}+\beta^{3}=\frac{-b^{3}+3 a b c}{a^{3}}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The region of the argand plane defined by $|z-i|+|z+i| \leq 4$ is
options: interior and boundary of an ellipse
questions.solution:
Given, $|z-i|+|z+i| \leq 4$ 
$\Rightarrow|z-(0+i)|+|z-(0-i)| \leq 0$
This equation represent the interior and boundary of ellipse with foci at $(0,1) \&$ $(0,-1)$, whose major axis is along the $y$-axis.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of common tangents to the circles $x^{2}+y^{2}-6 x-14 y+48=0$ and $x^{2}+y^{2}-6 x=0$ is
questions.solution:
For the first circle centre $=(3,7)$
$$
\text { Radius } r_{1}=\sqrt{3^{2}+7^{2}-48}=\sqrt{10}
$$
For the second circle, centre $(3,0)$; radius $r_{2}=3$
So, $r_{1}+r_{2} < d$ (distance between the centres)
$\therefore$ Circle don't cut and hence the number of common tangents $=4$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A circle has radius 3 and its centre lies on the line $y=x-1$. The equation of the circle, if it passes through $(7,3)$, is
questions.solution:
Let the centre of the circle be $(h, k)$. Since the centre lies on the line $y=x-1$ $\therefore k=h-1 ......(i)$
Since the circle passes through the point $(7,3)$, therefore, the distance of the centre from this point is the radius of the circle.
$\therefore \quad 3=\sqrt{(h-7)^{2}+(k-3)^{2}}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 3=\sqrt{(h-7)^{2}+(h-1-3)^{2}}$ $\Rightarrow \quad h=7$ or $h=4$
For $h=7$, we get $k=6$ and for $h=4$, we get $k=3$
Hence, the circles which satisfy the given conditions are:
$(x-7)^{2}+(y-6)^{2}=9$
or $x^{2}+y^{2}-14 x+12 y+76=0$
and $(x-4)^{2}+(y-3)^{2}=9$ or
$$
x^{2}+y^{2}-8 x-6 y+16=0
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In a model, it is shown that an arc of a bridge is semielliptical with major axis horizontal. If the length of the base is $9 \mathrm{~m}$ and the highest part of the bridge is $3 \mathrm{~m}$ from horizontal; the best approximation of the height of the arch, $2 \mathrm{~m}$ from the centre of the base is
questions.solution:
Equation of the semielliptical bridge $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ ...(1) Here, $2 \mathrm{a}=9$ $\begin{array}{l} \therefore \mathrm{a}=\frac{9}{2}, \quad \mathrm{~b}=3 \\ \therefore \frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{\frac{81}{4}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{9}=1 \end{array}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{4 \mathrm{x}^{2}}{81}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{9}=1$ ...(2)
Here, $\mathrm{OQ}=2 \mathrm{~m}$, let $\mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{y}_{1}$ $\therefore \mathrm{P}\left(2, \mathrm{y}_{1}\right)$ Since point P lies on the ellipse (2) $\therefore \frac{4 \times 4}{81}+\frac{\mathrm{y}_{1}^{2}}{9}=1$ $\Rightarrow \frac{y_{1}^{2}}{9}=1-\frac{16}{81}=\frac{81-16}{81}=\frac{65}{81}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}_{1}^{2}=\frac{65}{9} \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}_{1}=\frac{\sqrt{65}}{3} \simeq \frac{8}{3} \mathrm{~m}$ Hence, best approximation of the height of the arch $=\frac{8}{3} \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The maximum value of $\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{\mathrm{x}}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{y}=\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{\mathrm{x}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x}^{-\mathrm{x}}$
Then $\log y=-x \log x$
$\therefore \frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=-(1+\log x)$
or $\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\mathrm{y}(1+\log \mathrm{x})$
$\& \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=-\mathrm{y} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}-(1+\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=-\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{\mathrm{x}+1}-(1+\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}$
Now, $\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0 \Rightarrow 1+\log \mathrm{x}=0$
$\Rightarrow \log x=-1=-\log \mathrm{e}=\log \left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}\right)$
Also, $\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}$ at $\mathrm{x}=\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}\right)$ is $-\mathrm{e}^{1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}} < 0$
$\left[\because \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0\right]$
So, $x=\left(\frac{1}{e}\right)$ is a point of local maxima.
$\therefore$ Maximum value
$=\left(\right.$ value of $\mathrm{y}$ when $\left.\mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}\right)=\mathrm{e}^{\frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The two curves $y=3^{x}$ and $y=5^{x}$ intersect at an angle
questions.solution:
Given curves are $y=3^{x}$ ...(i)
and $y=5^{x}$ ...(ii)
intersect at the point $(0,1)$
Now, differentiating eqs. (i) and (ii) w.r.t. $x$, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{d y}{d x}=3^{x} \log 3 \text { and } \frac{d y}{d x}=5^{x} \log 5 \\
\Rightarrow\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(0,1)}=\log 3 \text { and }\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(0,1)}=\log 5 \\
\Rightarrow m_{1}=\log 3 \text { and } m_{2}=\log 5
\end{array}
$$
Angle between these curves is given by
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\tan \theta=\frac{m_{1}-m_{2}}{1+m_{1} m_{2}} \\
\Rightarrow \tan \theta=\frac{\log 3-\log 5}{1+\log 3 \cdot \log 5} \\
\Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\log 3-\log 5}{1+\log 3 \log 5}\right)
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the region bounded by the straight lines $x=0$ and $x=2$ and the curves $y=2^{x}$ and $\mathrm{y}=2 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{2}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Required area $=\int_{0}^{2}\left(\mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{1}\right) \mathrm{dx}$
$=\int_{0}^{2}\left(2^{x}-2 x+x^{2}\right) d x$ $=\left[\frac{2^{x}}{\log 2}-x^{2}+\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{2}$ $=\frac{4}{\log 2}-4+\frac{8}{3}-\frac{1}{\log 2}=\frac{3}{\log 2}-\frac{4}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\int_{1}^{4}|\mathrm{x}-3| \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \int_{1}^{4}|\mathrm{x}-3| \mathrm{dx}=\int_{1}^{3}-(\mathrm{x}-3) \mathrm{dx}+\int_{3}^{4}(\mathrm{x}-3) \mathrm{dx} \\
&=-\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2}-3 \mathrm{x}\right]_{1}^{3}+\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2}-3 \mathrm{x}\right]_{3}^{4} \\
&=-\frac{1}{2}\left[3^{2}-1^{2}\right]+3[3-1]+\frac{1}{2} \cdot\left[4^{2}-3^{2}\right]-3[4-3] \\
&=-\frac{1}{2}(8)+3(2)+\frac{1}{2} \cdot(7)-3(1) \\
&=-4+6+\frac{7}{2}-3=\frac{5}{2}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\left(\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)^{7}}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{d x}{\left(a^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{7}}$ Put $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a} \tan \theta \Rightarrow \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{a} \sec ^{2} \theta \mathrm{d} \theta$ limit at $x=0 \Rightarrow \theta=0 \& x=\infty \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}$ $\therefore I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{a \sec ^{2} \theta}{a^{14}\left(1+\tan ^{2} \theta\right)^{7}} d \theta$ $=\frac{1}{a^{13}} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1}{\sec ^{12} \theta} d \theta=\frac{1}{a^{13}} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cos ^{12} \theta \cdot d \theta$ But $\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin ^{2 m-1} \theta \cdot \cos ^{2 n-1} \theta \cdot \mathrm{d} \theta=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{n})$ $\& \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{n})=$
$\therefore \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}^{13}} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin ^{0} \theta \cdot \cos ^{12} \theta \mathrm{d} \theta$ $\therefore \mathrm{m}=\frac{1}{2}, \mathrm{n}=\frac{13}{2}$
$=\left(\frac{231}{2048} \cdot \frac{1}{a^{13}}\right) \pi$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $A$ be the centre of the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}-2 x-4 y$ $20=0$, and $B(1,7)$ and $D(4,-2)$ are points on the circle then, if tangents be drawn at $\mathrm{B}$ and $D$, which meet at $C$, then area of quadrilateral $A B C D$ is -
questions.solution: Here, centre is $A(1,2)$, and Tangent at $B$ $(1,7)$ is $x .1+y .7-1(x+1)-2(y+7)-20=0$ or $y=7$ $. .(1)$ Tangent at $D(4,-2)$ is $$ 3 x-4 y-20=0 $$ Solving (1) and (2), we get $C$ is (16,7) Area $A B C D=2$ (Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ ) $$ \begin{array}{l} =2 \times \frac{1}{2} A B \times B C \\ A B \times B C=5 \times 15=75 \text { units } \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(f(z)=\frac{7-z}{1-z^2}\), where \(z=1+2 i\), then \(|f(z)|\) is equal to :
questions.solution: \(\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{z}=1+2 \mathrm{i} \Rightarrow|\mathrm{z}|=\sqrt{1+4}=\sqrt{5} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{z})=\frac{7-\mathrm{z}}{1-\mathrm{z}^2}=\frac{7-1-2 \mathrm{i}}{1-(1+2 \mathrm{i})^2} \\ & =\frac{6-2 \mathrm{i}}{1-(1-4+4 \mathrm{i})}=\frac{6-2 \mathrm{i}}{4-4 \mathrm{i}}=\frac{3-\mathrm{i}}{2-2 \mathrm{i}} \\ & \Rightarrow|\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{z})|=\left|\frac{3-\mathrm{i}}{2-2 \mathrm{i}}\right|=\frac{|3-\mathrm{i}|}{|2-2 \mathrm{i}|} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{9+1}}{\sqrt{4+4}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{2}=\frac{|\mathrm{z}|}{2}\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation $z \bar{z}+(2-3 i) z+(2+3 i) \bar{z}+4=0$ represents a circle of radius
questions.solution:
Consider the equation
$z \bar{z}+(2-3 i) z+(2+3 i) \bar{z}+4=0$
Let $z=x+i y$ and $\bar{z}=x-i y, z \bar{z}=x^{2}+y^{2}$
Put value of $z, \bar{z} \quad$ and $z \bar{z}$ in equation (1), we get
$\begin{array}{l}
\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)+(2-3 i)(x+i y)+(2+3 i)(x-i y) \\
+4=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad 4 x+6 y+4+x^{2}+y^{2}=0
\end{array}$
Now, we make it perfect square
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+y^{2}+4 x+6 y+4+4+9=4+9 \\
\Rightarrow(x+2)^{2}+(y+3)^{2}=9
\end{array}$
This represents a circle of radius $3 .$

$$

questions.q: If \(\arg \left(\bar{z}_1\right)=\arg \left(z_2\right)\), then
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \bar{z}_1=\frac{\mathrm{z}_1 \overline{\mathrm{z}}_1}{\mathrm{z}_1}=\left|\mathrm{z}_1\right|^2 \mathrm{z}_1^{-1} \\

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Amplitude of $\frac{1+\sqrt{3} \mathrm{i}}{\sqrt{3}+1}$ is :
questions.solution:
$\operatorname{Let} r(\cos \theta+i \sin \theta)$
$=\frac{1+i \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}+1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}+1}+i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}+1}$
$\Rightarrow r \cos \theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}+1} ; r \sin \theta=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}+1}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The complex number $z$ satisfying $z+|z|$ $=1+7 i$, then the value of $|z|^2$ equals
questions.solution:
Let $z=x+i y$
So, $z+|z|=x+i y+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}=1+7 i$
$\Rightarrow\left(x+\sqrt{x^2+y^2}\right)+i y=1+7 i$
By comparing both sides, we get
$\begin{aligned}
& & x+\sqrt{x^2+y^2} & =1 \text { and } y=7 \\
\Rightarrow & & x+\sqrt{x^2+(7)^2} & =1 \\
\Rightarrow & & x+\sqrt{x^2+49} & =1 \\
\Rightarrow & & \sqrt{x^2+49} & =1-x \\
\Rightarrow & & x^2+49 & =1+x^2-2 x \\
\Rightarrow & & 2 x & =-48 \Rightarrow x=-24
\end{aligned}$
Therefore, $z=-24+7 i$
$\begin{array}{ll} 
& |z|=\sqrt{(-24)^2+(7)^2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & |z|^2=576+49=625
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the complex number $z$ lies on a circle with centre at the origin and radius $\frac{1}{4}$, then the complex number $-1+8 z$ lies on a circle with radius
questions.solution:
Given: $|z|=\frac{1}{4}$
Let $\mathrm{z}^{\prime}=-1+\overline{8} \mathrm{z}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{z}=\frac{\mathrm{z}^{\prime}+1}{8} \Rightarrow|\mathrm{z}|=\frac{\left|\mathrm{z}^{\prime}+1\right|}{8} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4}=\frac{\left|\mathrm{z}^{\prime}+1\right|}{8} \Rightarrow\left|\mathrm{z}^{\prime}+1\right|=2
\end{array}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{z}^{\prime}$ lies on a circle with centre $(-1,0)$ and radius 2 .

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x^{2}+x+1=0$, then the value of $\sum_{n=1}^{6}\left(x^{n}+\frac{1}{x^{n}}\right)^{2}$ is
questions.solution:
Given equation is $x^{2}+x+1=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=\omega$ and $x=\omega^{2}$
Case I : When $x=\omega$
Then
$\sum_{n=1}^{6}\left[x^{n}+\frac{1}{x^{n}}\right]^{2}=\sum_{n=1}^{6}\left[\omega^{n}+\omega^{2 n}\right]^{2}\left[\because \frac{1}{\omega}=\omega^{2}\right]$
$=\left(\omega+\omega^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\omega^{2}+\omega^{4}\right)^{2}+\left(\omega^{3}+\omega^{6}\right)^{2}$
$+\left(\omega^{4}+\omega^{8}\right)^{2}+\left(\omega^{5}+\omega^{10}\right)^{2}+\left(\omega^{6}+\omega^{12}\right)^{2}$
$\begin{array}{r}
=(-1)^{2}+(-1)^{2}+(2)^{2}+(-1)^{2} \\
+(-1)^{2}+(2)^{2}=12
\end{array}$
Case II: When $x=\omega^{2}$
Then
$\begin{array}{c}
\sum_{\mathrm{n}=1}^{6}\left[\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}}\right]^{2}=\sum_{\mathrm{n}=1}^{6}\left[\omega^{2 \mathrm{n}}+\omega^{\mathrm{n}}\right]^{2}\left[\because \frac{1}{\omega^{2}}=\omega\right] \\
=12
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x+i y=\frac{3}{2+\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}$, then $x^{2}+y^{2}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$x+i y$
$=\frac{3}{2+\cos \theta+i \sin \theta}=\frac{3(2+\cos \theta-i \sin \theta)}{(2+\cos \theta)^{2}+\sin ^{2} \theta}$
$\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{6+3 \cos \theta-3 i \sin \theta}{4+\cos ^{2} \theta+4 \cos \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta} \\
=\frac{6+3 \cos \theta-3 i \sin \theta}{5+4 \cos \theta} \\
=\left(\frac{6+3 \cos \theta}{5+4 \cos \theta}\right)+\left(\frac{-3 \sin \theta}{5+4 \cos \theta}\right)
\end{array}$
On equating real and imaginary parts, we get
$x=\frac{3(2+\cos \theta)}{5+4 \cos \theta}$
And $y=\frac{-3 \sin \theta}{5+4 \cos \theta}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore x^{2}+y^{2}=\frac{9\left[4+\cos ^{2} \theta+4 \cos \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta\right]}{(5+4 \cos \theta)^{2}} \\
=\frac{9}{5+4 \cos \theta}=4\left(\frac{6+3 \cos \theta}{5+4 \cos ^{2} \theta}\right)-3 \\
=4 x-3
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\cos \alpha+i \sin \alpha, b=\cos \beta+i \sin \beta$, $c=\cos \gamma+i \sin \gamma$ and $\frac{b}{c}+\frac{c}{a}+\frac{a}{b}=1$, then $\cos (\beta-\gamma)+\cos (\gamma-\alpha)+\cos (\alpha-\beta)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given: $a=\cos \alpha+i \sin \alpha$ $b=\cos \beta+i \sin \beta$
and $\mathrm{c}=\cos \gamma+\mathrm{i} \sin \gamma$
Now, $\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\cos \beta+\mathrm{i} \sin \beta}{\cos \gamma+\mathrm{i} \sin \gamma} \times \frac{\cos \gamma-\mathrm{i} \sin \gamma}{\cos \gamma-\mathrm{i} \sin \gamma}$
$\begin{aligned} \cos \beta \cdot \cos \gamma+\sin \beta \cdot \sin \gamma+\mathrm{i} & \\ &[\sin \beta \cdot \cos \gamma-\sin \gamma \cdot \cos \beta] \end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{c}}=\cos (\beta-\gamma)+\mathrm{i} \sin (\beta-\gamma)$
Similarly, $\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{a}}=\cos (\gamma-\alpha)+\mathrm{i} \sin (\gamma-\alpha)$ and $\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}=\cos (\alpha-\beta)+\mathrm{i} \sin (\alpha-\beta)$
On adding Eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii), we get $\cos (\beta-\alpha)+\cos (\gamma-\alpha)+\cos (\alpha+\beta)+i$
$\begin{array}{r}
{[\sin (\beta-\gamma)+\sin (\gamma-\alpha)+\sin (\alpha-\beta)]=1} \\
{\left[\because \frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{c}}+\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{a}}+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}=1\right]}
\end{array}$
On equating real parts, we get $\cos (\beta-\gamma)+\cos (\gamma-\alpha)+\cos (\alpha-\beta)=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $|z+4| \leq 3$, then the greatest and the least value of $|z+1|$ are
questions.solution:
$|z+4| \leq 3 \Rightarrow-3 \leq z+4 \leq 3$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow-6 \leq \mathrm{z}+1 \leq 0 \\
\Rightarrow 0 \leq-(\mathrm{z}+1) \leq 6 \\
\Rightarrow 0 \leq|\mathrm{z}+1| \leq 6
\end{array}$
Hence, the greatest and least values are 6 and 0.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A complex number $z$ is such that arg $\left(\frac{z-2}{z+2}\right)=\frac{z}{3}$. The points representing this complex number will lie on
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\arg \left(\frac{z-2}{z+2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3} \\
\Rightarrow \arg \left(\frac{x-2+i y}{x+2+i y}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3} \\
\Rightarrow \arg (x-2+i y)-\arg (x+2+i y)=\frac{\pi}{3} \\
\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x-2}\right)-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x+2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{4 y}{x^{2}+y^{2}-4}=\sqrt{3} \\
\Rightarrow \sqrt{3}\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)-4 y-4 \sqrt{3}=0
\end{array}$
which is an equation of a circle.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $|z| \geq 3$, then the least value of $\left|z+\frac{1}{4}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
$\left|z+\frac{1}{4}\right|$
$=\left|z-\left(\frac{-1}{4}\right)\right| \geq|z|-\left|\frac{-1}{4}\right|$
$=\left|(-z)-\frac{1}{4}\right| \geq\left|3-\frac{1}{4}\right|=\frac{11}{4}$
$\therefore \quad\left|z+\frac{1}{4}\right| \geq \frac{11}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The locus of $\mathrm{z}$ satisfying the inequality $\left|\frac{z+2 i}{2 z+i}\right| < 1$, where $z=x+i y$, is
questions.solution:
Let $z=x+i y$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { Given : }\left|\frac{z+2 i}{2 z+i}\right| < 1 \\
\Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{(x)^{2}+(y+2)^{2}}}{\sqrt{(2 x)^{2}+(2 y+1)^{2}}} < 1 \\
\Rightarrow x^{2}+y^{2}+4+4 y < 4 x^{2}+4 y^{2}+1+4 y
\end{array}
$$
$$
\Rightarrow 3 x^{2}+3 y^{2}>3 \Rightarrow x^{2}+y^{2}>1
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{n}$ is an integer which leaves remainder one when divided by three, then $(1+\sqrt{3} i)^{n}+(1-\sqrt{3} i)^{n}$ equals

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}^{2}-2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}+\mathrm{h}=0,|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|>\sqrt{\mathrm{h}}$, represents
questions.solution:
Vector equation of a sphere in central form with centre having position vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ and radius $R$ is
$\begin{array}{l}
|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=\mathrm{R} \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|^{2}=\mathrm{R}^{2} \\
\Rightarrow(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}) \cdot(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})=\mathrm{R}^{2} \\
\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{R}^{2} \\
\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}|^{2}-2(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|^{2}=\mathrm{R}^{2} \\
\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}|^{2}-2(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|^{2}-\mathrm{R}^{2}=0
\end{array}$
If $\mathrm{h}=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|^{2}-\mathrm{R}^{2}$ i.e. $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|>\sqrt{\mathrm{h}}$, then
$|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}|^{2}-2(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})+\mathrm{h}=0$
Hence, the given equation represents a sphere.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The simplified expression of $\sin \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)$, for any real number $x$ is given by
questions.solution:
Let $\tan ^{-1} x=\theta$
$\Rightarrow x=\tan \theta=\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}=\frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}$
$\Rightarrow x=\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}=\sin \theta$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}\left(1-\sin ^{2} \theta\right)=\sin ^{2} \theta$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}=\sin ^{2} \theta\left(1+x^{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \sin ^{2} \theta=\frac{x^{2}}{1+x^{2}} \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \theta=\sin ^{-1} \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x} &=\sin ^{-1} \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}} \\ \text { Now, } \sin \left(\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right) &=\sin \left(\sin ^{-1} \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}\right) \\ &=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\cos x+\cos ^{2} x=1$ then the value of $\sin ^{12} x+3 \sin ^{10} x+3 \sin ^{8} x+\sin ^{6} x-1$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given : $\cos x+\cos ^{2} x=1$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \cos x=1-\cos ^{2} x \Rightarrow \cos x=\sin ^{2} x \\
\Rightarrow \cos ^{2} x=\sin ^{4} x \Rightarrow 1-\sin ^{2} x=\sin ^{4} x \\
\Rightarrow \sin ^{4} x+\sin ^{2} x=1
\end{array}$
cubic both sides, we have
$\begin{array}{l}
\sin ^{12} x+\sin ^{6} x+3 \sin ^{6} x\left(\sin ^{4} x+\sin ^{2} x\right)=1 \\
\Rightarrow \sin ^{12} x+\sin ^{6} x+3 \sin ^{10} x+3 \sin ^{8} x=1 \\
\Rightarrow \sin ^{12} x+3 \sin ^{10} x+3 \sin ^{8} x+\sin ^{6} x-1=0
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\left(1+\omega-\omega^{2}\right)^{7}$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} &\left(1+\omega-\omega^{2}\right)^{7}=\left(-\omega^{2}-\omega^{2}\right)^{7}=\left(-2 \omega^{2}\right)^{7} \\ &=-128\left(\omega^{4}\right)^{3} \omega^{2}=-128 \omega^{2} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: One of the values of $\left(\frac{1+i}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2 / 3}$ is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\left(\frac{1+i}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2 / 3}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} i\right)^{2 / 3} \\
=&\left(\cos 45^{\circ}+i \sin 45^{\circ}\right)^{2 / 3} \\
=&\left(\cos \frac{2}{3} \times 45^{\circ}+i \sin \frac{2}{3} 45^{\circ}\right) \\
=& \cos 30^{\circ}+i \sin 30^{\circ} \\
=& \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+i \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{3}+i)
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\omega=\frac{-1+\sqrt{3} i}{2}$ then $\left(3+\omega+3 \omega^{2}\right)^{4}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\omega=\frac{-1+\sqrt{3} i}{2}$
Now, $1+\omega+\omega^{2}=0$, and $\omega^{3}=1$
then $\left(3+\omega+3 \omega^{2}\right)^{4}=\left(3+\omega+3 \omega^{2}+2 \omega-2 \omega\right)^{4}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\left(3+3 \omega+3 \omega^{2}-2 \omega\right)^{4}=\left[3\left(1+2+\omega^{2}\right)-2 \omega\right]^{4} \\
=(0-2 \omega)^{4}=16 \omega^{4}=16 \omega \quad\left(\because \omega^{3}=1\right)
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{a}=\cos 2 \alpha+\mathrm{i} \sin 2 \alpha, \mathrm{b}=\cos 2 \beta+\mathrm{i} \sin 2 \beta$, $c=\cos 2 \gamma+i \sin 2 \gamma$ and $d=\cos 2 \delta+i \sin 2 \delta$, then $\sqrt{\mathrm{abcd}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{abcd}}}=$
questions.solution:
We have,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\begin{array}{l}
\text { abcd }=\cos (2 \alpha+2 \beta+2 \gamma+2 \delta)+i \sin (2 \alpha+2 \beta \\
+2 \gamma+2 \delta) \\
\therefore \sqrt{\text { abcd }}=[\cos (2 \alpha+2 \beta+2 \gamma+2 \delta) \\
+i \sin (2 \alpha+2 \beta+2 \gamma+2 \delta)]^{1 / 2} \\
\text { or } \sqrt{a b c d} \\
=\cos (\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta)+i \sin (\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta) \ldots . .(1)
\end{array}
\end{array}
$$
[De Moivre's Theorem]
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{abcd}}}=\cos (\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta)-\mathrm{i} \sin (\alpha+\beta \\
+\gamma+\delta).....(2)
\end{array}
$$
Adding (1) and (2), we obtain
$$
\sqrt{\mathrm{abcd}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{abcd}}}=2 \cos (\alpha+\beta+\gamma+\delta)
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the rank of the matrix $\left(\begin{array}{ccc}-1 & 2 & 5 \\ 2 & -4 & a-4 \\ 1 & -2 & a+1\end{array}\right)$ is 1, then the value of a is
questions.solution:
Let
$\mathrm{A}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
-1 & 2 & 5 \\
2 & -4 & \mathrm{a}-4 \\
1 & -2 & \mathrm{a}+1
\end{array}\right|-\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
-1 & 2 & 5 \\
0 & 0 & \mathrm{a}+6 \\
0 & 0 & \mathrm{a}+6
\end{array}\right|$
$\left[\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}+2 \mathrm{R}_{1}, \mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}+\mathrm{R}_{1}\right]$
clearly rank of $A$ is 1 if $a=-6$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The product of all values of $(\cos \alpha+\mathrm{i} \sin \alpha)^{3 / 5}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} &(\cos \alpha+i \sin \alpha)^{3 / 5}=(\cos 3 \alpha+i \sin 3 \alpha)^{1 / 5} \\=&[\cos (2 k \pi+3 \alpha)+i \sin (2 k \pi+3 \alpha)]^{1 / 5} \\=&\left[\cos \left(\frac{2 k \pi+3 \alpha}{5}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{2 k \pi+3 \alpha}{5}\right)\right] \\ & \text { where } k=0,1,2,3,4 \end{aligned}$
Product of all values.
$\begin{array}{l}
=\left(\cos \frac{3 \alpha}{5}+i \sin \frac{3 \alpha}{5}\right) \cdot\left(\cos \left(\frac{2 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}\right)\right. \\
\left.+i \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}\right)\right) \cdot\left(\cos \frac{4 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}+i \sin \right. \\
\left.\frac{4 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}\right) \cdot\left(\cos \frac{6 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}+i \sin \frac{6 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}\right) \\
=\cos \left[\frac{3 \alpha}{5}+\frac{2 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}+\frac{4 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}+\frac{6 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}+\right. \\
\left.\frac{8 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}\right]+i \sin \left[\frac{3 \alpha}{5}+\frac{2 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}+\frac{4 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}\right. \\
\left.\quad+\frac{6 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}+\frac{8 \pi+3 \alpha}{5}\right]
\end{array}$
$=\cos \left[\frac{5}{2}\left(2 \cdot \frac{3 \alpha}{5}+\cdot(5-1) \cdot\left(\frac{2 \pi}{5}\right)\right)\right]$
$\quad+i \sin \left[\frac{5}{2}\left(2 \cdot \frac{3 \alpha}{5}+(5-1) \cdot\left(\frac{2 \pi}{5}\right)\right)\right]$
$=\cos \left[\frac{5}{2} \cdot\left(\frac{6 \alpha}{5}+\left(\frac{8 \pi}{5}\right)\right)\right]+$
$=\cos (3 \alpha+4 \pi)+i \sin (3 \alpha+4 \pi)$
$=\cos (4 \pi+3 \alpha)+i \sin (4 \pi+3 \alpha)$
$=\cos 3 \alpha+i \sin 3 \alpha$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \vec{b}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}$ then $t$ such that $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+\mathrm{t} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}$ is at right angle to $\vec{c}$ will be equal to
questions.solution:
We have,$\vec{a}+t \vec{b}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+t(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})$
It is $\perp$ to $\mathrm{c}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}$
If $3(1-t)+(2+2 t)+(3+t)(0)=0$ 
$\Rightarrow 3-3 \mathrm{t}+2+2 \mathrm{t}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=5$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: What is the area of a loop of the curve $r=\operatorname{asin} 3 \theta ?$
questions.solution:
If curver $=a \sin 3 \theta$ To trace the curve, we consider the following table : \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline 3 \theta= & 0 & \frac{\pi}{2} & \pi & \frac{3 \pi}{2} & 2 \pi & \frac{5 \pi}{2} & 3 \pi \\ \hline\theta= & 0 & \frac{\pi}{6} & \frac{\pi}{3} & \frac{\pi}{2} & \frac{2 \pi}{3} & \frac{5 \pi}{6} & \pi \\ \hliner= & 0 & \mathrm{a} & 0 & -\mathrm{a} & 0 & \mathrm{a} & 0 \\ \hline \end{array} Thus there is a loop between $\theta=0 \& \theta=\frac{\pi}{3}$ as r varies from $\mathrm{r}=0$ tor $=0$.
Hence, the area of the loop lying in the positive quadrant $=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} r^{2} \mathrm{~d} \theta$ $=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \sin ^{2} \phi \cdot \frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~d} \phi$ [On putting, $\left.3 \theta=\phi \Rightarrow \mathrm{d} \theta=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~d} \phi\right]$ $=\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{6} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin ^{2} \phi \mathrm{d} \phi$ $=\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{6} \cdot \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1-\cos 2 \phi}{2} \mathrm{~d} \phi$ $\left[\because \cos 2 \theta=1-2 \sin ^{2} \theta\right]$ $=\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{12} \cdot\left[\phi+\frac{\sin 2 \phi}{2}\right]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}$ $=\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{12} \cdot\left[\frac{\pi}{2}+\sin \pi\right]=\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2} \pi}{24}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of $\left(x \operatorname{cosec}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)-\mathrm{y}\right) \mathrm{dx}+\mathrm{xdy}=0$ is
questions.solution:
Given : $\left(x \operatorname{cosec}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-y\right) d x+x d y=0$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow\left(\frac{x}{\sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}-y\right) d x+x d y=0 \\
\Rightarrow\left[x-y \cdot \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right] d x+x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) d y=0 \\
\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y \sin \frac{y}{x}-x}{x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)} ...(1)
\end{array}$
Put $\frac{y}{x}=z \Rightarrow y=z x$
$\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=z .1+x \cdot \frac{d z}{d x}=z+x \frac{d z}{d x}$
$\therefore$ Equation (1) becomes
$z+x \frac{d z}{d x}=\frac{z x \cdot \sin z-x}{x \sin z}=z-\operatorname{cosec} z$
$\Rightarrow x \frac{d z}{d x}=-\operatorname{cosecz} \Rightarrow-\sin z d z=\frac{d x}{x}$
$\Rightarrow \log |x|=\cos z+c$
$\Rightarrow \log |x|-\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=c$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral $\int_{1}^{9} \mathrm{e}^{\sqrt{t}} \mathrm{dt}=$
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_{1}^{9} \mathrm{e}^{\sqrt{\mathrm{t}}} \mathrm{dt}$
Put $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{x}^{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{dt}=2 \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{dx}$
For limit : $x=1 \& x=3$
$\begin{array}{l}
I=\int_{1}^{3} e^{x} \cdot 2 x d x=2 \int_{1}^{3} e^{x} \cdot x d x \\
\Rightarrow I=2\left[\left\{x \cdot e^{x}\right\}_{1}^{3}-\int_{1}^{3} e^{x} d x\right] \\
=2\left[\left\{x e^{x}\right\}_{1}^{3}-\left\{e^{x}\right\}_{1}^{3}\right] \\
\Rightarrow I=2\left[3 e^{3}-e^{1}-e^{3}+e^{1}\right]=\left[2 e^{3}\right] \cdot 2=4 e^{3}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the area of the triangle on the complex plane formed by the points $z, z+i z$ and iz is 200 , then the value of $3|z|$ must be equal to
questions.solution:
Let $z=x+i y$, then
$z+i z=x+i y+i(x+i y)=(x-y)+i(x+y)$ and $i z=i(x+i y)=-y+i x$,
Then, the area of the triangle formed by these lines is
$\Delta=\frac{1}{2}\left\|\begin{array}{ccc}
\mathrm{x} & \mathrm{y} & 1 \\
(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}) & (\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}) & 1 \\
-\mathrm{y} & \mathrm{x} & 1
\end{array}\right\|$
Applying $\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}-\left(\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{3}\right)$
$\Delta=\frac{1}{2}\left\|\begin{array}{ccc}
\mathrm{x} & \mathrm{y} & 1 \\
0 & 0 & -1 \\
-\mathrm{y} & \mathrm{x} & 1
\end{array}\right\|=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}\right)$
(given)
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}|z|^{2}=200 \\
\quad|z|^{2}=400 \Rightarrow|z|=20 \\
\therefore 3|z|=3 \times 20=60
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $a^{x}=b^{y}=c^{z}=d^{u}$ and $a, b, c, d$ are in GP, then $x$, $y, z, u$ are in
questions.solution:
$a^{x}=b^{y}=c^{z}=d^{u t}$ Let, $a^{x}=b^{y}=c^{z}=d^{u t}=k$ $\Rightarrow a=k^{1 / x}, b=k^{1 / y}, c=k^{1 / z}, d=k^{1 / u}$...(i)
$a, b, c, d$ are in GP.
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \frac{b}{a}=\frac{c}{b}=\frac{d}{c} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{k^{1 / y}}{k^{1 / x}}=\frac{k^{1 / z}}{k^{1 / y}}=\frac{k^{1 / u}}{k^{1 / z}}\{\text { using Eq. (i) }\} \\
\Rightarrow k^{\frac{1}{y} \frac{1}{x}}=k^{\frac{1}{z} \frac{1}{y}}=\frac{1}{k^{u}} \frac{1}{z} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y}-\frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{z}-\frac{1}{y}=\frac{1}{u}-\frac{1}{z}
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore \frac{1}{x}, \frac{1}{y}, \frac{1}{z}, \frac{1}{u}$ are in A.P.
$\Rightarrow x, y, z, u$ are in H.P.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $S$ be a set containing $n$ elements. Then, number of binary operations on $\mathrm{S}$ is
questions.solution: The number of binary operations on a set $S$ having $n$ elements in $n^{n^{2}}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Argument of the complex number $\left(\frac{-1-3 i}{2+i}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
We have,
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{-1-3 \mathrm{i}}{2+\mathrm{i}}=\frac{-1-3 \mathrm{i}}{2+\mathrm{i}} \times \frac{2-\mathrm{i}}{2-\mathrm{i}}=\frac{-2-5 \mathrm{i}+3 \mathrm{i}^{2}}{4-\mathrm{i}^{2}} \\
=-1-\mathrm{i}
\end{array}$
Now, let us put $-1=r \cos \theta,-1=r \sin \theta$
Squaring and adding, $r^{2}=2$ i.e., $r=\sqrt{2}$
So that $\cos \theta=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}, \sin \theta=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}$
Therefore, $\theta=225^{\circ}$
Thus, argument is $225^{\circ}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: At how many points between the interval $(-\infty, \infty)$ is the function $f(x)=\sin x$ is not differentiable.
questions.solution: The function $f(x)=\sin x$ is differentiable for all $x \in R$. Therefore the number of points in the interval $(-\infty, \infty)$ where the function is not differentiable are zero.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The no. of points of discontinuity of the function $f(x)=x-[x]$ in the interval $(0,7)$ are
questions.solution:
The graph of the function $f(x)=x-[x]$ for the interval $(0,7)$ is shown below:
It is obvious from the above graph that the function $x-[x]$ is discontinuous at the points $x=1,2,3,4,5,6 .$ Therefore no. of points of discontinuity of the given function in the given interval are $6 .$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let \(f: R \rightarrow R\) be a function defined by \(f(x)=\min \{x+1,|x|+1\}\), Then which of the following is true?
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned} & f(x)=\min \{x+1,|x|+1\} \Rightarrow f(x) \\ & =x+1 \forall x \in R \end{aligned}\)
Hence, \(f(x)\) is differentiable everywhere for all \(x\) \(\in R\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $f$ be the function defined by
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}
\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}-2|x-1|-1}, & x \neq 1 \\
1 / 2, & x=1
\end{array}\right.$
options: The function is continuous for all values of $\mathrm{x}$
The function is continuous only for $x>1$
The function is not continuous at $x=1$
questions.solution:
For $x < 1, f(x)=\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}+2 x-3}=\frac{x+1}{x+3}$ $\therefore \lim _{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\frac{1}{2}$
For $x>1, f(x)=\frac{x^{2}-1}{x^{2}-2 x+1}=\frac{x+1}{x-1}$
$$
\therefore \lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 1^{+}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\infty
$$
$\therefore$ the function is not continuous at $x=1$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let \(f(x)=|\sin x|\). Then
options: \(\mathrm{f}\) is everywhere continuous but not differentiable at \(\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{n} \pi, \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{Z}\)
\(\mathrm{f}\) is everywhere continuous but not differentiable at \(\mathrm{x}=(2 \mathrm{n}+1) \frac{\pi}{2}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{Z} \text {. }\)
questions.solution: From the graph of \(f(x)=|\sin x|\), it is clear that \(f(x)\) is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at \(x=n \pi, n \in Z\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $f: R \rightarrow R$ is defined by
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\frac{2 \sin x-\sin 2 x}{2 x \cos x}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ a, & \text { if } x=0\end{array}\right.$
then the value of $a$ so that $f$ is continuous at 0 is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { (d) } f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
\frac{2 \sin x-\sin 2 x}{2 x \cos x}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\
a, & \text { if } x=0
\end{array}\right. \\
\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \sin x-\sin 2 x}{2 x \cos x}\left(\frac{0}{0} \text { form }\right) \\
=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \cos x-2 \cos 2 x}{2(\cos x-x \sin x)}
\end{array}
$$
$$
=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2-2}{2(1-0)}=0
$$
Since, $f(x)$ is contineous at $x=0$
$\therefore f(0)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x) \Rightarrow a=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{f}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}x^{2}, & x \leq 0 \\ 2 \sin x, x>0 & \text {, then } x=0 \text { is }\end{array}\right.$
questions.solution:
Given $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{rc}x^{2}, & x \leq 0 \\ 2 \sin x, & x>0,\end{array}\right.$
$$
f^{\prime}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
2 x, & x < 0 \\
\text { nondifferentiable, } & x=0 \\
2 \cos x, & x>0
\end{array}\right.
$$
So, $x=0$ is a critical point $f\left(0^{-}\right)>0$ as well as $\mathrm{f}\left(0^{+}\right)>0$ and $\mathrm{f}(0)=0$
Hence, it is a point of minima.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Everybody in a room shakes hands with everybody else. The total number of hand shakes is 66 . The total number of persons in the room is
questions.solution:
Let there are $\mathrm{n}$ persons in the room. The total number of hand shakes is same as the number of ways of selecting 2 out of $\mathrm{n}$.
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore{ }^{n} C_{2}=66 \Rightarrow \frac{n(n-1)}{2 !}=66 \\
\Rightarrow n^{2}-n-132=0 \\
\Rightarrow(n-12)(n+11)=0 \Rightarrow n=12
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=x+\frac{x}{1+x}+\frac{x}{(1+x)^2}+\ldots$ to $\infty$, then at $\mathrm{x}=0, \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$
questions.solution:
For $\mathrm{x} \neq 0$, we have,
$$
f(x)=x+\frac{x / 1+x}{1-\frac{1}{1+x}}=x+\frac{x / 1+x}{x / 1+x}=x+1
$$
For $x=0, f(x)=0$. Thus, $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}x+1, & x \neq 0 \\ 0, & x=0\end{array}\right.$ Clearly, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=1 \neq f(0)$.
So, $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is discontinuous and hence not differentiable at $\mathrm{x}=0$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}x \sin 1 / x & , \quad x \neq 0 \\ 0 & , x=0\end{array}\right.$ at $x=0$ is
questions.solution:
For function to be continuous :
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{f}(0+\mathrm{h})=\mathrm{f}(0-\mathrm{h})=\mathrm{f}(0) \\
\mathrm{f}(0+\mathrm{h})=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \mathrm{~h} \sin 1 / \mathrm{h}=0 \times(\text { a finite } \\
\text { quantity })=0 \\
\mathrm{f}(0-\mathrm{h})=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0}-\mathrm{h} \sin (1 /-\mathrm{h})=0 \times(\text { a finite } \\
\text { quantity })=0
\end{array}
$$
Also, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} x \sin 1 / x=0 \times($ a finite quantity $)$
$=0$
$\Rightarrow$ function is continuous at $x=0$
For function to be differentiable :
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0+\mathrm{h})=\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0-\mathrm{h}) \\
\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(0+\mathrm{h})=\frac{\mathrm{f}(0+\mathrm{h})-\mathrm{f}(0)}{\mathrm{h}} \\
=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\mathrm{h} \sin \frac{1}{\mathrm{~h}}-0}{\mathrm{~h}}=\lim _{\mathrm{h} \rightarrow 0} \sin \left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{~h}}\right)
\end{array}
$$
which does not exist.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The set of points of discontinuity of the function
$f(x)=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{(2 \sin x)^{2 n}}{3^{n}-(2 \cos x)^{2 n}}$ is given by
questions.solution:
We have, $f(x)=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{(2 \sin x)^{2 n}}{3^{n}-(2 \cos x)^{2 n}}$
$$
=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{(2 \sin x)^{2 n}}{(\sqrt{3})^{2 n}-(2 \cos x)^{2 n}}
$$
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is discontinuous when $(\sqrt{3})^{2 n}-(2 \cos x)^{2 n}=0$
i.e. $\cos x=\pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Rightarrow x=n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{6}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{l}\mathrm{mx}+1, \quad \mathrm{x} \leq \frac{\pi}{2} \\ \sin \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{n}, \mathrm{x}>\frac{\pi}{2}\end{array}\right.$ is continuous at $x=\frac{\pi}{2}$, then
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is continuous at $\mathrm{x}=\frac{\pi}{2}$.
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { So, } \lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{-}}{2}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi^{+}}{2}} f(x) \\
\Rightarrow m \frac{\pi}{2}+1=\sin \frac{\pi}{2}+n \\
\Rightarrow m \frac{\pi}{2}+1=1+n \Rightarrow \frac{m \pi}{2}=n
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}(1+|\sin x|)^{a / \sin x \mid} & ,-\frac{\pi}{6} < x < 0 \\ b & , x=0 \\ e^{\tan 2 x / \tan 3 x} & , 0 < x < -\frac{\pi}{6}\end{array}\right.$, then the value of $a$ and $b$, if $\mathrm{f}$ is continuous at $x=0$, are respectively.
questions.solution:
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}(1+|\sin x|)^{a /|\sin x|}, & -\frac{\pi}{6} < x < 0 \\ b & , \quad x=0 \\ e^{\tan 2 x / \tan 3 x} & , 0 < x < \frac{\pi}{6}\end{array}\right.$
For $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ to be continuous at $\mathrm{x}=0$
$\begin{array}{l}
\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=f(0)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x) \\
\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}}(1+|\sin x|)^{a /|\sin x|} \\
=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left\{|\sin x| \frac{a}{|\sin x|}\right\}=e^{a}
\end{array}$
$\text { Now, } \lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} e^{\tan 2 x / \tan 3 x}$
$=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} e^{\left(\frac{\tan 2 x}{2 x} \times 2 x\right) /\left(\frac{\tan 3 x}{3 x} \times 3 x\right)}$
$=\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 0^{+}} \mathrm{e}^{2 / 3}=\mathrm{e}^{2 / 3}$
Since, $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=0$.
$\therefore \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{e}^{2 / 3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\frac{2}{3}$ and $b=e^{2 / 3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=(1+\mathrm{x})^{2 / \mathrm{x}}$ for $\mathrm{x} \neq 0$ and $\mathrm{f}(0)=\mathrm{e}^{2}$ is
questions.solution: $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left[(1+x)^{1 / x}\right]^{2}=e^{2}=f(0)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_{\log \sqrt{\pi / 2}}^{\log \sqrt{\pi}} \mathrm{e}^{2 x} \sec ^2\left(\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{dx}$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{I}=\int_{\log \sqrt{\pi / 2}}^{\log \sqrt{\pi}} \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \sec ^2\left(\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{dx}$
Put $\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{dt}$
When
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x=\log \sqrt{\pi / 2}, t=e^{2 \log \sqrt{\pi / 2}} \\
& =\mathrm{e}^{\log \pi / 2}=\frac{\pi}{2} \\
& \text { When } \mathrm{x}=\log \sqrt{\pi}, \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{e}^{2 \log \sqrt{\pi}}=\pi \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$


$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \mathrm{I} & =\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^\pi \frac{1}{2} \sec ^2\left(\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{dt}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\frac{1}{3}}\left[\tan \frac{\mathrm{t}}{3} \cdot\right]_{\pi / 2}^\pi \\
& =\frac{3}{2}\left[\tan \frac{\pi}{3}-\tan \frac{\pi}{6}\right]=\frac{3}{2}\left[\sqrt{3}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right]=\sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
$\int_0^\pi\left[\cos ^2\left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}-\frac{x}{4}\right)-\cos ^2\left(\frac{11 \pi}{8}+\frac{x}{4}\right)\right] d x$
equals to
questions.solution:
$I=\int_0^\pi\left[\cos ^2\left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}-\frac{x}{4}\right)-\cos ^2\left(\frac{11 \pi}{8}+\frac{x}{4}\right)\right] d x$
$\begin{aligned} & =\int_0^\pi\left[\cos ^2\left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}-\frac{x}{4}\right)-\cos ^2\left(\pi+\left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}+\frac{x}{4}\right)\right] d x\right. \\ & =\int_0^\pi\left[\cos ^2\left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}-\frac{x}{4}\right)-\cos ^2\left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}+\frac{x}{4}\right)\right] d x\end{aligned}$
$=\int_0^\pi\left[\begin{array}{r}\cos \left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}-\frac{x}{4}+\frac{3 \pi}{8}+\frac{x}{4}\right) \\ \cdot \cos \left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}-\frac{x}{4}-\frac{3 \pi}{8}-\frac{x}{4}\right)\end{array}\right]$
$\begin{aligned} & =\int_0^\pi \cos \left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right) \cdot \cos \left(-\frac{x}{2}\right) \cdot d x \\ & =-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \int_0^\pi \cos \left(\frac{-x}{2}\right) d x\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \text { Let }-\frac{x}{2} & =t \Rightarrow d x=-2 d t \\ & =-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}-2 \cos t \cdot d t \\ & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cos t \cdot d t \\ & =\sqrt{2}[\sin t]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}=\sqrt{2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral $\int_{-1}^{3}(|x|+|x-1|) d x$ is
questions.solution:
We have
$$
|x|+|x-1|=\left\{\begin{array}{c}
-x-(x-1)=-2 x+1, \text { if } x \leq 0 \\
x-(x-1)=1, \text { if } 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\
x+x-1=2 x-1, \text { if } x \geq 1
\end{array}\right.
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \int_{-1}^{3}(|x|+|x-1|) d x \\
=& \int_{-1}^{0}(-2 x+1) d x+\int_{0}^{1} 1 d x+\int_{1}^{3}(2 x-1) d x \\
=&\left[-x^{2}+x\right]_{-1}^{0}+[x]_{0}^{1}+\left[x^{2}-x\right]_{1}^{3}=9
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If \(f^{\prime}(x)=f(x)+\int_0^1 f(x) d x\) and given \(f(0)=1\).
Then \(f(x)=\)
questions.solution:
Given \(f^{\prime}(x)=f(x)+\int_0^1 f(x) d x\) ...(1)
Differentiating we get,
\(f^{\prime \prime}(x)=f^{\prime}(x)+0 \Rightarrow \frac{f^{\prime \prime}(x)}{f^{\prime}(x)}=1\)
On integrating \(\int \frac{f^{\prime \prime}(x)}{f^{\prime}(x)} d x=\int d x\)
\(\Rightarrow \ln f^{\prime}(x)=x+c \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=e^{x+c}=k e^x\)
where \(\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{c}}\), a constant
Again integrate
\(\int f^{\prime}(x) d x=\int k e^x+D \Rightarrow f(x)=k e^x+D\) ...(2)
Put \(x=0\) in (1), \(f(0)=\mathrm{ke}^{\circ}+\mathrm{D} \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}+\mathrm{D}=1\)
(given \(f(0)=1)\) ...(3)
Also from (1), \(f^{\prime}(x)=f(x)+\int_0^1 f(x) d x\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{ke}^{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{ke}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{D}+\int_0^1\left(\mathrm{ke}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{D}\right) \mathrm{dx} \\
& \Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{ke}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{Dx}\right]_0^1+\mathrm{D}=0 \Rightarrow[\mathrm{ke}+\mathrm{D}-\mathrm{k}]+\mathrm{D}=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}(\mathrm{e}-1)+2 \mathrm{D}=0
\end{aligned}\)
Solving (3) and (4), we get
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{k}=\frac{2}{3-\mathrm{e}} \text { and } \mathrm{D}=\frac{1-\mathrm{e}}{3-\mathrm{e}} \\
& \therefore \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{2 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}}{3-\mathrm{e}}+\frac{1-\mathrm{e}}{3-\mathrm{e}}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The correct evaluation of $\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left|\sin ^{4} x\right| d x$ is
questions.solution:
$\int_{0}^{\pi}\left|\sin ^{4} x\right| d x=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin ^{4} x d x$
Applying gamma function,
$$
2 \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin ^{4} x d x=2 \frac{\Gamma(5 / 2) \cdot \Gamma(1 / 2)}{2 \cdot \Gamma(6 / 2)}=\frac{3 \pi}{8}
$$
Aliter : Using Walli's formula
$$
=2\left[\frac{3.1}{4.2}\right] \frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{3 \pi}{8}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Value of $\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}} d x$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin \mathrm{x}}}{\sqrt{\sin \mathrm{x}}+\sqrt{\cos \mathrm{x}}} \mathrm{dx}......(i)$
Then, I=
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin (\pi / 2-x)}}{\sqrt{\sin (\pi / 2-x)}+\sqrt{\cos (\pi / 2-x)}} d x \\
\Rightarrow \quad I=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x......(ii)
\end{array}
$$
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
2I
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}+\sqrt{\sin x}} d x+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}} d x \\
\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}} d x=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} 1 . d x=[x]_{0}^{\pi / 2}=\frac{\pi}{2}-0
\end{array}
$$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{\sin \mathrm{x}}}{\sqrt{\sin \mathrm{x}}+\sqrt{\cos \mathrm{x}}} \mathrm{dx}=\frac{\pi}{4}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(a+b-x)=f(x)$, then $\int_{a}^{b} x f(x) d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_{a}^{b} x f(x) d x$
Let $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{z} \Rightarrow-\mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{dz}$
When $x=a, z=b$ and when $x=b, z=a$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=-\int_{b}^{a}(a+b-z) f(z) d z \\
\mathrm{I}=(a+b) \int_{b}^{a} f(x) d x-\int_{a}^{b} x f(x) d x \\
\mathrm{I}=(a+b) \int_{b_{b}}^{a} f(x) d x-\mathrm{I} \\
2 \mathrm{I}=(a+b) \int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x \\
\text { Hence, } \mathrm{I}=\left(\frac{a+b}{2}\right) \int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin 2 x \cdot \log \tan x d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}(\log \tan \mathrm{x}) \cdot \sin 2 \mathrm{xdx} \ldots(\mathrm{i})$
$\mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\mathrm{x}\right) \sin 2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\mathrm{x}\right) \mathrm{dx}$
$\left[\because \int_{0}^{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}=\int_{0}^{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}\right]$
$\begin{array}{r}
\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cot \mathrm{x} \cdot \sin 2 \mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx} ...(ii) \\
{[\because \sin (\pi-2 \mathrm{x})=\sin 2 \mathrm{x}]}
\end{array}$
On adding eqs (i) and (ii), we get
$\begin{array}{c}
2 \mathrm{I} \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \tan \mathrm{x} \cdot \sin 2 \mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx}+\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \log \cot \mathrm{x} \sin 2 \mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx}  \\
=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin 2 x \log (\tan x \cdot \cot x) d x \\
{[\because \log \mathrm{m}+\log \mathrm{n}=\log (\mathrm{m} \cdot \mathrm{n})]}
\end{array}$
$\begin{aligned}
&=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin 2 x \log \operatorname{ld} x \\
\Rightarrow & \mathrm{I}=0[\because \log 1=0] \\
\therefore & \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin 2 x \log (\tan x) d x=0
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\int_{0}^{\sqrt{2}}\left[x^{2}\right] d x$, where $[.]$ is the greatest integer function, is
questions.solution:
$\int_{0}^{\sqrt{2}}\left[x^{2}\right] d x=\int_{0}^{1}\left[x^{2}\right] d x+\int_{1}^{\sqrt{2}}\left[x^{2}\right] d x$
$=\int_{0}^{1} 0 \mathrm{dx}+\int_{1}^{\sqrt{2}} 1 \mathrm{dx}$
$=[\mathrm{x}]_{1}^{\sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2}-1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $l(m, n)=\int_{0}^{1} t^{m}(1+t)^{n} d t$, then the expression for $l(m, n)$ in terms of $l(m+1, n+1)$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & l(\mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{n})=l=\int_{0}^{1} \mathrm{t}^{\mathrm{m}}(1+\mathrm{t})^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{dt} \\ \Rightarrow & l(\mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{n})=\left[(1+\mathrm{t})^{\mathrm{n}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{t}^{\mathrm{m}+1}}{\mathrm{~m}+1}\right]_{0}^{1} \\ &-\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{m}+1} \int_{0}^{1}(1+\mathrm{t})^{\mathrm{n}-1} \cdot \mathrm{t}^{\mathrm{m}+1} \cdot \mathrm{dt} \\=& \frac{2^{\mathrm{n}}}{\mathrm{m}+1}-\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{m}+1} \cdot l(\mathrm{~m}+1, \mathrm{n}-1) \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int\left(1+x-x^{-1}\right) e^{x+x^{-1}} d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\int\left(1+x-x^{-1}\right) e^{x+x^{-1}} d x$
$=\int\left[x \cdot e^{x+x^{-1}}\left(1-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)+e^{x+x^{-1}}\right] d x$
$\left[\because \int x f^{\prime}(x)+f(x) d x=x f(x)+C\right]$
$\therefore \int\left(1+x-x^{-1}\right) e^{x+x^{-1}} d x=x e^{x+x^{-1}}+C$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=x-[x]$, for every real number $x$, where $[x]$, is the integral part of $x$. Then, $\int_{-1}^{1} f(x) d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}-[\mathrm{x}],-1 \leq \mathrm{x} < 0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}+1$
When $0 \leq \mathrm{x} < 1$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}$
$\int_{-1}^{1} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}=\int_{-1}^{0} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\int_{0}^{1} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}$
$=\int_{-1}^{0}(\mathrm{x}+1) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\int_{0}^{1} \mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx}$
$=\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2}+\mathrm{x}\right]_{-1}^{0}+\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{1}$
$=0-\left[\frac{(-1)^{2}}{2}-1\right]+\frac{1}{2}=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The value of the integral
$\int_{-1 / 2}^{1 / 2}\left[\left(\frac{x+1}{x-1}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)^{2}-2\right]^{1 / 2} d x$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int \frac{d x}{\sin x-\cos x+\sqrt{2}}$ equals to
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
 \text { Let } I=\int \frac{d x}{\sin x-\cos x+\sqrt{2}} \\
=\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{2}\left(\sin x \sin \frac{\pi}{4}-\cos x \cos \frac{\pi}{4}+1\right)} \\
=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \int \frac{d x}{1-\cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)} \\
=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \int \frac{d x}{2 \sin ^{2}\left(\frac{x}{2}+\frac{\pi}{8}\right)} \\
=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}} \int \operatorname{cosec}^{2}\left(\frac{x}{2}+\frac{\pi}{8}\right) d x \\
=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{-\cot \left(\frac{x}{2}+\frac{\pi}{8}\right)}{\frac{1}{2}}+C \\
\left.=\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{\pi}{8}\right)+C
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of integral $\int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x}} d x$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{\frac{1-\mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}}} \mathrm{dx}$
$=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{1-x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x$
$=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x-\int_{0}^{1} \frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}} d x$
$=\left[\sin ^{-1} x\right]_{0}^{1}-\int_{0}^{1} \frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x$
Put $t^{2}=1-x^{2} \Rightarrow 2 t d t=-2 x d x$
$\Rightarrow t \mathrm{dt}=-x \mathrm{~d} x$
$\therefore \mathrm{I}=\left(\sin ^{-1} 1-\sin ^{-1} 0\right)+\int_{1}^{0} \frac{\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}$
$=\frac{\pi}{2}+[\mathrm{t}]_{1}^{0}=\frac{\pi}{2}-1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Value of $\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{\cot x}} d x$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{I} &=\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{\cot x}} \mathrm{dx} \\ &=\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}+\sqrt{\cos x}} \mathrm{dx} \end{aligned} ......(i)$
Then, $\mathrm{I}=\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{\sqrt{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\mathrm{x}\right)}}{\sqrt{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\mathrm{x}\right)}+\sqrt{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\mathrm{x}\right)}} \mathrm{dx}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{\sqrt{\cos \mathrm{x}}}{\sqrt{\cos \mathrm{x}}+\sqrt{\sin \mathrm{x}}} \mathrm{dx} ......(ii)$
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{\sqrt{\sin \mathrm{x}}+\sqrt{\cos \mathrm{x}}}{\sqrt{\cos \mathrm{x}}+\sqrt{\sin \mathrm{x}}} \mathrm{dx} \\
\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} 1 \cdot \mathrm{dx}=[\mathrm{x}]_{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3}=\frac{\pi}{3}-\frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{\pi}{6} \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\pi / 12
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral \(\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sqrt{\cos \theta} \sin ^3 \theta \mathrm{d} \theta\) is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& I=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sqrt{\cos \theta} \sin ^3 \theta d \theta \\
& =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sqrt{\cos \theta} \cdot \sin ^2 \theta \cdot \sin \theta d \theta
\end{aligned}\)
Put \(\cos \theta=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta=-\mathrm{dt}\) [Note that power of \(\sin \theta\) is odd] also, when
\(\theta=0, \mathrm{t}=1\), When \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}, \mathrm{t}=0\)
\(\therefore I=\int_1^0 \sqrt{t}\left(1-t^2\right) \cdot(-d t)=\int_0^1\left(t^{\frac{1}{2}}-t^{\frac{5}{2}}\right) d t\)
\(=\left[\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{t}^{\frac{3}{2}}-\frac{2}{7} \mathrm{t}^{\frac{7}{2}}\right]_0^1=\frac{2}{3}-\frac{2}{7}=\frac{8}{21}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\int_{-1}^{1}(\mathrm{x}-[\mathrm{x}]) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}$ (where [.] denotes greatest integer function) is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{I} &=\int_{-1}^{1}(\mathrm{x}-[\mathrm{x}]) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}=\int_{-1}^{1} \mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx}-\int_{-1}^{1}[\mathrm{x}] \mathrm{dx} \\ &=\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2}\right]_{-1}^{1}-\left[\int_{-1}^{0}[\mathrm{x}] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\int_{0}^{1}[\mathrm{x}] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}\right] \\ &=\frac{1}{2}[1-1]-\left[\int_{-1}^{0}(-1) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}+\int_{0}^{1} 0 . \mathrm{dx}\right] \\ &=\left[\begin{array}{c}\text { If }-1 \leq \mathrm{x} < 0,[\mathrm{x}]=-1] \\ \text { If } 0 \leq \mathrm{x} < 1,[\mathrm{x}]=0]\end{array}\right.\\ &=[-\mathrm{x}]_{-1}^{0}-0=0-[-0-(-1)]=1 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(f(x)\) is a quadratic in \(x\), then \(\int_0^1 f(x) d x\) is equal to
questions.solution:
If \(f(x)=a x^2+b x+c\),
\(\begin{aligned}
& \int_0^1 f(x) d x=\frac{1}{6}(2 a+3 b+6 c) \\
& f(0)=c, f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{a}{4}+\frac{b}{2}+c, f(1)=a+b+c \\
& \Rightarrow f(0)+4 f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+f(1)=2 a+3 b+6 c
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $I=\int_{0}^{1} x\left|x-\frac{1}{2}\right| d x$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{1} \mathrm{x}\left|\mathrm{x}-\frac{1}{2}\right| \mathrm{dx}$
$=-\int_{0}^{1 / 2} x\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right) d x+\int_{1 / 2}^{1} x\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right) d x$
$=\int_{0}^{1 / 2}\left(\frac{x}{2}-x^{2}\right) d x+\int_{1 / 2}^{1} x\left(x^{2}-\frac{x}{2}\right) d x$
$=\left[\frac{x^{2}}{4}-\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{1 / 2}+\left[\frac{x^{3}}{3}-\frac{x^{2}}{4}\right]_{1 / 2}^{1}$
$=\left(\frac{1}{16}-\frac{1}{24}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{24}+\frac{1}{16}\right)$
$=\left(\frac{6-4}{96}\right)+\left(\frac{32-24-4+6}{96}\right)$
$=\frac{12}{96}=\frac{1}{8}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Evaluate $\int_{\pi / 4}^{3 \pi / 4} \frac{1}{1+\cos x} d x$
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\int_{\pi / 4}^{3 \pi / 4} \frac{1}{1+\cos x} d x=\int_{\pi / 4}^{3 \pi / 4} \frac{1-\cos x}{\sin ^{2} x} d x \\
=\int_{\pi / 4}^{3 \pi / 4}\left(\operatorname{cosec}^{2} x-\cot x \operatorname{cosec} x\right) d x \\
=[-\cot x+\operatorname{cosec} x]_{\pi / 4}^{3 \pi / 4} \\
=(1+\sqrt{2})-(-1+\sqrt{2})=2
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
For what values of $\mathrm{m}$ can the expression $2 x^{2}+m x y+3 y^{2}-5 y-2$
be expressed as the product of two linear factors?
questions.solution:
The given expression is
$$
2 x^{2}+m x y+3 y^{2}-5 y-2
$$
Comparing the given expression with
$$
a x^{2}+2 h x y+b y^{2}+2 g x+2 f y+c
$$
we get
$$
a=2, h=\frac{m}{2}, b=3, c=-2, g=0, f=-\frac{5}{2}
$$
The given expression is resolvable into linear factors, if
$$
\begin{array}{l}
a b c+2 f g h-a f^{2}-b g^{2}-c h^{2}=0 \\
(2)(3)(-2)+2(0)-2\left(\frac{25}{4}\right)-0-(-2) \frac{m^{2}}{4}=0 \\
\Rightarrow-12-\frac{25}{2}+\frac{m^{2}}{2}=0 \\
\Rightarrow \frac{m^{2}}{2}=\frac{49}{2} \Rightarrow m^{2}=49 \Rightarrow m=\pm 7
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
$\int_{0}^{1}\left[\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{g}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x})-\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})\right] \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}$ is equal to :
$[$ Given $\mathrm{f}(0)=\mathrm{g}(0)=0]$
questions.solution:
Integrating by parts.
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\int f(x) g^{\prime \prime}(x) d x-\int f^{\prime \prime}(x) g(x) d x \\
=f(x) g^{\prime}(x)-\int f^{\prime}(x) g^{\prime}(x) d x \\
-f^{\prime}(x) g(x)+\int f^{\prime}(x) g^{\prime}(x) d x \\
=f(x) g^{\prime}(x)-f^{\prime}(x) g(x) \\
\text { Hence, } \int_{0}^{1} f(x) g^{\prime \prime}(x) d x-\int_{0}^{1} f^{\prime \prime}(x) g(x) d x \\
=f(1) g^{\prime}(1)-f^{\prime}(1) g(1)-f(0) g^{\prime}(0)+f^{\prime}(0) g(0) \\
=f(1) g^{\prime}(1)-f^{\prime}(1) g(1)
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $f(x), \mathrm{g}(x)$ and $\mathrm{h}(x)$ are three polynomials of degree 2 and
$$
\Delta(\mathrm{x})=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{h}(\mathrm{x}) \\
\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{g}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{h}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \\
\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{g}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x}) & \mathrm{h}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{x})
\end{array}\right|
$$
then $\Delta(x)$ is a polynomial of degree
questions.solution:
Since $f(x), g(x)$ and $h(x)$ are the polynomials of degree 2,
therefore $f^{\prime \prime}(x)=g^{\prime \prime \prime}(x)=h^{\prime \prime \prime}(x)=0$
$$
\begin{array}{c}
\text { Now, } \Delta(x)=\left|\begin{array}{lll}
f(x) & g(x) & h(x) \\
f^{\prime}(x) & g^{\prime}(x) & h^{\prime}(x) \\
f^{\prime \prime}(x) & g^{\prime \prime}(x) & h^{\prime \prime}(x)
\end{array}\right| \\
\Rightarrow \Delta^{\prime}(x)=\left|\begin{array}{lll}
f^{\prime}(x) & g^{\prime}(x) & h^{\prime}(x) \\
f^{\prime}(x) & g^{\prime}(x) & h^{\prime}(x) \\
f^{\prime \prime}(x) & g^{\prime \prime}(x) & h^{\prime \prime}(x)
\end{array}\right| \\
+\left|\begin{array}{lll}
f^{\prime \prime}(x) & g^{\prime \prime}(x) & h^{\prime \prime}(x) \\
f^{\prime \prime}(x) & g^{\prime \prime}(x) & h^{\prime \prime}(x)
\end{array}\right| \\
+\left|\begin{array}{lll}
f(x) & g(x) & h(x) \\
f^{\prime}(x) & g^{\prime}(x) & h^{\prime}(x) \\
f^{\prime \prime \prime}(x) & g^{\prime \prime}(x) & h^{\prime \prime}(x)
\end{array}\right| \\
\Rightarrow \Delta^{\prime}(x)=0+0+0=0 \\
\text { constant. }
\end{array}
$$
Thus, $\Delta(x)$ is the polynom ial of degree zero.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $I_{n}=\int \sin ^{n} x d x$, then $n I_{n}-(n-1) I_{n-2}$ equals
questions.solution:
We know that, if
$$
\begin{array}{l}
I_{n}=\int \sin ^{n} x d x, \text { then } \\
I_{n}=-\frac{\sin ^{n-1} x \cos x}{n}+\frac{n-1}{n} I_{n-2}
\end{array}
$$
where $n$ is a positive integer.
$$
\Rightarrow n I_{n}-(n-1) I_{n-2}=-\sin ^{n-1} x \cos x
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $g(x)$ is a polynomial satisfying $g(x) g(y)=g(x)+g(y)+g(x y)-2$ for all real $x$ and $y$ and $g(2)=5$ then $\operatorname{Lt}_{x \rightarrow 3} g(x)$ is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot \mathrm{g}(\mathrm{y})=\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{y})+\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x} \mathrm{y})-2 \ldots(1)$ 
Put $\mathrm{x}=1, \mathrm{y}=2$, then 
$\mathrm{g}(1) \cdot \mathrm{g}(2)=\mathrm{g}(1)+\mathrm{g}(2)+\mathrm{g}(2)-2$ 
$5 \mathrm{~g}(1)=\mathrm{g}(1)+5+5-2$ 
$4 \mathrm{~g}(1)=8 \quad \therefore \mathrm{g}(1)=2$
Put $y=\frac{1}{x}$ in equation (1), we get
$\begin{array}{l}
g(x) \cdot g\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=g(x)+g\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+g(1)-2 \\
g(x) \cdot g\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)=g(x)+g\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+2-2 \\
\quad[\because g(1)=2]
\end{array}$
This is valid only for the polynomial
$\therefore \quad g(x)=1 \pm x^{n}$ ...(2)
Now $g(2)=5$ (Given)
$\therefore 1 \pm 2^{\mathrm{n}}=5 \quad$ [Using equation $\left.(2)\right]$ 
$\pm 2^{\mathrm{n}}=4, \Rightarrow 2^{\mathrm{n}}=4,-4$
Since the value of $2^{\mathrm{n}}$ cannot be-Ve.
So, $2^{\mathrm{n}}=4, \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=2$
Now, put $\mathrm{n}=2$ in equation $(2)$, we get $g(x)=1 \pm x^{2}$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore \operatorname{Lt}_{x \rightarrow 3} g(x) &=\operatorname{Lt}_{x \rightarrow 3}\left(1 \pm x^{2}\right)=1 \pm(3)^{2} \\
&=1 \pm 9=10,-8
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The directrix of the parabola $y^{2}+4 x+3=0$ is
questions.solution:
The equation of the parabola is
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{y}^{2}+4 \mathrm{x}+3=0 \\
\text { or } \quad \mathrm{y}^{2}=-4\left(\mathrm{x}+\frac{3}{4}\right) ...(1)
\end{array}$
The directrix of the parabola 
$\mathrm{Y}^{2}=-4 \mathrm{aX}$ ...(2)
is $X=a$.
On comparing the equation (1) and (2), we get 
$\quad 4 a=4 \quad$ and $X=x+\frac{3}{4}$ 
or $\quad \mathrm{a}=1 \quad$ and $\mathrm{X}=\mathrm{x}+\frac{3}{4}$ 
Hence the directrix of the parabola (1) is
$x+\frac{3}{4}=1$ or $x-\frac{1}{4}=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_{0}^{2 x}(\sin x+|\sin x|) d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\int_{0}^{2 \pi}(\sin x+|\sin x|) d x$
$=\int_{0}^{\pi}(\sin x+\sin x) d x+$
$\quad \int_{\pi}^{2 \pi}(\sin x-\sin x) d x$
$=2 \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin x d x+0=-2 \int_{0}^{\pi} \cos x d x$
$=-2(\cos \pi-\cos 0)=-2(-1-1)=4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Find the number of ways in which five large books, four medium-size books, and three small books can be placed on a shelf so that all books of the same size are together.
questions.solution:
Let us make one packet for each of the books on the same size. Now, 3 packets can be arranged in $\mathrm{P}(3,3)=3 !$ ways 5 large books can be arranged in $5 !$ ways 4 medium size books can be arranged in $4 !$ ways
3 small books can be arranged in $3 !$ ways
$\therefore$ Required number of ways $=3 ! \times 5 ! \times 4 ! \times 3 !$ ways
