questions.q: The statement $\sim \mathrm{p} \vee \mathrm{q}$ is equivalent to

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider the set $\mathrm{Q}$ of rational numbers. Let $*$ be the operation on $Q$ defined by $a * b=a+b-a b$. The identity element under $*$ is
questions.solution:
An identity relation is one in which every element of a set is related to itself only. $\mathbf{a} * \mathbf{b}=\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a b}$
As in identity relation ' $a$ ' is related to 'a', so the correct option will be the one which gives the value of the relation $=$ 'a'. So, equating $a+b-a b=a$, we get $b(1-a)=0$ Now putting the values of $a$, we find $b$ and the option in which $\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{b}$, will be the answer. For $a=0, b=0$, so the correct option.
For $a=1, b(1-1)=0 \Rightarrow b$ can have multiple values.
For $\mathrm{a}=2, \mathrm{~b}(1-2)=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{b}=0$ but $\mathrm{a}=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{B}$ is a non-singular matrix and $\mathrm{A}$ is a square matrix, then $\operatorname{det}\left(\mathrm{B}^{-1} \mathrm{AB}\right)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
 \operatorname{det}\left(B^{-1} A B\right)=\operatorname{det}\left(B^{-1}\right) \operatorname{det} A \operatorname{det} B \\
=\operatorname{det}\left(B^{-1}\right) \cdot \operatorname{det} B \cdot \operatorname{det} A \\
=\operatorname{det}\left(B^{-1} B\right) \operatorname{det} A=\operatorname{det} I \cdot \operatorname{det} A \\
=1 \cdot \operatorname{det} A=\operatorname{det} A
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are two fixed points in a plane then locus of another point $C$ on the same plane such that $\mathrm{CA}+\mathrm{CB}=$ constant,$(>\mathrm{AB})$ is
questions.solution:
Let two fixed points be $\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{ae}, 0)$ and $\mathrm{B}(-\mathrm{ae},$, $0)$. Let $\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y})$ be a moving point such that $\mathrm{AC}+\mathrm{CB}=$ constant $=2 \mathrm{a}$ (say)
i.e., $\sqrt{(x-a e)^{2}+(y-0)^{2}}$ $+\sqrt{(x+a e)^{2}+(y-0)^{2}}=2 a$
$\text { Or } \sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+a^{2} e^{2}-2 a e x}$ $+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+a^{2} e^{2}+2 a e x}=2 a$ ...(1)
Or $l+\mathrm{m}=2 \mathrm{a}$ ...(2)
Where, $l^{2}=\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{e}^{2}-2 \mathrm{aex}$ ...(3)
and $m^{2}=x^{2}+y^{2}+a^{2} e^{2}+2 a e x$ ...(4)
From, (3) and (4)
$\begin{array}{l}
\quad \mathrm{m}^{2}-l^{2}=4 \mathrm{aex} \\
\text { or } \quad(\mathrm{m}-l)(l+\mathrm{m})=4 \mathrm{aex} \\
2 \mathrm{a}(\mathrm{m}-l)=4 \mathrm{aex} \quad[\text { From }(2)] \\
\mathrm{m}-l=2 \mathrm{ex} ...(5)
\end{array}$
Adding $(2)$ and $(5)$, we get
$\mathrm{m}=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{ex}$ ...(6)
From (4) and (6),
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{e}^{2} \mathrm{x}^{2}+2 \mathrm{aex}=\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{e}^{2}+2 \mathrm{aex} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{x}^{2}\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{2}\right)+\mathrm{y}^{2}=\mathrm{a}^{2}\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{2}\right)
\end{array}$
Dividing both sides by $\mathrm{a}^{2}\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{2}\right)$, we get
$\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{a^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right)}=1$
Or $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$, where $b^{2}=a^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right)$
This is the equation of ellipse.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the determinant $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 4 & 3 \\ 0 & 12 & 9 \\ 1 & 2 & 2\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta=&\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & 4 & 3 \\
0 & 12 & 9 \\
1 & 2 & 2
\end{array}\right| \\
=& 1(12 \times 2-2 \times 9)-4(0 \times 2-1 \times 9) \\
&+3(0 \times 2-1 \times 12) \\
=& 1(24-18)-4(0-9)+3(0-12) \\
=& 6+36-36=6
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the system of equations \(x+\lambda y+2=0\), \(\lambda x+y-2=0, \lambda x+\lambda y+3=0\) is consistent, then
questions.solution:
The system of equations will be consistent if
\(\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & \lambda & 2 \\
\lambda & 1 & -2 \\
\lambda & \lambda & 3
\end{array}\right|=0\)
To evaluate \(\Delta\) we use \(R_1 \rightarrow R_1+R_2\) followed by \(\mathrm{C}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_2-\mathrm{C}_1\) to obtain
\(\begin{aligned}
\Delta & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\lambda+1 & \lambda+1 & 0 \\
\lambda & 1 & -2 \\
\lambda & \lambda & 3
\end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\lambda+1 & 0 & 0 \\
\lambda & 1-\lambda & -2 \\
\lambda & 0 & 3
\end{array}\right| \\
& =3(\lambda+1)(1-\lambda)=3\left(1-\lambda^2\right)
\end{aligned}\)
For the system to be consistent, we must have \(1-\lambda^2=0\) or \(\lambda= \pm 1\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equations $2 x+3 y+4=0 ; 3 x+4 y+6=0$ and $4 x+5 y+8=0$ are
questions.solution:
Consider first two equations :
$2 x+3 y=-4$ and $3 x+4 y=-6$
We have $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ll}2 & 3 \\ 3 & 4\end{array}\right|=-1 \neq 0$
$$
\Delta_{\mathrm{x}}=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
-4 & 3 \\
-6 & 4
\end{array}\right|=2 \quad \text { and } \quad \Delta_{\mathrm{y}}=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
2 & -4 \\
3 & -6
\end{array}\right|=0
$$

$$
\therefore \mathrm{x}=-2 \text { and } \mathrm{y}=0
$$
Now this solution satisfies all the equations, so the equations are consistent with unique solution.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are in $\mathrm{A} . \mathrm{P} .$ then the value of $\left|\begin{array}{lll}x+1 & x+2 & x+a \\ x+2 & x+3 & x+b \\ x+3 & x+4 & x+c\end{array}\right|$ is:
questions.solution:
Given $a, b, c$ are in A.P.
$\therefore \quad 2 \mathrm{~b}=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c} ......(i)$
Now, $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x+1 & x+2 & x+a \\ x+2 & x+3 & x+b \\ x+3 & x+4 & x+c\end{array}\right|$
[Applying $\left.R_{2} \rightarrow 2 R_{2}\right]$
$$
\begin{array}{c}
=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\mathrm{x}+1 & \mathrm{x}+2 & \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{a} \\
2 \mathrm{x}+4 & 2 \mathrm{x}+6 & 2 \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{~b} \\
\mathrm{x}+3 & \mathrm{x}+4 & \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{c}
\end{array}\right| \\
=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\mathrm{x}+1 & \mathrm{x}+2 & \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{a} \\
2 \mathrm{x}+4 & 2 \mathrm{x}+6 & 2 \mathrm{x}+(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}) \\
\mathrm{x}+3 & \mathrm{x}+4 & \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{c}
\end{array}\right|
\end{array}
$$
[using equation (i)]
$$
=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
x+1 & x+2 & x+a \\
0 & 0 & 0 \\
x+3 & x+4 & x+c
\end{array}\right|=\frac{1}{2} \cdot 0=0
$$
$\left[\right.$ Applying $\left.\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}-\left(\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{3}\right)\right]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(\left|\begin{array}{ccc}3^2+k & 4^2 & 3^2+3+k \\ 4^2+k & 5^2 & 4^2+4+k \\ 5^2+k & 6^2 & 5^2+5+k\end{array}\right|=0\), then the value of \(k\) is
questions.solution:
Breaking the given determinant into two determinants, we get
\(\begin{aligned}
& \left|\begin{array}{lll}
3^2+\mathrm{k} & 4^2 & 3^2+\mathrm{k} \\
4^2+\mathrm{k} & 5^2 & 4^2+\mathrm{k} \\
5^2+\mathrm{k} & 6^2 & 5^2+\mathrm{k}
\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{lll}
3^2+\mathrm{k} & 4^2 & 3 \\
4^2+\mathrm{k} & 5^2 & 4 \\
5^2+\mathrm{k} & 6^2 & 5
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
& \Rightarrow 0+\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
9+\mathrm{k} & 16 & 3 \\
7 & 9 & 1 \\
9 & 11 & 1
\end{array}\right|=0
\end{aligned}\)
[Applying \(R_3-R_2\) and \(R_2-R_1\) in second det.]
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
9+\mathrm{k} & 16 & 3 \\
7 & 9 & 1 \\
2 & 2 & 0
\end{array}\right|=0\left[\text { Applying } \mathrm{R}_3-\mathrm{R}_2\right] \\
& \left.\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
9+\mathrm{k} & 7-\mathrm{k} & 3 \\
7 & 2 & 1 \\
2 & 0 & 0
\end{array}\right|=0 \text { [Applying } \mathrm{C}_2-\mathrm{C}_1\right] \\
& \Rightarrow 2(7-\mathrm{k}-6)=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=1
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\cos \mathrm{x} & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 \cos \mathrm{x} & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \cos \mathrm{x}\end{array}\right|$, then $\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} f(x) d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$f(x)=4 \cos ^{3} x-\cos x-2 \cos x=\cos 3 x$
[Expansion of determinant]
$$
\left.\therefore \quad \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} f(x) d x=\frac{\sin 3 x}{3}\right]_{0}^{\pi / 2}=-\frac{1}{3}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $x$ obtained from the equation $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x+\alpha & \beta & \gamma \\ \gamma & x+\beta & \alpha \\ \alpha & \beta & x+\gamma\end{array}\right|=0$ will be
questions.solution:
Given $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x+\alpha & \beta & \gamma \\ \gamma & x+\beta & \alpha \\ \alpha & \beta & x+\gamma\end{array}\right|=0$
Operate $\mathrm{C}_{1} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2}+\mathrm{C}_{3}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
x+\alpha+\beta+\gamma & \beta & \gamma \\
x+\alpha+\beta+\gamma & x+\beta & \alpha \\
x+\alpha+\beta+\gamma & \beta & x+\gamma
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
=(x+\alpha+\beta+\gamma)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & \beta & \gamma \\
1 & x+\beta & \alpha \\
1 & \beta & x+\gamma
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
\Rightarrow x+\alpha+\beta+\gamma=0 \Rightarrow x=-(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)
\end{array}$
Again if
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & \beta & \gamma \\
1 & x+\beta & \alpha \\
1 & \beta & \gamma
\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & \beta & \gamma \\
0 & x & \alpha-\gamma \\
0 & 0 & x
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
\Rightarrow x^{2}=0 \Rightarrow x=0
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$ Solutions of the equation are $x=0$, 
$-(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\Delta(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \cos x & 1-\cos x \\ 1+\sin x & \cos x & 1+\sin x-\cos x \\ \sin x & \sin x & 1\end{array}\right|$,
then $\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \Delta(x) d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\Delta(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \cos x & 1-\cos x \\ 1+\sin x & \cos x & 1+\sin x-\cos x \\ \sin x & \sin x & 1\end{array}\right|$
Applying $\mathrm{C}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{3}+\mathrm{C}_{2}-\mathrm{C}_{1}$
$\Delta(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & \cos x & 0 \\
1+\sin x & \cos x & 0 \\
\sin x & \sin x & 1
\end{array}\right|$
$=\cos x-\cos x(1+\sin x)$
$\quad\left[\because\right.$ expanding along $\left.C_{3}\right]=-\cos x \cdot \sin x$
$\quad \therefore \int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \Delta(x) d x=-\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \sin 2 x$
$=-\frac{1}{2}\left[-\frac{\cos 2 x}{2}\right]_{0}^{\pi / 4}$
$=+\frac{1}{2 \times 2}\left[\cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right]$
$=\frac{1}{4}(0-1)=-\frac{1}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\Delta_{r}=\left|\begin{array}{lll}2 r-1 & { }^{m} C_{r} & 1 \\ m^{2}-1 & 2^{m} & m+1 \\ \sin ^{2}\left(\mathrm{~m}^{2}\right) & \sin ^{2}(\mathrm{~m}) & \sin ^{2}(\mathrm{~m}+1)\end{array}\right|$, then the value of $\sum_{r=0}^{m} \Delta_{r}, i s$
questions.solution:
$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 \mathrm{r}-1 & \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{r}} & 1 \\ \mathrm{~m}^{2}-1 & 2^{\mathrm{m}} & \mathrm{m}+1 \\ \sin ^{2}\left(\mathrm{~m}^{2}\right) & \sin ^{2}(\mathrm{~m}) & \sin ^{2}(\mathrm{~m}+1)\end{array}\right|$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{m}} \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} &=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{m}}(2 \mathrm{r}-1) & \sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} & \sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{m}} 1 \\ \mathrm{~m}^{2}-1 & 2^{\mathrm{m}} & \mathrm{m}+1 \\ \sin ^{2}\left(\mathrm{~m}^{2}\right) & \sin ^{2}(\mathrm{~m}) & \sin ^{2}(\mathrm{~m}+1)\end{array}\right| \\ &=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\mathrm{m}^{2}-1 & 2^{\mathrm{m}} & \mathrm{m}+1 \\ \mathrm{~m}^{2}-1 & 2^{\mathrm{m}} & \mathrm{m}+1 \\ \sin ^{2}\left(\mathrm{~m}^{2}\right) & \sin ^{2}(\mathrm{~m}) & \sin ^{2}(\mathrm{~m}+1)\end{array}\right| \\ &=0(\because \mathrm{tworows} \text { are identical }) \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $a, b$ and $c$ are in $A P$, then determinant $\left|\begin{array}{lll}x+2 & x+3 & x+2 a \\ x+3 & x+4 & x+2 b \\ x+4 & x+5 & x+2 c\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll}x+2 & x+3 & x+2 a \\ x+3 & x+4 & x+2 b \\ x+4 & x+5 & x+2 c\end{array}\right|$
$=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
x+2 & x+3 & x+2 a \\
0 & 0 & 2(2 b-a-c) \\
x+4 & x+5 & x+2 c
\end{array}\right|$
(using $\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{R}_{2}-\mathrm{R}_{1}-\mathrm{R}_{3}$ )
But $\mathrm{a}$, b and c are in APusing $2 \mathrm{~b}=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}$, we get
$\Delta=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\mathrm{x}+2 & \mathrm{x}+3 & \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{a} \\
0 & 0 & 0 \\
\mathrm{x}+4 & \mathrm{x}+5 & \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{c}
\end{array}\right|=0$
Since, all elements of $\mathrm{R}_{2}$ are zero.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The value of the determinant
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & \cos (\alpha-\beta) & \cos \alpha \\
\cos (\alpha-\beta) & 1 & \cos \beta \\
\cos \alpha & \cos \beta & 1
\end{array}\right| \text { is }$
questions.solution:
On solving the determinant, we have
$\begin{aligned}
& 1\left(1-\cos ^{2} \beta\right)-\cos (\alpha-\beta)[\cos (\alpha-\beta)\\
-&\cos \alpha \cdot \cos \beta]+\cos \alpha[\cos \beta \cdot \cos (\alpha-\beta)\\
=& 1-\cos ^{2} \beta-\cos ^{2} \alpha-\cos ^{2}(\alpha-\beta) \\
=&+2 \cos \alpha \cdot \cos \beta \cdot \cos (\alpha-\beta) \\
=& 1-\cos ^{2} \beta-\cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos (\alpha-\beta) \\
=& 1-\cos ^{2} \beta-\cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos (\alpha-\beta) \cos (\alpha+\beta) \\
&[\cos (\alpha+\beta)+\cos (\alpha-\beta)-\cos (\alpha-\beta)] \\
=& 1-\cos ^{2} \beta-\cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \alpha \cdot \cos ^{2} \beta \\
=& 1-\cos ^{2} \beta-\cos ^{2} \alpha\left(1-\cos ^{2} \beta\right) \\
=& 1-\cos ^{2} \beta \cdot \sin ^{2} \alpha \cdot \sin ^{2} \beta \\
=&\left(1-\cos ^{2} \beta\right)-\sin ^{2} \alpha \cdot \sin ^{2} \beta-\sin ^{2} \alpha \cdot \sin ^{2} \beta \\
=&\left.\sin ^{2} \beta-\sin ^{2} \beta 1\right)=0
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x, y$ and $z$ are all distinct and $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x & x^{2} & 1+x^{3} \\ y & y^{2} & 1+y^{3} \\ z & z^{2} & 1+z^{3}\end{array}\right|=0$, then the value of $x y z$ is
questions.solution:
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\mathrm{x} & \mathrm{x}^{2} & 1+\mathrm{x}^{3} \\ \mathrm{y} & \mathrm{y}^{2} & 1+\mathrm{y}^{3} \\ \mathrm{z} & \mathrm{z}^{2} & 1+\mathrm{z}^{3}\end{array}\right|=0$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{lll}
\mathrm{x} & \mathrm{x}^{2} & 1 \\
\mathrm{y} & \mathrm{y}^{2} & 1 \\
\mathrm{z} & \mathrm{z}^{2} & 1
\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\mathrm{x} & \mathrm{x}^{2} & \mathrm{x}^{3} \\
\mathrm{y} & \mathrm{y}^{2} & \mathrm{y}^{3} \\
\mathrm{z} & \mathrm{z}^{2} & \mathrm{z}^{3}
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{lll}
\mathrm{x} & \mathrm{x}^{2} & 1 \\
\mathrm{y} & \mathrm{y}^{2} & 1 \\
\mathrm{z} & \mathrm{z}^{2} & 1
\end{array}\right|+\mathrm{xyz}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & \mathrm{x} & \mathrm{x}^{2} \\
1 & \mathrm{y} & \mathrm{y}^{2} \\
1 & \mathrm{z} & \mathrm{z}^{2}
\end{array}\right|=0
\end{array}
$$
$$
\Rightarrow(1+x y z)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
x & x^{2} & 1 \\
y & y^{2} & 1 \\
z & z^{2} & 1
\end{array}\right|=0
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow(1+x y z)\left[x\left(y^{2}-z^{2}\right)-y\left(x^{2}-z^{2}\right)\right. \\
\left.\quad+z\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)\right]=0 \\
\Rightarrow(1+x y z)(x-y)(y-z)(z-x)=0 \\
\Rightarrow 1+x y z=0 \Rightarrow x y z=-1
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The system of equations $2 x+y-5=0$, $x-2 y+1=9,2 x-14 y-a=0$, is consistent. Then, $a$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given system of equations are
$$
\begin{array}{l}
2 x+y-5=0 ...(i)\\
x-2 y+1=0...(ii)
\end{array}
$$
and $2 x-14 y-a=0...(ii)$
This system is consistent
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
2 & 1 & -5 \\
1 & -2 & 1 \\
2 & -14 & -\mathrm{a}
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
\Rightarrow 2(2 \mathrm{a}+14)-1(-\mathrm{a}-2)-5(-14+4)=0 \\
\Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{a}+28+\mathrm{a}+2+50=0 \\
\Rightarrow 5 \mathrm{a}=-80 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=-16
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The value of the determinant
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha & 1 \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha & 1 \\ \cos (\alpha+\beta) & -\sin (\alpha+\beta) & 1\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha & 1 \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha & 1 \\ \cos (\alpha+\beta) & -\sin (\alpha+\beta) & 1\end{array}\right|$
$\left[\right.$ Applying $\left.\mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}-\mathrm{R}_{1}(\cos \beta)+\mathrm{R}_{2}(\sin \beta)\right]$ $=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha & 1 \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1+\sin \beta-\cos \beta\end{array}\right|$ $=(1+\sin \beta-\cos \beta)\left(\cos ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \alpha\right)$ $=1+\sin \beta-\cos \beta$, which is independent of $\alpha .$

Determinant is a number associated to a square matrix

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the common tangents to the two hyperbolas $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ and $\frac{y^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{x^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$, are
questions.solution:
We have the two hyperbolas as
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1...(i) \\
\text { and } \frac{y^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{x^{2}}{b^{2}}=1...(ii)
\end{array}
$$
Any tangent to the hyperbola eq(i) $y=m x+c$
where $c=\pm \sqrt{a^{2} m^{2}-b^{2}} \ldots$ (iii)
But this tangent touches the parabola eq. (ii) also
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \frac{(m x+c)^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{x^{2}}{b^{2}}=1 \\
\Rightarrow b^{2}\left(m^{2} x^{2}+c^{2}+2 m c x\right)-a^{2} x^{2}=a^{2} b^{2} \\
\Rightarrow\left(b^{2} m^{2}-a^{2}\right) x^{2}+2 m c b^{2} x+b^{2}\left(c^{2}-a^{2}\right)=0
\end{array}
$$
For the tangency, it should have equal roots
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\left(2 m c b^{2}\right)^{2}=4\left(b^{2} m^{2}-a^{2}\right) \cdot b^{2}\left(c^{2}-a^{2}\right) \\
\Rightarrow & 4 m^{2} c^{2} b^{4}=4 b^{2}\left(b^{2} m^{2} c^{2}-b^{2} m^{2} a^{2}-a^{2} c^{2}+a^{4}\right) \\
\Rightarrow & c^{2}=a^{2}-b^{2} m^{2} \\
\Rightarrow & a^{2} m^{2}-b^{2}=a^{2}-b^{2} m^{2}[\text { using Eq. (iii) }] \\
\Rightarrow &\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right) m^{2}=a^{2}+b^{2} \\
\Rightarrow & m^{2}=1 \Rightarrow m=\pm 1
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence, the equation of common tangent are
$$
y=\pm x \pm \sqrt{a^{2}-b^{2}}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}, \mathrm{z}$ are different from zero and
$\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & b-y & c-z \\ a-x & b & c-z \\ a-x & b-y & c\end{array}\right|=0$ then the value of
the expression $\frac{a}{x}+\frac{b}{y}+\frac{c}{z}$ is
questions.solution:
$\left[\begin{array}{lll}a & b-y & c-z \\ a-x & b & c-z \\ a-x & b-y & c\end{array}\right]=0$
$\Rightarrow a(b c-b c+b z+c y-y z)-(b-y)$
$(a c-c x-a c+a z+c x-z x)+(c-z)$
$(a b-a y-b x+x y-a b+b x)=0$
$\Rightarrow a b z+a c y-a y z-a b z+b z x+a y z-$
$\Rightarrow a y z+b z x+c x y-2 x y z=0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{a}{x}+\frac{b}{y}+\frac{c}{z}-2=0$ (Dividing by $\left.x y z\right)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{a}{x}+\frac{b}{y}+\frac{c}{z}=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of a parallelogram with $3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ as diagonals is
questions.solution:
Given : diagonals $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}_{1}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}_{2}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\because$ Area of a parallelogram $=\frac{1}{2}\left|\mathrm{~d}_{1} \times \mathrm{d}_{2}\right|$
Now, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}_{1} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 3 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 & 4\end{array}\right|$
$=\hat{\mathrm{i}}(4-6)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(12+2)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(-9-1)$
$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}_{1} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}_{2}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-14 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-10 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\therefore\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}_{1} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}_{2}\right|=\sqrt{(-2)^{2}+(-14)^{2}+(-10)^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{4+196+100}=\sqrt{300}=2 \sqrt{75}$
$\therefore$ Area of parallelogram $=\frac{1}{2}\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}_{1} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}}_{2}\right|$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \sqrt{75}=\sqrt{75}$ square units

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The values of $\alpha$ for which the system of equation 
$x+y+z=1, x+2 y+4 z=\alpha, x+4 y+10 z=\alpha^{2}$ is consistent are given by
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{A}: \mathrm{B}=\left|\begin{array}{ccccc}1 & 1 & 1 & : & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 4 & : & \alpha \\ 1 & 4 & 10 & : & \alpha^{2}\end{array}\right|$
$\sim\left|\begin{array}{cccc}1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 3: & \alpha-1 \\ 0 & 3 & 9 & \alpha^{2}-1\end{array}\right|$
$\left[\begin{array}{l}\text { applying } \mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}-\mathrm{R}_{1} \\ \& \mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}-\mathrm{R}_{1}\end{array}\right]$
$\begin{array}{r}
\sim\left|\begin{array}{ccccc}
1 & 1 & 1 & : & 1 \\
0 & 1 & 3 & : & \alpha-1 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & : & \alpha^{2}-3 \alpha+2
\end{array}\right| \\
& {\left[\text { applying } \mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}-3 \mathrm{R}_{2}\right]}
\end{array}$
But the system is consistent
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \alpha^{2}-3 \alpha+2=0 \\
\Rightarrow(\alpha-2)(\alpha-1)=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=2 \text { or } \alpha=1
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The system of linear equations : $x+y+z=0,2 x$ $+y-z=0,3 x+2 y=0$ has :
questions.solution:
The system is homogenuous system. $\therefore$ it has either unique solution or infinite many solution depend on $|\mathrm{A}|$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore|A| &=\left|\begin{array}{rrr}
1 & 1 & 1 \\
2 & 1 & -1 \\
3 & 2 & 0
\end{array}\right|=2 \times 1+1(-3)+(4-3) \\
&=2-3+1=0
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: An equilateral triangle is inscribed in the parabola $y^{2}=4 x$ one of whose vertex is at the vertex of the parabola, the length of each side of the triangle is
questions.solution:
Let $A B=\ell$, then $A M=\ell \cos 30^{\circ}=\frac{\ell \sqrt{3}}{2}$ $\& \mathrm{BM}=\ell \sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{\ell}{2}$
So, the coordinates of $\mathrm{B}$ are $\left(\frac{\ell \sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{\ell}{2}\right)$ Since, $\mathrm{B}$ lies on $\mathrm{y}^{2}=4 \mathrm{x}$ $\begin{array}{l} \therefore \frac{\ell^{2}}{4}=4\left(\frac{\ell \sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \ell^{2}=\frac{16}{2} \cdot \sqrt{3} \ell \Rightarrow \ell=8 \sqrt{3} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the equations $x+a y-z=0,2 x-y+a z=0$, ax $+y+2 z=0$ have non-trivial solutions, then $a=$
questions.solution:
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \mathrm{a} & -1 \\ 2 & -1 & \mathrm{a} \\ \mathrm{a} & 1 & 2\end{array}\right|=0$
Applying $\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}-2 \mathrm{R}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}-$ $\mathrm{aR}_{1}$, we get
$$
\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & a & -1 \\
0 & -1-2 a & a+2 \\
0 & 1-a^{2} & 2+a
\end{array}\right|=0
$$
Expanding along $C_{1}$, we get
$$
(a+2)\left(a^{2}-2 a-2\right)=0 \Rightarrow a=-2,1 \pm \sqrt{3}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the system of equations $x+k y-z=0$, $3 x-k y-z=0$ and $x-3 y+z=0$, has non-zero solution, then $\mathrm{k}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
The system has non- zero solution, if
$\begin{aligned}
&\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & \mathrm{k} & -1 \\
3 & -\mathrm{k} & -1 \\
1 & -3 & 1
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
\Rightarrow & 1(-\mathrm{k}-3)-\mathrm{k}(3+1)-1(-9+\mathrm{k})=0 \\
\Rightarrow &-6 \mathrm{k}+6=0 \\
\Rightarrow & \mathrm{k}=1
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\alpha, \beta$ and $\gamma$ are the roots of $x^{3}+a x^{2}+b=0$, then the value of $\left|\begin{array}{lll}\alpha & \beta & \gamma \\ \beta & \gamma & \alpha \\ \gamma & \alpha & \beta\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution: $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are the roots of given equation, Therefore $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=-\mathrm{a}$ $\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha=0$ and $\alpha \beta \gamma=-\mathrm{b}$ Now, $\left|\begin{array}{lll}\alpha & \beta & \gamma \\ \beta & \gamma & \alpha \\ \gamma & \alpha & \beta\end{array}\right|=-(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)$ $\left(\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}+\gamma^{2}-\alpha \beta-\beta \gamma-\gamma \alpha\right)$ $=-(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)\left[(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)^{2}\right.$ $\left.=-(-\mathrm{a})\left(\mathrm{a}^{2}-0\right)=\mathrm{a}^{3} \quad-3(\alpha \beta+\beta \gamma+\gamma \alpha)\right]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $x, y, z$ are all positive and are the $p^{\text {th }}, q^{\text {th }}$ and $r^{\text {th }}$ terms of a geometric progression respectively, then the value of the determinant
$\left|\begin{array}{lll}\log x & p & 1 \\ \log y & q & 1 \\ \log z & r & 1\end{array}\right|$ equals
questions.solution:
Let $a$ and $R$ be the first term and common ratio of a GPrespectively.
So, $T_{p}=a R^{p-1}=x$
$T_{q}=a R^{q-1}=y$ and $T_{r}=a R^{r-1}=z$
$\Rightarrow \log x=\log a+(p-1) \log R$
$\log y=\log a+(q-1) \log R$
and $\log z=\log a+(r-1) \log R$
$\therefore\left|\begin{array}{lll}\log x & p & 1 \\ \log y & q & 1 \\ \log z & r & 1\end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{lll}\log a+(p-1) \log R & p & 1 \\ \log a+(q-1) \log R & q & 1 \\ \log a+(r-1) \log R & r & 1\end{array}\right|$
$=\left|\begin{array}{lll}\log a & p & 1 \\ \log a & q & 1 \\ \log a & r & 1\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{lll}(p-1) \log R & p & 1 \\ (q-1) \log R & q & 1 \\ (r-1) \log R & r & 1\end{array}\right|$
$=\log a\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & p & 1 \\ 1 & q & 1 \\ 1 & r & 1\end{array}\right|+\log R\left|\begin{array}{ccc}p-1 & p-1 & 1 \\ q-1 & q-1 & 1 \\ r-1 & r-1 & 1\end{array}\right|$
$\left(C_{2} \rightarrow C_{2}-C_{3}\right)$
$=0+0=0 \quad(\because$ two col umns are identical $)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The differential equation $\left|\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right|+|\mathrm{y}|+3=0$ admits
questions.solution:
$\left|\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right|+|\mathrm{y}|+3=0$ Since $\left|\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right| \geq 0,|\mathrm{y}| \geq 0$
$\therefore\left|\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right|+|\mathrm{y}|+3 \geq 3$
Hence $\left|\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right|+|\mathrm{y}|+3=0$ is not possible.
Therefore, the given differential equation has no solution.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The distance between the line $\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}+\lambda(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k})$ and the plane $\vec{r} \cdot(\hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+\hat{k})=5$, is
questions.solution:
The given line is
$$
\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}+\lambda(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k})
$$
On comparing it with $\vec{r}=\vec{a}+t \vec{b}$, we get
$\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \quad \vec{b}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}$
Also, the plane is
$$
\vec{r} \cdot(\hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+\hat{k})=5
$$
On comparing it with $\vec{r} \cdot \vec{n}=d$, we get $\vec{n}=\hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $d=5$

Since, $\vec{b} \cdot \vec{n}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+\hat{k})$
$$
=1-5+4=0
$$
$\therefore$ Given line is parallel to the given plane. Now, distance between the line and the plane is given by required distance
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\left|\frac{|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{n}-d|}{|\vec{n}|}\right| \\
=\frac{|(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}) \cdot(\hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+\hat{k})-5|}{\sqrt{1+25+1}} \\
=\frac{|2-10+3-5|}{\sqrt{27}}=\frac{10}{3 \sqrt{3}}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Solve : \(\left(D^2+6 D+8\right) y=e^{-2 x}\).
questions.solution:
The characteristic equation is \(p^2+6 p+8=0\)
\(\Rightarrow(\mathrm{p}+4)(\mathrm{p}+2)=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}=-4 \text { and }-2\)
The C.F. is \(\mathrm{Ae}^{-4 x}+\mathrm{Be}^{-2 x}\)
Particular integral,
\(\begin{aligned}
\text { P.I. } & =\frac{1}{D^2+6 D+8} e^{-2 x} \\
& =\frac{1}{(D+4)(D+2)} e^{-2 x}
\end{aligned}\)
Since, \(f(D)=(D+2) q(D)\)
\(=\frac{1}{\theta(-2)} \mathrm{xe}^{-2 \mathrm{x}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{xe}^{-2 \mathrm{x}}\)
Hence the general solution is
\(y=A e^{-4 x}+B e^{-2 x}+\frac{1}{2} x e^{-2 x}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The diffrerential equation \(\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{1}{x} \sin 2 y=x^3 \cos ^2 y\) represents a family of curves given by the equation
questions.solution:
The given equation can be converted to linear form by dividing both the sides by \(\cos ^2 \mathrm{y}\). We get
\(\sec ^2 \mathrm{y} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} 2 \tan \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x}^3\)
Put tan \(y=z \Rightarrow \sec ^2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d z}{d x}\)
The equation becomes \(\frac{d z}{d x}+\frac{2}{x} z=x^3\), which is linear in \(z\) The integrating factor is
\(\text {I.F. }=e^{\int \frac{2}{x} d x}=e^{2 \log x}=e^{\log x^2}=x^2\)
Hence, the solution is
\(z\left(x^2\right)=\int x^3\left(x^2\right) d x+a \Rightarrow x^2=\frac{x^6}{6}+a,\)
\(a\) is constant of integration.
\(\begin{aligned}
& \therefore(6 \tan y) x^2=x^6+6 a \Rightarrow 6 x^2 \tan y=x^6+c, \\
& \quad[c=6 a]
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The order and degree of the differential equation whose solution is $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{cx}+\mathrm{c}^{2}-3 \mathrm{c}^{3 / 2}+2$, where $\mathrm{c}$ is a parameter, is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{cx}+\mathrm{c}^{2}-3 \mathrm{c}^{3 / 2}+2$ Differentiating above with respect to $x$, we get $\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{c}$
Putting this value of $\mathrm{c}$ in (i), we get
$$
\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)^{2}-3\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)^{3 / 2}+2
$$
Clearly its order is ONE and after removing the fractional power we get the degree FOUR.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The solution of the differential equation
$\left(1+y^{2}\right)+\left(x-e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=0$
questions.solution:
$1+y^{2}+\left(x-e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=0$ 
$\Rightarrow\left(1+y^{2}\right) d x=\left(e^{\tan ^{-1} y}-x\right) d y$ 
$\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} y}-x}{1+y^{2}}$ 
$\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{1}{1+y^{2}} \cdot x=\frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} y}}{1+y^{2}}$
Which is the linear different equation of the form $\frac{d x}{d y}+R x=S$, where $R$ and $S$ are functions of $y$ or constant $(s)$
$$
\therefore \mathrm{I} . \mathrm{F}=\int_{e}^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} \cdot d y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}
$$
Hence required solution is
$$
\begin{array}{l}
x .(\mathrm{I} . \mathrm{F})=\int \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} y}}{1+y^{2}}(\mathrm{I} . \mathrm{F}) d y \\
\Rightarrow x \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} y}}{1+y^{2}}\left(e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\right) d y \\
\Rightarrow x \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int \frac{e^{2 \tan ^{-1} y}}{1+y^{2}} d y
\end{array}
$$
Put $t=\tan ^{-1} y$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \frac{d t}{d y}=\frac{1}{1+y^{2}} \Rightarrow d t=\frac{1}{1+y^{2}} \cdot d y \\
\therefore \int \frac{e^{2 \tan ^{-1} y}}{1+y^{2}} d y=\int e^{2 t} \cdot d t=\frac{e^{2 t}}{2}+K
\end{array}
$$
Hence equation (1) becomes,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\frac{1}{2} e^{2 t}+K \\
\Rightarrow x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\frac{1}{2} e^{2 \tan ^{-1} y}+K \\
\Rightarrow 2 x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=e^{2 \tan ^{-1} y}+K
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The integrating factor of $\frac{x d y}{d x}-y=x^{4}-3 x$ is
questions.solution:
Since $x \frac{d y}{d x}-y=x^{4}-3 x$
$$
\therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}=x^{3}-3
$$
Hence
$$
I F=e^{\int P d x}=e^{-\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{-\log x}=\frac{1}{x}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The differential equation $(3 x+4 y+1) d x+(4 x+5 y+1) d y=0$ represents a family of
questions.solution:
The given differential equation is
$(3 x+4 y+1) d x+(4 x+5 y+1) d y=0$
Comparing eq. (i) with $\mathrm{Mdx}+\mathrm{Ndy}=0$, we get
$M=3 x+4 y+1$
and $\mathrm{N}=4 \mathrm{x}+5 \mathrm{y}+1$
Here, $\frac{\partial \mathrm{M}}{\partial \mathrm{y}}=\frac{\partial \mathrm{N}}{\partial \mathrm{x}}=4$
Hence, eq. (i) is exact and solution is given by
$\begin{aligned}
& \int(3 x+4 y+1) d x+\int(5 y+1) d y=C \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{3 x^{2}}{2}+4 x y+x+\frac{5 y^{2}}{2}+y-C=0 \\
\Rightarrow & 3 x^{2}+8 x y+2 x+5 y^{2}+2 y-2 C=0 \\
\Rightarrow & 3 x^{2}+2.4 x y+2 x+5 y^{2}+2 y+C^{\prime}=0 \\
& \text { where, } C^{\prime}=-2 C
\end{aligned}$
On comparing eq. (ii) with standard form of conic section
$a x^{2}+2 h x y+b y^{2}+2 g x+2 f y+C=0$
We get,
$\mathrm{a}=3, \mathrm{~h}=4, \mathrm{~b}=5$
Here, $h^{2}-a b=16-15=1>0$
Hence, the solution of differential equation represents family of hyperbolas.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The differential equation corresponding to the family of curves $y=e^x(a x+b)$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { (b) } y=e^x(a x+b) \\
& \Rightarrow y \cdot e^{-x}=a x+b
\end{aligned}$
Differentiating both sides, we get
$e^{-x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)-y e^{-x}=a$
Again differentiating both sides
$\begin{array}{ll}
e^{-x}\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)-e^{-x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)+y e^{-x}-e^{-x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=0 \\
\Rightarrow & e^{-x}\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-2 \frac{d y}{d x}+y\right)=0 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-2\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)+y=0
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of $\frac{d^{2} x}{d y^{2}}-x=k$, where $k$ is a non-zero constant, vanishes when $y=0$ and tends of finite limit as $y$ tends to infinity, is
questions.solution:
We can write given differential equation as,
$\left(\mathrm{D}^{2}-1\right) \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{k}$ ...(i)
where, $D \equiv \frac{d}{d y}$ 
Its auxiliary equation is $m^{2}-1=0$, so that $\mathrm{m}=1,-1$
Hence, $C F=C_{1} e^{y}+C_{2} e^{-y}$.
where $\mathrm{C}_{1}, \mathrm{C}_{2}$ are arbitrary constants
Now, also $\mathrm{PI}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{D}^{2}-1} \mathrm{k}$
$\begin{array}{l}
=\mathrm{k} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{D}^{2}-1} \mathrm{e}^{0 . \mathrm{y}} \\
=K \cdot \frac{1}{0^{2}-1} e^{0 . \mathrm{y}}=-K
\end{array}$
So, solution of eq. (i) is 
$x=C_{1} e^{y}+C_{2} e^{-y}-k$ ...(ii)
Given that $x=0$, when $y=0$
So, $0=\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2}-\mathrm{k} \quad$ (From (ii)) 
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2}=\mathrm{k}$ ...(iii)
Multiplying both sides of eq. (ii) by $\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}$, we get
x. $\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{e}^{-2 \mathrm{y}}-\mathrm{ke}^{-\mathrm{y}}$ ...(iv)
Given that $x \rightarrow m$ when $y \rightarrow \infty, m$ being a finite quantity.
So, eq (iv) becomes
$\mathrm{x} \times 0=\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2} \times 0-(\mathrm{k} \times 0)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{C}_{1}=0$ ...(v)
From eqs. (iv) and (v), we get $\mathrm{C}_{1}=0$ and $\mathrm{C}_{2}=\mathrm{k}$
Hence, eq. (ii) becomes
$\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{ke}^{-\mathrm{y}}-\mathrm{k}=\mathrm{k}\left(\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}-1\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x \cdot \frac{f(x y)}{f^{\prime}(x y)}$, then $f(x y)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=x \cdot \frac{f(x y)}{f^{\prime}(x y)}$
i.e., $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{xy})=\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y})}{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y})}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{xy})}{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{xy})} \mathrm{d}(\mathrm{xy})=\mathrm{x} \mathrm{dx}$
$\Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{xy})}{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{xy})} \mathrm{d}(\mathrm{xy})=\int \mathrm{xdx}$
$\Rightarrow \log [\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{xy})]=\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2}+\mathrm{C}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{xy})=\mathrm{e}^{\left(\mathrm{x}^{2} / 2+\mathrm{C}\right)}$
$=\mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{k} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{2}}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of the differential equation $\left(1+y^{2}\right)+\left(x-e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=0$, is
questions.solution:
$\left(1+y^{2}\right)+\left(x-e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=0$
$\left(1+y^{2}\right) \frac{d x}{d y}+x=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}$
$\frac{d x}{d y}+\frac{x}{1+y^{2}}=\frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} y}}{\left(1+y^{2}\right)}$
$I F=e^{\int \frac{1}{1+y^{2}} d y}=e^{\tan ^{-1} y}$
$\Rightarrow x . e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=\int \frac{e^{\tan ^{-1} y}{1+y^{2}} \cdot e^{\tan ^{-1} y} \cdot d y}{}$
$\Rightarrow x\left(e^{\tan ^{-1} y}\right)=\frac{e^{2 \tan ^{-1} y}}{2}+c$
$\therefore 2 x e^{\tan ^{-1} y}=e^{2 \tan ^{-1} y}+K$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The differential equation of the rectangular hyperbola hyperbola, where axes are the asymptotes of the hyperbola, is
questions.solution:
The differential equation of the rectangular hyperbola $x y=c^{2}$ is
$\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0$
$\Rightarrow x \frac{d y}{d x}=-y$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y f^{\prime}(x)-y^{2}}{f(x)}$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{yf}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})-\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{yf}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}-\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dy}=\mathrm{y}^{2} \mathrm{dx} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{yf}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}-\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}^{2}}=\mathrm{dx} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{d}\left\{\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})}{\mathrm{y}}\right\}=\mathrm{dx}
\end{aligned}$
On integration, we get
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{f(x)}{y}=x+C \\
\Rightarrow f(x)=y(x+C)
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the gradient of the tangent at any point $(x, y)$ of a curve which passes through the point $\left(1, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ is $\left\{\frac{y}{x}-\sin ^{2}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)\right\}$, then equation of the curve is
questions.solution:
$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}-\sin ^{2}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$
Put $y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}$
$\begin{array}{l}
v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v-\sin ^{2} v \\
\Rightarrow-\operatorname{cosec}^{2} v d v=\frac{d x}{x}
\end{array}$
Integrating both sides, we get
$\begin{array}{l}
-\int \operatorname{cosec}^{2} v d v=\int \frac{d x}{x} \\
\Rightarrow \cot v=\log x+C \\
\cot \frac{y}{x}=\log x+C
\end{array}$
Curve passes through the point $\left(1, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \mathrm{C}=1 \\
\Rightarrow \cot \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}=\log \mathrm{x}+\log _{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{e} \\
\Rightarrow \cot \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}=\log \mathrm{xe} \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x} \cot ^{-1}(\log \mathrm{xe})
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{2 y x}{1+x^{2}}=\frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}$ is
questions.solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\frac{2 \mathrm{yx}}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}=\frac{1}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)^{2}}$
which is a linear differ ential equation.
Here, $\mathrm{P}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}, \mathrm{Q}=\frac{1}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)^{2}}$
Now, IF = $\mathrm{e}^{\int P d x}$ $=\int_{\mathrm{e}^{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}^{\int \frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{1+\mathrm{dx}}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}=\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)$
$\therefore$ Solution of differential equation is
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { y. }\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\int \frac{1}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}} \cdot\left(1+x^{2}\right) d x+C \\
\Rightarrow y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\int \frac{1}{1+x^{2}} d x+C \\
\Rightarrow y\left(1+x^{2}\right)=\tan ^{-1} x+C
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The general solution of the differential equation $\left(1+y^{2}\right) d x+\left(1+x^{2}\right) d y=0$ is
questions.solution:
$\left(1+y^{2}\right) d x+\left(1+x^{2}\right) d y=0$ $\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}=0$
On integrating, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\tan ^{-1} x+\tan ^{-1} y=\tan ^{-1} C \\
\Rightarrow \frac{x+y}{1-x y}=C \\
\Rightarrow x+y=C(1-x y)
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The order and degree of the differential equation
$\rho=\frac{\left[1+\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)^{2}\right]^{3 / 2}}{\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}}$ are, respectively

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}=(4 x+y+1)^{2}$, is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=(4 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+1)^{2}...(i) \\
\text { Put } 4 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+1=\mathrm{v} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}-4 \\
\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}-4=\mathrm{v}^{2} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{v}^{2}+4}=\mathrm{dx} \quad(\because \text { From eq. (i)) } \\
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2}\right)=\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C} \\
\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+1}{2}\right)=2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C} \\
\Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+1=2 \tan (2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{C})
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x=-9$ is a root of $\left|\begin{array}{lll}x & 3 & 7 \\ 2 & x & 2 \\ 7 & 6 & x\end{array}\right|=0$, then other two roots are
questions.solution:
Given $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x & 3 & 7 \\ 2 & x & 2 \\ 7 & 6 & x\end{array}\right|=0$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow x\left[x^{2}-12\right]-3[2 x-14]+7[12-7 x]=0 \\
\Rightarrow x^{3}-67 x+126=0
\end{array}$
But given $(x=9)$ is a root of given determinant.
$\therefore(\mathrm{x}+9)$ is a factor
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{3}+9 \mathrm{x}^{2}-9 \mathrm{x}^{2}-81 \mathrm{x}+14 \mathrm{x}+126=0 \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{2}(\mathrm{x}+9)-9 \mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}+9)+14(\mathrm{x}+9)=0
\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow(x+9)\left(x^{2}-9 x+14\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow(x+9)\left(x^{2}-7 x-2 x+14\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow(x+9)(x-7)(x-2)=0$
$\Rightarrow x=-9,7,2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The solution of the differential equation
$\left\{1+x \sqrt{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)}\right\} d x+\left\{\sqrt{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)}-1\right\} y d y=$ 0 is
questions.solution:
Rearranging the equation, we have
$$
\begin{array}{l}
d x-y d y+\sqrt{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)}(x d x+y d y)=0 \\
\Rightarrow d x-y d y+\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)} d\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)=0
\end{array}
$$
On integrating, we get
$$
x-\frac{y^{2}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \int \sqrt{t} d t=C,\left\{t=\sqrt{\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)}\right\}
$$
or $x-\frac{y^{2}}{2}+\frac{1}{3}\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}=C$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the I.F. of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}+5 y$ $=\cos x$ is $\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{Adx}}$, then $\mathrm{A}=$
questions.solution:
The I.F. of the differential equation
$\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q$ is $e^{\int P d x} .$ Here $P=5$ therefore
I.F. $={ }_{\mathrm{e}} \int 5 \mathrm{dx} .$ Hence $\mathrm{A}=5$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In a culture the bacteria count is $1,00,000$. The number is increased by $10 \%$ in 2 hours. In how many hours will the count reach $2,00,000$ if the rate of growth of bacteria is proportional to the number present.
questions.solution:
Let y denote the number of bacteria at any instant $\mathrm{t} \cdot$ then according to the question
$$
\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}} \alpha \mathrm{y} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{k} \mathrm{dt}......(i)
$$
$\mathrm{k}$ is the constant of proportionality, taken to be + ve on integrating (i), 
we get $\log y=k t+c......(ii)$
$\mathrm{c}$ is a parameter. let $\mathrm{y}_{0}$ be the initial number of bacteria
i.e., at $t=0$ using this in (ii), $c=\log y_{0}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \log y=k t+\log y_{0} \\
\Rightarrow \log \frac{y}{y_{0}}=k t......(iii)
\end{array}
$$
$\mathrm{y}=\left(\mathrm{y}_{0}+\frac{10}{100} \mathrm{y}_{0}\right)=\frac{11 \mathrm{y}_{0}}{10}$, when $\mathrm{t}=2$
So, from (iii), we get $\log \frac{\frac{11 \mathrm{y}_{0}}{10}}{\mathrm{y}_{0}}=\mathrm{k}$ (2) 
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{2} \log \frac{11}{10}......(iv)$
Using (iv) in (iii) $\log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}_{0}}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\log \frac{11}{10}\right) \mathrm{t}......(v)$
let the number of bacteria become 1,00 , 000 to $2,00,000$ in $\mathrm{t}_{1}$ hours. i.e., $\mathrm{y}=2 \mathrm{y}_{0}$ when $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}_{1}$ hours. from (v)
$$
\log \frac{2 \mathrm{y}_{0}}{\mathrm{y}_{0}}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\log \frac{11}{10}\right) \mathrm{t}_{1} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{1}=\frac{2 \log 2}{\log \frac{11}{10}}
$$
Hence, the reqd. no. of hours $=\frac{2 \log 2}{\log \frac{11}{10}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of $\frac{d v}{d t}+\frac{k}{m} v=-g$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}+1}{2 x y}$, satisfying $y(1)=0$ is given by
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}+1}{2 x y} \\
\Rightarrow 2 x y d y=\left(x^{2}+1\right) d x+y^{2} d x \\
\Rightarrow \frac{x d\left(y^{2}\right)-y^{2} d x}{x^{2}}=\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}}\right) \cdot d x \\
\Rightarrow \int d\left(\frac{y^{2}}{x}\right)=\int\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right) d x \\
\Rightarrow \frac{y^{2}}{x}=x-\frac{1}{x}+C \\
\Rightarrow y^{2}=\left(x^{2}-1+C x\right) \\
\text { When } x=1, y=0 \\
\Rightarrow 0=1-1+C \\
\Rightarrow C=0
\end{array}$
$\therefore \quad$ The solution is $x^{2}-y^{2}=1$ i.e., hyperbola.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The order and degree of the differential equation $\left(1+4 \frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2 / 3}=4 \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}$ are respectively
questions.solution:
Given differential equation is
$$
\left(1+4 \frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}}=4 \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}
$$
$$
\left(1+4 \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)^{2}=4^{3}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}\right)^{3}
$$
Here, order is 2 and degree is 3 .

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}=\sin (x+y) \tan (x+y)-1$ is
questions.solution:
$$
 \begin{array}{l}
\frac{d y}{d x}=\sin (x+y) \tan (x+y)-1 \\
\text { Put } x+y=z \\
\Rightarrow 1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d z}{d x} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{d z}{d x}-1=\sin z \tan z-1 \\
\Rightarrow \int \frac{\cos z}{\sin ^{2} z} d z=\int d x
\end{array}
$$
Putting $\sin z=t \Rightarrow \cos z d z=d t$, we have
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\int \frac{1}{t^{2}} d t=x-c \Rightarrow-\frac{1}{t}=x-c \\
\Rightarrow-\operatorname{cosec} z=x-c \Rightarrow x+\operatorname{cosec}(x+y)=c
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of normal to the curve $y=(1+x)^{y}+\sin ^{-1}\left(\sin ^{2} x\right)$ at $x=0$ is
questions.solution:
Given curve is
$y=(1+x)^{y}+\sin ^{-1}\left(\sin ^{2} x\right)$
On differentiating w.r.t $x$, we get
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=(1+\mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{y}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{y}}{1+\mathrm{x}}+\log (1+\mathrm{x}) \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right] \\
\Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)_{\mathrm{at}(0,1)}=\frac{2 \sin \mathrm{x} \cos \mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{4} \mathrm{x}}}
\end{array}$
Slope of normal at $(x=0)=-1$
$\therefore \quad$ Equation of normal at $x=0$ and $y=1$ is $\begin{aligned} y-1 &=-1(x-0) \\ \Rightarrow y-1 &=-x \Rightarrow x+y=1 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $(\cos x)^y=(\sin y)^x$, then $\frac{d y}{d x}$ equals
questions.solution:
$(\cos x)^y=(\sin y)^x$
Taking log on both sides
$y \ln (\cos x)=x \ln (\sin y)$
On differentiating both sides w.r.t $x$, we get
$\begin{aligned}
& y \cdot\left(\frac{1}{\cos x}(-\sin x)\right)+\frac{d y}{d x}(\ln (\cos x)) \\
& =x\left(\frac{1}{\sin y} \times \cos y\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+\ln (\sin y) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left(\ln (\cos x)-\frac{x \cos y}{\sin y}\right)=\ln (\sin y)+y \tan x \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}(\ln (\cos x)-x \cot y)=\ln (\sin y)+y \tan x \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\ln (\sin y)+y \tan x}{\ln (\cos x)-x \cot y}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The distance moved by the particle in time $t$ is given by $\mathrm{s}=\mathrm{t}^{3}-12 \mathrm{t}^{2}+6 \mathrm{t}+8$. At the instant, when its acceleration is zero the velocity is:
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{s}=\mathrm{t}^{3}-12 \mathrm{t}^{2}+6 \mathrm{t}+8 \\
\frac{\mathrm{ds}}{\mathrm{dt}}=3 \mathrm{t}^{2}-24 \mathrm{t}+6 \\
\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{~s}}{\mathrm{dt}^{2}}=6 \mathrm{t}-24 \\
\text { Acceleration }=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad 6 \mathrm{t}-24=0 \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{t}=4 \\
\text { Required velocity } \left.=\frac{\mathrm{ds}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right]_{\mathrm{t}=4} \\
=3 \times(4)^{2}-24 \times 4+6 \\
=48-96+6=42 \text { units }
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The condition that the line $l x+m y=1$ may be normal to the curve $y^{2}=4 a x$, is
questions.solution:
Let $P\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ be a point on the curve
$$
y^{2}=4 a x...(i)
$$
On differentiating $y^{2}=4 a x$ w.r.t. $^{\prime} x^{\prime}$, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=4 a \\
\Rightarrow\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)}=\frac{2 a}{y_{1}}
\end{array}
$$
Thus, the equation of normal at $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ is
$y-y_{1}=-\frac{y_{1}}{2 a}\left(x-x_{1}\right)$
$\Rightarrow y_{1} x+2 a y=y_{1}\left(x_{1}+2 a\right)$...(i)
But $l x+m y=1$ ...(ii)
is alsoa normal. Therefore, coefficients of eqs. (ii) and (iii), must be proportional. i.e., $\frac{y_{1}}{l}=\frac{2 a}{m}=\frac{y_{1}\left(x_{1}+2 a\right)}{1}$ $\Rightarrow y_{1}=\frac{2 a l}{m}$ and $x_{1}=\frac{1}{l}-2 a$ must be proportional.

Putting these values of $x_{1}$ and $y_{1}$ in eq. (i), we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left(\frac{2 a l}{m}\right)^{2}=4 a\left(\frac{1}{l}-2 a\right) \\
\Rightarrow \frac{4 a^{2} l^{2}}{m^{2}}=\frac{4 a-8 a^{2} l}{l} \\
\Rightarrow a l^{3}=m^{2}-2 a m^{2} l \Rightarrow a l^{3}+2 a l m^{2}=m^{2}
\end{array}
$$

questions.q:
$x=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+t^{2}}}\right)$,
$y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt{1+t^{2}}}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, $x=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+t^{2}}}\right)$
and $y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt{1+t^{2}}}\right)$
$$
\Rightarrow x=\tan ^{-1} t \text { and } y=\tan ^{-1} t
$$
$$
\therefore y=x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=1
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Area enclosed by the curve $\pi\left[4(x-\sqrt{2})^{2}+y^{2}\right]=8$ is
questions.solution:
The given curve is $\begin{array}{l} \pi\left[4(x-\sqrt{2})^{2}+y^{2}\right]=8 \\ 4(x-\sqrt{2})^{2}+y^{2}=\frac{8}{\pi} \\ (x-\sqrt{2})^{2}+\left(\frac{y}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{2}{\pi} \\ \frac{(x-\sqrt{2})^{2}}{\left(\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}\right)^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{\left(2 \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}\right)^{2}}=1 ...(1) \end{array}$ This is the equation of the ellipse having centre $(\sqrt{2}, 0)$ Observe the figure of ellipse (1). The centre Pis $(\sqrt{2}, 0)$. A and B are $\left(\sqrt{2},-\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}, 0\right)$ and $\left(\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}, 0\right)$ respectively.
The required area $=4 \times$ area of figure $\mathrm{PQB}$ $\begin{array}{l} =4 \times \int_{\sqrt{2}}^{\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}} y d x \\ =4 \times \int_{\sqrt{2}}^{\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}} \sqrt{\frac{8}{\pi}-4(x-\sqrt{2})^{2}} d x \\ =4 \times 2 \int_{\sqrt{2}}^{\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}} \sqrt{\left(\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}\right)^{2}-(x-\sqrt{2})^{2}} d x \\ =8\left[\frac{x-\sqrt{2}}{2} \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}-(x-\sqrt{2})^{2}}+\right. \left.\frac{(2 / \pi)}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2 / \pi}}\right)\right]_{\sqrt{2}}^{\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}} \end{array}$ $\left[\because \int \sqrt{\mathrm{a}^{2}-\mathrm{x}^{2}} \mathrm{dx}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{x} \sqrt{\mathrm{a}^{2}-\mathrm{x}^{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{a}}+\mathrm{C}\right]$ $=8\left[\left\{\frac{\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}-\sqrt{2}}{2} \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}-\left(\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{\frac{2}{\mathrm{x}}}-\sqrt{2}\right)^{2}}\right.\right.$ $\left.+\frac{1}{\pi} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}}\right)\right\}-\left\{\frac{\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{2}}{2}\right.$ $\left.\left.\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}-(\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{2})^{2}}+\frac{1}{\pi} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2 / \pi}}\right)\right\}\right]$ $=8\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}}(0)+\frac{1}{\pi} \sin ^{-1}(1)-0-0\right]$ $=8\left(\frac{1}{\pi} \times \frac{\pi}{2}\right)=4$ square units.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A random variable X follows binomial distribution with mean $\alpha$ and variance $\beta$. Then
questions.solution:
Mean, $\mathrm{np}=\alpha$; and variance, $\mathrm{npq}=\beta$ where $\mathrm{n}$ $=$ number of trials and $\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}=1$
So, $\frac{\mathrm{npq}}{\mathrm{np}}=\frac{\beta}{\alpha} \Rightarrow \mathrm{q}=\frac{\beta}{\alpha} \Rightarrow(1-\mathrm{p})=\frac{\beta}{\alpha}$
$\because 0 < \mathrm{p} < 1$
$\therefore-1 < -\mathrm{p} < 0 \Rightarrow 0 < 1-\mathrm{p} < 1$
$\Rightarrow 0 < \frac{\beta}{\alpha} < 1 \Rightarrow 0 < \beta < \alpha$

questions.q: The coordinates of a moving particle at time \(t\) are given by \(x=c t^2\) and \(y=b t^2\). The speed of the particle is given by
questions.solution:

& \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{ct} \text { and } \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{bt} \\
& \therefore \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\left\{\left(\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)+\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)^2\right\}} \\
& =\sqrt{\left\{(2 c t)^2+(2 b t)^2\right\}}=2 t \sqrt{\left(c^2+b^2\right)} \\
&
\end{aligned}\)

questions.q: If $\left(G^{*}\right)$ is a group and the order of an element $a \in G$ is 10 , then the order of the inverse of $a$ * a is
questions.solution:
We know that, let $(\mathrm{G}, 0)$ be a group \& e be the identity then

&=\left(\mathrm{a}^{-1}\right)^{-1}=\mathrm{a} .

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\lambda$ does the line $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x}+\lambda$ touches the ellipse $9 x^{2}+16 y^{2}=144$ is/are
questions.solution:
$\because$ Equation of ellipse is $9 x^{2}+16 y^{2}=144$ or $\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1$
Comparing this with $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ then we get $a^{2}=16$ and $b^{2}=9$ and comparing the line $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x}+\lambda$ with $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}+\mathrm{c}$ $\therefore \mathrm{m}=1$ and $\mathrm{c}=\lambda$
If the line $y=x+\lambda$ touches the ellipse $9 x^{2}$ $+16 y^{2}=144$, then $c^{2}=a^{2} m^{2}+b^{2}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \lambda^{2}=16 \times 1^{2}+9 \Rightarrow \lambda^{2}=25 \\
\therefore \lambda=\pm 5
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The minimum area of the triangle formed by any tangent to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ with the coordinate axes is
questions.solution:
Equation of tangent at $(a \cos \theta, b \sin \theta)$ to the ellipse is $\frac{x}{a} \cos \theta+\frac{y}{b} \sin \theta=1$
Coordinates of $P$ and $Q$ are $\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\cos \theta}, 0\right)$ and $\left(0, \frac{\mathrm{b}}{\sin \theta}\right)$, respectively. Area of $\Delta \mathrm{OPQ}=\frac{1}{2}\left|\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\cos \theta} \times \frac{\mathrm{b}}{\sin \theta}\right|=\frac{\mathrm{ab}}{|\sin 2 \theta|}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If a tangent having slope of $-\frac{4}{3}$ to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{18}+\frac{y^{2}}{32}=1$ intersects the major and minor axes in points $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ respectively, then the area of $\Delta O A B$ is equal to ( $O$ is the centre of the ellipse)
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{x}_{1}, \mathrm{y}_{1}\right)$ be a point on the ellipse.
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{18}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{32}=1 \\
\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{x}_{1}^{2}}{18}+\frac{\mathrm{y}_{1}^{2}}{32}=1 \ldots \text { (i) }
\end{array}$
The equation of the tangent at $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ is $\frac{x x_{1}}{18}+\frac{y y_{1}}{32}=1$. This meets the axes at $\mathrm{A}\left(\frac{18}{\mathrm{x}_{1}}, 0\right)$ and $\mathrm{B}\left(0, \frac{32}{\mathrm{y}_{1}}\right)$. It is given that slope of the tangent at $\left(\mathrm{x}_{1}, \mathrm{y}_{1}\right)$ is $-\frac{4}{3}$
So, $-\frac{x_{1}}{18} \cdot \frac{32}{y_{1}}=-\frac{4}{3}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{x}_{1}}{\mathrm{y}_{1}}=\frac{3}{4}$ 
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{x}_{1}}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}_{1}}{4}=\mathrm{K}$ 
$\therefore \mathrm{x}_{1}=3 \mathrm{~K}$ and $\mathrm{y}_{1}=4 \mathrm{~K}$
Putting $x_{1}, y_{1}$ in (i), we get 
$K^{2}=1$
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \text { Area of } \Delta \mathrm{OAB}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{OA} . \mathrm{OB} \\
=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{18}{\mathrm{x}_{1}} \cdot \frac{32}{\mathrm{y}_{1}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{(18)(32)}{(3 \mathrm{~K})(4 \mathrm{~K})}=\frac{24}{\mathrm{~K}^{2}} \\
=24 \text { sq units } \quad\left(\because \mathrm{K}^{2}=1\right)
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{\cos ^2 \alpha}+\frac{y^2}{\sin ^2 \alpha}=1$, where $\alpha \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$. Then, locus of point of intersection of one of the directrix and tangent at upper end of minor axis is
questions.solution:
From the given ellipse, $\sin ^2 \alpha=\cos ^2 \alpha\left(1-e^2\right)$ $\Rightarrow \quad e=\sqrt{1-\tan ^2 \alpha}$ and $\quad x=\frac{a}{e} \Rightarrow h=\frac{\cos \alpha}{\sqrt{1-\tan ^2 \alpha}}$ $y_2=b \Rightarrow k=\sin \alpha$
So, $h=\frac{\sqrt{1-k^2}}{\sqrt{1-\frac{k^2}{\left(1-k^2\right)}}}=\frac{\left(1-k^2\right)}{\sqrt{1-2 k^2}}$ $\Rightarrow \quad h^2=\frac{\left(1-k^2\right)^2}{\left(1-2 k^2\right)}$ Hence, the locus of required point $x^2\left(1-2 y^2\right)=\left(1-y^2\right)^2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The conic represented by $x=2(\cos t+\sin t), y=5(\cos t-\sin t)$ is
questions.solution:
From given equations
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{x}{2}=\cos t+\sin t ......(i)\\
\frac{y}{5}=\cos t-\sin t ......(ii)
\end{array}
$$
Eliminating t from (i) and (ii), we have $\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{4}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{25}=2 \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{8}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{50}=1$, which is an ellipse.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If line $y=2 x+c$ is a normal to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{16}=1$, then
questions.solution:
If the line $y=m x+c$ is a normal to the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$, then $\mathrm{c}^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{m}^{2}\left(\mathrm{a}^{2}-\mathrm{b}^{2}\right)^{2}}{\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2} \mathrm{~m}^{2}}$
$\left[\right.$ Here, $\mathrm{m}=2, \mathrm{a}^{2}=9$ and $\left.\mathrm{b}^{2}=16\right]$ $=\frac{(2)^{2}(9-16)^{2}}{9+16 \times(2)^{2}}$ $=\frac{4 \times 49}{9+64}=\frac{4 \times 49}{73}=\frac{196}{73}$
$\therefore \quad c=\frac{14}{\sqrt{73}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the ellipse with focus at \((\pm 5,0)\) and \(x=\frac{36}{5}\) as one directrix is
questions.solution:
We have \(a e=5 \quad\) [Since focus is \((\pm \mathrm{ae}, 0)]\) and \(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{36}{5}\left[\right.\) since directrix is \(\left.\mathrm{x}= \pm \frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{e}}\right]\)
On solving we get \(\mathrm{a}=6\) and \(\mathrm{e}=\frac{5}{6}\)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{b}^2=\mathrm{a}^2\left(1-\mathrm{e}^2\right)=36\left(1-\frac{25}{36}\right)=11\)
Thus, the required equation of the ellipse is \(\frac{x^2}{36}+\frac{y^2}{11}=1\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the axes are shifted to the point $(1,-2)$ with out solution, then the equation $2 x^{2}+y^{2}-4 x+4 y=0$ becomes
questions.solution:
Substituting $x=X+1$ and $y=Y-2$ in given equation, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
2(X+1)^{2}+(Y-2)^{2}-4(X+1)+4(Y-2)=0 \\
\Rightarrow 2 X^{2}+Y^{2}=6
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}}}$ and $\mathrm{f}(0)=0$, then $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=$
questions.solution:
Given : $f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x}}, f(0)=0$
$\Rightarrow \int \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}=\int \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}}} \mathrm{dx}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\int \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}}} \mathrm{dx}$
Let $1+\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{t}^{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{t}^{2}-1$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{dx}=2 \mathrm{t} \cdot \mathrm{dt}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\int \frac{\mathrm{t}^{2}-1}{\mathrm{t}} \cdot 2 \mathrm{t} \mathrm{dt}=2 \int\left(\mathrm{t}^{2}-1\right) \mathrm{dt} \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=2\left[\frac{\mathrm{t}^{3}}{3}-\mathrm{t}\right]+\mathrm{c} \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=2\left[\frac{(1+\mathrm{x})^{3 / 2}}{3}-(1+\mathrm{x})^{1 / 2}\right]+\mathrm{c} ...(1)
\end{array}$
But $\mathrm{f}(0)=0 \Rightarrow 2\left[\frac{1}{3}-1\right]+\mathrm{c}=0$
$\Rightarrow-\frac{4}{3}+\mathrm{c}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\frac{4}{3}$
$\therefore$ Equation (1) becomes
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=2\left[\frac{(1+\mathrm{x})^{3 / 2}}{3}-(1+\mathrm{x})^{1 / 2}\right]+\frac{4}{3}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{2}{3}\left[(1+\mathrm{x})^{3 / 2}-3(1+\mathrm{x})^{1 / 2}+2\right]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A point \(P\) moves such that the sum of twice its distance from the origin and its distance from the \(y\)-axis is a constant equal to 3 . P describes
options: A circle with its centre at \((-1,0)\) and radius \(2 \sqrt{3}\)
An ellipse centred at \((-1,0)\) and of eccentricity \(\frac{1}{2}\)
A hyperbola centred at \((1,0)\) and of eccentricity 2
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& 2 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{y}^2}+\mathrm{x}=3 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{(\mathrm{x}+1)^2}{4}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^2}{3}=1 .
\end{aligned}\)
This represents an ellipse centred at \((-1,0)\) and
\(\text { of eccentricity }=\sqrt{\frac{4-3}{4}}=\frac{1}{2} \text {. }\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The foci of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ and the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{144}-\frac{y^{2}}{81}=\frac{1}{25}$ coincide then value of $b^{2}$ is
questions.solution:
Given ellipse : $\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$
Now $b^{2}=a^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right)$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow b^{2}=16\left(1-e^{2}\right), \Rightarrow \frac{b^{2}}{16}=1-e^{2} \\
\Rightarrow e^{2}=1-\frac{b^{2}}{16}=\frac{16-b^{2}}{16} \Rightarrow e=\frac{\sqrt{16-b^{2}}}{4} \\
\text { Foci }=(\pm a e, 0)=\left(\pm \sqrt{16-b^{2}}, 0\right)
\end{array}
$$
Given hyperbola : $\frac{x^{2}}{144}-\frac{y^{2}}{81}=\frac{1}{25}$
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{x^{2}}{\left(\frac{12}{5}\right)^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{\left(\frac{9}{5}\right)^{2}}=1
$$
Now, $b^{2}=a^{2}\left(e^{2}-1\right)$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow\left(\frac{9}{5}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{12}{5}\right)^{2}\left(e^{2}-1\right) \\
\Rightarrow\left(\frac{9}{12}\right)^{2}=e^{2}-1 \\
\Rightarrow e^{2}=1+\frac{81}{144}=\frac{144+81}{144} \\
\Rightarrow e=\frac{15}{12}=\frac{5}{4} \\
\text { Foci }=(\pm a e, o)=(\pm 3,0)
\end{array}
$$
Since foci of the given ellipse and hyperbola coincide, therefore
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\sqrt{16-b^{2}}=3 \Rightarrow 16-b^{2}=9 \\
\therefore b^{2}=7
\end{array}
$$

questions.q: The eccentricity of the ellipse whose major axis is three times the minor axis is:

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the curves $\frac{x^2}{a}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1$ and $y^3=16 x$ intersect at right angles, then ' $a$ ' equals to
questions.solution:
For slope, differentiating both curves
$\frac{2 x}{a}+\frac{2 y}{4} y^{\prime}=0 \Rightarrow y^{\prime}=-\frac{4 x}{a y}$......(i)
and
$3 y^2 y^1=16 \Rightarrow y^1=\frac{16}{3 y^2}$.......(ii)
$\because$ Both curves are orthogonal
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore & \frac{-4 x}{a y} \times \frac{16}{3 y^2} & =-1 \\
\Rightarrow & & 64 x & =3 y^3 a \\
\Rightarrow & & a & =\frac{64 x}{3 y^3}=\frac{64 x}{3(16 x)} \\
\Rightarrow & & a & =\frac{4}{3}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The locus of mid points of tangents intercepted between the axes of ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ will be
questions.solution:
Let mid-point of part PQ which is in between the axis is $\mathrm{R}\left(\mathrm{x}_{1}, \mathrm{y}_{1}\right)$, then coordinates of $P$ and $Q$ will be $\left(2 x_{1}, 0\right)$ and $\left(0,2 \mathrm{y}_{1}\right)$, respectively.
$\therefore$ Equation of line $\mathrm{PQ}$ is $\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2 \mathrm{x}_{1}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}}{2 \mathrm{y}_{1}}=1$
$\Rightarrow y=-\left(\frac{y_{1}}{x_{1}}\right) x+2 y_{1}$
If this line touches the ellipse
$\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$
then it will satisfy the condition, $c^{2}=a^{2} m^{2}+b^{2}$

So, $\left(2 y_{1}\right)^{2}=a^{2}\left(\frac{-y_{1}}{x_{1}}\right)^{2}+b^{2}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow 4 y_{1}^{2}=\left\{\frac{a^{2} y_{1}^{2}}{x_{1}^{2}}\right\}+b^{2} \\
\Rightarrow 4=\left(\frac{a^{2}}{x_{1}^{2}}\right)+\left(\frac{b^{2}}{y_{1}^{2}}\right) \Rightarrow\left(\frac{a^{2}}{x_{1}^{2}}\right)+\left(\frac{b^{2}}{y_{1}^{2}}\right)=4
\end{array}$
$\therefore$ Required locus of $\left(\mathrm{x}_{1}, \mathrm{y}_{1}\right)$ is
$\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{b}^{2}}{\mathrm{y}^{2}}\right)=4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let \(\mathrm{A}=\{1,2,3,4,5\}\) and the functions \(\mathrm{f}: \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{A}\) and \(\mathrm{g}: \mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{A}\) be defined by \(\mathrm{f}(1)=3, \mathrm{f}(2)=5, \mathrm{f}(3)=3, \mathrm{f}(4)=1, \mathrm{f}(5)=2 ; \mathrm{g}(1)=4\), \(\mathrm{g}(2)=1, \mathrm{~g}(3)=1, \mathrm{~g}(4)=2, \mathrm{~g}(5)=3\). Then
questions.solution:
We have
\(\begin{aligned}
& (\mathrm{gof})(1)=\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{f}(1))=\mathrm{g}(3)=1 \\
& (\mathrm{fog})(1)=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(1))=\mathrm{f}(4)=1 \\
& (\mathrm{gof})(2)=\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{f}(2))=\mathrm{g}(5)=3 \\
& (\mathrm{fog})(2)=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(2))=\mathrm{f}(1)=3 \\
& (\mathrm{gof})(3)=\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{f}(3))=\mathrm{g}(3)=1 \\
& (\mathrm{fog})(3)=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(3))=\mathrm{f}(1)=3 \\
& (\mathrm{gof})(4)=\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{f}(4))=\mathrm{g}(1)=4 \\
& (\mathrm{fog})(4)=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(4))=\mathrm{f}(2)=5 \\
& (\mathrm{gof})(5)=\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{f}(5))=\mathrm{g}(2)=1 \\
& (\mathrm{fog})(5)=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{g}(5))=\mathrm{f}(3)=3 \\
& \therefore \mathrm{gof}=\{(1,1),(2,3),(3,1),(4,4),(5,1)\} \\
& \text { and fog }=\{(1,1),(2,3),(3,3),(4,5),(5,3)\} .
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\frac{|x+3|+x}{x+2}>1$, then $x \in$
questions.solution:
We have $\frac{|x+3|+x}{x+2}>1$
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{|x+3|+x}{x+2}-1>0 \Rightarrow \frac{|x+3|-2}{x+2}>0
$$
Now, two cases arise :
Case I : When $x+3 \geq 0$, i.e. $x \geq-3$. Then,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{|x+3|-2}{x+2}>0 \Rightarrow \frac{x+3-2}{x+2}>0 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{x+1}{x+2}>0 \\
& \Rightarrow\{(x+1)>0 \text { and } x+2>0\} \\
& \text { or }\{x+1 < 0 \text { and } x+2 < 0\} \\
& \Rightarrow\{x>-1 \text { and } x>-2\} \\
& \text { or }\{x < -1 \text { and } x < -2\} \\
& \Rightarrow x>-1 \text { or } x < -2 \\
& \Rightarrow x \in(-1, \infty) \text { or } x \in(-\infty,-2) \\
& \Rightarrow x \in(-3,-2) \cup(-1, \infty)[\text { Since } x \geq-3]
\end{aligned}
$$

Case II : When $\mathrm{x}+3 < 0$, i.e. $\mathrm{x} < -3$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{|x+3|-2}{x+2}>0 \Rightarrow \frac{-x-3-2}{x+2}>0 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{-(x+5)}{x+2}>0 \Rightarrow \frac{x+5}{x+2} < 0 \\
& \Rightarrow(x+5 < 0 \text { and } x+2>0) \text { or }(x+5>0) \\
& \text { and } x+2 < 0) \\
& \Rightarrow(x < -5 \text { and } x>-2) \text { or }(x>-5 \text { and } x < -2)
\end{aligned}
$$
it is not possible.
$$
\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \in(-5,-2)
$$
Combining (i) and (ii), the required solution is $x \in(-5,-2) \cup(-1, \infty)$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Range of the function \(f(x)=\frac{x^2+x+2}{x^2+x+1} ; x \in R\) is
questions.solution:
We have
\(\begin{aligned}
f(x) & =\frac{x^2+x+2}{x^2+x+1}=\frac{\left(x^2+x+1\right)+1}{x^2+x+1} \\
& =1+\frac{1}{\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+\frac{3}{4}}
\end{aligned}\)
We can see here that as \(x \rightarrow \infty, f(x) \rightarrow 1\) which is the \(\min\) value of \(f(x)\). i.e. \(f_{\min }=1\). Also \(f(x)\) is max when \(\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+\frac{3}{4}\) is min which is so when \(x=-1 / 2\)
i.e. when \(\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+\frac{3}{4}=\frac{3}{4}\).
\(\therefore f_{\max }=1+\frac{1}{3 / 4}=7 / 3 \quad \therefore R_f=(1,7 / 3]\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function \(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}-1}+\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}+1\) is -
options: neither an odd nor an even function
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=\frac{x}{e^x-1}+\frac{x}{2}+1 \\
& =\frac{2 x+x e^x-x}{2\left(e^x-1\right)}+1=\frac{x+x e^x}{2\left(e^x-1\right)}+1 \\
& f(-x)=\frac{-x-x e^x}{2\left(e^x-1\right)}+1=\frac{x+x e^x}{2\left(e^x-1\right)}+1 \\
& \therefore f(-x)=f(x) \text { for all } x \\
& \therefore f(x) \text { is an even function. }
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $f: R \rightarrow R, g: R \rightarrow R$ be two functions such that
$f(x)=2 x-3, g(x)=x^{3}+5$. The function $(f \circ g)^{-1}(x)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, $f(x)=2 x-3, g(x)=x^{3}+5$
$(f \circ g) x=f\left(x^{3}+5\right)=2\left(x^{3}+5\right)-3=2 x^{3}+7$
Let $y=(f \circ g) x=2 x^{3}+7$
$$
\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}-7}{2}\right)^{1 / 3} \Rightarrow(\text { fog })^{-1} \mathrm{x}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}-7}{2}\right)^{1 / 3}
$$

Note that if a continuous function has more than one roots, then the function is always many-one.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The domain and range of the function $f$ given by $f(x)=2-|x-5|$ is
questions.solution:
Given $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=2-|\mathrm{x}-5|$
Domain of $f(x)$ is defined for all real values of $x$.
Since, $|x-5| \geq 0 \Rightarrow-|x-5| \leq 0$
$\Rightarrow 2-|x-5| \leq 2 \Rightarrow f(x) \leq 2$
Hence, range of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is $(-\infty, 2]$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f: R \rightarrow R$ bedefined as $f(x)=x^{2}+1$, find $f^{-1}(-5)$.
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{f}^{-1}(-5)=\mathrm{x}$. Then, $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=-5$
$$
\Rightarrow x^{2}+1=-5 \Rightarrow x^{2}=-6 \Rightarrow x=\pm \sqrt{-6}
$$
which does not belong to $\mathrm{R}$.
$$
\therefore \mathrm{f}^{-1}(-5)=\phi
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The imaginary part of $\frac{(1+\mathrm{i})^{2}}{\mathrm{i}(2 \mathrm{i}-1)}$ is
questions.solution:
$\frac{(1+\mathrm{i})^{2}}{\mathrm{i}(2 \mathrm{i}-1)}=\frac{1+\mathrm{i}^{2}+2 \mathrm{i}}{2 \mathrm{i}^{2}-\mathrm{i}}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & \frac{1-1+2 \mathrm{i}}{-2-\mathrm{i}}=-\frac{2 \mathrm{i}}{2+\mathrm{i}} \times \frac{(2-\mathrm{i})}{(2-\mathrm{i})}=\frac{-4 \mathrm{i}+2 \mathrm{i}^{2}}{4-\mathrm{i}^{2}} \\ &\left[\because \mathrm{i}^{2}=-1\right] \end{aligned}$
$\therefore=\frac{-4 \mathrm{i}-2}{4+1}=\frac{-2-4 \mathrm{i}}{5} \Rightarrow \frac{-2}{5}-\frac{4 \mathrm{i}}{5}$
$\therefore$ The imaginary part $=-\frac{4}{5}$

questions.q: The simultaneous equations $\mathrm{Kx}+2 \mathrm{y}-\mathrm{z}=1$, $(\mathrm{K}-1) \mathrm{y}-2 \mathrm{z}=2$ and $(\mathrm{K}+2) \mathrm{z}=3$ have only one solution when
questions.solution:
For only one solution $|\mathrm{A}| \neq 0$
$\begin{array}{l}
\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\mathrm{k} & 2 & -1 \\
0 & \mathrm{k}-1 & -2 \\
0 & 0 & \mathrm{k}+2
\end{array}\right| \neq 0 \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}(\mathrm{k}-1)(\mathrm{k}+2) \neq 0 \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{k} \neq 0, \mathrm{k} \neq 1, \mathrm{k} \neq-2 . \quad \therefore \mathrm{k}=-1
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The volume of the tetrahedron with vertices $\mathrm{P}(-1,2,0), \mathrm{Q}(2,1,-3), \mathrm{R}(1,0,1)$ and $\mathrm{S}(3,-2,3)$ is
questions.solution:
Given : The vertices of tetrahedron are $\mathrm{P}(-1,2,0), \mathrm{Q}(2,1,-3), \mathrm{R}(1,0,1) \& \mathrm{~S}(3,-2,3)$
$\therefore$ Volume of tetrahedron $=\frac{1}{6}[\overrightarrow{P Q} \overrightarrow{P R} \overrightarrow{P S}]$
Now,
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}=(2+1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(1-2) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(-3) \hat{\mathrm{k}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
Similarly, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PR}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{PS}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
$\therefore$ Volume of tetrahedron
$=\frac{1}{6}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
3 & -1 & -3 \\
2 & -2 & 1 \\
4 & -4 & 3
\end{array}\right|=\frac{2}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{y}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{y}^{2}}$, then the value of $\mathrm{y}$ is
questions.solution:
Given $\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{y}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{y}^{2}}$
$$
\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}-\mathrm{y}=\sqrt{1+\mathrm{y}^{2}}
$$
Squaring both side, we have
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{y}^{2}-2 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{y}=1+\mathrm{y}^{2} \\
\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}-1
\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow y=\frac{e^{2 x}-1}{2 e^{x}} \Rightarrow y=\frac{1}{2}\left[e^{x}-e^{-x}\right]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of surjective functions from $\mathrm{A}$ to $\mathrm{B}$ where $\mathrm{A}=\{1,2,3,4\}$ and $\mathrm{B}=\{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}\}$ is
questions.solution:
If $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are two sets having $\mathrm{m}$ and $\mathrm{n}$ elements such that
$$
1 \leq \mathrm{n} \leq \mathrm{m}=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}(-1)^{\mathrm{n}-\mathrm{r} \mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{r}^{\mathrm{m}}
$$
Number of surjection from A to B
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\sum_{r=1}^{n}(-1)^{2-r}{ }^{2} C_{r}(r)^{4} \\
=(-1)^{2-1}{ }^{2} C_{1}(1)^{4}+(-1)^{2-2}{ }^{2} C_{2}(2)^{4}=-2+16 \\
=14
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $g(x)$ is a polynomial satisfying $\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{g}(\mathrm{y})=\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{y})+\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{xy})-2$ for all real $x$ and $y$ and $g(2)=5$ then $\operatorname{Lt}_{x \rightarrow 3} g(x)$ is
questions.solution:
$g(x) \cdot g(y)=g(x)+g(y)+g(x y)-2$
Put $x=1, y=2$, then
$$
\begin{array}{l}
g(1) \cdot g(2)=g(1)+g(2)+g(2)-2 \\
5 g(1)=g(1)+5+5-2 \\
4 g(1)=8 \quad \therefore g(1)=2
\end{array}
$$
Put $\mathrm{y}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}$ in equation (1), we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot \mathrm{g}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)=\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)+\mathrm{g}(1)-2 \\
\mathrm{~g}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot \mathrm{g}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)=\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})+\mathrm{g}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right)+2-2 \\
{[\because \mathrm{g}(1)=2]}
\end{array}
$$
This is valid only for the polynomial
$$
\therefore \quad \mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})=1 \pm \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}
.....(2)$$
$\operatorname{Now} g(2)=5$
(Given)
$\begin{aligned} \therefore 1 & \pm 2^{n}=5 &\text { [Using equation (2)] }\\ & \pm 2^{\mathrm{n}}=4, \Rightarrow 2^{n}=4,-4 \end{aligned}$
Since the value of $2^{n}$ cannot be-Ve.
So, $2^{n}=4, \Rightarrow n=2$
Now, put $\mathrm{n}=2$ in equation (2), we get $g(x)=1 \pm x^{2}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad \operatorname{Lt}_{x \rightarrow 3} g(x) &=\operatorname{Lt}_{x \rightarrow 3}\left(1 \pm x^{2}\right)=1 \pm(3)^{2} \\
&=1 \pm 9=10,-8
\end{aligned}
$$
