questions.q:
The domain of the function
$f(x)=\frac{1}{\log _{10}(1-x)}+\sqrt{x+2}$ is
questions.solution:
$x+2 \geq 0$, i.e., $x \geq-2$ or $-2 \leq x$ $\log _{10}(1-x) 
eq 0$ $\Rightarrow 1-x 
eq 1 \Rightarrow x 
eq 0$ Again, $1-x>0$ $\Rightarrow 1>x \Rightarrow x < 1$
Combining all the results for values of $x$, we get
$-2 \leq x < 0 	ext { and } 0 < x < 1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation $\lambda x^{2}+4 x y+y^{2}+\lambda x+3 y+2=0$ represents a parabola, if $\lambda$ is
questions.solution:
We have the given equation as
$$
\lambda x^{2}+4 x y+y^{2}+\lambda x+3 y+2=0
$$
On comparing this equation with $a x^{2}+2 h x y+b y^{2}+2 g x+2 f y+c=0$, we get
$$
a=\lambda, h=2, b=1, g=\frac{\lambda}{2}, f=\frac{3}{2}, c=2
$$
Since, given equation represents a parabola
$$
\therefore h^{2}=a b \Rightarrow 4=\lambda \cdot 1 \Rightarrow \lambda=4
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A tower $A B$ leans towards west making an angle $\alpha$ with the vertical. The angular elevation of $B$, the top most point of the tower is $\beta$ as observed from a point $C$ due east of $A$ at a distance ' $d$ ' from $A$. If the angular elevation of $B$ from a point $D$ due east of $C$ at a distance $2 d$ from $C$ is $r$, then $2 \tan \alpha$ can be given as
questions.solution:
By $\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{n}$ theorem at $\mathrm{C}$ $(\mathrm{d}+2 \mathrm{~d}) \cot \beta=\mathrm{d} \cot \gamma-2 \mathrm{~d} \cot \left(90^{\circ}+\alpha\right)$
$\begin{array}{l} 3 d \cot \beta=d \cot \gamma+2 d \tan \alpha \\ \Rightarrow 3 \cot \beta=\cot \gamma+2 \tan \alpha \\ \therefore 2 \tan \alpha=3 \cot \beta-\cot \gamma \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the sum $\sum_{n=1}^{13}\left(i^{n}+i^{n+1}\right)$ where $\mathrm{i}=\sqrt{-1}$ equals
questions.solution:
$\sum_{n=1}^{13}\left(i^{n}+i^{n+1}\right)=\sum_{n=1}^{13} i^{n}+\sum_{n=1}^{13} i^{n+1}$
$=\mathrm{i}\left(\frac{1-\mathrm{i}^{13}}{1-\mathrm{i}}\right)+\mathrm{i}^{2}\left(\frac{1-\mathrm{i}^{13}}{1-\mathrm{i}}\right)$
$=\mathrm{i} \frac{(1-\mathrm{i})}{(1-\mathrm{i})}-\frac{(1-\mathrm{i})}{(1-\mathrm{i})}=\mathrm{i}-1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The angle of depressions of the top and the foot of a chimney as seen from the top of a second chimney, which is $150 \mathrm{~m}$ high and standing on the same level as the first are $\theta$ and $\phi$ respectively, then the distance between their tops when $\tan \theta=\frac{4}{3}$ and $\tan \phi=\frac{5}{2}$ is
questions.solution:
Given : $\tan \theta=\frac{4}{3}$ and $\tan \phi=\frac{5}{2}$
$\operatorname{In} \Delta \mathrm{ABE}$ $\tan \phi=\frac{150}{\mathrm{~d}}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{d}=150 \cot \phi$ $=150 \times \frac{2}{5}=60 \mathrm{~m}$ In $\triangle \mathrm{DCE}$ $\tan \theta=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{d}}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{4}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{d}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=\frac{4}{3} \times 60 \Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=80 \mathrm{~m}$ Now in $\Delta \mathrm{DCE}$ $\mathrm{DE}^{2}=\mathrm{DC}^{2}+\mathrm{CE}^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{2}=60^{2}+80^{2}=10000$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=100 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: \(A \operatorname{rod~} \mathrm{AB~} 13 \mathrm{ft}\) long moves with its ends \(\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}\) on two perpendicular lines \(\mathrm{OX}\) and \(\mathrm{OY}\) respectively. If the end \(\mathrm{A}\) is \(12 \mathrm{ft}\) from \(\mathrm{O}\) and is slipping away at \(2 \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{ft} / \mathrm{sec}\). then the end \(B\) is moving at
questions.solution:
At time t, let \(\mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{x}\) and \(\mathrm{OB}=\mathrm{y}\). Then \(\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{ft} / \mathrm{sec}\).
and we have to find \(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}}\). From right angled \(\triangle \mathrm{AOB}\), we have \(\mathrm{AB}^2=\mathrm{OA}^2+\mathrm{OB}^2\) \(13^2=\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{y}^2\) ...(1) Differentiating with respect to \(t\), we get \(\therefore \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}+\mathrm{y} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}}=0\) ...(2) When \(\mathrm{OA}=12\), i.e. \(\mathrm{x}=12\) then from (1), \(13^2=12^2+y^2\) or \(\mathrm{y}=\sqrt{13^2-12^2}=5\). Putting the values of \(y\) and \(\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}\) in (2), we get \(12 \times \frac{5}{2}+5 \times \frac{d y}{d t}=0\) or \(30+5 \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}}=0 ; \quad \therefore \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-6 \mathrm{ft} / \mathrm{sec}\). Hence the end B is moving at the rate of \(6 \mathrm{ft} / \mathrm{sec}\). The negative sign shows the downward tendency of B i.e. y decreases when \(t\) increases)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{H}$ is the harmonic mean between $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{Q}$, then the value of $\frac{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{P}}+\frac{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{Q}}$ is
questions.solution:
Given : $\mathrm{H}$ is the harmonic mean between $\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{Q}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \mathrm{H}=\frac{2 \mathrm{PQ}}{\mathrm{P}+\mathrm{Q}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{H}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}+\mathrm{Q}}{2 \mathrm{PQ}} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{2}{\mathrm{H}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{Q}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{P}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{P}}+\frac{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{Q}}=2
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the chord of the hyperbola $25 x^{2}-16 y^{2}=400$ that is bisected at point $(5,3)$ is:
questions.solution:
The equation of the chord, having mid-point as $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$, of the hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$ is given by 
$T=S_{1} ......(i)$
where, $T=\frac{x x_{1}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y y_{1}}{b^{2}}-1$ and
$$
S_{1}=\frac{x_{1}^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y_{1}^{2}}{b^{2}}-1
$$
According to the question,
$\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)=(5,3)$ and $a^{2}=16, b^{2}=25$ as $25 x^{2}-16 y^{2}=400$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \frac{x^{2}}{16}-\frac{y^{2}}{25}=1 \therefore \frac{5 x}{16}-\frac{3 y}{25}=\frac{25}{16}-\frac{9}{25}
\end{array}
$$
[Using (i)]
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \quad 125 x-48 y=625-144 \Rightarrow 125 x-48 y=481
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the hyperbola with vertices $(3,0),(-3,0)$ and semi-latus rectum 4 is given by :
questions.solution:
We have $a=3$ and $\frac{b^{2}}{a}=4 \Rightarrow b^{2}=12$
Hence, the equation of the hyperbola is
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{x^{2}}{9}-\frac{y^{2}}{12}=1 \\
\Rightarrow 4 x^{2}-3 y^{2}=36 \Rightarrow 4 x^{2}-3 y^{2}-36=0
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the eccentricity of the hyperbola $x^{2}-y^{2} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \alpha=25$ is $\sqrt{5}$ times the eccentricity of the ellipse $x^{2} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \alpha+y^{2}=5$ then $\alpha$ is equal to :

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the rectangular hyperbola is $x^{2}-y^{2}=64$. Then, which of the following is not correct?
options: The length of latus rectum is 16
The asymptotes are parallel to each other
questions.solution:
Given equation of rectangular hyperbola is $x^{2}-y^{2}=8^{2}$
Length of latusrectum $=2 \times(8)=16$ and eccentricity $=\sqrt{2}$
The asymptotes are perpendicular lines.
So, $x \pm y=0$
Now, directrices are
$x=\pm \frac{8}{\sqrt{2}}=\pm 4 \sqrt{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the eccentricity of the hyperbola \(x^2-y^2 \sec ^2 \theta=4\) is \(\sqrt{3}\) times the eccentricity of the ellipse \(x^2 \sec ^2 \theta+y^2=16\), then the value of \(\theta\) equals
questions.solution:
Given: \(x^2-y^2 \sec ^2 \theta\)
\(=4\) and \(x^2-\sec ^2 \theta+y^2=16\)
\(\Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{4}-\frac{y^2}{4 \cos ^2 \theta}=1\)
and \(\frac{x^2}{16 \cos ^2 \theta}+\frac{y^2}{16}=1\)
According to problem
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{4+4 \cos ^2 \theta}{4}=3\left(\frac{16-16 \cos ^2 \theta}{16}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow 1+\cos ^2 \theta=3\left(1-\cos ^2 \theta\right) \Rightarrow 4 \cos ^2 \theta=2 \\
& \Rightarrow \cos \theta= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $e$ is the eccentricity of hyperbola $\frac{x^2}{a^2}-\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ and $\theta$ is the angle between the asymptotes, then $\cos \frac{\theta}{2}$ is
questions.solution:
$\because \theta$ is the angle of asymptotes.
Then, $\tan \theta=\left|\frac{\frac{b}{a}+\frac{b}{a}}{1-\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)^2}\right|$
$\Rightarrow \quad \tan \frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{b}{a}$
Therefore, $\cos \frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{a}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}=\frac{a}{\sqrt{a^2\left(1+\frac{b^2}{a^2}\right)}}$
$\begin{aligned}
\because & e & =\sqrt{1+\frac{b^2}{a^2}} \\
\Rightarrow & \cos \frac{\theta}{2} & =\frac{a}{\sqrt{a^2 e^2}}=\frac{a}{a e}=\frac{1}{e}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the line $l x+m y-n=0$ will be a normal to the hyperbola, then $\frac{a^{2}}{l^{2}}-\frac{b^{2}}{m^{2}}=\frac{\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)^{2}}{k}$, where $k$ is equal to
questions.solution:
The equation of any normal
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { to } \frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1 \text { is } a x \cos \phi+b y \cot \phi \\
&=a^{2}+b^{2} \\
\Rightarrow & a x \cos \phi+b y \cot \phi-\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)=0 \ldots \ldots \text { (i) }
\end{aligned}$
The straight line $l x+m y-n=0$ will be normal to the hyperbola
$\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$, then eq. (i) and $l x+m y-n=$ 0 represent the same line,
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \frac{a \cos \phi}{l}=\frac{b \cot \phi}{m}=\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}}{n} \\
\Rightarrow \sec \phi=\frac{n a}{l\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)} \\
\text { and } \tan \phi=\frac{n b}{m\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)} \\
\frac{n^{2} a^{2}}{l^{2}\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)^{2}}-\frac{n^{2} b^{2}}{m^{2}\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)^{2}}=1 \\
{\left[\because \sec ^{2} \phi-\tan ^{2} \phi=1\right]}
\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{a^{2}}{l^{2}}-\frac{b^{2}}{m^{2}}=\frac{\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)^{2}}{n^{2}}$
But given equation of normal is
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{a^{2}}{l^{2}}-\frac{b^{2}}{m^{2}}=\frac{\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right)^{2}}{k} \\
\therefore k=n^{2}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line $2 x+\sqrt{6} y=2$ is a tangent to the curve $x^{2}-2 y^{2}=4$. The point of contact is
questions.solution:
Solving the equation of line and curve, we get
$\begin{array}{l}
x^{2}-2\left\{\frac{2-2 x}{\sqrt{6}}\right\}^{2}=4 \\
\Rightarrow x^{2}-\frac{1}{3} \times 4\left(1+x^{2}-2 x\right)=4 \\
\Rightarrow 3 x^{2}-4-4 x^{2}+8 x=12 \\
\Rightarrow x^{2}-8 x+16=0 \\
\Rightarrow(x-4)^{2}=0 \Rightarrow x=4
\end{array}$
and $\sqrt{6} \cdot y=2-2(4)=-6$
$\Rightarrow y=-\sqrt{6}$
$\therefore$ Point of contact is $(4,-\sqrt{6})$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The mid point of the chord $4 x-3 y=5$ of the hyperbola $2 x^{2}-3 y^{2}=12$ is
questions.solution:
Solving $4 x-3 y=5$ and $2 x^{2}-3 y^{2}=12$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore 2\left(\frac{5+3 y}{4}\right)^{2}-3 y^{2}=12 \\
\Rightarrow \frac{\left(25+9 y^{2}+30 y\right)}{8}-3 y^{2}=12 \\
\Rightarrow 15 y^{2}-30 y+71=0 \\
\Rightarrow y=\frac{30 \pm \sqrt{900-4260}}{30}=1 \pm \frac{\sqrt{-3360}}{30}
\end{array}
$$
Also, $2 x^{2}-3\left(\frac{4 x-5}{3}\right)^{2}=12$
$$
\Rightarrow 10 x^{2}-40 x+61=0
$$
$$
\Rightarrow x=\frac{40 \pm \sqrt{1600-4 \times 10 \times 61}}{2 \times 10}
$$
$=\frac{40 \pm \sqrt{-840}}{20}=2 \pm \frac{\sqrt{-840}}{20}$
$\therefore$ Points are $A\left(2+\frac{\sqrt{-840}}{20}, 1+\frac{\sqrt{-3360}}{30}\right)$
and $B\left(2-\frac{\sqrt{-840}}{20}, 1-\frac{\sqrt{-3360}}{30}\right)$.
$\therefore$ Mid point of $A B$ is $(2,1)$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $P Q$ is a double ordinate of hyperbola $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}-\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1 .$ Such that $O P Q$ is an equilateral triangle, $O$ being the centre of the hyperbola, then the eccentricity ' $e$ ' of the hyperbola satisfies
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{a} \sec \theta, \mathrm{b} \tan \theta), \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{a} \sec \theta,-\mathrm{b} \tan \theta)$ be end points of double ordinates and $(0,0)$ is the centre of the hyperbola. So, $\mathrm{PQ}=2 \mathrm{~b} \tan \theta$
$\mathrm{OQ}=\mathrm{OP}=\sqrt{\mathrm{a}^{2} \sec ^{2} \theta+\mathrm{b}^{2} \tan ^{2} \theta}$ Since, $\mathrm{OQ}=\mathrm{OP}=\mathrm{PQ}$ $\therefore 4 b^{2} \tan ^{2} \theta=a^{2} \sec ^{2} \theta+b^{2} \tan ^{2} \theta$ $\Rightarrow 3 b^{2} \tan ^{2} \theta=a^{2} \sec ^{2} \theta$ $\Rightarrow 3 b^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta=a^{2}$ $\Rightarrow 3 a^{2}\left(e^{2}-1\right) \sin ^{2} \theta=a^{2}$ $\Rightarrow 3\left(e^{2}-1\right) \sin ^{2} \theta=1$ $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3\left(e^{2}-1\right)}=\sin ^{2} \theta < 1,\left(\because \sin ^{2} \theta < 1\right)$ $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{e^{2}-1} < 3 \Rightarrow e^{2}-1>\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow e^{2}>\frac{4}{3}$ $\therefore e>\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ be three unit vectors such that $\vec{a} \times(\vec{b} \times \vec{c})=\frac{1}{2} \vec{b}, \vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$ being non-parallel. If $\theta_{1}$ is the angle between $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ and $\theta_{2}$ is the angle between $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{c}$, then
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})=\frac{1}{2} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \\
(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}-(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{2} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \quad \text { [Vector triple product] } \\
\left(|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}| \cos \theta_{2}\right) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}-\left(|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}| \cos \theta_{1}\right) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{2} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \\
\quad[\because|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}||\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|=1]
\end{array}$
Equating the coefficients of $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ on both sides, we get
$\begin{array}{l}
\cos \theta_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \text { and }-\cos \theta_{1}=0 \\
\Rightarrow \cos \theta_{2}=\cos \frac{\pi}{3} \text { and } \cos \theta_{1}=\frac{\pi}{2} \\
\Rightarrow \theta_{2}=\frac{\pi}{3} \text { and } \theta_{1}=\frac{\pi}{2}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{dy}=\left[\mathrm{y} \sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)-\mathrm{x}\right] \mathrm{dx}$ and $y(1)=\frac{\pi}{2}$, then the value of $\cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given : $x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right) d y=\left[y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x\right] d x$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)-x}{x \sin \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)}$
Put $\frac{y}{x}=z \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=z \cdot 1+x \cdot \frac{d z}{d x}$
$\therefore x \cdot \frac{d z}{d x}+z=\frac{z x \sin z-x}{x \sin z}=z-\operatorname{cosec} z$
$\Rightarrow x \frac{d z}{d x}=-\operatorname{cosec} z \Rightarrow-\sin z d z=\frac{d x}{x}$
$\Rightarrow \cos z=\log x+c$
$\Rightarrow \cos \left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\log x+c$
But $y(1)=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow x=1, y=\frac{\pi}{2}$
$\therefore \cos \frac{\pi}{2}=\log (1)+\mathrm{c} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=0$
$\therefore \cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)=\log \mathrm{x}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The differential equation of the system of all circles of radius $r$ in the $X Y$ plane is
questions.solution:
The equation of the family of circles of radius $r$ is
$(x-a)^{2}+(y-b)^{2}=r^{2}$ ...(1)
Where a \& b are arbitrary constants.
Since equation (1) contains two arbitrary constants, we differentiate it two times w.r.t $x \&$ the differential equation will be of second order.
Differentiating (1) w.r.t. $x$, we get
$\begin{array}{l}
2(x-a)+2(y-b) \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\
\Rightarrow(x-a)+(y-b) \frac{d y}{d x}=0 ...(2)
\end{array}$
Differentiating $(2)$ w.r.t. $x$, we get
$\begin{array}{l}
1+(y-b) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}=0 ...(3) \\\
\Rightarrow(y-b)=-\frac{1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}}{\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}} ...(4)
\end{array}$
On putting the value of $(y-b)$ in equation (2), we get
$x-a=\frac{\left[1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right] \frac{d y}{d x}}{\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}}$ ...(5)
Substituting the values of $(x-a) \&(x-b)$ in (1), we get
$\begin{array}{l}
{\left[1+\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)^{2}\right]^{2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)^{2}} \\
\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}\right)^{2}+\frac{\left[1+\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)^{2}\right]^{2}}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}\right)^{2}}=\mathrm{r}^{2} \\
\Rightarrow\left[1+\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)^{2}\right]^{3}=\mathrm{r}^{2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}\right)^{2}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral $\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log (\tan x) \mathrm{d} x=$
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log (\tan \mathrm{x}) \mathrm{dx}$ ...(1)
Then, $I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)\right] d x$
$\begin{aligned}
\left[\because \int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x\right] \\
\Rightarrow I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log (\cot x) d x \\
\Rightarrow I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \left(\frac{1}{\tan x}\right) \mathrm{dx}
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{l}\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log (\tan \mathrm{x})^{-1} \mathrm{~d} \mathrm{x}= & -\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log (\tan \mathrm{x}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=-\mathrm{I} \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=0 & \\ & {[\text { Using eq. (1)] }}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: \(\mathrm{P}\) is a variable point on the hyperbola \(\frac{x^2}{a^2}-\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1\) whose vertex is \(A(a, 0)\) The locus of the middle point of AP is
questions.solution:
Let \(\left(\mathrm{x}_1, \mathrm{y}_1\right)\) be the mid point of the variable chord AP (where A is fixed while Pvaries) of the hyperbola \(\frac{x^2}{a^2}-\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1\)
Equation of the chord having \(\left(\mathrm{x}_1, \mathrm{y}_1\right)\) as its mid points is
\(\frac{\mathrm{xx}_1}{\mathrm{a}^2}-\frac{\mathrm{yy}_1}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_1^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}-\frac{\mathrm{y}_1^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2} \quad\left(\mathrm{~T}=\mathrm{S}_1\right)\)
As it passes through the fixed point \(A(a, 0)\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \frac{\mathrm{ax}_1}{\mathrm{a}^2}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_1^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}-\frac{\mathrm{y}_1^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2} \text { or } \frac{4 \mathrm{x}_1^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}-\frac{4 \mathrm{x}_1}{\mathrm{a}}-\frac{4 \mathrm{y}_1^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{4 \mathrm{x}_1^2-4 \mathrm{ax}_1+\mathrm{a}^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}-\frac{4 \mathrm{y}_1^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=1 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{\left(2 \mathrm{x}_1-\mathrm{a}\right)^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}-\frac{4 \mathrm{y}_1^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=1
\end{aligned}\)
Hence locus of \(\left(x_1, y_1\right)\) is \(\frac{(2 x-a)^2}{a^2}-\frac{4 y^2}{b^2}=1\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The combined equation of the asymptotes of the hyperbola $2 x^{2}+5 x y+2 y^{2}+4 x+5 y=0$ is $-$
questions.solution:
Let the equation of asymptotes be $2 x^{2}+5 x y+2 y^{2}+4 x+5 y+\lambda=0 \ldots \ldots .(1)$
This equation represents a pair of straight lines,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore a b c+2 f g h-a f^{2}-b g^{2}-c h^{2}=0 \\
\therefore 4 \lambda+25-\frac{25}{2}-8-\lambda \times \frac{25}{4}=0 \\
\Rightarrow-\frac{9 \lambda}{4}+\frac{9}{2}=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=2
\end{array}
$$
Putting the value of $\lambda$ in eq. (1), we get $2 x^{2}+5 x y+2 y^{2}+4 x+5 y+2=0$ this is the equation of the asymptotes.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Equation $\frac{1}{r}=\frac{1}{8}+\frac{3}{8} \cos \theta$ represents
questions.solution:
Given, equation is
$$
\frac{1}{r}=\frac{1}{8}+\frac{3}{8} \cos \theta \text { or } \frac{8}{\mathrm{r}}=1+3 \cos \theta
$$
which is the form of $\frac{l}{r}=1+e \cos \theta$
$$
\because \quad e=3>0
$$
$\therefore \quad$ Given equation is a hyperbola.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(e_1\) is the eccentricity of the ellipse \(\frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{25}=1\) and \(e_2\) is the eccentricity of the hyperbola passing through the foci of the ellipse and \(e_1 e_2=1\), then equation of the hyperbola is :
questions.solution:
The eccentricity of \(\frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{25}=1\) is
\(\begin{aligned}
e_1 & =\sqrt{1-\frac{16}{25}}=\frac{3}{5} \\
\therefore e_2 & =\frac{5}{3}\left(\because e_1 e_2=1\right)
\end{aligned}\)
\(\Rightarrow\) foci of ellipse \(=(0, \pm 3)\)
\(\Rightarrow\) Equation of hyperbola is
\(\frac{x^2}{16}-\frac{y^2}{9}=-1\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Which of the following is INCORRECT for the hyperbola $x^{2}-2 y^{2}-2 x+8 y-1=0$
options: Length of the transverse axis is $2 \sqrt{3}$
Length of the conjugate axis is $2 \sqrt{6}$
questions.solution:
The equation of the hyperbola is
$x^{2}-2 y^{2}-2 x+8 y-1=0$ or $\quad(x-1)^{2}-2(y-2)^{2}+6=0$
or $\frac{(x-1)^{2}}{-6}+\frac{(y-2)^{2}}{3}=1$
or $\frac{(y-2)^{2}}{3}-\frac{(x-1)^{2}}{6}=1$
or $\quad \frac{Y^{2}}{3}-\frac{X^{2}}{6}=1$
where $X=x-1$ and $Y=y-2$
$\therefore$ The centre $=(0,0)$ in the $\mathrm{X}$ - $\mathrm{Y}$ co-ordinates.
$\therefore$ The centre $=(1,2)$ in the $\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}$ co-ordinates, using $(2)$.
If the transverse axis be of length $2 \mathrm{a}$, then a $=\sqrt{3}$, since in the equation (1) the transverse axis is parallel to the y-axis. If the conjugate axis is of length $2 \mathrm{~b}$, then $b=\sqrt{6}$
But $b^{2}=a^{2}\left(e^{2}-1\right)$
$\therefore 6=3\left(\mathrm{e}^{2}-1\right), \quad \therefore \mathrm{e}^{2}=3$ or $\quad \mathrm{e}=\sqrt{3}$
The length of the transverse axis $=2 \sqrt{3}$.
The length of the conjugate axis $=2 \sqrt{6}$.
Latus rectum $=\frac{2 \mathrm{~b}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{2 \times 6}{\sqrt{3}}=4 \sqrt{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{F}$ is function such that $\mathrm{F}(0)=2, \mathrm{~F}(1)=3$, $\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{x}+2)=2 \mathrm{~F}(\mathrm{x})-\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{x}+1)$ for $\mathrm{x} \geq 0$, then $\mathrm{F}(5)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$F(x+2)=2 F(x)-F(x+1)$...(i)
Putting $x=0$, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
F(2)=2 F(0)-F(1) \\
\Rightarrow F(2)=2(2)-3 \\
\quad\{\because F(0)=2, F(1)=3\}
\end{array}
$$
$$
\Rightarrow F(2)=4-3 \Rightarrow F(2)=1
$$
Putting $x=1$, in eq. (i), we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
F(3) &=2 F(1)-F(2) \\
&=2(3)-1 \quad\{\because F(1)=3, F(2)=1\} \\
\Rightarrow F(3)=5 &
\end{aligned}
$$
Putting $x=2$, in eq. (i), we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
F(4)=2 F(2)-F(3) \\
\quad=2(1)-5 \quad\{\because F(2)=1, F(3)=5\} \\
\Rightarrow F(4)=-3
\end{array}
$$
Putting $x=3$, in eq. (i), we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
F(5)=2 F(3)-F(4) \\
\quad=2(5)+3 \quad\{: F(3)=5, F(4)=-3\} \\
\Rightarrow F(5)=13
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the circle $x^{2}+y^{2}=a^{2}$ intersects the hyperbola $x y=c^{2}$ in four points $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)$ for $i=1,2,3$ and 4, then $y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}+y_{4}$ equals
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
x^{2} y^{2}=c^{4} \\
\Rightarrow y^{2}\left(a^{2}-y^{2}\right)=c^{4} \\
\Rightarrow y^{4}-a^{2} y^{2}+c^{4}=0
\end{array}
$$
Let $y_{1}, y_{2}, y_{3}$ and $y_{4}$ are the roots.
$$
\therefore y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}+y_{4}=0
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of tangents to the hyperbola $3 x^{2}-2 y^{2}=6$, which is perpendicular to the line $x-3 y=3$, are
questions.solution:
Equation of hyperbola is
$\begin{aligned}
& 3 x^{2}-2 y^{2}=6 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{x^{2}}{2}-\frac{y^{2}}{3}=1
\end{aligned}$
So, $a^{2}=2$ and $b^{2}=3$
Given, equation of line is $x-3 y=3$.
$\therefore \quad$ Slope of given line $=\frac{1}{3}$
$\therefore \quad$ Slope of line perpendicular to given line, $\mathrm{m}=-3$
The equation of tangents are
$\begin{aligned}
y &=m x \pm \sqrt{a^{2} m^{2}-b^{2}} \\
&=-3 x \pm \sqrt{2 \times 9-3} \\
&=-3 x \pm \sqrt{18-3} \\
&=-3 x \pm \sqrt{15}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The integral \(\int \frac{\sin ^2 x}{\cos ^4 x} d x\) is
options: a polynomial of degree 5 in \(\sin x\)
a polynomial of degree 4 in \(\tan x\)
a polynomial of degree 3 in \(\tan x\)
a polynomial of degree 5 in \(\cos x\)
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text {Let } I_{2,4}=\int \frac{\sin ^2 x}{\cos ^4 x} d x=\frac{\sin x}{3 \cdot \cos ^3 x}-\frac{1}{3} I_{0,2} \\
& =\frac{\sin x}{3 \cos ^3 x}-\frac{1}{3} \tan x+c \\
& =\frac{1}{3} \tan x\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right)-\frac{1}{3} \tan x+c \\
& =\frac{1}{3} \tan ^3 x+c
\end{aligned}\)
ALTERNATE :
\(\begin{aligned}
& \int \frac{\sin ^2 x}{\cos ^4 x} d x=\int \tan ^2 x \cdot \sec ^2 x d x \\
& \text { put } \tan x=t \\
& =\int t^2 \cdot d t=\frac{1}{3} t^3+c=\frac{1}{3} \tan ^3 x+c
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\int \frac{x^{1 / 2}}{\sqrt{1-x^3}} d x=\frac{2}{3} \operatorname{gof}(x)+C$, then
questions.solution:
Put $\mathrm{x}^{3 / 2}=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2} \mathrm{x}^{1 / 2} \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{dt}$
$\therefore$ integral is
$$
\int \frac{\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{dt}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{t}^2}}=\frac{2}{3} \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{C}=\frac{2}{3} \sin ^{-1}\left(\mathrm{x}^{3 / 2}\right)+\mathrm{C}
$$

questions.q: $\int\left(27 \mathrm{e}^{9 \mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{e}^{12 \mathrm{x}}\right)^{1 / 3} \mathrm{dx}$ is equal to

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int \frac{1}{x^{2}\left(x^{4}+1\right)^{3 / 4}} d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}\left(x^{4}+1\right)^{3 / 4}}=\int \frac{d x}{x^{5}\left(1+\frac{1}{x^{4}}\right)^{3 / 4}}$
Put $1+\frac{1}{x^{4}}=t \Rightarrow-\frac{4}{x^{5}} d x=d t$
So, integral is
$$
\mathrm{I}=-\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}^{3 / 4}}=-\mathrm{t}^{\frac{1}{4}}+\mathrm{c}=-\left(1+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{4}}\right)^{1 / 4}+\mathrm{c}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{n}}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \tan ^{\mathrm{n}} \theta \mathrm{d} \theta$ then $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{n}}+\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{n}-2}$ is :
questions.solution:
Given: $u_{n}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \tan ^{n} \theta d \theta$
$\begin{array}{l}
=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \tan ^{2} \theta \tan ^{n-2} \theta d \theta \\
=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left(\sec ^{2} \theta-1\right) \tan ^{n-2} \theta d \theta
\end{array}$
${=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \sec ^{2} \theta \tan ^{n-2} \theta d \theta}$
$-\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \tan ^{n-2} \theta d \theta$
$=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \sec ^{2} \theta \tan ^{n-2} \theta d \theta-u_{n-2}$
$\Rightarrow u_{n}+u_{n-2}=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \sec ^{2} \theta \tan ^{\mathrm{n}-2} \theta \mathrm{d} \theta$
$=\left.\frac{\tan ^{\mathrm{n}-1} \theta}{\mathrm{n}-1}\right|_{0} ^{\pi / 4}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}-1}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int \frac{d x}{\cos x+\sqrt{3} \sin x}$ equals
questions.solution:
$\int \frac{d x}{\cos x+\sqrt{3} \sin x}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\frac{1}{2} \cos x+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sin x}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\cos \frac{\pi}{3} \cos x+\sin \frac{\pi}{3} \sin x}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\cos \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \int \sec \left(x-\frac{\pi}{3}\right) d x$
$=\frac{1}{2} \log \tan \left(\frac{x}{2}-\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+C$
$=\frac{1}{2} \log \tan \left(\frac{x}{2}+\frac{\pi}{12}\right)+C$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\frac{d}{d x}\left[a \tan ^{-1} x+b \log \left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)\right]=\frac{1}{x^{4}-1}$ $\Rightarrow a-2 b$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\frac{d}{d x}\left[a \tan ^{-1} x+b \log \left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)\right]=\frac{1}{x^{4}-1}$
On integrating both sides, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
a \tan ^{-1} x+b \log \left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right) \\
\quad=\frac{1}{2} \int\left[\frac{1}{x^{2}-1}-\frac{1}{x^{2}+1}\right] d x \\
\Rightarrow a \tan ^{-1} x+b \log \left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right) \\
\quad=\frac{1}{4} \log \left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} x \\
\Rightarrow a=-\frac{1}{2}, b=\frac{1}{4} \\
\therefore a-2 b=-\frac{1}{2}-2\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)=-1
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Evaluate $\int \frac{x^{2}+4}{x^{4}+16} \mathrm{dx}$.
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}+4}{\mathrm{x}^{4}+16} \mathrm{dx}$ $=\int \frac{1+\frac{4}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}+\frac{16}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}} \mathrm{dx}=\int \frac{1+\frac{4}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}}{\left(\mathrm{x}-\frac{4}{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{2}+8} \mathrm{dx}$
Putting $x-\frac{4}{x}=t$
So that $\left(1+\frac{4}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right) \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{dt}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}^{2}+(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}} \\
=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{t}}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right)+\mathrm{C} \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}-\frac{4}{\mathrm{x}}}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right)+\mathrm{C} \\
=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}-4}{2 \mathrm{x} \sqrt{2}}\right)+\mathrm{C}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int\left(\frac{2-\sin 2 x}{1-\cos 2 x}\right) e^{x} d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned}
 let I &=\int\left(\frac{2-\sin 2 x}{1-\cos 2 x}\right) e^{x} d x \\
&=\int\left(\frac{2-2 \sin x \cos x}{2 \sin ^{2} x}\right) e^{x} d x \\
&=\int \operatorname{cosec}^{2} x e^{x} d x-\int \cot x e^{x} d x \\
&=-\cot x e^{x}-\int(-\cot x) e^{x} d x \\
&-\int \cot x e^{x} d x+c \\
&=-\cot x e^{x}+c
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\int \frac{3 x+1}{(x-3)(x-5)} d x$ $=\int \frac{-5}{(x-3)} d x+\int \frac{B}{(x-5)} d x$, then the value of $B$ is
questions.solution:
We have,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{3 x+1}{(x-3)(x-5)}=\frac{-5}{x-3}+\frac{B}{x-5} \\
3 x+1=-5(x-5)+B(x-3) \\
\text { Put } x=5 \\
3(5)+1=B(5-3) \\
16=2 B \text { or } B=8
\end{array}
$$

questions.q: A box contains 9 tickets numbered 1 to 9 inclusive. If 3 tickets are drawn from the box one at a time, the probability that they are alternatively either \{odd, even, odd $\}$ or \{even, odd, even \} is
questions.solution:
No. of tickets $=9$
No. of odd numbered tickets $=5$
No. of even numbered tickets $=4$
Required probability $=P$ odd, even, odd $\}$ + P(even, odd, even)
$=\frac{{ }^{5} \mathrm{C}_{1}}{{ }^{9} \mathrm{C}_{1}} \times \frac{{ }^{4} \mathrm{C}_{1}}{{ }^{8} \mathrm{C}_{1}} \times \frac{{ }^{4} \mathrm{C}_{1}}{{ }^{7} \mathrm{C}_{1}}+\frac{{ }^{4} \mathrm{C}_{1}}{{ }^{9} \mathrm{C}_{1}} \times \frac{{ }^{5} \mathrm{C}_{1}}{{ }^{8} \mathrm{C}_{1}} \times \frac{{ }^{3} \mathrm{C}_{1}}{{ }^{7} \mathrm{C}_{1}}$
$=\frac{5}{9} \times \frac{4}{8} \times \frac{4}{7}+\frac{4}{9} \times \frac{5}{8} \times \frac{3}{7}=\frac{5}{18}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If
\(\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \alpha}{1+\alpha^2}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \beta}{1+\beta^2}\right)=2 \tan ^{-1} x \text {, }\)
then \(\mathrm{x}=\)
questions.solution:
\(\begin{array}{r}
\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \alpha}{1+\alpha^2}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \beta}{1+\beta^2}\right)=2 \tan ^{-1} x \\
\Rightarrow \quad 2 \tan ^{-1} \alpha+2 \tan ^{-1} \beta=2 \tan ^{-1} x \\
\quad\left[\because 2 \tan ^{-1} x=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right)\right] \\
\Rightarrow \quad \tan ^{-1} \alpha+\tan ^{-1} \beta=2 \tan ^{-1} x \\
{\left[\because 2 \tan ^{-1} x=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right)\right]}
\end{array}\)
\(\begin{aligned} & \tan ^{-1} \alpha+\tan ^{-1} \beta=\tan ^{-1} x \\ & \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{1-\alpha \beta}\right)=\tan ^{-1} x \\ & \quad\left[\because \tan ^{-1} x+\tan ^{-1} y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+y}{1-x y}\right)\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{\alpha+\beta}{1-\alpha \beta}\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{2}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{3}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{7}{8}$ is
questions.solution:
$\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{2}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{3}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{7}{8}$
$=\left[\frac{\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{7}{8}-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{7}{8}}{1-\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{7}{8}-\frac{7}{8} \times \frac{1}{2}}\right]$
$\left[\because \tan ^{-1} x+\tan ^{-1} y+\tan ^{-1} z\right.$
$\left.\quad=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+y+z-x y z}{1-x y-y z-z x}\right)\right]$
$=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{41}{24}-\frac{7}{48}}{1-\frac{1}{6}-\frac{7}{24}-\frac{7}{16}}\right]$
$=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{75}{48}}{1-\frac{43}{48}}\right]=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{75}{48-43}\right)$
$=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{75}{5}\right]=\tan ^{-1} 15$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The Value of
$\sin ^{-1}\left\{\cot \left(\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{2-\sqrt{3}}{4}}+\cos ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{12}}{4}+\sec ^{-1} \sqrt{2}\right)\right\}$
is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \sin ^{-1}\left\{\cot \binom{\left.\left.\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{2-\sqrt{3}}{4}}+\cos ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{12}}{4}\right)\right\}}{+\sec ^{-1} \sqrt{2}}\right\} \\ & =\sin ^{-1}\left\{\cot \binom{\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{4-2 \sqrt{3}}{8}}+\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)}{+\sec ^{-1}(\sqrt{2})}\right\}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\sin ^{-1}\left\{\cot \left(\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{(\sqrt{3}-1)^2}{8}}+\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right\} \\ & =\sin ^{-1}\left\{\cot \left(\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}-1}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right)+\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right\} \\ & \begin{aligned} \because \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}-1}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right) & =\frac{\pi}{12} \\ & =\sin ^{-1}\left\{\cot \left(\frac{\pi}{12}+\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right\} \\ & =\sin ^{-1}\left\{\cot \left(\frac{\pi+2 \pi+3 \pi}{12}\right)\right\} \\ & =\sin ^{-1}\left\{\cot \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right\} \\ & =\sin ^{-1}(0)=0\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\cos \left(\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{8}\right)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{8}=\theta$, where $0 < \theta < \frac{\pi}{2}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{8}=\frac{1}{2} \theta \\
\Rightarrow \cos \left(\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{8}\right)=\cos \frac{1}{2} \theta \\
\text { Now, } \cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{8}=\theta \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{1}{8} \\
\Rightarrow 2 \cos ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}-1=\frac{1}{8}
\end{array}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \cos ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}=& \frac{9}{16} \Rightarrow \cos \frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{3}{4} \\ &\left[\because 0 < \frac{\theta}{2} < \frac{\pi}{4}, \text { so } \cos \frac{\theta}{2} \neq-\frac{3}{4}\right] \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of a directrix of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{25}=1$ is
questions.solution: Equation of ellipse $\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{\mathrm{~b}^{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}^{2}}=1$, where $a>b$. Given, $\frac{x^{2}}{16}+\frac{y^{2}}{75}=1 \Rightarrow b=4, a=5$ But $\mathrm{e}=\sqrt{1-\frac{\mathrm{b}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}^{2}}}=\sqrt{1-\frac{16}{25}}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}=\frac{3}{5}$ $\therefore$ equation of directrix $\mathrm{y}=\pm \frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{e}}$ $\therefore \quad y=\pm \frac{5}{3 / 5} \Rightarrow 3 y=\pm 25$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $X$ is a poisson variate such that $2 \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}=0)+\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}=2)=2 \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}=1)$ then $\mathrm{E}(\mathrm{X})$ is equal to
questions.solution:
If $2 \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}=0)+\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}=2)=2 \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}=1)$
Let probability distribution of $X$ be given by
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X}=\mathrm{r})=\frac{\mathrm{m}^{\mathrm{r}} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{r} !} \text { where } \mathrm{r}=0,1,2, \ldots \ldots \\
\therefore 2\left[\frac{\mathrm{m}^{0} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{m}}}{0 !}\right]+\left[\frac{\mathrm{m}^{2} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{m}}}{2 !}\right]=2\left[\frac{\mathrm{m} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{m}}}{1 !}\right] \\
\Rightarrow 2+\frac{\mathrm{m}^{2}}{2}=2 \mathrm{~m} \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}^{2}-4 \mathrm{~m}+4=0 \\
\Rightarrow(\mathrm{m}-2)^{2}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=2
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of \(\sin \left(\cot ^{-1}\left(\cos \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)\right)\right.\) is
questions.solution:
We have, \(\sin \left[\cot ^{-1}\left(\cos \left(\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)\right)\right]\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& =\sin \left[\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\tan ^2\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)}}\right)\right] \\
& =\sin \left(\cot ^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}\right) \\
& =\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\cot ^2\left\{\cot ^{-1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}\right\}}} \\
& =\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\frac{1}{1+x^2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{1+x^2}{2+x^2}} \\
&
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)+\cot ^{-1}(3)$ is
questions.solution:
Consider $\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)+\cot ^{-1} 3$ We have, $\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right)=\cot ^{-1} 2$ $\therefore$ From equation (i), we have $\cos ^{-1} 2+\cot ^{-1} 3=\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{2}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{3}$
$=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}}{1-\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{3}}\right)$ $=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{5 / 6}{\frac{6-1}{6}}\right)=\tan ^{-1} 1=\frac{\pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The principal value of $\sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \frac{5 \pi}{3}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\theta=\sin ^{-1}\left[\sin \frac{5 \pi}{3}\right]$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow & \sin \theta=\sin \frac{5 \pi}{3}=\sin \left[2 \pi-\frac{\pi}{3}\right] \\
\Rightarrow & \sin \theta=-\sin \frac{\pi}{3}=\sin \left(\frac{-\pi}{3}\right) \\
&(\because \sin (-\theta)=-\sin \theta)
\end{aligned}
$$
Therefore, principal value of $\sin ^{-1}$ $\left[\sin \frac{5 \pi}{3}\right]$ is $\frac{-\pi}{3}$, as principal value of $\sin ^{-}$
${ }^{1} x$ lies between $\frac{-\pi}{2}$ and $\frac{\pi}{2}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\operatorname{cosec} \tan ^{-1} x-\tan \cot ^{-1} x\right.$ ) is
questions.solution:
$2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\operatorname{cosectan}^{-1} x-\tan \cot ^{-1} x\right)$
$=2 \tan ^{-1}\left\{\operatorname{cosec}\left\{\operatorname{cosec}^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}{x}\right\}\right.$
$\left.-\tan \left\{\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{x}\right\}\right]$
$=2 \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}{x}-\frac{1}{x}\right]$
$=2 \tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}-1}}{x}\right\}$
$=2 \tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\sec \theta-1}{\tan \theta}\right\}($ put $x=\tan \theta)$
$=2 \tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{1-\cos \theta}{\sin \theta}\right\}$
$=2 \tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cdot \cos \frac{\theta}{2}}\right\}$
$=2 \tan ^{-1} \tan \frac{\theta}{2}$
$=2 \cdot \frac{\theta}{2}=\theta=\tan ^{-1} x$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\lim _{x \rightarrow \pi / 4} \frac{\tan x-1}{\cos 2 x}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\lim _{x \rightarrow \pi / 4} \frac{\tan x-1}{\cos 2 x}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+h\right)-1}{\cos 2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}+h\right)}$
$=\left[\because x=\frac{\pi}{4}+h\right]$
$=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(\frac{1+\tan h}{1-\tan h}\right)-1}{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+2 h\right)}$
$=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{1+\tan h-1+\tan h}{-\sin 2 h(1-\tanh )}$
$=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-2 \tan h}{2 \sin h \cos h(1-\tan h)}$
$=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-1}{\cos ^{2} h(1-\tan h)}=-1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1} x\right)^{1 / x}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{y}=\lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{x}\right)$
Taking log on both sides, we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\log \mathrm{y}=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{x} \log \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1} x\right) \text { form } \\
\left(\operatorname{form} \frac{\infty}{\infty}\right) \\
=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\left(-\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\right)}{2}-\tan ^{-1} x
\end{array}
$$
(using L'Hospitals' rule)
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\frac{2 x}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2}}}{-\left(\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\right)} \\
=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{-2 x}{1+x^{2}}=0 \Rightarrow y=e^{\circ}=1
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Evaluate $\lim _{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{\sqrt{(x+7)}-3 \sqrt{(2 x-3)}}{\sqrt[3]{(x+6)}-2 \sqrt[3]{(3 x-5)}}$.
questions.solution:
We have
$$
L=\lim _{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{\sqrt{(x+7)}-3 \sqrt{(2 x-3)}}{\sqrt[3]{(x+6)}-2 \sqrt[3]{(3 x-5)}}\left(\frac{0}{0} \text { form }\right)
$$
Let $x-2=t$ such that when $x \rightarrow 2, t \rightarrow 0$. Then
$$
\begin{array}{l}
L=\lim _{t \rightarrow 0} \frac{(t+9)^{\frac{1}{2}}-3(2 t+1)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{(t+8)^{\frac{1}{3}}-2(3 t+1)^{\frac{1}{3}}} \quad\left(\frac{0}{0} \text { form }\right) \\
=\frac{3}{2} \lim _{t \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(1+\frac{t}{9}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}-(2 t+1)^{\frac{1}{2}}}{\left(1+\frac{t}{8}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}-(3 t+1)^{\frac{1}{3}}} \quad\left(\frac{0}{0} \text { form }\right) \\
=\frac{3}{2} \lim _{t \rightarrow 0} \frac{\frac{1}{2} \frac{t}{9}-(2 t) \frac{1}{2}-(3 t) \frac{1}{3}}{3}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{1}{\frac{18}{2}}-1=\frac{1}{24}-1 \quad 23
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin \left(\pi \cos ^2 x\right)}{x^2}$
equal to
questions.solution:
$\text {  } \begin{aligned}
\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} & \frac{\sin \left(\pi \cos ^2 x\right)}{x^2} \\
& =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin \left(\pi-\pi \cos ^2 x\right)}{x^2} \\
& =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin \left[\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)\right]}{x^2} \times \frac{\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)}{\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)} \\
& =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin \left[\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)\right]}{\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)} \times \frac{\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)}{x^2}
\end{aligned}$
at $x \rightarrow 0, \pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)$ tends to zero.
So,
$\begin{aligned}
& \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin \left[\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)\right]}{\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)}=1 \\
& =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\pi\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right)}{x^2}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\pi \sin ^2 x}{x^2} \\
& =\pi \lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\sin x}{x}\right)^2=\pi(1)^2=\pi
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{A}=\{1,2,3, \ldots ., \mathrm{n}\}$ and $\mathrm{B}=\{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}\}$, then the number of functions from A to B that are onto is
questions.solution:
Number of onto functions: If A\& B are two sets having $m$ \& $n$ elements respectively such that $1 \leq \mathrm{n} \leq \mathrm{m}$ then number of onto functions from A to B is
$\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}(-1)^{\mathrm{n}-\mathrm{r}}{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{r}^{\mathrm{n}}$
Given $\mathrm{A}=\{1,2,3,-\cdots, \mathrm{n}\} \& \mathrm{~B}=\{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}\}$
$\therefore$ Number of onto functions
$=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{3}(-1)^{3-\mathrm{r}} \cdot{ }^{3} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}(\mathrm{r})^{\mathrm{n}}$
$=(-1)^{3-1}{ }^{3} \mathrm{C}_{1}(1)^{\mathrm{n}}+(-1)^{3-2}{ }^{3} \mathrm{C}_{2}(2)^{\mathrm{n}}+{ }^{3} \mathrm{C}_{3}(3)^{\mathrm{n}}(-1)^{3-3}$
$={ }^{3} \mathrm{C}_{1}-{ }^{3} \mathrm{C}_{2} 2^{\mathrm{n}}+{ }^{3} \mathrm{C}_{3} 3^{\mathrm{n}}$
$=\frac{3 !}{2 ! 1 !}-\frac{3 !}{2 ! 1 !} 2^{\mathrm{n}}+\frac{3 !}{3 ! 0 !} 3^{\mathrm{n}}$
$=3-3 \cdot 2^{\mathrm{n}}+3^{\mathrm{n}}$
$=3^{\mathrm{n}}-3\left(2^{\mathrm{n}}-1\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{x} \int_0^x(1+\sin 3 t)^{\frac{1}{t}}dt$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\int_0^x(1+\sin 3 t)^{\frac{1}{t}} d t}{x}$
Differentiating numerator and denominator
$\begin{aligned}
& & L=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{(1+\sin 3 x)^{\frac{1}{x}}}{1} \\
\Rightarrow & L & =e^{\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{x} \sin 3 x} \Rightarrow L=e^{\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{3 \sin 3 x}{3 x}} \\
\Rightarrow & L & =e^{3 \times 1}=e^3
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{((a-n) n x-\tan x) \sin n x}{x^2}=0\), where \(n\) is non-zero real number, then a is equal to
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 0} \mathrm{n} \frac{\sin \mathrm{nx}}{\mathrm{nx}} \cdot \lim _{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 0}\left((\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{n}) \mathrm{n}-\frac{\tan \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}((\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{n}) \mathrm{n}-1)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{n}) \mathrm{n}=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{n}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}} .
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{x^{3} \cot x}{1-\cos x}$ is
questions.solution:
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{x^{3} \cot x}{1-\cos x}$
$=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{x^{3} \cot x}{1-\cos x} \times \frac{1+\cos x}{1+\cos x}\right)$
$=\operatorname{x}_{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{3}{\sin x}\right) \times \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \cos x \times \lim _{x \rightarrow 0}(1+\cos x)=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The values of constants $\mathrm{a}$ and $\mathrm{b}$, so that $\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x+1}-a x-b\right)=0$ are
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
 \lim _{x \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x+1}-a x-b\right)=0 \\
\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow \infty}\left[\frac{x^{2}(1-a)-(a+b) x-b+1}{x+1}\right]=0 \\
\Rightarrow 1-a=0 \text { and } a+b=0 \Rightarrow a=1, b=-1
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:

Then, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left\{\frac{f(1+x)}{f(1)}\right\}^{1 / x}$ equals to
questions.solution:
$\lim _{\mathrm{e} \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}[\log \mathrm{f}(1+\mathrm{x})-\log \mathrm{f}(1)]$
$=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1+\mathrm{x}) / \mathrm{f}(1+\mathrm{x})}{1}$
$=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(1) / \mathrm{f}(1)}=\mathrm{e}^{613}=\mathrm{e}^{2}$

questions.solution:

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A steam boat is moving at velocity \(V\) when steam is shut off. Given that the retardation at any subsequent time is equal to the magnitude of the velocity at that time. The velocity v in time t after steam is shut off is
questions.solution:
The retardation at time \(\mathrm{t}=-\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}\). Hence, the differential equation is
\(-\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{v} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{v}}=-\mathrm{dt}\) ...(i)
Integrating, we get \(\log \mathrm{v}=-\mathrm{t}+\mathrm{c}\) ...(ii)
When \(\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{v}=\mathrm{V} \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=\log \mathrm{V}\)
The equation (ii) becomes \(\log \mathrm{v}=-\mathrm{t}+\log \mathrm{V}\)
\(\Rightarrow \log \frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{V}}=-\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{V}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\mathrm{Ve}^{-\mathrm{t}}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The temperature \(T\) of a cooling object drops at a rate proportional to the difference \(\mathrm{T}-\mathrm{S}\), where \(\mathrm{S}\) is constant temperature of surrounding medium. If initially \(\mathrm{T}=150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\), find the temperature of the cooling object at any time \(t\).
questions.solution:
Let \(\mathrm{T}\) be the temperature of the cooling object at any time \(t\).
\(\frac{\mathrm{dT}}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto(\mathrm{T}-\mathrm{S}) \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dT}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{k}(\mathrm{T}-\mathrm{S}) \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}-\mathrm{S}=\mathrm{ce}^{\mathrm{kt}},\)
where \(\mathrm{k}\) is negative.
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{S}+\mathrm{ce}^{\mathrm{kt}}\)
When \(\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{~T}=150 \Rightarrow 150=\mathrm{S}+\mathrm{c}\)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=150-\mathrm{S}\)
\(\therefore\) The temperature of the cooling object at any time is
\(\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{S}+(150-\mathrm{S}) \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{kt}}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In a culture the bacteria count is $1,00,000$. The number is increased by $10 \%$ in 2 hours. In how many hours will the count reach 2,00,000 if the rate of growth of bacteria is proportional to the number present.
questions.solution:
Let $y$ denote the number of bacteria at any instant $t \cdot$ then according to the question
$$
\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}} \alpha \mathrm{y} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{kdt}
$$
$\mathrm{k}$ is the constant of proportionality, taken to be + ve on integrating (i), we get
$$
\log y=k t+c
$$
$\mathrm{c}$ is a parameter. let $\mathrm{y}_0$ be the initial number of bacteria
i.e., at $\mathrm{t}=0$ using this in (ii), $\mathrm{c}=\log \mathrm{y}_0$


$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad \log \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{kt}+\log \mathrm{y}_0 \Rightarrow \log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}_0}=\mathrm{kt} \\
& \mathrm{y}=\left(\mathrm{y}_0+\frac{10}{100} \mathrm{y}_0\right)=\frac{11 \mathrm{y}_0}{10} \text {, when } \mathrm{t}=2 \\
& \text { So, from (iii), we get } \log \frac{\frac{11 \mathrm{y}_0}{10}}{\mathrm{y}_0}=\mathrm{k} \text { (2) } \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{2} \log \frac{11}{10}
\end{aligned}
$$
Using (iv) in (iii) $\log \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}_0}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\log \frac{11}{10}\right) \mathrm{t}$
let the number of bacteria become $1,00,000$ to $2,00,000$ in $t_1$ hours. i.e., $y=2 y_0$ when $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}_1$ hours. from $(\mathrm{v})$
$$
\log \frac{2 \mathrm{y}_0}{\mathrm{y}_0}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\log \frac{11}{10}\right) \mathrm{t}_1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_1=\frac{2 \log 2}{\log \frac{11}{10}}
$$
Hence, the reqd. no. of hours $=\frac{2 \log 2}{\log \frac{11}{10}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \((\mathrm{G}, *)\) is a group such that \((\mathrm{a} * \mathrm{~b})^2=(\mathrm{a} * \mathrm{a}) *\) \(\left(b^* b\right.\) ) for all a, b, \(\in \mathrm{G}\), then \(\mathrm{G}\) is
questions.solution:
\((\mathrm{a} * \mathrm{~b})^2=(\mathrm{a} * \mathrm{a}) *(\mathrm{~b} * \mathrm{~b})\) for all \(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathrm{G}\)
\(\Rightarrow(\mathrm{a} * \mathrm{~b}) *(\mathrm{a} * \mathrm{~b})=(\mathrm{a} * \mathrm{a}) *(\mathrm{~b} * \mathrm{~b})\) for all
\(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathrm{G}\)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{a} *(\mathrm{~b} * \mathrm{a}) * \mathrm{~b}=\mathrm{a} *(\mathrm{a} * \mathrm{~b}) * \mathrm{~b}\) for all \(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathrm{G}\)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{b} * \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{a} * \mathrm{~b}\) for all \(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathrm{G}\) (by cancellation laws)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{G}\) is abelian

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider the switching circuit Logical expression corresponding to the complimentary to the above circuit is
questions.solution:
Logical expression corresponding to above circuit is \(=a^{\prime}+b^{\prime}+c\)
Logical expression corresponding to the complimentary to the above circuit is
\(\begin{aligned}
& \left(a^{\prime}+b^{\prime}+c\right)^{\prime}=\left(a^{\prime}+b^{\prime}\right)^{\prime} \cdot c^{\prime} \\
& =\left(a^{\prime}\right)^{\prime} \cdot\left(b^{\prime}\right)^{\prime} \cdot c^{\prime} \\
& =a \cdot b \cdot c^{\prime} \quad\left[\left(a^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}=a ;\left(b^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}=b\right]
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The proposition $\sim(p \Leftrightarrow q)$ is equivalent to
questions.solution:
$\sim(p \Leftrightarrow q) \equiv \sim[(p \Rightarrow q) \wedge(q \Rightarrow p)]$
$\equiv \sim(p \Rightarrow q) \vee(\sim(q \Rightarrow p))$
$(\because$ De-Morgan's law $)$
$\equiv(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(q \wedge \sim p)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The statement $(\mathrm{p} \Rightarrow \mathrm{q}) \Leftrightarrow(\sim p \wedge q)$ is a
questions.solution:
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c}
\hline \mathrm{p} & \mathbf{q} & \mathbf{p} & \mathbf{p} \Rightarrow \mathbf{q} & \sim \mathbf{p}^{\wedge} \mathbf{q} & (\mathbf{p} \Rightarrow \mathbf{q}) \Leftrightarrow\left(\sim \mathbf{p}^{\wedge} \mathbf{q}\right) \\
\hline \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{F} \\
\mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{T} \\
\mathrm{F} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{T} \\
\mathrm{F} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{T} & \mathrm{F} & \mathrm{F} \\
\hline
\end{array}
Hence, given statement is neither tautology nor contradiction.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The conditional $(p \wedge q) \Rightarrow p$ is
questions.solution:
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hlinep & q & p \wedge q & (p \wedge q) \Rightarrow p \\
\hlineT & T & T & T \\
\hlineT & F & F & T \\
\hlineF & T & F & T \\
\hlineF & F & F & T \\
\hline
\end{array}
$\therefore(p \wedge q) \Rightarrow p$ is a tautology.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{A}(\theta)=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & \tan \theta \\ -\tan \theta & 1\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{I}$, then $\left(\cos ^{2} \theta\right) \mathrm{B}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{A}(\theta)=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & \tan \theta \\ -\tan \theta & 1\end{array}\right] \& \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{I}$
$\mathrm{B}=\mathrm{I} \mathrm{A}^{-1}, \mathrm{~A}_{11}=1, \mathrm{~A}_{12}=+\tan \theta$
$\mathrm{A}_{21}=-\tan \theta, \mathrm{A}_{22}=1$
$\therefore \mathrm{A}|=| \begin{array}{cc}1 & \tan \theta \\ -\tan \theta & 1\end{array} \mid=1+\tan ^{2} \theta=\sec ^{2} \theta$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{\sec ^{2} \theta} \cdot\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -\tan \theta \\ \tan \theta & 1\end{array}\right]$
$\mathrm{B}=\frac{1}{\sec ^{2} \theta} \cdot\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -\tan \theta \\ \tan \theta & 1\end{array}\right]$
$\mathrm{B}=\frac{1}{\sec ^{2} \theta} \cdot\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -\tan \theta \\ \tan \theta & 1\end{array}\right]$
$\mathrm{B}=\frac{1}{\sec ^{2} \theta} \cdot \mathrm{A}(-\theta)$
$\Rightarrow\left(\sec ^{2} \theta\right) \cdot \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{A}(-\theta)$
$\Rightarrow\left(\cos ^{2} \theta\right)^{-1} \cdot \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{A}(-\theta)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider an infinite geometric series with the first term a and common ratio $r$. If its sum is 4 and the second term is $\frac{3}{4}$, then
questions.solution:
First term $=a \&$ common ratio $=r$
Given $\mathrm{S}_{\infty}=4 \& \mathrm{a}_{2}=\frac{3}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{a}}{1-\mathrm{r}}=4$ ...(1)
$\& \mathrm{ar}=\frac{3}{4}\left[\because \mathrm{S}_{\infty}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{1-\mathrm{r}} \& \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{ar}^{\mathrm{n}-1}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\frac{3}{4 \mathrm{r}}$
$\therefore$ Equation (1) becomes $\frac{3}{4 r(1-r)}=4$
$\Rightarrow 16 r^{2}-16 r+3=0$
$\Rightarrow(4 r-3)(4 r-1)=0$
$\Rightarrow r=\frac{3}{4}$ or $r=\frac{1}{4}$
when $r=\frac{1}{4}$ then $a=\frac{3}{4 \times \frac{1}{4}}=3$
$\therefore a=3 \& \mathrm{r}=\frac{1}{4}$

$\sim \mathrm{p} \vee \mathrm{q}$ means $\mathrm{F} \vee \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{F}, \sim \mathrm{r}$ means $\mathrm{F}$
$\therefore \quad[(\sim \mathrm{p} \vee \mathrm{q}) \wedge \sim \mathrm{r}] \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}$ means $\mathrm{T}$

$\Rightarrow \sim S: \sim\left(\sim p^{\wedge} q\right) \wedge \sim(p \wedge \sim q)$
$\Rightarrow \sim S:(p \vee \sim q) \wedge(\sim p \vee q)$

questions.q: If matrix $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}3 & -2 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{k}} \operatorname{adj}(\mathrm{A})$, then $k$ is
questions.solution:
If $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}3 & -2 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{k} \operatorname{adj}(\mathrm{A})$......(i)
Also, we know $\mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{\operatorname{adj}(\mathrm{A})}{|\mathrm{A}|}$......(ii)
$\therefore$ By comparing (i) and (ii) 
$|\mathrm{A}|=k$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow|\mathrm{A}|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
3 & -2 & 4 \\
1 & 2 & -1 \\
0 & 1 & 1
\end{array}\right| \\
=3(2+1)+2(1+0)+4(1-0) \\
=9+2+4=15
\end{array}
$$

questions.q: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 3 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right]$, then rank (A) is equal to
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 3 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & -5 & -3 \\ 0 & -9 & -2\end{array}\right]$
$\left[\right.$ Applying $\left.\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}-2 \mathrm{R}_{1}, \mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}-3 \mathrm{R}_{1}\right]$
$\approx\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & -5 & -3 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{17}{5}\end{array}\right] \quad\left[\mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}-\frac{9}{5} \mathrm{R}_{2}\right]$
$\therefore \quad \operatorname{rank}(\mathrm{A})=3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $A, B$ are two square matrices, such that $A B=A, B A=B$, then $(A+B)^7$ equals
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{rlrl}\text {  } \because & A B & =A \\ \Rightarrow & & B A B & =B A\end{array}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad B^2=B \\
& \text { and } \quad B A=B \\
& \Rightarrow \quad A B A=A B \Rightarrow A^2=A \\
& \text { Now, }(A+B)^2=A^2+A B+B A+B^2 \\
& =A+A+B+B=2(A+B) \\
& \text { and }(A+B)^2=(A+B)^2(A+B)=2^2(A+B) \\
& \text { Also, }(A+B)^4=(A+B)^3(A+B)=2^3(A+B) \\
& \text { Hence, }(A+B)^7=2^6(A+B)
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & c & -b \\ -c & 0 & a \\ b & -a & 0\end{array}\right]$ and $B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}a^{2} & a b & a c \\ a b & b^{2} & b c \\ a c & b c & c^{2}\end{array}\right]$, then $A B$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{AB}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & \mathrm{c} & -\mathrm{b} \\ -\mathrm{c} & 0 & \mathrm{a} \\ \mathrm{b} & -\mathrm{a} & 0\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{a}^{2} & \mathrm{ab} & \mathrm{ac} \\ \mathrm{ab} & \mathrm{b}^{2} & \mathrm{bc} \\ \mathrm{ac} & \mathrm{bc} & \mathrm{c}^{2}\end{array}\right]$
$$
\begin{aligned}
A B &=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
a b c-a b c & b^{2} c-b^{2} c & b c^{2}-b c^{2} \\
-a^{2} c+a^{2} c & -a b c+a b c & -a c+a c \\
a^{2} b-a^{2} b & a b^{2}-a b^{2} & a b c-a b c
\end{array}\right] \\
&=\left[\begin{array}{lll}
0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0
\end{array}\right]=0
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The rank of the matrix $\left[\begin{array}{ccc}-1 & 2 & 5 \\ 2 & -4 & a-4 \\ 1 & -2 & a+1\end{array}\right]$ is
questions.solution:
Let
$$
\begin{array}{c}
A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
-1 & 2 & 5 \\
2 & -4 & a-4 \\
1 & -2 & a+1
\end{array}\right] \sim\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
-1 & 2 & 5 \\
0 & 0 & a+6 \\
0 & 0 & a+6
\end{array}\right] \\
{\left[\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}+2 \mathrm{R}_{1}, \mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}+\mathrm{R}_{1}\right]}
\end{array}
$$
Clearly rank of $\mathrm{A}$ is 1 if $a=-6$
Also, for $a=1,|\mathrm{~A}|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}-1 & 2 & 5 \\ 2 & -4 & -3 \\ 1 & -2 & 2\end{array}\right|=0$
and $\left|\begin{array}{cc}2 & 5 \\ -4 & -3\end{array}\right|=-6+20=14 \neq 0$
$\therefore$ rank of $\mathrm{A}$ is 2 if $a=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}3 & 2 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]\), then \(\left(A^{-1}\right)^3\) is equal to
questions.solution:
\(|\mathrm{A}|=\left|\begin{array}{ll}
3 & 2 \\
0 & 1
\end{array}\right|=3-0=3 \neq 0 \quad \therefore \mathrm{A}^{-1} \text { exists }\)
Adj. \(A=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & 0 \\ -2 & 3\end{array}\right]^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -2 \\ 0 & 3\end{array}\right]\)
\(\therefore \quad \mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{3}\left[\begin{array}{cc}
1 & -2 \\
0 & 3
\end{array}\right]\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \therefore\left(A^{-1}\right)^3=\frac{1}{27}\left[\begin{array}{cc}
1 & -2 \\
1 & 3
\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}
1 & -2 \\
0 & 3
\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}
1 & -2 \\
0 & 3
\end{array}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{27}\left[\begin{array}{cc}
1 & -8 \\
0 & 9
\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}
1 & -2 \\
0 & 3
\end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{27}\left[\begin{array}{cc}
1 & -26 \\
0 & 27
\end{array}\right]
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the matrix $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{rrr}1 & 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 & -3 \\ 0 & 1 & 2\end{array}\right]$ then $\operatorname{adj}(\operatorname{adj} A)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 & -3 \\ 0 & 1 & 2\end{array}\right]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{I}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right], \mathrm{J}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}0 & 1 \\ -1 & 0\end{array}\right]$ and $\mathrm{B}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}\cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ -\sin \theta & \cos \theta\end{array}\right]$, then $\mathrm{B}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}\quad \mathrm{B} &=\left[\begin{array}{cc}
\cos \theta & \sin \theta \\
-\sin \theta & \cos \theta
\end{array}\right] \\
&=\left[\begin{array}{cc}
\cos \theta & 0 \\
0 & \cos \theta
\end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & \sin \theta \\
-\sin \theta & 0
\end{array}\right] \\
&=\cos \theta\left[\begin{array}{ll}
1 & 0 \\
0 & 1
\end{array}\right]+\sin \theta\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & 1 \\
-1 & 0
\end{array}\right] \\
&=\mathrm{I} \cos \theta+\mathrm{J} \sin \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If the points $\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right),\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$ and $\left(x_{3}, y_{3}\right)$ are collinear, then the rank of the matrix
$\left[\begin{array}{lll}x_{1} & y_{1} & 1 \\ x_{2} & y_{2} & 1 \\ x_{3} & y_{3} & 1\end{array}\right]$ will always be less than
questions.solution:
The given matrix is $\left[\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{x}_{1} & \mathrm{y}_{1} & 1 \\ \mathrm{x}_{2} & \mathrm{y}_{2} & 1 \\ \mathrm{x}_{3} & \mathrm{y}_{3} & 1\end{array}\right]$, using $R_{2} \rightarrow R_{2}-R_{1}, R_{3} \rightarrow R_{3}-R_{1}$
$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}
\mathrm{x}_{1} & \mathrm{y}_{1} & 1 \\
\mathrm{x}_{2}-\mathrm{x}_{1} & \mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{1} & 0 \\
\mathrm{x}_{3}-\mathrm{x}_{1} & \mathrm{y}_{3}-\mathrm{y}_{1} & 0
\end{array}\right]=0 \\
&(\because \text { pointsare collinear i.e., area of triangle }=0) \\
& \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{x}_{2}-\mathrm{x}_{1} & \mathrm{y}_{2}-\mathrm{y}_{1} \\
\mathrm{x}_{3}-\mathrm{x}_{1} & \mathrm{y}_{3}-\mathrm{y}_{1}
\end{array}\right|=0
\end{aligned}$
So, the rank of matrix is always less than 2 .

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & -5 & 7 \\ 0 & 7 & 9 \\ 11 & 8 & 9\end{array}\right]$, then trace of matrix A is
questions.solution:
We knowthat, $\operatorname{tr}(A)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i i}$
$\therefore$ If $\mathrm{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & -5 & 7 \\ 0 & 7 & 9 \\ 11 & 8 & 9\end{array}\right]$, then $\operatorname{tr}(\mathrm{A})=1+7+9=17$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Mean and standard deviation of marks obtained in some particular subject by four classes are given below. Report the class with best performance
questions.solution:
Performance of the class will be best if mean of the marks obtained is maximum but standard deviation of the marks obtained is minimum.
Hence the class which has mean and standard deviation of the marks obtained as 75 and 5 respectively performs best.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $y$ be the number of people in a village at time t. Assume that the rate of change of the population is proportional to the number of people in the village at any time and further assume that the population never increases in time. Then the population of the village at any fixed time $t$ is given by
questions.solution:
According to the question,
$\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \mathrm{y} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{ky}$
Separating the variables, we get $\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{kdt}$
Integrating both sides, we get $\int \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{y}}=\int \mathrm{k} \mathrm{dt}$ 
$\log y=k t+M($ as $y$ cannot $b e-v e)$ 
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{kt}+\mathrm{M}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{M}} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{kt}}$ 
$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{C} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{kt}}$, where $\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{M}}$
Constant $\mathrm{k}$ cannot be positive because the population never increases in time. And another constant $\mathrm{C}$ cannot be negative because of $\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{M}}>0$ always.
Hence $y=\mathrm{Ce}^{\mathrm{kt}}$, for some constants $\mathrm{C} \geq 0$ and $\mathrm{k} \leq 0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\vec{a}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ are one of the sides and medians respectively, of a triangle through the same vertex, then area of the triangle is
questions.solution:
In the figure, $\mathrm{OAC}$ is a triangle and $\mathrm{OB}$ is a median such that $\begin{array}{l} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OA}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OB}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OC}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \text { (say) } \end{array}$
$\begin{array}{l} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OA}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OC}}=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OB}} \\ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \\ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})-(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ =(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+9 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \end{array}$ Now, the area of the triangle, $\Delta=\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OA}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OC}}|=\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|$ Here, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\left|\begin{array}{lll}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \mathrm{k} \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 3 & 9 & 5\end{array}\right|$ $=\hat{\mathrm{i}}(-5-9)-\hat{\mathrm{j}}(5-3)+\hat{\mathrm{k}}(9+3)$ $=-14 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+1 \hat{2} \mathrm{k}=2(-7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$ $\begin{aligned} \therefore|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}| &=2 \sqrt{(-7)^{2}+(-1)^{2}+(6)^{2}} \\ &=2 \sqrt{49+1+36}=2 \sqrt{86} \end{aligned}$ $\therefore \Delta=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \sqrt{86}=\sqrt{86}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the normal to the rectangular hyperbola $x y=$ $\mathrm{c}^{2}$ at the point $\left(\mathrm{ct}, \frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{t}}\right)$ meets the curve again at $\left(\mathrm{ct}^{\prime}, \frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{t}^{\prime}}\right)$, then
questions.solution:
The equation of tangent at $\left(\mathrm{ct}, \frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{t}}\right)$ is 
$\mathrm{ty}=\mathrm{t}^{3} \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{ct}^{4}+\mathrm{c}$
If it passes through $\left(\mathrm{ct}^{\prime}, \frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{t}^{\prime}}\right)$ then
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \frac{t c}{t^{\prime}}=t^{3} c t^{\prime}-c t^{4}+c \\
\Rightarrow t=t^{3} t^{\prime 2}-t^{4} t^{\prime}+t^{\prime} \\
\Rightarrow t \cdot t^{\prime}=t^{3} t^{\prime}\left(t^{\prime} . t\right) \Rightarrow t^{3} t^{\prime}=-1
\end{array}$
Note : If we take the co-ordinate axes along the asymptotes of a rectangular hyperbola, then the general equation $x^{2}-y^{2}=a^{2}$ becomes $x y=c^{2}$, where $c$ is a constant.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(f(x)=x^2-4 x-5\), then \(f(A)\), where \(A=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right]\) equals
questions.solution:
Given \(f(x)=x^2-4 x-5\)
\(\therefore f(A)=A^2-4 A-5 I\)
Now, \(A^2=\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll}9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9\end{array}\right]\)
\(\begin{aligned}
\therefore \mathrm{A}^2-4 \mathrm{~A}-5 \mathrm{I} & =\left[\begin{array}{lll}
9 & 8 & 8 \\
8 & 9 & 8 \\
8 & 8 & 9
\end{array}\right]   -4\left[\begin{array}{lll}
1 & 2 & 2 \\
2 & 1 & 2 \\
2 & 2 & 1
\end{array}\right]-5\left[\begin{array}{lll}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{array}\right]
\end{aligned}\)
\(=\left[\begin{array}{lll}
0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0
\end{array}\right]=\mathrm{O}\)

questions.q: If $A$ and $B$ are matrices and $B=A B A^{-1}$ then the value of $(A+B)(A-B)$ is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{B}=\mathrm{ABA}^{-1}$ (Given)
But $B=B A A^{-A}$
$\therefore \mathrm{ABA}^{-1}=\mathrm{BAA}^{-1} \Rightarrow \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{BA}$ $\mathrm{Now}(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B})(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{B})=\mathrm{A}^{2}-\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{BA}-\mathrm{B}^{2}$ $=\mathrm{A}^{2}-\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{AB}-\mathrm{B}^{2} \quad[\because \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{BA}]$ $=\mathrm{A}^{2}-\mathrm{B}^{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int \sin ^{-1}\left\{\frac{(2 x+2)}{\sqrt{4 x^{2}+8 x+13}}\right\} d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$$
\text { (a) Let } \begin{array}{l}
I=\int \sin ^{-1}\left\{\frac{2 x+2}{\sqrt{4 x^{2}+8 x+13}}\right\} d x \\
\Rightarrow I=\int \sin ^{-1}\left\{\frac{2 x+2}{\sqrt{4 x^{2}+8 x+4+9}}\right\} d x \\
\Rightarrow I=\int \sin ^{-1}\left\{\frac{2 x+2}{\sqrt{(2 x+2)^{2}+3^{2}}}\right\} d x
\end{array}
$$
$$
\text { Substituting } 2 x+2=3 \tan \theta \text {, }
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow 2 d x=3 \sec ^{2} \theta d \theta, \text { we get } \\
I=\int \sin ^{-1}\left\{\frac{3 \tan \theta}{3 \sec \theta}\right\} \cdot \frac{3}{2} \sec ^{2} \theta d \theta \\
\Rightarrow I=\frac{3}{2} \int \sin ^{-1}(\sin \theta) \cdot \sec ^{2} \theta d \theta \\
\Rightarrow I=\frac{3}{2} \int \theta \sec ^{2} \theta d \theta \\
\Rightarrow I=\frac{3}{2}\left[\theta \tan \theta-\int \tan \theta d \theta\right]
\end{array}
$$
(integrating by parts)
$$
\begin{aligned}
I &=\frac{3}{2}[\theta \tan \theta-\log |\sec \theta|]+c \\
&=\frac{3}{2}\left[\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x+2}{3}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{2 x+2}{3}\right)\right.\\
&\left.\quad-\log \sqrt{1+\left(\frac{2 x+2}{3}\right)^{2}}\right]+c \\
&=(x+1) \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x+2}{3}\right) \\
&-\frac{3}{4} \log \left(\frac{4 x^{2}+8 x+13}{9}\right)+c
\end{aligned}
$$
