& \Rightarrow-1 < 1-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{5} < 1 \Rightarrow 0 < \mathrm{x} < 10, \mathrm{x} \neq 5
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Sum of the series
\(\frac{1}{1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 4}+\frac{4}{3 \cdot 4 \cdot 5 \cdot 6}+\frac{9}{5 \cdot 6 \cdot 7 \cdot 8}+\frac{16}{7 \cdot 8 \cdot 9 \cdot 10}+\ldots \ldots=\)
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& T_n=\frac{n^2}{(2 n-1) \cdot 2 n \cdot(2 n+1) \cdot(2 n+2)} \\
& =\frac{n}{2(2 n-1)(2 n+1)(2 n+2)} \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{12(2 n-1)}+\frac{1}{4(2 n+1)}-\frac{1}{3(2 n+2)}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{24}\left[\frac{1}{2 n-1}+\frac{3}{2 n+1}-\frac{4}{2 n+2}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{24}\left[\frac{1}{2 n-1}+\frac{4-1}{2 n+1}-\frac{4}{2 n+2}\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{2 n-1}-\frac{1}{2 n+1}+4\left(\frac{1}{2 n+1}-\frac{1}{2 n+2}\right)\right] \\
&
\end{aligned}\)
Putting \(\mathrm{n}=1,2,3,4\) and adding, we get
\(\begin{aligned}
S= & \frac{1}{24}\left[\left(1-\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{5}-\frac{1}{7}+\ldots .\right)\right. \\
& \left.+4\left(\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{5}-\frac{1}{6}+\ldots\right)\right] \\
= & \frac{1}{24}\left[1+4\left\{\log _e 2-\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)\right\}\right] \\
= & \frac{1}{24}\left(4 \log _e 2-1\right)=\frac{1}{6} \log _e 2-\frac{1}{24}
\end{aligned}\)
Note that sum of \(\mathrm{n}\) terms of the first series is
\(1-\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{n}+1}\) which tends to 1 as \(\mathrm{n} \rightarrow \infty\).
Thus the sum of the first infinite series is 1.

questions.q: An equation of the plane passing through the line of intersection of the planes $x+y+z=6$ and $2 \mathrm{x}+3 \mathrm{y}+4 \mathrm{z}+5=0$ and passing through $(1,1,1)$ is
questions.solution:
The equation of the plane through the line of intersection of the given planes is $(x+y+z-6)+\lambda(2 x+3 y+4 z+5)=0$
If equation (1) passes through $(1,1,1)$, we have
$-3+14 \lambda=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{3}{14}$
Putting $\lambda=\frac{3}{14}$ in $(1)$, we obtain the equation of the required plane as
$\begin{array}{l}
(x+y+z-6)+\frac{3}{14}(2 x+3 y+4 z+5)=0 \\
\Rightarrow 20 x+23 y+26 z-69=0
\end{array}$

questions.q: The number of subsets of $\{1,2,3, \ldots, 9\}$ containing at least one odd number is
questions.solution:
The total number of subsets of given set is $2^{9}=512$
Even numbers are $\{2,4,6,8\}$.
Case I : When selecting only one even number $={ }^{4} C_{1}=4$
Case II : When selecting only two even numbers $={ }^{4} C_{2}=6$
Case III : When selecting only three even numbers $={ }^{4} C_{3}=4$
Case IV : When selecting only four even numbers $={ }^{4} C_{4}=1$
$\therefore$ Required number of ways
$$
=512-(4+6+4+1)-1=496
$$
[Here, we subtract 1 for due to the null set]

$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The relation $\mathrm{R}$ defined on the set $\mathrm{A}=\{1,2,3$, $4,5\}$ by $R=\left\{(x, y):\left|x^2-y^2\right| < 16\right\}$ is given by
questions.solution:
We have $\mathrm{R}=\left\{(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}):\left|\mathrm{x}^2-\mathrm{y}^2\right| < 16\right\}$
Let $\mathrm{x}=1,\left|\mathrm{x}^2-\mathrm{y}^2\right| < 16 \Rightarrow\left|1-\mathrm{y}^2\right| < 16$
$\Rightarrow\left|y^2-1\right| < 16 \Rightarrow y=1,2,3,4$
Let $x=2,\left|x^2-y^2\right| < 16 \Rightarrow\left|4-y^2\right| < 16$
$\Rightarrow\left|y^2-4\right| < 16 \Rightarrow y=1,2,3,4$
Let $x=3,\left|x^2-y^2\right| < 16 \Rightarrow\left|9-y^2\right| < 16$
$\Rightarrow\left|y^2-9\right| < 16 \Rightarrow y=1,2,3,4$
Let $x=4,\left|x^2-y^2\right| < 16 \Rightarrow\left|16-y^2\right| < 16$
$\Rightarrow\left|y^2-16\right| < 16 \Rightarrow y=1,2,3,4,5$
Let $\mathrm{x}=5,\left|\mathrm{x}^2-\mathrm{y}^2\right| < 16 \Rightarrow\left|25-\mathrm{y}^2\right| < 16$
$\Rightarrow\left|\mathrm{y}^2-25\right| < 16 \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=4,5$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{R}=\{(1,1),(1,2),(1,3),(1,4),(2,1),(2$,
$2),(2,3),(2,4),(3,1),(3,2),(3,3),(3,4),(4,1)$,
$(4,2),(4,3),(4,4),(4,5),(5,4),(5,5)\}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $A$ and $B$ be two sets such that $A \times B$ con sists of 6 elements. If three elements $\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{B}$ are $(1,4),(2,6)$ and $(3,6)$, find $\mathrm{B} \times \mathrm{A}$.
questions.solution:
Since, $(1,4),(2,6)$ and $(3,6)$ are the elements of $\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{B}$, therefore $1,2,3$ are the elements of $A$ and 4,6 are the elements of $B$.
Also, $\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{B}$ has 6 elements
$\therefore \quad A=\{1,2,3\}$
and $B=\{4,6\}$
$\therefore B \times A=\{(4,1),(4,2),(4,3),(6,1),(6,2)$
$(6,3)\}$
and $B=\{4,6\}$
$\therefore \mathrm{B} \times \mathrm{A}=\{(4,1),(4,2),(4,3),(6,1),(6,2),$,
$(6,3)\}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If N denote the set of all natural numbers and $\mathrm{R}$ be the relation on $N \times N$ defined by $(a, b) R(c, d)$, if $a d(b+c)=b c(a+d)$, then $R$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}\text { (a,b) } \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}) \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{ad}(\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})=\mathrm{bc}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{d}) \\ \text { Reflexive }: \mathrm{ab}(\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{a})=\mathrm{ba}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}), \forall \mathrm{ab} \subset \mathrm{N} \\ \therefore(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}) \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}) \\ \text { So, Ris reflexive, } \\ \text { Symmetric }: \mathrm{ad}(\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})=\mathrm{bc}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{d}) \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{bc}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{d})=\mathrm{ad}(\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}) \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{cd}(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{a})=\mathrm{da}(\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{b}) \\ \Rightarrow(\mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}) \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b})\end{array}$
So, R is symmetric.
Transitive : For $(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}),(\mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}),(\mathrm{e}, \mathrm{f}) \in \mathrm{N} \times \mathrm{N}$
Let $(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}) \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}),(\mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}) \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{e}, \mathrm{f})$
$\therefore \mathrm{ad}(\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})=\mathrm{bc}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{d}), \mathrm{cf}(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{e})=\mathrm{de}(\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{f})$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{adb}+\mathrm{adc}-\mathrm{bca}+\mathrm{bcd} \ldots(\mathrm{i})$
and cfd $+\mathrm{cfe}=\mathrm{dec}+\mathrm{def} \ldots(\mathrm{ii})$
On multiplying eq. $(\mathrm{i})$ by ef and eq. (ii) by
ab and then adding, we have
adbef $+\mathrm{adcef}+\mathrm{cfdab}+\mathrm{cfeab}$
$=\mathrm{bcaef}+\mathrm{bcdef}+\mathrm{decab}+\mathrm{defab}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{adcf}(\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{e})=\mathrm{bcde}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{f})$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{af}(\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{e})=$ be $(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{f})$
$\Rightarrow(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}) \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{e}+\mathrm{f}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: n the set \(\mathbf{N} \times \mathbf{N}\), the relation \(\mathrm{R}\) is defined by (a, b) \(R(c, d) \Leftrightarrow a d=b c\). Then \(R\) is
options: reflexive and transitive but not symmetric
symmetric and transitive but not reflexive
questions.solution:
The relation is defined on \(\mathbf{N} \times \mathbf{N}\). since the elements of \(\mathbf{N} \times \mathbf{N}\) are ordered pairs hence the relation on \(\mathbf{N} \times \mathbf{N}\) relates two ordered pairs. Now,
(1) \(\forall \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b} \in \mathbf{N} \quad \mathrm{ab}=\mathrm{ba}\)
\(\therefore(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}) \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}) \forall(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}) \in \mathbf{N} \times \mathbf{N}\). Thus \(\mathrm{R}\) is refiexive
(ii) Let
\(\begin{aligned}
& (a, b) R(c, d) \Leftrightarrow a d=b c \forall(a, b) \text { and }(c, d) \in N \times N \\
& \Rightarrow b c=a d \Rightarrow c b=d a \Rightarrow(c, d) R(a, b) .
\end{aligned}\)
Thus \(R\) is symmetric
(iii) Again let (a,b) R (c,d) and (c,d) R (e,f)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{ad}=\mathrm{bc}\) and \(\mathrm{cf}=\mathrm{de}\)
\(\Rightarrow\) adcf \(=\) bcde \(\Rightarrow\) af \(=\) de \(\Rightarrow(a, b) R(e, f)\)
Thus \(\mathrm{R}\) is transitive.
Thus \(\mathrm{R}\) is equivalence relation.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the normal to the curve $y=f(x)$ at $(3,4)$ makes an angle $\frac{3 \pi}{4}$ with the positive $x$-axis, then $f^{\prime}(3)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Slope of the normal at $(3,4)$ is the value of
$\begin{array}{l}
-\frac{1}{f^{\prime}(x)} \text { at } x=3 \text { or }-\frac{1}{f^{\prime}(3)}=\tan \frac{3 \pi}{4}=1 \\
\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(3)=1
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $(-4,5)$ is one vertex and $7 x-y+8=0$ is one diagonal of a square, then the equation of second diagonal is
questions.solution:
One vertex of square is $(-4,5)$ and equation of one diagonal is $7 x-y+8=0$
Diagonal of a square are perpendicular and bisect each other
Let the equation of the other diagonal be $y=m x$ $+\mathrm{c}$ where $\mathrm{m}$ is the slope of the line and $\mathrm{c}$ is the $y-$ intercept.
Since this line passes through $(-4,5)$
$$
\therefore \quad 5=-4 \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{c}
$$
Since this line is at right angle to the line
$$
7 x-y+8=0 \text { or } y=7 x+8 \text {, having slope }=7 \text {, }
$$
$$
\therefore \quad 7 \times \mathrm{m}=-1 \text { or } \mathrm{m}=\frac{-1}{7}
$$
Putting this value of $m$ in equation (i) we get
$$
5=-4 \times\left(\frac{-1}{7}\right)+\mathrm{c}
$$
or $5=\frac{4}{7}+\mathrm{c} \quad$ or $\quad \mathrm{c}=5-\frac{4}{7}=\frac{31}{7}$
Hence equation of the other diagonal is
$$
y=-\frac{1}{7} \mathrm{x}+\frac{31}{7} \text { or } 7 \mathrm{y}=-\mathrm{x}+31
$$
or $x+7 y-31=0$ or $x+7 y=31$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $3 \sin \theta+5 \cos \theta=5$, then the value of $5 \sin \theta-3 \cos \theta$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$3 \sin \theta+5 \cos \theta=5 \Rightarrow 3 \sin \theta=5(1-\cos \theta)$ $\Rightarrow 3.2 \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cos \frac{\theta}{2}=5.2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}$ $\left(\because \sin \theta=2 \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cos \frac{\theta}{2}\right)$ and $\left.1-\cos \theta=2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}\right)$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \tan \frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{3}{5} \\
\text { Now, } 5 \sin \theta-3 \cos \theta \\
=5 \cdot \frac{2 \tan \frac{\theta}{2}}{1+\tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}-3 \cdot \frac{1-\tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}{1+\tan ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}} \\
=5 \cdot \frac{2 \cdot \frac{3}{5}}{\left(1+\frac{9}{25}\right)}-3 \cdot \frac{\left(1-\frac{9}{25}\right)}{\left(1+\frac{9}{25}\right)} \\
=\frac{6-3 \cdot \frac{16}{25}}{1+\frac{9}{25}}=\frac{150-48}{34}=\frac{102}{34}=3
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line joining two points $A(2,0), B(3,1)$ is rotated about $A$ in anti-clockwise direction through an angle of $15^{\circ}$. The equation of the line in the now position, is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l} \text { Slope of } \mathrm{AB}=\frac{1}{1} \\ \Rightarrow \tan \theta=\mathrm{m}_{1}=1 \text { or } \theta=45^{\circ} \end{array}$ Thus, slope of new line is $\tan \left(45^{\circ}+15^{\circ}\right)$ $=\tan 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}$ $(\because$ it is rotated anti-clockwise, so the angle will be $45^{\circ}+15^{\circ}=60^{\circ}$ )
Hence, the equation is $y=\sqrt{3} x+c$ But it passes through $(2,0)$ So, $c=-2 \sqrt{3}$ Thus, required equation is $y=\sqrt{3} x-2 \sqrt{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The length of longer diagonal of the parallelogram constructed on $5 a+2 b$ and $a-3 b$, if it is given that $|\mathrm{a}|=2 \sqrt{2},|\mathrm{~b}|=3$ and the angle between $a$ and $b$ is $\frac{\pi}{4}$, is
questions.solution:
Given : $|\mathbf{a}|=2 \sqrt{2},|\mathbf{b}|=3$
One diagonal is $5 \mathbf{a}+2 \mathbf{b}+\mathbf{a}-3 \mathbf{b}=6 \mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}$
Length of one diagonal
$\begin{array}{l}
=|6 \mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}| \\
=\sqrt{36 \mathbf{a}^{2}+\mathbf{b}^{2}-2 \times 6|\mathbf{a}| \cdot|\mathbf{b}| \cdot \cos 45^{\circ}} \\
=\sqrt{36 \times 8+9-12 \times 2 \sqrt{2} \times 3 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}} \\
=\sqrt{288+9-12 \times 6}=\sqrt{225}=15
\end{array}$
other diagonal is $(5 \mathbf{a}+2 \mathbf{b})-(\mathbf{a}-3 \mathbf{b})$ $=4 \mathbf{a}+5 \mathbf{b}$
Its length is
$\begin{array}{l}
=\sqrt{(4 a)^{2}+\left(5 b^{2}\right)+2 \times|4 a||5 b| \cos 45^{\circ}} \\
=\sqrt{16 \times 8+25 \times 9+40 \times 6}=\sqrt{593}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution: $\cos 2 \alpha+\cos 2 \beta+\cos 2 \gamma$ $\quad+\sin ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \beta+\sin ^{2} \gamma$ $\quad\left(\cos ^{2} \alpha-\sin ^{2} \alpha\right)+\left(\cos ^{2} \beta-\sin ^{2} \beta\right)$ $\quad\left(\cos ^{2} \gamma-\sin ^{2} \gamma\right)+\sin ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \beta+\sin ^{2} \gamma$ $=\cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In a triangle $A B C$ as shown in the diagram, where $A B=5, A C=10$ and $B D=1$, then perimeter of $\triangle A B C$ is
questions.solution:
$\because A D$ divides $\angle B A C$ is two equal parts.
So, by angle bisector formula
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{B D}{D C}=\frac{A B}{A C} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{D C}=\frac{5}{10} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & D C=2
\end{aligned}$
Therefore, $B C=B D+D C=1+2=3$
Hence, perimeter of $\triangle A B C=A B+B C+C A$
$=5+3+10=18$

questions.q: The solution of the differential equation $y d x+\left(x-y^{3}\right) d y=0$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $u=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{x^{3}+y^{3}}{x+y}\right\}$, then $x \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}+y \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}=$
questions.solution:
Euler's theorem $x \frac{\partial z}{\partial x}+y \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}=n z$
$\begin{array}{l}
\text { Given : } U=\tan ^{-1} \frac{x^{3}+y^{3}}{x+y} \\
\Rightarrow \tan U=\frac{x^{3}+y^{3}}{x+y}=z \text { (let) } \\
n=3-1=2 \\
\therefore x \frac{\partial z}{\partial x}+y \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}=2 z
\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\partial}{\partial \mathrm{x}} \tan \mathrm{U}+\mathrm{y} \frac{\partial}{\partial \mathrm{y}} \cdot \tan \mathrm{U}=2 \tan \mathrm{U}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \cdot \sec ^{2} \mathrm{U} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathrm{U}}{\partial \mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{y} \cdot \sec ^{2} \mathrm{U} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathrm{U}}{\partial \mathrm{y}}=2 \tan \mathrm{U}$
$\Rightarrow \sec ^{2} \mathrm{U} \cdot\left[\mathrm{x} \frac{\partial \mathrm{U}}{\partial \mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{y} \frac{\partial \mathrm{U}}{\partial \mathrm{y}}\right]=2 \tan \mathrm{U}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathrm{U}}{\partial \mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{y} \frac{\partial \mathrm{U}}{\partial \mathrm{y}}=2 \cdot \frac{\sin \mathrm{U}}{\cos \mathrm{U}} \cdot \cos ^{2} \mathrm{U}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\partial \mathrm{U}}{\partial \mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{y} \frac{\partial \mathrm{U}}{\partial \mathrm{y}}=\sin 2 \mathrm{U}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: What is the least value of $\mathrm{k}$ such that the function $\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{kx}+1$ is strictly increasing on $(1,2)$
questions.solution:
Let $f(x)=x^{2}+k x+1$ 
$f^{\prime}(x)=2 x+k$
$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is strictly increasing on $(1,2)$
if $f^{\prime}(x)>0$ for $x \in(1,2)$
$\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{k}>0 \quad$ for $\mathrm{x} \in(1,2)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}>-2 \mathrm{x} \quad$ for $\mathrm{x} \in(1,2)$
Now, $1 < x < 2 \Rightarrow 2 < 2 x < 4$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow-2>-2 x>-4 \\
\Rightarrow-4 < -2 x < -2 \\
\Rightarrow k \geq-2
\end{array}$
Hence least value of $\mathrm{k}=-2$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The length of three sides of a trapezium are equal, each being 10 cms . Then, the maximum area $\left(\mathrm{cm}^2\right)$ of the trapezium is
questions.solution: Let, $\quad A P=x \mathrm{~cm}$ $\because \triangle A P D \sim \triangle B Q C$ So, $B Q=x \mathrm{~cm}$ Now, Area $(A)=\frac{1}{2}(10+(10+2 x)) \times$ height In $\triangle A P D, D P=h=\sqrt{10^2-x^2}=\sqrt{100-x^2}$ $\therefore \quad A(x)=\frac{1}{2}(2(10+x))\left(\sqrt{100-x^2}\right)$ $=(10+x) \sqrt{100-y^2}$ For maximum area, find $A^{\prime}(x)$ $\begin{aligned} A^{\prime}(x) & =(10+x)\left(\frac{-2 x}{2 \sqrt{100-x^2}}\right)+\left(\sqrt{100-x^2}\right) \\ & =\frac{-x(10+x)+100-x^2}{\sqrt{100-x^2}} \\ & =\frac{-2 x^2-10 x+100}{\sqrt{100-x^2}} \end{aligned}$ Now, $A^{\prime}(x)=0$ gives $2 x^2+10 x-100=0 \Rightarrow x=5 \text { and }-10$ $\because x \neq-10$ (Negative) $\therefore$ Area will be maximum at $x=5$ So, $\begin{aligned} A(5) & =(10+5) \sqrt{100-25}=15 \sqrt{75} \\ & =75 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of straight line through the intersection of the lines $x-2 y=1$ and $x+3 y=2$ and parallel $3 x+4 y=0$ is
questions.solution:
The intersection point of lines $x-2 y=1$ and $x+3 y=2$ is $\left(\frac{7}{5}, \frac{1}{5}\right)$

Since, required line is parallel to $3 x+4 y=0$.
Therefore, the slope of required line is $-\frac{3}{4}$.
$\therefore$ Equation of required line which passes through $\left(\frac{7}{5}, \frac{1}{5}\right)$ is given by
$$
\begin{array}{l}
y-\frac{1}{5}=-\frac{3}{4}\left(x-\frac{7}{5}\right) \\
\Rightarrow \frac{3 x}{4}+y=\frac{21}{20}+\frac{1}{5} \\
\Rightarrow 3 x+4 y-5=0
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A straight line through the point $\mathrm{A}(3,4)$ is such that its intercept between the axes is bisected at A, its equation is
questions.solution:
A is mid point of line PQ. $$ \therefore 3=\frac{\mathrm{a}+0}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=6 $$ and $4=\frac{0+b}{2} \Rightarrow b=8$
Thus, equation of line is $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{x}{6}+\frac{y}{8}=1 \\ \Rightarrow 4 x+3 y=24 \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A spherical balloon is expanding. If the radius is increasing at the rate of 2 centimeters per minute, the rate at which the volume increases (in cubic centimeters per minute) when the radius is 5 , centimetres is
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{r}$ and $\mathrm{V}$ be the respectively radius and volume of the balloon. Let $\mathrm{t}$ represents the time. The rate of increament in radius is $\frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{minute} .$ The volume of the balloon is given by
$\mathrm{V}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^{3}$
Differentiating w.r. to $\mathrm{t}$, we get
$\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(3 \mathrm{r}^{2} \frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)$
Substituting the values of and $\frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dt}}$, we get
$\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(3 \times 5^{2} \times 2\right)=200 \pi \mathrm{cm}^{3} /$ minute

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the lines $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{k}}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{1}$ intersect, then the value of $\mathrm{k}$ is
questions.solution:
We have the lines
$$
\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}...(i)
$$
and $\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-k}{2}=\frac{z}{1}$...(ii)
Let a point $(2 r+1,3 r-1,4 r+1)$ be on the line Eq. (i). If this is an intersection point of both the lines, then it will lie on Eq. (ii), also
$$
\therefore \frac{2 r+1-3}{1}=\frac{3 r-1-k}{2}=\frac{4 r+1}{1}...(iii)
$$
Taking first and third part of eq. (iii), we get $2 r-2=4 r+1$
$$
\Rightarrow r=-\frac{3}{2}
$$
Taking second and third part of eq. (iii), we get
$$
\begin{array}{l}
3 r-1-k=8 r+2 \\
\Rightarrow 3 r-1-k-8 r-2=0 \Rightarrow k=-5 r-3 \\
\Rightarrow k=-5\left(-\frac{3}{2}\right)-3 \quad\left(\because r=-\frac{3}{2}\right) \\
\Rightarrow k=\frac{15}{2}-3 \Rightarrow k=\frac{9}{2}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A line passes through $(2,2)$ and is perpendicular to the line $3 x+y=3$. What is its $y$-intercept?
questions.solution:
Equation of line perpendicular to $3 x+y-3=0$ is $x-3 y+c=0$
Since, it passes through $(2,2)$
$$
\therefore \mathrm{c}=4
$$
$\therefore$ Equation of line perpendicular to $3 x+y-3=0$ is $x-3 y+4=0$
For $y$ - intercept, put $x=0$
$$
\therefore y=\frac{4}{3}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the plane which bisects the angle between the planes $3 x-6 y+2 z+5=0$ and $4 x-12 y+3 z-3=0$ which contains the origin is
questions.solution:
Equation of plane bisecting the angle containing origin is (making constant term of same sign)
$$
\frac{-3 x+6 y-2 z-5}{\sqrt{3^{2}+6^{2}+2^{2}}}=+\left[\frac{4 x-12 y+3 z-3}{\sqrt{4^{2}+12^{2}+3^{2}}}\right]
$$
or $\frac{-3 x+6 y-2 z-5}{7}=\frac{4 x-12 y+3 z-3}{13}$
or $67 x-162 y+47 z+44=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the line passing through \((1,2,3)\) and parallel to the planes \(r \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=5\) and r. \((3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=6\) is
questions.solution:
The equation of line passing through \((1,2\),
3) and parallel to \(b\) is given by
\(r=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda\left(b_1 \hat{i}+b_2 \hat{j}+b_3 \hat{k}\right)\) ...(i)
The equations of the given planes are
\(\begin{aligned}
& r \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=5 \quad ...(ii)\\
& \text { and } r \cdot(3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})=6 \quad ...(iii)
\end{aligned}\)
The line in eq. (i) and plane in eq. (ii) are parallel. Therefore, the normal to the plane of eq. (ii) and the given line are perpendicular.
\(\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot \lambda\left(b_1 \hat{i}+b_2 \hat{j}+b_3 \hat{k}\right)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \lambda\left(b_1-b_2+2 b_3\right)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad\left(b_1-b_2+2 b_3\right)=0 \quad ...(iv)
\end{aligned}\)
Similarly,
\(\begin{aligned}
& (3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \cdot \lambda\left(b_1 \hat{i}+b_2 \hat{j}+b_3 \hat{k}\right)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \lambda\left(3 b_1+b_2+b_3\right)=0 \quad ...(v)
\end{aligned}\)
From eqs. (iv) and (v), we obtain
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{b_1}{(-1) \times 1-1 \times 2}=\frac{b_2}{2 \times 3-1 \times 1}=\frac{b_3}{1 \times 1-3(-1)} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{b_1}{-3}=\frac{b_2}{5}=\frac{b_3}{4}
\end{aligned}\)
Therefore, the direction ratios of \(b\) are \(-3,5\) and 4 .
\(\therefore \mathrm{b}=\mathrm{b}_1 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{b}_2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{b}_3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}=-3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}\)
Substituting the value of \(b\) in eq. (i), we obtain
\(r=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(-3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k})\)
This is the equation of the required line.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the angle $\theta$ between the line $\frac{x+1}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}$ $=\frac{z-2}{2}$ and the plane $2 x-y+\sqrt{\lambda} z+4=0$ is such that $\sin \theta=\frac{1}{3}$ then the value of $\lambda$ is
questions.solution:
If $\theta$ is the angle between line and plane then $\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)$ is the angle between line and normal to plane given by
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)=\frac{(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\sqrt{\lambda} \hat{k})}{3 \sqrt{4+1+\lambda}} \\
& \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)=\frac{2-2+2 \sqrt{\lambda}}{3 \times \sqrt{5}+\lambda} \\
& \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{2 \sqrt{\lambda}}{3 \sqrt{5}+\lambda}=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow 4 \lambda=5+\lambda \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{5}{3} .
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A sphere of constant radius \(2 \mathrm{k}\) passes through the origin and meets the axes in A, B, C. The locus of the centroid of the tetrahedron \(\mathrm{OABC}\) is
questions.solution:
Let co-ordinate of the points A, B, C be \((\mathrm{a}, 0,0),(0, \mathrm{~b}, 0)\) and \((0,0, \mathrm{c})\) respectively. Then equation of the sphere \(\mathrm{OABC}\) is
\(x^2+y^2+z^2-a x-b y-c z=0\)
Radius of this sphere is equal to \(2 \mathrm{k}\)
\(\therefore \mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2+\mathrm{c}^2=4(2 \mathrm{k})^2=16 \mathrm{k}^2\)
Also let (z,y, z) be the co-ordinates of the centroid of the tetrahedron \(\mathrm{OABC}\), then
\(\mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{y}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~b}, \mathrm{z}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{c}\)
so that \(\mathrm{a}=4 \mathrm{x}, \mathrm{b}=4 \mathrm{y}, \mathrm{c}=4 \mathrm{z}\)
Eliminating a, b, c from (1) and (2), the required locus is
\(\begin{aligned}
& 16 x^2+16 y^2+16 z^2=16 k^2 \\
& \text { or } x^2+y^2+z^2=k^2
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the distance between the planes $8 x+12 y-14 z=2$ and $4 x+6 y-7 z=2$ can be expressed as $\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}$, then the value of $\frac{N(N+1)}{2}$ is
questions.solution:
$P_1: 4 x+6 y-7 z=2$
$P_2: 8 x+12 y-14 z=2 \Rightarrow 4 x+6 y-7 z=1$
Distance between $P_1$ and $P_2$
$\begin{aligned}
& =\left|\frac{d_2-d_1}{\sqrt{a_1 a_2+b_1 b_2+c_1 c_2}}\right| \\
& =\left|\frac{2-1}{\sqrt{4 \times 4+6 \times 6+(-7) \times(-7)}}\right| \\
& =\frac{1}{\sqrt{16+36+49}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{101}}
\end{aligned}$
By comparing with $\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}, N=101$
Therefore, $\frac{N(N+1)}{2}=\frac{101 \times 102}{2}=5151$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If vector equation of the line $\frac{x-2}{2}=\frac{2 y-5}{-3}=z+1$, is
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\left(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{5}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}\right)+\lambda\left(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{3}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{p} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right)$ then $\mathrm{p}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
The given line is $\frac{x-2}{2}=\frac{2 y-5}{-3}=z+1$,
$$
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x-2}{2}=\frac{y-\frac{5}{2}}{-\frac{3}{2}}=\frac{z+1}{0}
$$
This shows that the given line passes through the point $\left(2, \frac{5}{2},-1\right)$ and has direction ratios $\left(2, \frac{-3}{2}, 0\right)$. Thus, given line passes through the point having position vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{5}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and is parallel to the vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=\left(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{3}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-0 \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right) \cdot$ So, its vector equation is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\left(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{5}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}\right)+\lambda\left(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{3}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-0 \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right)$ 
Hence, $\mathrm{p}=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of ' $a$ ' for which the lines $\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-9}{2}=\frac{z-13}{3}$ and $\frac{x-a}{-1}=\frac{y-7}{2}$ $=\frac{z+2}{-3}$ intersect, is
questions.solution:
Let the two lines be
$\frac{x-\alpha_1}{l_1}=\frac{y-\beta_1}{m_1}=\frac{z-\gamma_1}{n_1}$.....(i)
and
$\frac{x-\alpha_2}{I_2}=\frac{y-\beta_2}{m_2}=\frac{z-\gamma_2}{n_2}$.....(ii)
These lines will be coplaner, if
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\alpha_2-\alpha_1 & \beta_2-\beta_1 & \gamma_2-\gamma_1 \\
l_1 & m_1 & n_1 \\
l_2 & m_2 & n_2
\end{array}\right|=0$
Therefore, from the given lines,
$\begin{aligned}
& \left|\begin{array}{ccc}
2-a & 9-7 & 13+2 \\
1 & 2 & 3 \\
-1 & 2 & -3
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
2-a & 2 & 15 \\
1 & 2 & 3 \\
-1 & 2 & -3
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
& \Rightarrow(2-a)(-6-6)-2(-3+3)+15(2+2)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad-24+12 a+60=0 \Rightarrow 12 a=-36 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad a=-3
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the right bisector plane of the segment joining \((2,3,4)\) and \((6,7,8)\) is
questions.solution:
If the given points be \(\mathrm{A}(2,3,4)\) and \(\mathrm{B}(6\), \(7,8)\), then their mid-point \(\mathrm{N}(4,5,6)\) must lie on the plane. The direction ratios of \(A B\) are \(4,4,4\), i.e. \(1,1,1\).
\(\therefore \quad\) The required plane passes through \(\mathrm{N}(4,5\), \(6)\) and is normal to \(\mathrm{AB}\). Thus its equation is \(1(x-4)+1(y-5)+1(z-6)=0 \Rightarrow x+y+z=15\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: What is the angle between the line \(6 x=4 y=3 z\) and the plane \(3 x+2 y-3 z=4\) ?
questions.solution:
The equation of the given line is
\(6 x=4 y=3 z\)
which is written in symmetric form as
\(\frac{x-0}{1 / 6}=\frac{y-0}{1 / 4}=\frac{z-0}{1 / 3}\)
Direction ratios of this line are \(\left(\frac{1}{6}, \frac{1}{4}, \frac{1}{3}\right)\) and equation of the plane is \(3 x+2 y-3 z-4=0\) If \(\theta\) be the angle between line and plane, then direction ratios of the normal to this plane is (3, \(2,-3\) )
\(\begin{aligned}
\sin \theta & =\left|\frac{a_1 a_2+b_1 b_2+c_1 c_2}{\sqrt{a_1^2+b_1^2+c_1^2} \sqrt{a_2^2+b_2^2+c_2^2}}\right| \\
& =\left|\frac{\frac{1}{6} \times 3+\frac{1}{4} \times 2+\frac{1}{3}(-3)}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{36}+\frac{1}{16}+\frac{1}{9}} \sqrt{9+4+9}}\right|=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \theta & =0^{\circ}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The line which passes through the origin and intersect the two lines $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+3}{4}=\frac{z-5}{3}, \frac{x-4}{2}=\frac{y+3}{3}=\frac{z-14}{4}$, is
questions.solution:
Let the line be $\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{\mathrm{c}}......(i)$
If line (i) intersects with the line $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+3}{4}=\frac{z-5}{3}$, then
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
a & b & c \\
2 & 4 & 3 \\
4 & -3 & 14
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad 9 a-7 b-10 c=0......(ii)
\end{array}
$$
from (i) and (ii), we have
$\frac{a}{1}=\frac{b}{-3}=\frac{c}{5}$
$\therefore \quad$ The line is $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{-3}=\frac{z}{5}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let the line $\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-5}=\frac{z+2}{2}$ lie in the plane $x+3 y-\alpha z+\beta=0$. Then $(\alpha, \beta)$ equals
questions.solution:
$\because$ The line $\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-5}=\frac{z+2}{2}$ lie in the plane
$$
x+3 y-\alpha z+\beta=0
$$
$\therefore \operatorname{Pt}(2,1,-2)$ lies on the plane i.e. $2+3+$ $2 \alpha+\beta=0$
or $2 \alpha+\beta+5=0$
$\ldots .(\mathrm{i})$
Also normal to plane will be perpendicular to line,
$\therefore 3 \times 1-5 \times 3+2 \times(-\alpha)=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=-6$
From equation (i) then, $\beta=7$
$\therefore(\alpha, \beta)=(-6,7)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The radius of the sphere
$x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=49,2 x+3 y-z-5 \sqrt{14}=0$ is
questions.solution:
The sphere $x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=49$ has centre at the origin $(0,0,0)$ and radius 7.
Disance of the plane $2 x+3 y-z-5 \sqrt{14}=0$ from the origin $$ \begin{array}{l} =\frac{|2(0)+3(0)-(0)-5 \sqrt{14}|}{\sqrt{2^{2}+3^{2}+(-1)^{2}}} \\ =\frac{|-5 \sqrt{14}|}{\sqrt{14}}=\frac{5 \sqrt{14}}{\sqrt{14}}=5 \end{array} $$ Thus in Figure; $\mathrm{OP}=7, \mathrm{ON}=5$ $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{NP}^{2} &=\mathrm{OP}^{2}-\mathrm{ON}^{2}=(7)^{2}-(5)^{2} \\ &=49-25=24 \end{aligned} $$ $\therefore \quad N P=2 \sqrt{6} \quad$ Hence the radius of the circle $$ =\mathrm{NP}=2 \sqrt{6} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Atetrahedron has vertices at $\mathrm{O}(0,0,0), \mathrm{A}(1,2,1)$ $B(2,1,3)$ and $C(-1,1,2)$. Then the angle between the faces $\mathrm{OAB}$ and $\mathrm{ABC}$ will be

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If a line segment $O P$ makes angles of $\frac{\pi}{4}$ and $\frac{\pi}{3}$ with $X$-axis and $Y$-axis, respectively. Then, the direction cosines are
questions.solution:
$\cos \alpha, \cos \beta, \cos \gamma$ i.e. $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.
Let $\alpha, \beta$ and $\gamma$ be the angles made by the line segment OP with X-axis, Y-axis and Z-axis, respectively.
Given: $\alpha=\frac{\pi}{4}$ and $\beta=\frac{\pi}{3}$
We know that, $\cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1$
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \quad \cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{4}+\cos ^{2} \frac{\pi}{3}+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^{2} \gamma=\frac{1}{4} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \cos \gamma=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\
\therefore \quad \gamma=\frac{\pi}{4}
\end{array}$
Hence, direction cosines are $\cos \alpha, \cos \beta, \cos \gamma$ i.e. $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If a plane passing through the point $(2,2,1)$ and is perpendicular to the planes $3 x+2 y+4 z+1=0$ and $2 x+y+3 z+2=0$. Then, the equation of the plane is
questions.solution:
Equation of plane passing through $(2,2,1)$ is
$\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{a}(\mathrm{x}-2)+\mathrm{b}(\mathrm{y}-2)+\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{z}-1)=0 \\ & \text { Since, above plane is perpendicular to } \\ & 3 \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{y}+4 \mathrm{z}+1=0 \\ & \text { and } 2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+3 \mathrm{z}+2=0 \\ \therefore & 3 \mathrm{a}+2 \mathrm{~b}+4 \mathrm{c}=0 \\ & \text { and } 2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+3 \mathrm{c}=0 \\ & {\left[\because \text { for perpendicular, } \mathrm{a}_{1} \mathrm{a}_{2}\right.} \\ & \left.\quad+\mathrm{b}_{1} \mathrm{~b}_{2}+\mathrm{c}_{1} \mathrm{c}_{2}=0\right]\end{array}$
On multiplying eq. (iii) by 2 , we get $4 a+2 b+6 c=0$
On subtracting eq. (iv) from eq. (ii), we get
$\Rightarrow \quad c=\frac{-a}{2}$
On putting $\mathrm{c}=\frac{-\mathrm{a}}{2}$ in eq. (iii), we get $\mathrm{b}$
$=\frac{-\mathrm{a}}{2}$
On putting $b=\frac{-a}{2}$ and $c=\frac{-a}{2}$ in eq. (i),
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { we get } a(x-2)-\frac{a}{2}(y-2)-\frac{a}{2}(z-1)=0 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{a}{2}[2(x-2)-(y-2)-(z-1)]=0 \\
\Rightarrow & 2 x-4-y+2-z+1=0 \\
\Rightarrow & 2 x-y-z-1=0
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the points $(1,2,3)$ and $(2,-1,0)$ lie on the opposite sides of the plane $2 x+3 y-2 z=k$, then
questions.solution:
The points $(1,2,3)$ and $(2,-1,0)$ lie on the opposite sides of the plane $2 x+3 y-2 z-k=0$
So, $(2+6-6-\mathrm{k})(4-3-\mathrm{k}) < 0$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow(\mathrm{k}-1)(\mathrm{k}-2) < 0 \\
\therefore 1 < \mathrm{k} < 2
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If a plane meets the coordinate axes at $A, B$ and $C$ such that the centroid of the triangle is $(1,2,4)$, then the equation of the plane is
questions.solution:
Let the equation of the plane is
$\frac{x}{\alpha}+\frac{y}{\beta}+\frac{z}{\gamma}=1$
Then, $\mathrm{A}(\alpha, 0,0), \mathrm{B}(0, \beta, 0)$ and $\mathrm{C}(0,0, \gamma)$ are the points on the coordinate axes, The centroid of the triangle is $(1,2,4)$.
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \frac{\alpha}{3}=1 \Rightarrow \alpha=3 \\
\frac{\beta}{3}=2 \Rightarrow \beta=6
\end{array}$
and $\frac{\gamma}{3}=4 \Rightarrow \gamma=12$
$\therefore \quad$ The equation of the plane is
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{6}+\frac{z}{12}=1 \\
\Rightarrow & 4 x+2 y+z=12
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $P$ is a fixed point $(a, a, a)$ on a line through the origin equally inclined to the axes, then anyplane through $P$ perpendicular to $O P$, makes intercepts on the axes, the sum of whose reciprocals is equal to
questions.solution:
Since, the line is equally inclined to the axes and passes through the origin, its direction ratios are $1,1,1$.
So, its equation is $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z}{1}$.
A point $P$ on it is given by $(a, a, a)$. So, equation of the plane through $P(a, a, a)$ and perpendicular to OP is
$1(x-a)+1(y-a)+1(z-a)=0$
$(\because$ OP is normal to the plane $)$
i.e., $x+y+z=3 a$
$\Rightarrow \frac{x}{3 a}+\frac{y}{3 a}+\frac{z}{3 a}=1$
Intercepts on axes are $3 \mathrm{a}, 3 \mathrm{a}$ and $3 \mathrm{a}$, therefore sum of reciprocals of these intercepts.
$=\frac{1}{3 a}+\frac{1}{3 a}+\frac{1}{3 a}=\frac{1}{a}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Two lines $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}$ and $\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-k}{2}=z$ intersect at a point, if $k$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{3}=\frac{z-1}{4}=r(\text { say }) \\
\Rightarrow x=2 r+1, y=3 r-1, z=4 r+1
\end{array}$
Since, the two lines intersect.
So, putting above values in second line, we get
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{2 r+1-3}{1}=\frac{3 r-1-k}{2}=\frac{4 r+1}{1} \\
2 r-2=4 r+1
\end{array}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & \mathrm{r}=-3 / 2 \\ & \text { Also } 3 \mathrm{r}-1-\mathrm{k}=8 \mathrm{r}+2 \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{k}=-5 \mathrm{r}-3=\frac{15}{2}-3=\frac{9}{2} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The distance of the point $(-5,-5,-10)$ from the point of intersection of the line $\mathrm{r} .=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\lambda(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$ and the plane $r \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})=5$ is
questions.solution:
Given equation of line is
$$
\begin{gathered}
r=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \\
(x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k})=(2+3 \lambda) \hat{i}+(-1+4 \lambda) \hat{j}+(2+2 \lambda) \hat{k}
\end{gathered}
$$
Any point on the line is
$$
(2+3 \lambda,-1+4 \lambda, 2+2 \lambda)
$$
Since it also lie on the plane $r \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { So, }[(2+3 \lambda) \hat{i}+(-1+4 \lambda) \hat{j}+(2+2 \lambda) \hat{k}] \cdot(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k})=5 \\
& \Rightarrow 2+3 \lambda+1-4 \lambda+2+2 \lambda=5 \Rightarrow \lambda=0
\end{aligned}
$$
Therefore, coordinate of the point of intersection of line and plane is $(2,-1,2)$.
$\therefore$ Distance
$$
\mathrm{d}=\sqrt{(2+1)^2+(-1+5)^2+(2+10)^2}=13
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The vector equation of the symmetrical form of equation of straight line $\frac{x-5}{3}=\frac{y+4}{7}=\frac{z-6}{2}$ is
questions.solution:
$\frac{x-x_{1}}{a}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{b}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{c}$ have vector form
$$
=\left(\mathrm{x}_{1} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{y}_{1} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{z}_{1} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right)+\lambda(\mathrm{a} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{b} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\mathrm{c} \hat{\mathrm{k}})
$$
Required equation in vector form is
$$
\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\mu(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+7 \mathrm{j}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If a plane meets the coordinate axes at $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ in such a way that the centroid of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$ is at the point $(1,2,3)$, then equation of the plane is
questions.solution:
Let the equation of the required plane be $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}+\frac{z}{c}=1$
This meets the coordinate axes at $A, B$ and $\mathrm{C}$, the coordinates of the centroid of $\triangle \mathrm{ABC}$
are $\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{3}, \frac{\mathrm{b}}{3}, \frac{\mathrm{c}}{3}\right)$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \frac{\mathrm{a}}{3}=1, \frac{\mathrm{b}}{3}=2, \frac{\mathrm{c}}{3}=3 \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=3, \mathrm{~b}=6, \mathrm{c}=9
\end{array}
$$
Hence, the equation of the plane is
$$
\frac{x}{3}+\frac{y}{6}+\frac{z}{9}=1
$$

questions.q: If $z=\frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{1+i \sqrt{3}}$, then arg $(z)$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Domain of the function $f(\mathrm{x})=\log _{x} \cos x$, is
questions.solution:
We have
$f(x)=\log _{x} \cos x$ $f(x)$ is defined for $\cos x>0$ $x>0, x \neq 1$ $\cos x>0 \Rightarrow-\frac{\pi}{2} < x < \frac{\pi}{2}$ Also, $x>0, x \neq 1$ $\therefore$ Domain of $f$ is $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)-\{1\}$
$\therefore$ Domain of $f$ is $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)-\{1\}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The angle between the lines whose direction cosines satisfy the equations $l+m+n=0$, $l^{2}+m^{2}-n^{2}=0$ is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
 l+m+n=0, \Rightarrow \\
\text { and } l^{2}+m^{2}-n^{2}=0 \\
\therefore(-m-n)^{2}+m^{2}-n^{2}=0 \\
\Rightarrow 2 m^{2}+2 m n=0 \\
\Rightarrow 2 m(m+n)=0 \\
\Rightarrow m=0 \text { or } m+n=0 \\
\text { If } m=0, \text { then } l= \\
\therefore \frac{l_{1}}{-1}=\frac{m_{1}}{0}=\frac{n}{1}
\end{array}
$$
and if $m+n=0 \Rightarrow m=-n$, then $l=0$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \frac{l_{2}}{0}=\frac{m_{2}}{-1}=\frac{n_{2}}{1} \\
\text { ie., }\left(l_{1}, m_{1}, n_{1}\right)=(-1,0,1) \\
\text { and }\left(l_{2}, m_{2}, n_{2}\right)=(0,-1,1) \\
\therefore \cos \theta=\frac{0+0+1}{\sqrt{1+0+1} \sqrt{0+1+1}}=\frac{1}{2} \\
\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{3}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\mathrm{m}_{1}, \mathrm{~m}_{2}, \mathrm{~m}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{4}$ are respectively the magnitudes of the vectors
$\vec{a}_{1}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{a}_{2}=3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}$
$\vec{a}_{3}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}, \text { and } \vec{a}_{4}=-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$
then the correct order of $\mathrm{m}_{1}, \mathrm{~m}_{2}, \mathrm{~m}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{4}$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The perimeter of the triangle with vertices at $(1,0,0),(0,1,0)$ and $(0,0,1)$ is
questions.solution:
Let $A=(1,0,0), B=(0,1,0)$ and $C=(0,0,1)$
So, $A B=\sqrt{(0-1)^{2}+(1-0)^{2}+0^{2}}=\sqrt{2}$
$B C=\sqrt{0^{2}+(0-1)^{2}+(1-0)^{2}}=\sqrt{2}$ and $C A=\sqrt{(1-0)^{2}+0^{2}+(0-1)^{2}}=\sqrt{2}$ $\therefore$ Perimeter of triangle
$\therefore$ Perimeter of triangle $=A B+B C+C A=\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{2}=3 \sqrt{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The shortest distance between the straight lines through the points $\mathrm{A}_{1}=(6,2,2)$ and $A_{2}=(-4,0,-1)$, in the directions of $(1,-2,2)$ and $(3,-2,-2)$ is
questions.solution:
Equation of first line,
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{x-6}{1}=\frac{y-2}{-2}=\frac{z-2}{2}=k \text { (say) } \\
\therefore x=k+6, y=-2 k+2, z=2 k+2
\end{array}$
Hence, general point on the first line, $\mathrm{P} \equiv(\mathrm{k}+6,-2 \mathrm{k}+2,2 \mathrm{k}+2)$
Equation of second line,
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{\mathrm{x}+4}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}}{-2}=\frac{\mathrm{Z}+1}{-2}=l \text { (say) }\\
\therefore \mathrm{x}=3 l-4, \mathrm{y}=-2 l, \mathrm{z}=-2 l-1
\end{array}$
Hence, general point on the second line, $Q \equiv(3 l-4,-2 l,-2 l-1)$
Direction ratios of $\mathrm{PQ}$ are
$3 l-4-\mathrm{k}-6,-2 l+2 \mathrm{k}-2,-2 l-1-2 \mathrm{k}-2$
i.e. $3 l-\mathrm{k}-10,-2 l+2 \mathrm{k}-2,-2 l-2 \mathrm{k}-3$
Now $|\mathrm{PQ}|$ will be the shortest distance between the two lines if PQ is perpendicular to both the lines. Hence,
$\begin{array}{l}
1(3 l-\mathrm{k}-10)+(-2) \\
(-2 l+2 \mathrm{k}-2)+2(-2 l-2 \mathrm{k}-3)=0
\end{array}$
and $3(3 l-\mathrm{k}-10)+(-2)(-2 l$
$+2 \mathrm{k}-2)+(-2)(-2 \mathrm{l}-2 \mathrm{k}-3)=0$
i.e. $3 l-9 \mathrm{k}=12$ or $l-3 \mathrm{k}=4$ ...(i)
and $17 l-3 \mathrm{k}=20$ ...(ii)
Subtracting equation (i) from (ii), we get
$16 \mathrm{l}=16$
$\therefore l=1$
Putting this value of 1 in equation (i), we get
$\begin{aligned}
-3 \mathrm{k} &=3, \therefore \mathrm{k}=-1 \\
\therefore \mathrm{P} & \equiv(-1+6,-2(-1)+2,2(-1)+2) \\
& \equiv(5,4,0)
\end{aligned}$
Similarly, $\mathrm{Q}=(-1,-2,-3)$
Hence, shortest distance, PQ,
$\begin{array}{l}
=\sqrt{(-1-5)^{2}+(-2-4)^{2}+(-3-0)^{2}} \\
=\sqrt{(-6)^{2}+(-6)^{2}+(-3)^{2}}=\sqrt{36+36+9} \\
=9 \text { units }
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The radius of the sphere $x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=12 x+4 y+3 z$ is
questions.solution:
Given equation of sphere is
$$
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}-12 x-4 y-3 z=0
$$
Centre of sphere is $\left(6,2, \frac{3}{2}\right)$.
$$
\begin{array}{r}
\therefore \text { Radius of sphere }=\sqrt{(6)^{2}+(2)^{2}+\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}} \\
=\sqrt{36+4+\frac{9}{4}}=\sqrt{\frac{169}{4}}=\frac{13}{2}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Probability of getting positive integral roots of the equation $x^{2}-n=0$ for the integer $n, 1 \leq n \leq 40$ is
questions.solution:
$x^{2}-n=0 \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{n}$
For each integral value of $x \in[1,40]$, There is a positive root. Hence for 40 integral values of $\mathrm{x}$ from 1 to 40 , there are 40 positive roots, out of which only six roots $1,2,3,4,5$ and 6 are positive integral roots. Hence, probability of getting positive integral roots $=\frac{6}{40}=\frac{3}{20}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The length of the straight line $\mathrm{x}-3 \mathrm{y}=1$ intercepted by the hyperbola $x^{2}-4 y^{2}=1$ is
questions.solution:
Given : equation of line, $x-3 y=1$ (1)
and hyperbola $x^{2}-4 y^{2}=1$ (2)
putting $x=1+3 y$ in equation $(2)$, we get
$\begin{array}{l}
(1+3 y)^{2}-4 y^{2}=1 \Rightarrow 1+9 y^{2}+6 y-4 y^{2}=1 \\
\Rightarrow 5 y^{2}+6 y=0 \Rightarrow y(5 y+6)=0 \\
\Rightarrow y=0 \text { or } y=-\frac{6}{5} \\
\therefore x=1 \text { for } y=0 \& x=1-\frac{18}{5}=\frac{-13}{5}
\end{array}$
for $y=-6 / 5$
$\therefore$ the line $(1)$ cuts the hyperbola $(2)$ in at most two point.
$\therefore$ co-ordinates of points are $\mathrm{P}(1,0)$ \&
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{Q}\left(-\frac{13}{5},-\frac{6}{5}\right) \\
\therefore \mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{\left(1+\frac{13}{5}\right)^{2}+\left(0+\frac{6}{5}\right)^{2}}
\end{array}$
$=\sqrt{\left(\frac{18}{5}\right)^{2}+\frac{36}{25}}=\sqrt{\frac{324+36}{25}}=\sqrt{\frac{360}{25}}$
$\therefore$ length of straight line $\mathrm{PQ}=\frac{6 \sqrt{10}}{5}$ units.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The curve described parametrically by $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{t}^{2}+2 \mathrm{t}-1, \mathrm{y}=3 \mathrm{t}+5$ represents
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\text { Given } x=t^{2}+2 t-1 \& y=3 t+5 \\
\Rightarrow x=t^{2}+2 t+1-2 \& y=3 t+3+2 \\
\Rightarrow x=(t+1)^{2}-2 \& y=3(t+1)+2  \quad(2)\\
\Rightarrow(t+1)=\sqrt{x+2} \quad \ldots \ldots \ldots(1)
\end{array}$
$\therefore$ Equation (2) becomes [using equation (1)] 
$y=3 \sqrt{x+2}+2$ $\Rightarrow y-2=3 \sqrt{x+2}$
$\Rightarrow y-2=3 \sqrt{x+2}$ squaring both sides, we get
squaring both sid $(y-2)^{2}=9(x+2)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{Y}^{2}=9 \mathrm{X}$ where $\mathrm{Y}=\mathrm{y}-2 \& \mathrm{X}=\mathrm{x}+2$
This equation represents a parabola

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A variable plane remains at constant distance $\mathrm{p}$, from the origin.If it meets coordinate axes at points $A, B, C$ then the locus of the centroid of $\Delta$ $\mathrm{ABC}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{A} \equiv(\mathrm{a}, 0,0), \mathrm{B} \equiv(0, \mathrm{~b}, 0), \mathrm{C} \equiv(0,0, \mathrm{c})$, then equation of the plane is $\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}+\frac{z}{c}=1$
Its distance from the origin, $\frac{1}{a^{2}}+\frac{1}{b^{2}}+\frac{1}{c^{2}}=\frac{1}{p^{2}} \ldots$ (i)
If $(x, y, z)$ be centroid of $\Delta A B C$, then
$$
\mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{3}, \mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{b}}{3}, \mathrm{z}=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{3}...(ii)
$$
Eliminating a,b,c from (i) and (ii) required locus is
$x^{-2}+y^{-2}+z^{-2}=9 p^{-2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A tetrahedron has vertices at $\mathrm{O}(0,0,0)$, $A(1,-2,1), B(-2,1,1)$ and $C(1,-1,2)$. Then, the angle between the faces $O A B$ and $A B C$ will be
questions.solution: Vector perpendicular to face $\mathrm{OAB}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_{1}$ $\begin{array}{l} =\overrightarrow{\mathrm{OA}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{OB}} \\ =(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \times(-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ =(-2-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(-2-1) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(1-4) \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ =-3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{array}$ Vector perpendicular to face $\mathrm{ABC}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{n}}_{2}$. $\begin{array}{l} =\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AC}} \\ =(-3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}) \times(\hat{j}+\hat{k}) \\ =-3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \end{array}$ Since, angle between faces is equal to angle between their normals. $\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \cos \theta=\frac{\vec{n}_{1} \cdot \vec{n}_{2}}{\left|n_{1}\right|\left|n_{2}\right|} \\=& \frac{(-3)(3)+(-3)(3)+(-3)(-3)}{\sqrt{9+9+9} \sqrt{9+9+9}} \\=& \frac{-9-9+9}{\sqrt{27} \sqrt{27}}=-\frac{1}{3} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{-1}{3}\right) \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The shortest distance between the lines $x=y+2=6 z-6$ and $x+1=2 y=-12 z$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The general solution of the differential equation $\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 \frac{d y}{d x}+y=2 e^{3 x}$ is given by
questions.solution:
Given : $\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}+2 \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\mathrm{y}=2 \mathrm{e}^{3 \mathrm{x}}$
The auxiliary equation is
$\mathrm{D}^{2}+2 \mathrm{D}+1=0$ or $\mathrm{m}^{2}+2 \mathrm{~m}+1=0$
$\Rightarrow(\mathrm{m}+1)(\mathrm{m}+1)=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=-1,-1$
i.e., repeated roots
$\therefore$ Complementary function $=\left(\mathrm{c}_{1}+\mathrm{c}_{2} \mathrm{x}\right) \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}$
Now Particular Integral (P.I.)
$\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{1}{D^{2}+2 D+1} \cdot 2 e^{3 x} \quad[D=3] \\
\text { P.I. }=\frac{1}{3^{2}+2.3+1} \cdot 2 \cdot e^{3 x}=\frac{2 e^{3 x}}{16}=\frac{e^{3 x}}{8}
\end{array}$
Solution $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{C} . \mathrm{F} .+\mathrm{P} . \mathrm{I}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\left(\mathrm{c}_{1}+\mathrm{c}_{2} \mathrm{x}\right) \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}+\frac{\mathrm{e}^{3 \mathrm{x}}}{8}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $(2,7,3)$ is one end of a diameter of the sphere $x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}-6 x-12 y-2 z+20=0$, then the coordinates of the other end of the diameter are
questions.solution:
Given sphere is
$\begin{array}{l}
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}-6 x-12 y-2 z+20=0 \\
\text { Centre } \equiv(3,6,1)
\end{array}$
Here, one end of diameter is $(2,7,3)$.
Let the other end of the diameter be $(x, y, z)$
Centre of the sphere will be the mid-point of the ends of diameter.
So, $(3,6,1)=\left(\frac{2+x}{2}, \frac{7+y}{2}, \frac{3+z}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2+\mathrm{x}=6 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=4$ $\Rightarrow \quad 7+\mathrm{y}=12 \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=5$ and $3+\mathrm{z}=2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{z}=-$
and $3+z=2 \Rightarrow z=-1,5,-1)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of positive integral solutions of the equation $x_{1} x_{2} x_{3} x_{4} x_{5}=1050$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $x_{1} x_{2} x_{3} x_{4} x_{5}=1050$
$\Rightarrow x_{1} x_{2} x_{3} x_{4} x_{5}=2 \times 3 \times 5^{2} \times 7$
Each of 2,3 or 7 can take 5 places and $5^{2}$ can be disposed in 15 ways.
Hence, number of positive integral solution $=5^{3} \times 15=1875$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The shortest distance between the lines $\frac{x-7}{3}=\frac{y+4}{-16}=\frac{z-6}{7}$ and $\frac{x-10}{3}=\frac{y-30}{8}=\frac{4-z}{5}$ is
questions.solution:
Given, lines are $\frac{x-7}{3}=\frac{y+4}{-16}=\frac{z-6}{7}$ and $\frac{x-10}{3}=\frac{y-30}{8}=\frac{4-z}{5}$
The vector form of given lines are $\mathrm{r}=7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\lambda(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-16 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$ and $\mathrm{r}=10 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+30 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\mu(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+8 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}})$
On comparing these equations with $\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{a}_{1}+\lambda \mathrm{b}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{a}_{2}+\mu \mathrm{b}_{2}$, we get
$\begin{array}{l}
\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{1}=7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\
\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{2}=10 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+30 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}
\end{array}$
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}_{1}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-16 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}_{2}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+8 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
Shortest distance $=\left|\frac{\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{2}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}_{1} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}_{2}\right)}{\left|\mathrm{b}_{1} \times \mathrm{b}_{2}\right|}\right|$
$=\left|\frac{(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+34 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \cdot(24 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+36 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+72 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{84}\right|$
$=\left|\frac{72+1224-144}{84}\right|=\left|\frac{1152}{84}\right|=\frac{288}{21}$ units

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of $\left(D^{2}+16\right) y=\cos 4 x$ is
questions.solution:
If $\left(D^{2}+16\right) y=\cos 4 x$
Here the auxiliary equation is $\mathrm{m}^{2}+16=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=\pm 4$
$\therefore$ Complementary function
$=(A \cos 4 x+B \sin 4 x)$
\& Particular Integral (P.I.)
$=\frac{1}{\mathrm{D}^{2}+16} \cdot \cos 4 \mathrm{x}$ 
But $\frac{1}{\mathrm{D}^{2}+\mathrm{a}^{2}} \cos \mathrm{ax}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2 \mathrm{a}} \sin \mathrm{ax}$
$\therefore$ P.I. $=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2 \times 4} \cdot \sin 4 \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{8} \sin 4 \mathrm{x}$
$\therefore$ Solution $\mathrm{y}=$ Complementary function + Particular Integral
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{A} \cos 4 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{B} \sin 4 \mathrm{x}+\frac{\mathrm{x}}{8} \sin 4 \mathrm{x}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Solution of the differential equation $x d y-y d x-\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} d x=0$ is
questions.solution:
The given differential equation is
$\begin{array}{l}
x d y-y d x-\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}} d x=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad x d y=\left(y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}\right) d x \\
\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\left(y+\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}\right.}{x}
\end{array}$
This is the linear differential equation.
Put $y=v x$, so that $\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}$. Then
$\begin{array}{c}
v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v x+\sqrt{x^{2}+v^{2} x^{2}}}{x} \\
\Rightarrow \quad v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=v+\sqrt{1+v^{2}} \\
\frac{d v}{\sqrt{1+v^{2}}}=\frac{d x}{x}
\end{array}$
Integrating both sides, we get
$\int \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}}}=\int \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}}$
$\Rightarrow  \log \left(\mathrm{v}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}}\right)=\log \mathrm{x}+\log \mathrm{C}$
$\Rightarrow\mathrm{v}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}}=\mathrm{Cx}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}+\sqrt{1+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}}=\mathrm{Cx} \quad[\because \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{vx}]$
$\mathrm{y}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}}=\mathrm{Cx}^{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The distance of the point $(1,-2,3)$ from the plane $x-y+z=5$ measured parallel to the line $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{3}=\frac{z-1}{-6}$ is
questions.solution:
Equation of the line through $(1,-2,3)$ parallel to the line $\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{3}=\frac{z-1}{-6}$ is $\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{-6}=r$ (say)
Then any point on (1) is $(2 r+1,3 r-2,-6 r$ $+3$ )
If this point lies on the plane $x-y+z=5$ then
$$
(2 r+1)-(3 r-2)+(-6 r+3)=5 \Rightarrow r=\frac{1}{7}
$$
Hence the point is $\left(\frac{9}{7},-\frac{11}{7}, \frac{15}{7}\right)$
Distance between $(1,-2,3)$ and
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left(\frac{9}{7},-\frac{11}{7}, \frac{15}{7}\right) \\
=\sqrt{\left(\frac{4}{49}+\frac{9}{49}+\frac{36}{49}\right)}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{49}{49}\right)}=1
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The two lines $x=m y+n, z=p y+q$ and $x=m^{\prime} y+n^{\prime}$, $z=p^{\prime} y+q^{\prime}$ are perpendicular to each other, if
questions.solution:
Given lines are
$x=m y+n, z=p y+q$
and $x=m^{\prime} y+n^{\prime}, z=p^{\prime} y+q^{\prime}$
Above equations can be rewritten as
$\frac{x-n}{m}=\frac{y-0}{1}=\frac{z-q}{p}$
and $\frac{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{n}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{m}^{\prime}}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-0}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{q}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{p}^{\prime}}$
Lines will be perpendicular, if $\mathrm{mm}^{\prime}+1+\mathrm{pp}^{\prime}=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{mm}^{\prime}+\mathrm{pp}^{\prime}=-1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: \(\frac{\cos \theta}{1-\tan \theta}+\frac{\sin \theta}{1-\cot \theta}\) is equal to
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\cos \theta}{1-\tan \theta}+\frac{\sin \theta}{1-\cot \theta} \\
& =\frac{\cos \theta}{1-\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}+\frac{\sin \theta}{1-\frac{\cos \theta}{\sin \theta}} \\
& =\frac{\cos ^2 \theta}{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}-\frac{\sin ^2 \theta}{\cos \theta-\sin \theta} \\
& =\frac{\cos ^2 \theta-\sin ^2 \theta}{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}=\cos \theta+\sin \theta
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $3 \cos x \neq 2 \sin x$, then the general solution of $\sin ^{2} x-\cos 2 x=2-\sin 2 x$ is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
 \sin ^{2} x-\cos 2 x=2-\sin 2 x \\
\Rightarrow 1-\cos ^{2} x-\left(2 \cos ^{2}-1\right)=2-2 \sin x \cos x \\
\Rightarrow-3 \cos ^{2} x+2 \sin x \cos x=0 \\
\Rightarrow \cos x(2 \sin x-3 \cos x)=0 \\
\Rightarrow \cos x=0, \quad(\because 2 \sin x-3 \cos x \neq 0) \\
\Rightarrow x=2 n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{2} \\
\Rightarrow x=(4 n \pm 1) \frac{\pi}{2}, n \in Z
\end{array}
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow x=2 n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{2} \\
\Rightarrow x=(4 n \pm 1) \frac{\pi}{2}, n \in Z \\
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of $\sin x=-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $\sin x=-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=-\sin \frac{\pi}{3}$
Hence, $\sin x=\sin \frac{4 \pi}{3}$, which gives $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{n} \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{4 \pi}{3}$, where $\mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{Z}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $P=\operatorname{cosec} \frac{\pi}{8}+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{2 \pi}{8}+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{3 \pi}{8}$ $+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{13 \pi}{8}+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{14 \pi}{8}+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{15 \pi}{8}$ and $\phi=8 \sin \frac{\pi}{18} \sin \frac{5 \pi}{18} \sin \frac{7 \pi}{18}$, then the value of $P+Q$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text {  } \begin{array}{r}
P=\operatorname{cosec} \frac{\pi}{8}+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{2 \pi}{8}+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{3 \pi}{8} \\
+\operatorname{cosec}\left(2 \pi-\frac{3 \pi}{8}\right) \\
+\operatorname{cosec}\left(2 \pi+\frac{2 \pi}{8}\right)+\operatorname{cosec}\left(2 \pi-\frac{\pi}{8}\right) \\
=\operatorname{cosec} \frac{\pi}{8}+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{2 \pi}{8}+\operatorname{cosec} \frac{3 \pi}{8}-\operatorname{cosec} \frac{3 \pi}{8} \\
-\operatorname{cosec} \frac{2 \pi}{8}-\operatorname{cosec} \frac{\pi}{8}=0
\end{array}
\end{aligned}$
and $Q=8 \sin 10^{\circ} \sin 50^{\circ} \sin 70^{\circ}$
$\begin{aligned}
& =8 \sin 10^{\circ} \sin \left(60^{\circ}-10^{\circ}\right) \sin \left(60^{\circ}+10^{\circ}\right) \\
& =\frac{8 \sin 30^{\circ}}{4}=1
\end{aligned}$
Hence, $P+Q=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(2 y \cos \theta=x \sin \theta\) and \(2 x \sec \theta-y \operatorname{cosec} \theta=3\), then \(x^2+4 y^2=\)
questions.solution:
We have \(2 \mathrm{y} \cos \theta=\mathrm{x} \sin \theta\)
or \(\frac{\cos \theta}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{\sin \theta}{2 \mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{k}\) (say)
Then \(\cos \theta=\mathrm{kx}\) and \(\sin \theta=2 \mathrm{ky}\) ...(1)
Again \(2 \mathrm{x} \sec \theta-\mathrm{y} \operatorname{cosec} \theta=3\)
or \(\frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{\cos \theta}-\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\sin \theta}=3\)
or \(\frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{kx}}-\frac{\mathrm{y}}{2 \mathrm{ky}}=3 \quad\) [from(1)]
or \(\frac{2}{\mathrm{k}}-\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{k}}=3\), giving \(\mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{2}\)
We now get \(\cos \theta=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}\) and \(\sin \theta=\mathrm{y}\)
Squaring and adding we get
\(\begin{aligned}
& \cos ^2 \theta+\sin ^2 \theta=\frac{x^2}{4+y^2} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{4}+y^2=1, \text { or } x^2+4 y^2=4
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\sin ^{-1} x+\sin ^{-1} y=\frac{\pi}{2}$, then $\cos ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} y$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given $\sin ^{-1} x+\sin ^{-1} y=\frac{\pi}{2}$
we know that $\sin ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} x=\frac{\pi}{2}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \sin ^{-1} x=\pi / 2-\cos ^{-1} x \\
\therefore \text { Equation (1) becomes. }
\end{array}$
$\frac{\pi}{2}-\cos ^{-1} x+\frac{\pi}{2}-\cos ^{-1} y=\frac{\pi}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \cos ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} y=\frac{\pi}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The period of \(\tan 3 \theta\) is
questions.solution: \(\tan \theta\) is of period \(\pi\) so that \(\tan 3 \theta\) is of period \(\pi / 3\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of the equation $\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin \theta+1=0$, lies in the interval
questions.solution:
We have,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin \theta+1=0 \Rightarrow 1-\sin ^{2} \theta+\sin \theta+1 \\
=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta=-1(\because \sin \theta \neq 2) \Rightarrow \theta=3 \pi / 2 \\
\therefore \theta \in\left(\frac{5 \pi}{4}, \frac{7 \pi}{4}\right)
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\tan \theta=\sqrt{\mathrm{n}}$ for some non-square natural number $\mathrm{n}$, then $\sec 2 \theta$ is
questions.solution:
$\cos 2 \theta=\frac{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}=\frac{1-\mathrm{n}}{1+\mathrm{n}} \Rightarrow \sec 2 \theta=\frac{1+\mathrm{n}}{1-\mathrm{n}}$
Since, $\mathrm{n}$ is non-square natural number $\therefore \mathrm{n} \neq 1$
$\therefore \sec 2 \theta=\frac{1+\mathrm{n}}{1-\mathrm{n}}=$ rational number

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Find the value of $\sin 12^{\circ} \sin 48^{\circ} \sin 54^{\circ}$.
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
y^{2}=2 a(x-a) \\
\sin 12^{\circ} \sin 48^{\circ} \sin 54^{\circ} \\
=\sin 12^{\circ} \sin \left(60^{\circ}-12^{\circ}\right) \sin \left(90^{\circ}-36^{\circ}\right) \\
=\frac{\sin 12^{\circ} \sin \left(60^{\circ}-12^{\circ}\right) \sin 72^{\circ} \cos 36^{\circ}}{\sin 72^{\circ}} \\
=\frac{\left[\sin 12^{\circ} \sin \left(60^{\circ}-12^{\circ}\right) \sin \left(60^{\circ}+12^{\circ}\right) \cos 36^{\circ}\right]}{\sin 72^{\circ}} \\
=\frac{\sin 12^{\circ}\left(\sin ^{2} 60^{\circ}-\sin ^{2} 12^{\circ}\right) \cos 36^{\circ}}{\sin 72^{\circ}} \\
=\frac{\sin 12^{\circ}\left(\frac{3}{4}-\sin ^{2} 12^{\circ}\right) \cos 36^{\circ}}{\sin 72^{\circ}} \\
=\frac{\left.3 \sin 12^{\circ}-4 \sin ^{3} 12^{\circ}\right)}{4} \cdot \frac{\cos 36^{\circ}}{\sin 72^{\circ}} \\
=\frac{\sin 72^{\circ}}{24 \cos 36^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{1}{8} \\
=\frac{\sin 72^{\circ}}{8}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of values of \(x\) in \([0,2 \pi]\) satisfying the equation \(|\cos \mathrm{x}-\sin \mathrm{x}| \geq \sqrt{2}\), is
questions.solution:
Given equations is \(|\cos x-\sin x| \geq \sqrt{2}\)
Since \(|\cos \mathrm{x}-\sin \mathrm{x}| \leq \sqrt{1+1}=\sqrt{2}\),
\(\therefore\) We must have \(|\cos \mathrm{x}-\sin \mathrm{x}|=\sqrt{2}\)
\(\Rightarrow\left|\cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right|=1 \Rightarrow \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=1,-1\)
\(\therefore x+\frac{\pi}{4}=0,2 \pi, 4 \pi, 6 \pi, \ldots\). or \(\pi, 3 \pi, 5 \pi, \ldots .\).
\(\therefore \quad x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{7 \pi}{4}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $12 \cot ^{2} \theta-31 \operatorname{cosec} \theta+32=0$, then the value of $\sin \theta$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} 12 & \cot ^{2} \theta-31 \operatorname{cosec} \theta+32=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 12\left(\operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta-1\right)-31 \operatorname{cosec} \theta+32=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 12 \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta-31 \operatorname{cosec} \theta+20=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 12 \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta-16 \operatorname{cosec} \theta-15 \operatorname{cosec} \theta \\ &+20=0 \\ \Rightarrow &(4 \operatorname{cosec} \theta-5)(3 \operatorname{cosec} \theta-4)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \operatorname{cosec} \theta=\frac{5}{4}, \frac{4}{3} \quad \therefore \sin \theta=\frac{4}{5}, \frac{3}{4} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of sphere concentric with the sphere $x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}-4 x-6 y-8 z-5=0$ and which passes through the origin, is
questions.solution:
The equation of the sphere concentric with the sphere
$$
\begin{array}{l}
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}-4 x-6 y-8 z-5=0 \text { is } \\
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}-4 x-6 y-8 z+c=0...(i)
\end{array}
$$
Since, this sphere eq. (i) passes through origin, therefore
$$
\begin{array}{l}
0+0+0-0-0-0+\mathrm{c}=0 \\
\Rightarrow c=0
\end{array}
$$
Hence, the required equation of sphere is
$$
x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}-4 x-6 y-8 z=0
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of integral values of $K$, for which the equation $7 \cos x+5 \sin x=2 K+1$ has a solution, is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
-\sqrt{7^{2}+5^{2}} \leq(7 \cos x+5 \sin x) \leq \sqrt{7^{2}+5^{2}} \\
\Rightarrow-\sqrt{74} \leq(2 K+1) \leq \sqrt{74} \\
\Rightarrow-8.6 \leq(2 K+1) \leq 8.6 \\
\Rightarrow-9.6 \leq 2 K \leq 7.6 \\
\Rightarrow-4.8 \leq K \leq 3.8
\end{array}$
So, integral values of $\mathrm{K}$ are $-4,-3,-2,-1,0,1,2,3$ (eight values)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $f(2)=4$ and $f^{\prime}(2)=1$,
then $\lim _{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{x f(2)-2 f(x)}{x-2}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let Let $f(x)=a x+b$
Given $f(2)=4 \& f^{\prime}(2)=1$
$\therefore f(2)=a \cdot 2+b=4 \Rightarrow 2 a+b=4$ ...(1)
$\begin{array}{l}
\& f^{\prime}(x)=a \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(2)=a=1 \Rightarrow a=1 \\
\therefore 2 \times 1+b=4 \Rightarrow b=2 \text { [using equation (1)] } \\
\therefore f(x)=x+2
\end{array}$
Now, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{x f(2)-2 f(x)}{x-2}$
$\begin{array}{l}
=\lim _{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{4 x-2(x+2)}{x-2}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{2 x-4}{x-2} \\
=\lim _{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{2(x-2)}{(x-2)}=2
\end{array}$
