questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The position vector of $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are $2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ and $2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$
The length of the internal bisector of $\angle \mathrm{BOA}$ of triangle $\mathrm{AOB}$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $\mathrm{OA}=|2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}|=3 ; \mathrm{OB}=|2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}|=6$ Since the internal bisector divides opposite side in the
ratio of adjacent sides $$ \therefore \frac{\mathrm{AC}}{\mathrm{BC}}=\frac{3}{6}=\frac{1}{2} $$ where $\mathrm{OC}$ is the bisector of $\angle \mathrm{BOA}$. $\therefore$ Position vector of $C$ is $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{2(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})+(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}})}{2+1}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{8}{3} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \therefore \mathrm{OC}=\left|2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{8}{3} \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}\right|=\sqrt{\frac{136}{9}} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(\bar{p}=\lambda(\vec{u} \times \bar{v})+\mu(\vec{v} \times \bar{w})+v(\vec{w} \times \bar{u})\) and \([\overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{w}}]=\frac{1}{5}\), then \(\lambda+\mu+v\) is equal to
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}=\lambda(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}})+\mu(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{w}})+\mathrm{v}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{w}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}}) \\
& \Rightarrow \vec{p} \cdot \vec{w}=\lambda(\vec{u} \times \vec{v}) \cdot \vec{w}+\mu(\vec{v} \times \vec{w}) \cdot \vec{w} \\
& +v(\vec{w} \times \vec{u}) \cdot \vec{w} \\
& =\lambda[\overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{w}}]+0+0=\frac{\lambda}{5} \Rightarrow \lambda=5(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{w}}) \\
&
\end{aligned}\)
Similarly, \(\mu=5(\vec{p} \cdot \vec{u})\) and \(v=5(\vec{p} \cdot \vec{v})\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \lambda+\mu+\mathrm{v}=5(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{w}})+5(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}})+5(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}) \\
& 5 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \cdot(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{w}})
\end{aligned}\)
Hence, \(\lambda+\mu+v\) depends on the vectors

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the unit vectors $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are inclined at $2 \theta$ and $|\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}| \lt 1$, then if $0 \lt \theta \lt \pi, \theta$ lies in the interval.
questions.solution:
$\because \mathrm{a}$ and b are unit vectors.
We have,
$\begin{aligned}
|a-b| & =\sqrt{(a-b)^2} \\
& =\sqrt{(a-b) \cdot(a-b)} \\
& =\sqrt{|a|^2+|b|^2-2 a \cdot b}
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} &=\sqrt{1+1-2 \cos 2 \theta} \\ &=\sqrt{2(1-\cos 2 \theta)}=2|\sin \theta| \\ & \because|a-b| \lt 1 \Rightarrow \mid \sin \theta \left\lvert\, \lt \frac{1}{2}\right. \\ & \text { Hence, } \theta \in\left[0, \frac{\pi}{6}\right) \text { or }\left(\frac{5 \pi}{6}, \pi\right]\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $(\vec{a} \times \vec{b})^{2}+(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b})^{2}=676$ and $|\vec{b}|=2$, then $|\vec{a}|$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { Since, }(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})^{2}+(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})^{2}=676 \\
\Rightarrow(|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}| \sin \theta \hat{\mathrm{n}})^{2}+(|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}| \cos \theta)^{2}=676 \\
\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|^{2} \cdot|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|^{2}\left(\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta\right)=676 \\
\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}(2)^{2}=676 \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|^{2}=169 \\
\Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|=13
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: P, Q, R, S are the points \((-2,3,4),(-4,4,6),(4,3,5)\) and \((0,1,2)\). Then projection of \(P Q\) on \(R S\) is
questions.solution: The four points are \(P(-2,3,4), Q(-4,4,6), R(4\), \(3,5), \mathrm{S}(0,1,2)\). \(\therefore\) direction ratios of RS are \(0-4,1-3,2-5\), i.e., \(4,2,3\). Dividing each by \(\sqrt{(4)^2+(2)^2+(3)^2}\), i.e., \(\sqrt{29}\), the actual direction cosines' of RS are \(\frac{4}{\sqrt{29}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{29}}, \frac{3}{\sqrt{29}} \text {. }\) \(\therefore\) Projection of \(\mathrm{PQ}\) on \(\mathrm{RS}\) \(\begin{aligned} & =\frac{4}{\sqrt{29}}(-4+2)+\frac{2}{\sqrt{29}}(4-3)+\frac{3}{\sqrt{29}}(6-4) \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{29}}(-8+2+6)=0 \end{aligned}\) which implies that \(\mathrm{PQ} \perp \mathrm{RS}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\vec{c}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{k}$ then $|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|$. $\vec{a}$ is equal to :

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A girls walks \(4 \mathrm{~km}\) towards West. Then, she walks \(3 \mathrm{~km}\) in a direction \(30^{\circ}\) East to North and stops. The girls displacement from her initial point of departure is
questions.solution:
Let \(\mathrm{O}\) and \(\mathrm{B}\) be the initial and final positions of the girl respectively. Then, the girl's position can be shown as in the figure. Now, we have \(\mathrm{OA}=-4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}\) \(\mathrm{AB}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}|\mathrm{AB}| \cos 60^{\circ}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}|\mathrm{AB}| \sin 60^{\circ}\)
(AB \(\cos 60^{\circ}\) is component of \(\mathrm{AB}\) along \(\mathrm{X}\)-axis and \(\mathrm{AB} \sin 60^{\circ}\) is component of \(\mathrm{AB}\) along \(\mathrm{Y}\) axis) \(=\hat{\mathrm{i}}\left(3 \times \frac{1}{2}\right)+\hat{\mathrm{j}}\left(3 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=\frac{3}{2} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}\) By the triangle law of vector addition, we have \(\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{OB}=\mathrm{OA}+\mathrm{AB}=(-4 \hat{\mathrm{i}})+\left(\frac{3}{2} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}\right) \\ & =\left(-4+\frac{3}{2}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & =\left(\frac{-8+3}{2}\right) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}=\frac{-5}{2} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}} \end{aligned}\) Hence the girl's displacement from her initial point of departure is \(\frac{-5}{2} \hat{i}+\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{j}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}\) are three unit vectors such that \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}\) is not parallel to \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}\) and \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})=\frac{1}{2} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}\), then the angle between \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}\) and \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}\) is
questions.solution:
It is given that \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})=\frac{1}{2} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}\)
Note: \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})=(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}-(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}-(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad\left(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}-\frac{1}{2}\right) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}-(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=0 \quad ...(i)
\end{aligned}\)
Since \(\bar{b}\) and \(\bar{c}\) are non-parallel, therefore for the existence of relation (i), the coeff. of \(\bar{b}\) and \(\overline{\mathrm{c}}\) should vanish separately. Therefore, we get \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}-\frac{1}{2}=0\) i.e., \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{2}\) and \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=0\) Since \(\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}\) are the unit vectors therefore
\(\overline{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} \cdot\left(\because \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\theta\right)\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: What is the length of the projection of $3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}$ on the $\mathrm{xy}$-plane ?
questions.solution:
$x y$-plane is perpendicular to $z$ - axis. Let the vector
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=3 \mathrm{i}+4 \mathrm{j}+5 \mathrm{k}$ make angle $\theta$ with $\mathrm{z}$ - axis, then it makes $90-\theta$ with $x y$-plane. unit vector along $\mathrm{z}$-axis is $\mathrm{k}$.
So, $\cos \theta=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{k}}}{|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}| \cdot|\hat{\mathrm{k}}|}=\frac{(3 \mathrm{i}+4 \mathrm{j}+5 \mathrm{k}) \cdot \mathrm{k}}{|3 \mathrm{i}+4 \mathrm{j}+5 \mathrm{k}|}$
$$
=\frac{5}{5 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}
$$
Hence angle with $x y-$ plane $\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{4}$
projection of $\vec{a}$ on xy plane $=|\vec{a}| \cdot \cos \frac{\pi}{4}$ $=5 \sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=5$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If vectors $a \hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \hat{i}+b \hat{j}+\hat{k}$ and $\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}$ $(a 
eq b 
eq c 
eq 1)$ are coplanar, then find $ rac{1}{1-a}+ rac{1}{1-b}+ rac{1}{1-c}$
questions.solution:
Since vectors are coplanar
$\therefore\left|\begin{array}{lll}a & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & b & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & c\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & 1 & 1 \\ 1-a & b-1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1-b & c-1\end{array}\right|=0 \quad$ [Using $R_{2}-R_{1}$
$\left.\mathrm{R}_{3}-\mathrm{R}_{2}\right]$
$\Rightarrow a(b-1)(c-1)-(1-a)\{(c-1)-(1-b)\}=0$
$\Rightarrow a(1-b)(1-c)+(1-a)(1-c)+(1-a)(1-b)$
$=0$
$\Rightarrow(a-1+1)(1-b)(1-c)+(1-a)(1-c)$
$+(1-a)(1-b)=0$
$\Rightarrow(1-b)(1-c)+(1-a)(1-c)+(1-a)(1-b)$
$=(1-a)(1-b)(1-c)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{1-a}+\frac{1}{1-b}+\frac{1}{1-c}=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $((\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \times(\vec{c} \times \vec{d})) \cdot(\vec{a} \times \vec{d})=0$, then which of the following is always true?
options: either $\vec{a}$ or $\vec{d}$ must lie in the plane of $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$
either $\vec{b}$ or $\vec{c}$ must lie in the plane of $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{d}$
either $\vec{a}$ or $\vec{b}$ must lie in the plane of $\vec{c}$ and $\vec{d}$
questions.solution:
$((\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \times(\vec{c} \times \vec{d})) \cdot(\vec{a} \times \vec{d})=0$ $([\vec{a} \vec{c} \vec{d}] \vec{b}-[\vec{b} \vec{c} \vec{d}] \vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{a} \times \vec{d})=0$ $[\vec{a} \vec{c} \vec{d}][\vec{b} \vec{a} \vec{d}]=0$
Either $\vec{c}$ or $\vec{b}$ must lie in the plane of $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{d}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $|\vec{a}|=3,|\vec{b}|=2,|\vec{c}|=1$ then the value of $|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}|$ is (given that $\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=0$ )
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(\vec{a}+\vec{b}+\vec{c})^{2}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow|\vec{a}|^{2}+|\vec{b}|^{2}+|\vec{c}|^{2}+2(\vec{a} \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 9+4+1+2(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a})=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}+\vec{b} \cdot \vec{c}+\vec{c} \cdot \vec{a}=7 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ and $\mathrm{c}$ are three non-coplanar vectors, then $(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{c}) \cdot[(\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}) \times(\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{c})$ equals
questions.solution:
$[\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}] \cdot[(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}) \times(\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c})$
$=(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c}) \cdot[\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{b}-\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{c}-\mathrm{b} \times \mathrm{b}+\mathrm{b} \times \mathrm{c}]$
$=\mathrm{a} \cdot(\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{b})-\mathrm{a} \cdot(\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{c})+\mathrm{a} \cdot(\mathrm{b} \times \mathrm{c})+\mathrm{b}$
$(\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{b})-\mathrm{b}(\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{c})+\mathrm{b} \cdot(\mathrm{b} \times \mathrm{c})-\mathrm{c} \cdot(\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{b})$
$+\mathrm{c} \cdot(\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{c})-\mathrm{c} \cdot(\mathrm{b} \times \mathrm{c})$
$=\mathrm{a} \cdot(\mathrm{b} \times \mathrm{c})-\mathrm{b} \cdot(\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{c})-\mathrm{c} \cdot(\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{b})$
$=[\mathrm{a} \mathrm{b} \mathrm{c}]-[\mathrm{b} \mathrm{a} \mathrm{c}]-[\mathrm{c} \mathrm{a} \mathrm{b}]$
$=[\mathrm{a} \mathrm{b} \mathrm{c}]+[\mathrm{a} \mathrm{b} \mathrm{c}]-[\mathrm{a} \mathrm{b} \mathrm{c}]$
$=[\mathrm{a} \mathrm{b} \mathrm{c}]=\mathrm{a} \cdot(\mathrm{b} \times \mathrm{c})$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For any three vectors $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{c},[\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}, \mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}, \mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a}]$ is
questions.solution:
$[a+b, b+c, c+a]$
$=(a+b) \cdot[(b+c) \times(c+a)]$
$=(a+b) \cdot[b \times c+b \times a+c \times c+c \times a]$
$=(a+b) \cdot(b \times c+b \times a+c \times a)$
$=a \cdot(\because c+c)+a \cdot(b \times a)+a \cdot(c \times a)+b .$
$\quad(b \times c)+b .(b \times a)+b \cdot(c \times a)$
$=a \cdot(b \times c)+b \cdot(c \times a)$
$=[a b c]+[a b c]$
$=[a b c]+[a b c]=2[a b c]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $r=\alpha \mathrm{b} \times \mathrm{c}+\beta \mathrm{c} \times \mathrm{a}+\gamma \mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{b}$ and [a b c] $=2$, then $\alpha+\beta+\gamma$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathbf{r} \cdot \mathbf{a}=\alpha(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})+\beta(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{c} \times \mathbf{a})+\gamma(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}) \\
=\alpha[\mathbf{a b c}]+0+0
\end{array}$
Similarly, $\mathbf{r} . \mathbf{b}=\beta[\mathbf{a b c}]$ and $\mathbf{r} \cdot \mathbf{c}=\gamma[\mathbf{a b c}]$
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{r} \cdot(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c})=\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{r} \cdot \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{r} \cdot \mathbf{b}+\mathbf{r} \cdot \mathbf{c}) \\
=\frac{1}{2}(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)[\mathbf{a b c}] \\
=\frac{1}{2}(\alpha+\beta+\gamma) \times 2=\alpha+\beta+\gamma
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are three non-coplanar vectors and $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}, \mathrm{r}$ are reciprocal vectors, then $(l a+m b+n c)$. $(l \mathrm{p}+m \mathrm{q}+n \mathrm{r})$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}$ and $\mathrm{r}$ are reciprocal vectors of $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}$ and c respectively.
$\begin{array}{l}
\text { So, } \boldsymbol{p . r}=1, \boldsymbol{p . b}=0=\boldsymbol{p . c} \\
\boldsymbol{q . a}=0, \boldsymbol{q . b}=1, \boldsymbol{q . c}=0 \\
\boldsymbol{r} \cdot \boldsymbol{a}=0, \boldsymbol{r . b}=0, \boldsymbol{r . c}=1 \\
\therefore(\boldsymbol{l a}+\boldsymbol{m b}+\boldsymbol{n c}) \cdot(\boldsymbol{l p}+\boldsymbol{m q}+\boldsymbol{n} \boldsymbol{r}) \\
=\boldsymbol{l}^{\mathbf{2}}+\boldsymbol{m}^{\mathbf{2}}+\boldsymbol{n}^{\mathbf{2}}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The volume of the tetrahedron included between the plane $3 x+4 y-5 z-60=0$ and the coordinate planes is
questions.solution:
The given equation of the plane is $3 x+4 y-5 z-60=0$. It can be written in the form $\frac{x}{20}+\frac{y}{15}+\frac{z}{-12}=1$
which meets the coordinate axes at the pointsA $(20,0,0), \mathrm{B}(0,15,0)$ and $\mathrm{C}(0,0,-12)$. The coordinates of the origin are $\mathrm{O}(0,0,0)$. Therefore, volume of the tetrahedron OABC is $=$
$=\frac{1}{6}\left\|\begin{array}{ccc}
20 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 15 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & -12
\end{array}\right\|=\frac{1}{6}|20 \times 15 \times(-12)|=600$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The vector $\mathbf{b}=3 \mathbf{j}+4 \mathbf{k}$ is to be written as the sum of a vector $\mathbf{b}_{1}$ parallel to $\mathbf{a}=\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j}$ and a vector $\mathbf{b}_{2}$ perpendicular to a. Then $\mathbf{b}_{1}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\mathbf{b}_{1} \| \mathbf{a} \Rightarrow \mathbf{b}_{1}=\mathrm{a}(\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j})$
$\mathbf{b}_{2}=\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{b}_{1}=(3-\mathrm{a}) \mathbf{i}-\mathrm{a} \mathbf{j}+4 \mathbf{k}$
Also, $\mathbf{b}_{2} \cdot \mathbf{a}=0$
$\Rightarrow(3-\mathrm{a})-\mathrm{a} \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\frac{3}{2}$
$\therefore \mathbf{b}_{1}=\frac{3}{2}(\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j})$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $3 \mathrm{p}$ and $4 \mathrm{p}$ are resultant of a force $5 \mathrm{p}$, then the angle between $3 \mathrm{p}$ and $5 \mathrm{p}$ is
questions.solution:
$$ \quad \mathrm{Q}=\frac{\mathrm{R} \sin \alpha}{\sin (\alpha+\beta)} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text { Also, }(5 \mathrm{P})^{2}=(4 \mathrm{P})^{2}+(3 \mathrm{P})^{2} \\ \quad+2(4 \mathrm{P})(3 \mathrm{P}) \cos (\alpha+\beta) \\ \Rightarrow 25 \mathrm{P}^{2}=16 \mathrm{p}^{2}+9 \mathrm{P}^{2}+24 \mathrm{P}^{2} \cos (\alpha+\beta) \\ \Rightarrow 24 \mathrm{P}^{2} \cos (\alpha+\beta)=0 \\ \Rightarrow \cos (\alpha+\beta)=0=\cos 90^{\circ} \\ \Rightarrow \alpha+\beta=90^{\circ} \end{array} $$
$$ \begin{array}{l} \text { Now, } 4 \mathrm{P}=\frac{5 \mathrm{P} \sin \alpha}{\sin 90^{\circ}} \\ \Rightarrow \sin \alpha=\frac{4}{5} \\ \Rightarrow \alpha=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $a, b, c$ are three non-zero vectors such that $\boldsymbol{a}+\boldsymbol{b}+\boldsymbol{c}=0$ and $m=\boldsymbol{a} \cdot \boldsymbol{b}+\boldsymbol{b} \cdot \boldsymbol{c}+\boldsymbol{c} \cdot \boldsymbol{a}$, then
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { Here, } \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}=0 \\
\Rightarrow|\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}|^{2}=0 \\
\Rightarrow|\mathbf{a}|^{2}+|\mathbf{b}|^{2}+\mid \mathbf{c}^{2}+2\{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}+\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{c}+\mathbf{c} \cdot \mathbf{a}\}=0 \\
\Rightarrow \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}+\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{c}+\mathbf{c} \cdot \mathbf{a} \\
=-\frac{1}{2}\left\{|\mathbf{a}|^{2}+|\mathbf{b}|^{2}+|\mathbf{c}|^{2}\right\} < 0 \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{m} < 0
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: \(\mathrm{ABC}\) is a triangle and \(\mathrm{P}\) is any point on \(\mathrm{BC}\) such that \(\overrightarrow{P Q}\) is the resultant of the vectors \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AP}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{PB}}\) and \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PC}}\), then
options: the position of \(Q\) depends on position of \(P\)
questions.solution:
We have \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AP}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PB}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PC}}\) \(\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PC}} \\ & \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PC}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CP}} \\ & =\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CP}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PQ}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CQ}} . \end{aligned}\)
\(\therefore \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{CQ}\) and \(\mathrm{AB} \| \mathrm{CQ}\) \(\therefore \mathrm{ABQC}\) is a parallelogram. \(\therefore \mathrm{Q}\) is a fixed point.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\sqrt{\log _{10} x^{2}}$. The set of all values of $x$ for which $f(x)$ is real, is
questions.solution:
$f(x)=\sqrt{\log _{10} x^{2}}$ is real, if
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\log _{10} x^{2} \geq 0 \\
\Rightarrow x^{2} \geq 1 \\
\Rightarrow x < -1 \text { and } x>1 \\
\Rightarrow x \in(-\infty,-1] \cup[1, \infty)
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A rod of length $l$ slides with its ends on two perpendicular lines. Then, the locus of its mid point is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A unit vector perpendicular to the plane formed by the points $(1,0,1),(0,2,2)$ and $(3,3,0)$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{A}(1,0,1), \mathrm{B}(0,2,2)$ and $\mathrm{C}(3,3,0)$ be the given points, then
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}=-\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{BC}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\
\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{BC}}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\
-1 & 2 & 1 \\
3 & 1 & -2
\end{array}\right|=-5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\
\therefore \text { unit vector } \perp \text { to the plane: } \mathrm{ABC} \\
=\pm \frac{1}{5 \sqrt{3}}(-5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-7 \hat{\mathrm{k}})
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The volume of the parallelopiped whose edges are represented by $a=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$, $\mathbf{b}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $\mathbf{c}=3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ is
questions.solution:
Volume of parallelopiped is
Now,
$\begin{aligned}
& =[a b c]=a \cdot(b \times c) \\
& \mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\
1 & 2 & -1 \\
3 & -1 & 2
\end{array}\right| \\
& =\hat{\mathbf{j}}(4-1)-\hat{\mathbf{j}}(2+3)+\hat{\mathbf{k}}(-1-6) \\
& =3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-5 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-7 \hat{\mathbf{k}}
\end{aligned}$
and $\mathbf{a} \cdot(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})=(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-5 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-7 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$
$\begin{aligned}
& =(3 \times 2)+(-3 \times-5)+(4 \times-7) \\
& =6+15-28=-7
\end{aligned}$
Hence, volume of parallelopiped is 7 .

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The projection of the vector $\boldsymbol{i}-2 \boldsymbol{j}+\boldsymbol{k}$ on the vector $4 i-4 j+7 k$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\mathbf{a}=\mathbf{i}-2 \mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k}$ and $\mathbf{b}=4 \mathbf{i}-4 \mathbf{j}+7 \mathbf{k}$, then projection of $\mathbf{a}$ on $\mathbf{b}$ is equal to
$$
\frac{\text { a.b }}{|\mathbf{b}|}=\frac{4+8+7}{\sqrt{16+16+49}}=\frac{19}{9}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The center and radius of the sphere $x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+3 x-4 z+1=0$ are
questions.solution:
Since the centre and radius of the sphere $x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}+2 u x+2 v y+2 w z+d=0$ are $(-u,-v,-w)$ and $\sqrt{u^{2}+v^{2}+w^{2}-d}$ respectively. So, for the sphere
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}^{2}+\mathrm{z}^{2}+3 \mathrm{x}-4 \mathrm{z}+1 &=0, \\ \text { Centre } & \equiv\left(-\frac{3}{2}, 0,2\right), \text { and } \\ \text { Radius } &=\sqrt{\left(-\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}+0^{2}+(2)^{2}-1} \\ &=\sqrt{\frac{9}{4}+4-1}=\frac{\sqrt{21}}{2} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The length of the parabola $y^{2}=12 x$ cut off by the latus-rectum is
questions.solution:
On comparing the equation of the parabola $y^{2}=12 x$ with the standard equation, $y^{2}=4$ ax, we get $4 a=12$ or $a=3$
Hence, the focus, point $\mathrm{C}$ will be at $(3,0)$ and the extremities of the latus-rectum $\mathrm{AB}$ will be at $(a, 2 a)$ and $(a,-2 a)$. So the coordinates of A and $B$ are $(3,6)$ and $(3,-6)$ respectively. Now we need to find the length ( curve $\mathrm{AOB}$ ) of the parabola. As it is not a straight line so we cannot directly find the length of this curve as we cannot directly apply Pythagorous theorem. Let us consider a small length $\mathrm{ds}$ on the parabola. Using pythagorous theorem for this length, $\mathrm{ds}=\sqrt{(\mathrm{dx})^{2}+(\mathrm{dy})^{2}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dy}}\right)^{2}+1 \mathrm{dy}}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{s}=\int_{-6}^{6}\left[\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dy}}\right)^{2}+1}\right] \cdot \mathrm{dy}$ ...(1) From $y^{2}=12 x \Rightarrow x=\frac{y^{2}}{12}$ $\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{2 y}{12}=\frac{y}{6} \quad$ Putting in (1), $s=\int_{-6}^{6}\left[\sqrt{\left(\frac{y}{6}\right)^{2}+1}\right] d y=2 \int_{0}^{6} \sqrt{\frac{y^{2}+36}{36}} d y$ $=\frac{2}{6} \int_{0}^{6} \sqrt{y^{2}+6^{2}} d y$ Using $\int \sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}=\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}$ $+\frac{a^{2}}{2} \log \left(x+\sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}\right)+C$ We get $s=\frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{y}{2} \sqrt{6^{2}+y^{2}}+\frac{6^{2}}{2}\right.$ $\left.\log \left(y+\sqrt{6^{2}+y^{2}}\right)+C\right]_{0}^{6}$ $=\frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{6}{2} \sqrt{6^{2}+6^{2}}+18 \log \left(6+\sqrt{6^{2}+6^{2}}\right)\right.$ $\left.\quad+C-0-18 \log \left(0+\sqrt{6^{2}+0}\right)-C\right]$ $=\frac{1}{3}[3.6 \sqrt{2}+18 \log (6+6 \sqrt{2})-18 \log 6]$ $=6 \sqrt{2}+6 \log \frac{6(1+\sqrt{2})}{6}$ $=6[(\sqrt{2}+\log (1+\sqrt{2})]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The plane through the point $(-1,-1,-1)$ and containing the line of intersection of the planes $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})=0$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=0$ is
questions.solution:
The plane containing the line of intersection of the planes $\vec{r} \cdot(\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})=0 \quad$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} .(\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=0$ is
$\begin{array}{l}
\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda[\hat{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})]=0 \\
\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot[\hat{\mathrm{i}}+(3+\lambda) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2 \lambda-1) \hat{\mathrm{k}}]=0 ...(1)
\end{array}$
Since the plane (i) passes through the point $(-1,-1,-1)$ or $(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}})$, so this point must satisfy (i). Hence,
$\begin{array}{l}
(-\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot[\hat{i}+(3 \times \lambda) \hat{j}+(2 \lambda-1) \hat{k}]=0 \\
\Rightarrow-1-(3+\lambda)-(2 \lambda-1)=0 \\
\Rightarrow-3 \lambda-3=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=-1
\end{array}$
Substituting this value for $\lambda$ in (i), we get the required plane $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The probability that a certain kind of component will survive a given shock test is $\frac{3}{4}$. The probability that exactly 2 of the next 4 components tested survive is
questions.solution:
The probability that a component survives is $\mathrm{p}=\frac{3}{4}$. Then $\mathrm{q}=1-\mathrm{p}=1-\frac{3}{4}=\frac{1}{4}$
$[\because \mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q}=1]$
$\mathrm{n}$ takes the value 4 and $\mathrm{r}=2$. Hence the required probability is
${ }^{n} C_{r} p^{r} q^{n-r}={ }^{4} C_{2}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{2}\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{2}$
$=6 \times \frac{3 \times 3 \times 1}{4 \times 4 \times 4 \times 4}=\frac{27}{128}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $I=\int \frac{x^{5}}{\sqrt{1+x^{3}}} d x$, then I is equal to
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{\mathrm{x}^{5}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{3}}} \mathrm{dx}=\int \frac{\mathrm{x}^{3} \cdot \mathrm{x}^{2}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{3}}} \mathrm{dx}$
Let $1+x^{3}=t^{2}$, so that $3 x^{2} d x=2 t d t$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow x^{2} d x=\frac{2}{3} t d t \\
\left.\therefore I=\int \frac{\left(t^{2}-1\right) \frac{2}{3} t d t}{t}=\frac{2}{3} \int t^{2}-1\right) d t \\
=\frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{t^{3}}{3}-t\right)+C=\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{\left(1+x^{3}\right)^{3 / 2}}{3}-\left(1+x^{3}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}\right]+C \\
=\frac{2}{9}\left(1+x^{3}\right)^{3 / 2}-\frac{2}{3}\left(1+x^{3}\right)^{1 / 2}+C
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of real tangents through $(3,5)$ that can be drawn to the ellipses $3 \mathrm{x}^{2}+5 \mathrm{y}^{2}=32$ and $25 x^{2}+9 y^{2}=450$ is
questions.solution:
Given : Equations of ellipses
$\begin{array}{l}
3 x^{2}+5 y^{2}=32 \\
\& 25 x^{2}+9 y^{2}=450
\end{array}$
Tangents to the ellipse (1) \& (2) are passing through the point $(3,5)$
$$
\therefore 3(3)^{2}+5(5)^{2}-32=27+75-32>0
$$
So the given point lies outsides the ellipse. Hence, two real tangents can be drawn from the point to the ellipse,
$\& 25(3)^{2}+9(5)^{2}-450=225+225-450=0$
$\therefore$ The point lie on the ellipse. Hence one real tangent can be drawn.
$\therefore$ No. of real tangents $=3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The non integer roots of
$x^{4}-3 x^{3}-2 x^{2}+3 x+1=0$
questions.solution:
Given $x^{4}-3 x^{3}-2 x^{2}+3 x+1=0$
By using Hit \& trial method, we have $(\mathrm{x}-1)$ is a factor of given equation
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore(x-1)\left(x^{3}-2 x^{2}-4 x-1\right)=0 \\
\Rightarrow(x-1)\left[x^{3}+x^{2}-3 x^{2}-3 x-x-1\right]=0 \\
\Rightarrow(x-1)\left[x^{2}(x+1)-3 x(x+1)-1(x+1)\right]=0 \\
\Rightarrow(x-1)(x+1)\left(x^{2}-3 x-1\right)=0 \\
\therefore x=1,-1 \text { or } x^{2}-3 x-1=0 \\
\text { Now } x^{2}-3 x-1=0 \\
\Rightarrow x=\frac{3 \pm \sqrt{9+4}}{2}
\end{array}$
$\left[\because \mathrm{x}=\frac{-\mathrm{b} \pm \sqrt{\mathrm{b}^{2}-4 \mathrm{ac}}}{2 \mathrm{a}}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{3 \pm \sqrt{13}}{2}$
$\therefore$ non-integer roots of given equation are
$\frac{1}{2}(3+\sqrt{13}), \frac{1}{2}(3-\sqrt{13})$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\vec{a}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}), \vec{a} \vec{b}=1$ and $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\hat{j}-\hat{k}$, then $\vec{b}$ is
questions.solution: $\because(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \times \vec{a}=(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a}) \vec{b}-(\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}) \vec{a}$ $\therefore \quad(\hat{j}-\hat{k}) \times(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+k)=(\sqrt{3})^{2}(\vec{b})-(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+k)$ $\Rightarrow 3 \hat{b}=3 \hat{i} \Rightarrow \hat{b}=\hat{i}$

questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathbf{a}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $\mathbf{b}=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$, then the angle $\theta$ between $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ is given by
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\cos \theta=\frac{a_{1} b_{1}+a_{2} b_{2}+a_{3} b_{3}}{\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+a_{3}^{2}} \sqrt{b_{1}^{2}+b_{2}^{2}+b_{3}^{2}}} \\
=\frac{1 \times 2+(-1) \times(-1)+2 \times(1)}{\sqrt{1+1+4} \sqrt{4+1+1}} \\
=\frac{2+2+2}{6}=\frac{6}{6}=1
\end{array}$
So, $\theta=0^{\circ}$ or $\theta=2 \pi$
$\begin{array}{ll}\because & \sec 2 \pi=1 \\ \therefore & 2 \pi=\sec ^{-1}(1) \\ \Rightarrow & \theta=\sec ^{-1}(1)\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The moment about the point $\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ of a force represented by $\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$ acting through the point $2 \mathrm{i}+3 \mathrm{j}+\mathrm{k}$ is
questions.solution:
Here, $r=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})-(i+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{i}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}} \text { and } \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{i}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}$
Then, the required moment is given by
$r \times F=(i+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \times(i+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$ 
$=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|=3 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}$ 
$\therefore$ Moment about given point $=3 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The probability density $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ of a continuous random variable is given by $f(x)=$ $\mathrm{Ke}^{-|\mathrm{x}|},-\infty < \mathrm{x} < \infty$. Then the value of $\mathrm{K}$ is
questions.solution:
Since $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is the probability density function of random variable $\mathrm{X}$.
$\therefore \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=1$
Now we have
$\begin{array}{l}
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mathrm{Ke}^{-|\mathrm{x}|} \mathrm{dx}=1 \Rightarrow 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} \mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-|\mathrm{x}|} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}=1 \\
\Rightarrow 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} \mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}=1 \\
\Rightarrow-2 \mathrm{~K} \cdot\left[\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}\right]_{0}^{\infty}=1 \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~K}=1 \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{2}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathbf{a}=\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k}, \mathbf{b}=\mathbf{i}+3 \mathbf{j}+5 \mathbf{k}$ and $\mathbf{c}=7 \mathbf{i}+9 \mathbf{j}+11 \mathbf{k}$, then the area of Parallelogram having diagonals $\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}$ is
questions.solution:
$$
 \begin{array}{l}
\mathbf{a}=\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k}, b=\mathbf{i}+3 \mathbf{j}+5 \mathbf{k} \\
\text { and } \mathbf{c}=7 \mathbf{i}+9 \mathbf{j}+11 \mathbf{k} \\
\text { Let } \mathbf{A}=\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b} \\
=(\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k})+(\mathbf{i}+3 \mathbf{j}+5 \mathbf{k}) \\
=2 \mathbf{i}+4 \mathbf{j}+6 \mathbf{k} \\
\text { and } \mathbf{B}=\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c} \\
=(\mathbf{i}+3 \mathbf{j}+5 \mathbf{k})+(7 \mathbf{i}+9 \mathbf{j}+11 \mathbf{k}) \\
=8 \mathbf{i}+12 \mathbf{j}+16 \mathbf{k}
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$ Area of parallelogram
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{1}{2}|\mathbf{A} \times \mathbf{B}| \\
(\because \text { A and B are diagonals }) \\
=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
\mathbf{i} & \mathbf{j} & \mathbf{k} \\
2 & 4 & 6 \\
8 & 12 & 16
\end{array}\right| \\
=\frac{1}{2}|\mathbf{i}(64-72)-\mathbf{j}(32-48)+\mathbf{k}(24-32)| \\
=\frac{1}{2} \vdash 8 \mathbf{i}+16 \mathbf{j}-8 \mathbf{k} \mid \\
=\sqrt{(-4)^{2}+(8)^{2}+(-4)^{2}} \\
=\sqrt{96}=4 \sqrt{6} \mathrm{sq} \text { units }
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: An unprepared student takes five-questions of true-false type quiz and guesses every answer. What is the probability that the student will pass the quiz if at least four correct answers is the passing grade?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{n}=$ total number of ways $=2^{5}=32$
Since each answer can be true or false
$\& \mathrm{~m}=$ favourable number of ways
$\begin{array}{l}
={ }^{5} \mathrm{C}_{4}+{ }^{5} \mathrm{C}_{5} \\
=\frac{5 !}{4 ! 1 !}+\frac{5 !}{5 ! 0 !}=5+1=6 \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=6
\end{array}$
Since to pass the quiz, student must give 4 or 5 true answers.
Hence, $p=\frac{m}{n} \Rightarrow p=\frac{6}{32} \Rightarrow p=\frac{3}{16}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A transformer has 200 windings in the primary and 400 windings in the secondary. The primary is connected to an AC supply of 110 V and a current of 10 A flows in it. The voltage across the secondary and the current in it, respectively, are
questions.solution:
Voltage across secondary coil is
$E_s=E_\rho \times \frac{N_s}{N_p}=\frac{110 \times 400}{200}=220 \mathrm{~V}$
Current in secondary coil is
$I_s=I_p \times \frac{E_p}{E_s}=\frac{10 \times 110}{220}=5 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current \(i\) passed in any instrument in an A.C. circuit is \(\mathrm{i}=2 \sin \omega \mathrm{t}\) amp and potential difference applied is given by \(\mathrm{V}=5 \cos \omega \mathrm{t}\) volt. Power loss in the instrument is:
questions.solution:
\(i=2 \sin \omega t\)
\(\mathrm{V}=5 \cos \omega \mathrm{t}\)
\(\mathrm{I}(\max )=2 \mathrm{amp}, \mathrm{V}=5 \mathrm{~V}\)
\(\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{VI}=5 \times 2=10\) watt.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The primary of a transformer has 400 turns while the secondary has 2000 turns. If the power output from the secondary at 1000 V is 12 kW . The resistance of the primary is $0.2 \Omega$ and that of the secondary is $2 \Omega$. If the efficiency of the transformer is $80 \%$, then the power loss in the primary is
questions.solution:
$N_p=400, \mathrm{N_s}=2000$ and $E_s=1000 \mathrm{~V}$
Now, $\frac{E_p}{E_s}=\frac{N_p}{N_s}$ or $\quad E_p=\frac{E_s \times N_p}{N_s}=\frac{1000 \times 400}{2000}=200 \mathrm{~V}$
Since the efficiency is $80 \%$, the input power is
$12 \mathrm{~kW} \times \frac{100}{80}=15 \mathrm{~kW}$
$\therefore$ Current in the primary is
$\begin{aligned} I_p & =\frac{\text { Input power }}{\text { Input voltage }}=\frac{15 \mathrm{~kW}}{200 \mathrm{~V}} \\ & =\frac{15 \times 1000}{200}=75 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Power loss in primary $=I_\rho^2 R_\rho=(75)^2 \times 0.2$
$\begin{aligned} & =1125 \mathrm{~W}=1.125 \mathrm{~kW} \\ & =1125 \mathrm{~W}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In A.C. circuit in which inductance and capacitance are joined in series. Current is found to be maximum when the value of inductance is \(0.5 \mathrm{H}\) and the value of capacitance is \(8 \mu \mathrm{F}\). The angular frequency of applied alternating voltage will be:
questions.solution:
Current is max, when at resonance condition
So, \(\omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.5 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{4 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
& \omega=\frac{1 \times 1000}{2} \\
& \omega=500 \mathrm{~Hz}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An alternating voltage of $220 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~Hz}$ frequency is applied across a capacitor of capacitance $2 \mu \mathrm{F}$. The impedence of the circuit is
questions.solution:
Impedence of a capacitor is $\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}=1 / \omega \mathrm{C}$
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \mathrm{fC}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}=\frac{5000}{\pi}$

questions.solution:

$$
\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \mathrm{fC}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}}=\frac{5000}{\pi}
$$

questions.q: In the given circuit the reading of voltmeter $V_{1}$ and $V_{2}$ are 300 volt each. The reading of the voltmeter $V_{3}$ and ammeter $A$ are respectively
questions.solution:
As $V_{L}=V_{C}=300 V$, resonance will take place $\therefore V_{R}=220 V ; I=\frac{220}{100}=2.2 \mathrm{~A}$
reading of $V_{3}=220 \mathrm{~V}$ and reading of $A=2.2 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An A.C. source is connected to a resistive circuit. Which of the following is true?
options: Current leads ahead of voltage in phase
Current lags behind voltage in phase
Current and voltage are in same phase
Any of the above may be true depending upon the value of resistance.
questions.solution: When resistance is connected to A.C source, then current \& voltage are in same phase.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $i_{1}=3 \sin \omega t$ and $i_{2}=4 \cos \omega t$, then $i_{3}$ is
questions.solution:
From Kirchhoff's current law,
$\begin{array}{l}
i_{3}=i_{1}+i_{2}=3 \sin \omega t+4 \sin \left(\omega t+90^{\circ}\right) \\
=\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}+2(3)(4) \cos 90^{\circ}} \sin (\omega t+\phi) \\
\text { where } \tan \phi=\frac{4 \sin 90^{\circ}}{3+4 \cos 90^{\circ}}=\frac{4}{3} \\
\therefore i_{3}=5 \sin \left(\omega t+53^{\circ}\right)
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The value of inductance $L$ for which the current is maximum in series $L C R$ circuit with $C=10 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and $\omega=1000 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$
questions.solution:
Maximum current flows in the circuit in resonance condition Current in the LCR circuit
$i=\frac{V}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{L}-X_{C}\right)^{2}}}$
For current to be maximum denominator should be minimum $\left(X_{L}-X_{C}\right)^{2}=0$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}} \Rightarrow \omega \mathrm{L}=\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}} \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{L}=\frac{1}{\omega^{2} \mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{(100)^{2} \times 10 \times 10^{-6}} \\
\quad \mathrm{~L}=\frac{1}{10} \mathrm{H}=0.1 \mathrm{H}=100 \mathrm{mH}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An $L-C-R$ circuit contains $R=50 \Omega, L=1 \mathrm{mH}$ and $\mathrm{C}=0.1 \mu \mathrm{F}$. The impedence of the circuit will be minimum for a frequency of

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current graph for resonance in $L C$ circuit is
questions.solution:
In LC circuit, if $X_{\mathrm{L}}=X_{C}$ then $\omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}$
$\mathrm{I}_{0} \infty, \text { so } \mathrm{Z}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{0}}{\mathrm{I}_{0}}=0$
As $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}$ is the natural frequency of LC circuit, therefore for an LC circuit if the frequency of applied AC becomes equal to the natural frequency of an $\mathrm{AC}$ circuit then the amplitude of current becomes infinite due to zero impedance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following graphs represents the correct variation of inductive reactance $X_L$ with angular frequency $\omega$ ?
questions.solution:
Since, $\begin{aligned} X_L & =\omega L\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad X_L \propto \omega$
Hence, inductive reactance increases linearly with angular frequency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an A.C. circuit resistance \(R\), inductance \(L\) and capacitance \(C\) are in parallel. The value of \(R\) is \(50 \Omega\), inductive reactance \(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=40 \Omega\) and capacitive reactance \(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}=25 \Omega\). Then the reading of ammeter will be:
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{I}=\sqrt{\mathrm{I}^2{ }_{\mathrm{R}}+\left(\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}}-\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{L}}\right)^2} \\
& \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{200}{50}=4 \mathrm{~A} \\
& \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}}=\frac{200}{25}=8 \mathrm{~A} \\
& \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{L}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}}=\frac{200}{40}=5 \mathrm{~A} \\
& \mathrm{I}=\sqrt{(4)^2+(8-5)^2} \\
& \mathrm{I}=\sqrt{16+9}=\sqrt{25} \\
& \mathrm{I}=5 \mathrm{~A}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What direct current will produce the same amount of thermal energy in a resistance \(R=\) \(2 \Omega\) as an alternating current that a peak value of \(4.24 \mathrm{~A}\) and frequency \(50 \mathrm{~Hz}\) ?
questions.solution:
Peak current, \(\mathrm{I}_0=4.24 \mathrm{~A}\)
\(\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_0}{\sqrt{2}}=\left(\frac{4.24}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\)
Thermal energy produced,
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{d}}^2 \mathrm{RT}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}^2 \mathrm{RT} \\
& \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{d}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}^2}
\end{aligned}\)
Direct current,
\(I_d=\sqrt{\left(\frac{4.24}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}=\frac{4.24}{\sqrt{2}}=2.99 \mathrm{~A} \simeq 3 \mathrm{~A}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a series \(L C R\) circuit the frequency of a \(10 \mathrm{~V}\) a.c. voltage source is adjusted in such a fashion that the reactance of the inductor measures \(15 \Omega\) and that of the capacitor \(11 \Omega\). If \(\mathrm{R}=3 \Omega\), the potential difference across the series combination of \(L\) and \(C\) will be:
questions.solution:
\(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=15 \Omega ; \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}=11 \Omega ; \mathrm{R}=3 \Omega\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text { Impedance, } \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)^2} \\
& =\sqrt{9+16}=5 \Omega
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the adjoining circuit, if the reading of voltmeter $\mathrm{V}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{V}_{2}$ are 300 volts each, then the reading voltmeter $\mathrm{V}_{3}$ and ammeter A are respectively

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The turn ratio of transformers is given as $2: 3 .$ If the current through the primary coil is $3 \mathrm{~A}$, thus calculate the current through load resistance
questions.solution:
Transformation ratio, $\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{S}}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{S}}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{S}}}$
i.e. $\frac{3}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{S}}}=\frac{3}{2}$ or, $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{S}}=2 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an $\mathrm{AC}$ circuit, the potential across an inductance and resistance joined in series are respectively $16 \mathrm{~V}$ and $20 \mathrm{~V}$. The total potential difference across the circuit is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil of resistance $10 \Omega$ and inductance $5 \mathrm{H}$ is connected to a $100 \mathrm{~V}$ battery. Then the energy stored in the coil is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a series RLC circuit $\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=2 \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}$. The impedance of the circuit and phase difference between $V$ and I respectively will be
questions.solution:
Given, $\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=2 \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}$ $\mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^{2}+\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)^{2}}$ $=\sqrt{\left(2 \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)^{2}+\left(2 \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)^{2}}$ $=\sqrt{4 \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}^{2}+\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}^{2}}$
$$ \begin{array}{l} =\sqrt{5} X_{C}=\frac{\sqrt{5} R}{2} \quad\left[\because R=2 X_{C}\right] \\ \tan \phi=\frac{\left(X_{L}-X_{C}\right)}{R}=\frac{\left(2 X_{C}-X_{C}\right)}{2 X_{C}} \end{array} $$ $$ \tan \phi=\frac{1}{2} ; \phi=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A potential difference of $2 \mathrm{~V}$ is applied between the opposite faces of a Ge crystal plate of area $1 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and thickness $0.5 \mathrm{~mm}$. If the concentration of electrons in Ge is $2 \times 10^{19} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$ and mobilities of electrons and holes are $0.36 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~V}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ and $0.14$ $\mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{~V}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ respectively, then the current flowing through the plate will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The following series $\mathrm{L}-\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{R}$ circuit, when driven by an emf source of angular frequency 70 kilo-radians per second, the circuit effectively behaves like
questions.solution: Impedance, $Z=\sqrt{\left(X_{L} \sim X_{C}\right)^{2}+R^{2}}$ or $Z=\sqrt{\left(\omega L \sim \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^{2}+R^{2}}$ Inductive reactance $$ X_{L}=\omega L=70 \times 10^{3} \times 100 \times 10^{-6}=7 \Omega $$ Capacitance reactance $\begin{aligned} X_{C} &=\frac{1}{\omega C}=\frac{1}{70 \times 10^{3} \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} \\ &=\frac{1}{7 \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{10^{2}}{7}=\frac{100}{7} \\ \text { As } X_{C}>X_{L} & \end{aligned}$ So, circuit behaves like R-C circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An AM wave has $1800 \mathrm{~W}$ of total power content. For $100 \%$ modulation the carrier should have power content equal to
questions.solution:
Total power $P_{t}=P_{c}\left[1+\frac{m a^{2}}{2}\right] \because m a^{2}=1$
$$
\therefore 1800=P_{c}\left[1+\frac{1}{2}\right] \Rightarrow P_{c}=1200 \mathrm{~W}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Colpitt oscillator the feedback network consists of
questions.solution: In a colpitt oscillator, the feed-back network consists of two capacitors and one inductor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In nature, the electric charge of any system is always equal to
options: half integral multiple of the least amount of charge
square of the least amount of charge
integral multiple of the least amount of charge
questions.solution: According to quantization of charge, the charge of any system is an integral multiple of the charge of electron which is the least amount of charge on any system.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: RMS value of $\mathrm{AC}$ is _____ of the peak value.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small piece of metal wire is dragged across the gap between the pole pieces of a magnet in $0.5$ second. The magnetic flux between the pole pieces is known to be $8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~Wb}$. The emf induced in the wire is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Q-factor can be increased by having a coil of
questions.solution:
Q-factor is given by $\mathrm{Q}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{C}}}$
$\therefore$ If resistance $\mathrm{R}$ is decreased, $\mathrm{Q}$ increases and inductance $\mathrm{L}$ is increased, $\mathrm{Q}$ increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Current in the LCR circuit becomes extremely large when
options: frequency of AC supply is increased
frequency of AC supply is decreased
inductive reactance becomes equal to capacitive reactance
questions.solution:
Current through an LCR circuit is maximum when impedance is minimum. Now impedance
$Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(\omega L-\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^{2}}$ is minimum at resonance frequency when $\omega \mathrm{L}=\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}}$ and $\mathrm{Z}=\mathrm{R}=$ minium i.e., inductive reactance $(\omega \mathrm{L})$ is equal to capacitive reactance $(1 / \omega C)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A resistor $\mathrm{R}$, an inductor $\mathrm{L}$ and capacitor $\mathrm{C}$ are connected in series to an oscillator of frequency n. If the resonant frequency is $n_{r}$, then the current lags behind voltage, when
questions.solution:
When reactance of inductance is mroe than the reactance of cndenser, the current will lag behind the voltage.
Thus $\omega L < \frac{1}{\omega c}$ or $\omega>\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}$
or $\mathrm{n}>\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}$ or $\mathrm{n}>\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{r}}$
$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{T}}=$ resonant frequency

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductive circuit contains a resistance of $10 \Omega$ and an inductance of $2.0 \mathrm{H}$. Ifan $\mathrm{AC}$ voltage of $120 \mathrm{~V}$ and frequency of $60 \mathrm{~Hz}$ is applied to this circuit, the current in the circuit would be nearly
questions.solution:
Here, impedence $\mathrm{z}=\sqrt{R^{2}+X_{L}^{2}}$ 
$\therefore \sqrt{(10)^{2}+(2 \pi \times 60 \times 2)^{2}}=753.7$
$\therefore$ Current $\mathrm{i}=\frac{V}{Z}=\frac{120}{753.7}=0.159 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The output voltage of a transfor mer connected to $220 \mathrm{~V}$ line is $1100 \mathrm{~V}$ at 2 Acurrent. Itsefficiency is $100 \%$. The current coming from the line is
questions.solution:
For $100 \%$ efficiency
For $100 \%$ enciency- $V_{s} i_{s}=V_{p} i_{p}$ $1100 \times 2=220 \times i_{p}$ i.e., Primary current, $i_{p}=10 \mathrm{~A}$ PART - II (CHEMISTRY)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is the ground state energy of positronium? (The ground state energy of hydrogen is \(-13.6 \mathrm{eV}\))
questions.solution:
For positronium reduced mass \(=\frac{\mathrm{mm}}{\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{m}}\)
\(=\frac{\mathrm{m}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{2}\)
Hence Rydberg's constant for positronium is half of that for hydrogen atom.
Hence, ground state energy for positronium
\(=-\frac{13.6}{2}=-6.8 \mathrm{eV}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hydrogen atom is in an excited state of principal quantum number $(n)$, it emits a photon of wavelength $(\lambda)$, when it returns to the ground state. The value of $n$ is
questions.solution:
As $\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right)$ $\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right)$
Multiply both sides by $\lambda$
$1=\lambda R\left(1-\frac{1}{n^{2}}\right)$ 
or $\frac{1}{\lambda R}=1-\frac{1}{n^{2}}$ 
or $\quad \frac{1}{n^{2}}=1-\frac{1}{\lambda R}=\frac{\lambda R-1}{\lambda R}$ 
or $\quad n=\sqrt{\frac{\lambda R}{\lambda R-1}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron makes a transition from an excited state to the ground state of a hydrogen - like atom. Then
options: kinetic energy decreases, potential energy increases but total energy remains same
kinetic energy and total energy decrease but potential energy increases
its kinetic energy increases but potential energy and total energy decrease
kinetic energy, potential energy and total energy decrease

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of radii of the first three Bohr orbits is
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electron in hydrogen atom first jumps from third excited state to second excited state and then from second excited to the first excited state. The ratio of the wavelength $\lambda_{1}: \lambda_{2}$ emitted in the two cases is
questions.solution: The wave number $(\bar{v})$ of the radiation $$ \begin{array}{l} =\frac{1}{\lambda} \\ =R_{\infty}\left[\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right] \end{array} $$ Now for case (I) $n_{1}=3, n_{2}=2$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}=R_{\infty}\left[\frac{1}{9} \frac{-1}{4}\right], R_{\infty}=\text { Rydberg constant } \\ \frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}=R_{\infty}\left[\frac{4-9}{36}\right]=\frac{-5 R_{\infty}}{36} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_{1}=\frac{-36}{5 R_{\infty}} \\ \frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}=R_{\infty}\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{1}\right]=\frac{-3 R_{\infty}}{4} \Rightarrow \lambda_{2}=\frac{-4}{3 R_{\infty}} \\ \Rightarrow \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\frac{-36}{5 R_{\infty}} \times \frac{3 R_{\infty}}{-4} \Rightarrow \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\frac{27}{5} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hydrogen atom is in ground state. Then to get six lines in emission spectrum, wavelength of incident radiation should be
questions.solution:
Number of possible spectral lines emitted when an electron jumps back to ground
$$
\text { state from } \mathrm{n}^{\text {th }} \text { orbit }=\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-1)}{2}
$$
Here, $\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}-1)}{2}=6 \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=4$
Wavelength $\lambda$ from transition from $\mathrm{n}=1$ to $\mathrm{n}=4$ is given by,
$$
\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right) \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{16}{15 R}=975 Å
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of minimum wavelengths of Balmer and Paschen series of hydrogen atom will be
questions.solution:
Minimum wavelength for Balmer series,
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\lambda_{\min B}}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{\infty^2}\right) \\ & \frac{1}{\lambda_{\min B}}=\frac{R}{4} \text { or } \lambda_{\min B}=\frac{4}{R}\ldots(i)\end{aligned}$ 
Similarly for Paschen series,
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\lambda_{\min P}}=R\left(\frac{1}{3^2}-\frac{1}{\infty^2}\right) \\ & \frac{1}{\lambda_{\min P}}=\frac{R}{9} \text { or } \lambda_{\min P}=\frac{9}{R}\ldots(ii)\end{aligned}$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$\frac{\lambda_{\min \theta}}{\lambda_{\min P}}=\frac{4}{9}=4: 9$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The angular momentum of an electron in Bohr's hydrogen atom whose energy is $-3.4 \mathrm{eV}$, is
questions.solution:
Energy of electron in nth orbit of hydrogen atom
$\begin{aligned}
& E_{n}=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} \mathrm{eV} \\
\Rightarrow & 3.4=-\frac{13.6}{n^{2}} \Rightarrow n^{2}=4
\end{aligned}$
or, $\mathrm{n}=2$
Angular momentum of electron
$\mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~h}}{2 \pi}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\pi}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the electron in the hydrogen atom jumps from third orbit to second orbit, the wavelength of the emitted radiation in term of Rydberg constant is
questions.solution: As we know $\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right)=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right)$ $\quad \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{9-4}{36}\right)=\frac{5 \mathrm{R}}{36}$ $\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{36}{5 \mathrm{R}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In hydrogen discharge tube, it is observed that through a given cross-section $3.31 \times 10^{15}$ electrons are moving from right to left and $3.12 \times 10^{5}$ protons are moving from left to right. The current in the discharge tube and its direction will be
questions.solution:
Here, number of electrons $\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{e}}=3.13 \times 10^{15}$ and number of protons $\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{p}}=3.12 \times 10^{15}$ Current $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{e}}+\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}}$
$\begin{array}{l}
=3.13 \times 10^{15} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-17}+3.12 \times 10^{15} \\
\times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \\
=1 \times 10^{-3}=1 \mathrm{~mA}
\end{array}$
Now, due to excess charge on electrons, the direction of the current will be towards right.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As the electron in Bohr's orbit of hydrogen atom passes from state $n=2$ to, $n=1$, the $\mathrm{KE}(\mathrm{K})$ and the potential energy (U) changes as
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{KE}$ of an electron in nth orbit : $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{n}} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}$ and PE of an electron in nth orbit :
$\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{n}} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}$
$\therefore$ When an electron passes from state $\mathrm{n}=2$ to $\mathrm{n}=1$ $\frac{\mathrm{K}_{2}}{\mathrm{~K}_{1}}=\frac{1^{2}}{2^{2}}=\frac{1}{4}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\text { or } \mathrm{K}_{1}=4 \mathrm{~K}_{2} \\
\frac{\mathrm{U}_{2}}{\mathrm{U}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{l}^{2}}{2^{2}}=\frac{1}{4} \\
\text { or } \mathrm{U}_{1}=4 \mathrm{U}_{2}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a certain metallic surface is illuminated with monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda$, the stopping potential for photoelectric current $3 V_{0}$. When the same surface is illuminated with a light of wave length $2 \lambda$, the stopping potential is $V_{0}$. The threshold wavelength for this surface to photoelectric effect is
questions.solution:
Here, case (i) $\mathrm{e}\left(3 \mathrm{~V}_{0}\right)=\frac{\text { hc }}{\lambda}-\phi_{0}$
Case (ii) $\mathrm{eV}_{0}=\frac{\text { hc }}{2 \lambda}-$ From eqs. (i) and (ii),
Fromeqs. (i) and (ii), $\frac{3 h c}{2 \lambda}-3 \phi_{0}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi_{0}$ or $\frac{3 h c}{2 \lambda}-\frac{h c}{\lambda}=3 \phi_{0}-\phi_{0}=\frac{h c}{2 \lambda}=2 \phi_{0}$
or $\frac{3 h c}{2 \lambda}-\frac{h c}{\lambda}$ or, $\phi_{0}=\frac{h c}{4 \lambda}$
$\therefore$ Threshold wavelength
$\lambda_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\phi_{0}}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{hc}} \times 4 \lambda=4 \lambda$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The values $+\frac{1}{2}$ and $-\frac{1}{2}$ of spin quantum number show
options: rotation of electron clockwise and anticlockwise directions respectively
rotation of electron anti-clockwise and clockwise directions respectively
rotation in any direction according to convention
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The frequency of incident light falling on a photosensitive metal plate is doubled, the kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is
questions.solution: Since, $\lambda_{r}>\lambda_{y}>\lambda_{g}$ the wevelength of red light is greater than threshold wavelength and hence no electrons are emitted.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An arc of radius $r$ carries charge. The linear density of charge is $\lambda$ and the arc subtends an angle $\frac{\pi}{3}$ at the centre. What is electric potential at the centre?
questions.solution: Length of the $\operatorname{arc}=r \theta=\frac{r \pi}{3}$ Charge on the $\operatorname{arc}=\frac{r \pi}{3} \times \lambda$ $\therefore$ Potential at centre $v$ $$ =\frac{k q}{r}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \times \frac{r \pi}{3} \frac{\lambda}{r}=\frac{\lambda}{12 \varepsilon_{0}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Using an AC voltmeter the potential difference in the electrical line in a house is read to be $234 \mathrm{~V}$. If line frequency is known to be 50 cycles/s, the equation for the line voltage is
questions.solution:
$E=E_{0} \sin \omega t$
Voltmeter read rms value $\therefore E_{0}=\sqrt{2} \times 234 \mathrm{~V}=331 \mathrm{~V}$
and $\omega t=2 \pi n t=2 \pi \times 50 \times t=100 \pi t$ Thus, the equation of the line voltage $E=331 \sin (100 \pi t)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron microscope is used to probe the atomic arrangements to a resolution of $5 Å$. What should be the electric potential to which the electrons need to be accelerated?
questions.solution:
We have, $\quad d \sin \varphi=n \lambda$
For $\phi=90^{\circ}$ and $\mathrm{n}=1$, we get $\mathrm{d}=\lambda$
$\begin{array}{l}
\text { But } \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{meV}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{h}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{meV}}} \\
=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34}\right)^{2}}{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times \mathrm{V}}\right)} \\
=\sqrt{\frac{1.5}{\mathrm{~V}} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}} \therefore \mathrm{d}=\sqrt{\frac{1.5}{\mathrm{~V}}} \times 10^{-9}
\end{array}$
or $5 \times 10^{-10}=\sqrt{\frac{1.5}{\mathrm{~V}}} \times 10^{-9}$ or $0.5=\sqrt{\frac{1.5}{\mathrm{~V}}}$
or $0.5 \times 0.5=\frac{1.5}{\mathrm{~V}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}=\frac{1.5}{0.5 \times 0.5}=6 \mathrm{~V}$
No option is matching with the exact answer. But $5 \mathrm{~V}$ is approximately equal to the exact potential. Therefore, option (b) should be the correct option.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Twelve wires of each of resistance 6 ohms are connected to form a cube as shown in the figure. The current enters at a corner $A$ and leaves at the diagonally opposite corner $\mathrm{G}$. The joint resistance across the corners $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{G}$ are
questions.solution:
Let a total current of 6 I enter at A. It divides into three equal parts, each of $2 \mathrm{I}$, along $\mathrm{AE}$, $\mathrm{AB}$ and $\mathrm{AD}$. At $\mathrm{E}, \mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{D}$ each the current 2I divides into two equal parts, each of $\mathrm{I}$, along $\mathrm{EF}, \mathrm{EH}, \mathrm{BF}, \mathrm{BC}, \mathrm{DH}$ and $\mathrm{DC}$. At $\mathrm{F}, \mathrm{H}$ and $\mathrm{C}$, the two currents each of $\mathrm{I}$, combine together to give a current of $2 \mathrm{I}$ at each corner. Thus, at $\mathrm{G}$ we get the same current 6 I as shown in the figure.
Let $\mathrm{r}$ be the value of resistance of each $\mathrm{arm}$ of the cube and $\mathrm{R}$ be the joint resistance across the conners A and G. Applying Kirchhoff's law along the loop AEFGA, we get $2 \mathrm{Ir}+\mathrm{Ir}+2 \mathrm{Ir}=\mathrm{E}$ or $5 \mathrm{Ir}=\mathrm{E}$ ...(1) Also, by Ohm's law, $\begin{array}{l} 6 \mathrm{I} \times \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{E} \\ \Rightarrow 6 \mathrm{IR}=5 \mathrm{Ir}, \text { using }(1) \end{array}$ or, $\mathrm{R}=\frac{5}{6} \mathrm{r}$. Here, $r=6 \Omega$ $\therefore \mathrm{R}=\frac{5}{6} \times 6=5 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: From figure shown below a series LCR circuit connected to a variable frequency $200 \mathrm{~V}$ source. $\mathrm{L}=5 \mathrm{H}, \mathrm{C}=80 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and $\mathrm{R}=40 \Omega$. Then the source frequency which drive the circuit at resonance is
questions.solution:
At resonance, impedance of circuit is minimum. When impedance of capacitor and inductor is same, $\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}$ 
Resonance frequency $v=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}$
$\begin{aligned} v &=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{5 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{400 \times 10^{-6}}} \\ &=\frac{1}{2 \pi \times 20 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &=\frac{10^{3}}{40 \pi}=\frac{1000}{40 \pi}=\frac{25}{\pi} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is the angular momentum of an electron in the fourth orbit of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom?
questions.solution:
Angular momentum in any stationary orbit is $\mathrm{mvr}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}$ for $4^{\text {th }}$ orbit, $\mathrm{n}=4$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{mvI}=\frac{4 \mathrm{~h}}{2 \pi}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The transition of an electrom from $n_{2}=5,6, \ldots \ldots \ldots$. to $n_{1}=4$ gives rise to
questions.solution: According to Bohr's, Brackett series is obtained when an electron jumps to $4^{\text {th }}$ orbit from any other outer orbit

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ground state energy of hydrogen atom is $13.6 \mathrm{eV}$. What is the potential energy of the electron in this state?
questions.solution:
Total energy of electron
$\mathrm{E}=-\frac{\mathrm{K} \mathrm{Ze}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}+\mathrm{P} . \mathrm{E}$
Potential energy in the orbit P.E $=\frac{-\mathrm{KZe}^{2}}{\mathrm{r}}$ 
$\therefore$ P.E. $=2 \times \mathrm{E} \Rightarrow$ P.E. $=2 \times(-13.6)=-27.2 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the electron in hydrogen atom jumps from third orbit to second orbit, the wavelength of the emitted radiation is given by:
questions.solution:
We have, \(\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right)\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right) \\
& \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right) \\
& \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{9-4}{36}\right) \\
& \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R} \times \frac{5}{36} \\
& \lambda=\frac{36}{5 \mathrm{R}}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The minimum wavelength in Lymann series of hydrogen spectra is \(91.2 \mathrm{~nm}\), the longest wavelength in this series must be
questions.solution: \(\mathrm{eV}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{\min }}\) or \(\lambda_{\min }=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{eV}}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The longest wavelength that can be analysed by a sodium chloride crystal of spacing $\mathrm{d}=2.82$ $\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{o}}$ in the second order is
questions.solution: Bragg's condition is $2 \mathrm{dsin} \theta=\mathrm{n} \lambda$, for second order $\mathrm{n}=2, \sin \theta=1 .$ For longest $\lambda, 2 \mathrm{~d}=2 \lambda$ $\Rightarrow \lambda=\mathrm{d}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Vidicon works on the principle of
questions.solution: The vidicon is a storage-type camera tube in which a charge-density pattern is formed by the imaged scene radiation on a photoconductive surface which is then scanned by a beam of low-velocity electrons. The fluctuating voltage coupled out to a video amplifier can be used to reproduce the scene being imaged. The electrical charge produced by an image will remain in the face plate until it is scanned or until the charge dissipates.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The wavelength of an electron for transition from a state $n_{1}$ to $n_{2}$ is $\frac{9}{8 R}$. Which of the following wavelengths is possible for a transition from $\mathrm{n}_{2}$ to $\mathrm{n}_{1}$
questions.solution: Energy released in transition from state $\mathrm{n}_{1}$ to $\mathrm{n}_{2}$ is equal to the energy absorbed in transistion from state $\mathrm{n}_{2}$ to $\mathrm{n}_{1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a hydrogen atom is raised from ground energy level to excited energy level, then
options: potential energy increases and kinetic energy decreases
kinetic energy increases and potential energy decreases
questions.solution: As $\mathrm{r}$ increase, the potential energy increases. Thus, it decreases kinetic energy of hydrogen atom. So, when an atom jumps from one energy level to the higher level, its potential energy increases and kinetic energy decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of the energies of the hydrogen atom in its first to second excited states is
questions.solution:
Ist excited state corresponds to $\mathrm{n}=2$ 2nd excited state corresponds to $\mathrm{n}=3$
As $E \propto \frac{1}{n^{2}}$
$$
\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{E}_{1}}{\mathrm{E}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{2}^{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}^{2}}=\frac{3^{2}}{2^{2}}=\frac{9}{4}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If hydrogen atom is its ground state absorbs, $10.2 \mathrm{eV}$ of energy. The orbital angularmomentum is increase by

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a double slit experiment, $5^{\text {th }}$ dark fringe is formed opposite to one of the slits, the wavelength of light is
questions.solution:
For dark fringe, $$ \frac{x d}{D}=(2 m-1) \frac{\lambda}{2} $$ Here, $m=5, x=\frac{d}{2}$
$\therefore \frac{d}{2} \cdot \frac{d}{D}=(2 \times 5-1) \frac{\lambda}{2}$ or $\frac{d^{2}}{D}=9 \lambda$ $\therefore$ Wavelength, $\lambda=\frac{d^{2}}{9 D}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two electrons one moving in opposite direction with speeds $0.8 \mathrm{c}$ and $0.4 \mathrm{c}$ where $\mathrm{c}$ is the speed of light in vacuum. Then the relative speed is about
questions.solution:
Relative speed is given by
$u=\frac{u^{\prime}+v}{1+\frac{u^{\prime} v}{c^{2}}}$
Here, $u^{\prime}=0.8 \mathrm{c}$ and $\mathrm{v}=0.4 \mathrm{c}$
$\therefore \mathrm{u}=\frac{0.8 \mathrm{c}+0.4 \mathrm{c}}{1+\frac{(0.8 \mathrm{c})(0.4 \mathrm{c})}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}}=\frac{1.2 \mathrm{c}}{1.32}=0.9 \mathrm{c}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A condenser of capacitance $\mathrm{C}$ is fully charged by a $200 \mathrm{~V}$ supply. It is then discharged through a small coil of resistance wire embedded in thermally insulated block of specific heat $250 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K}$ and of mass $100 \mathrm{~g}$. If the temperature of the block rises by $0.4 \mathrm{~K}$, then the value of $\mathrm{C}$ is
questions.solution:
The energy stored in the capacitor
$\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C} \times(200)^{2}=2 \mathrm{C} \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~J}$
This energy is used to heat up the block. Let $\Delta \theta$ be the rise in temperature, then heat energy
$\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{ms} \Delta \theta=0.1 \times 250 \times 0.4=10 \mathrm{~J}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Now, } \\ & 2 \mathrm{C} \times 10^{4}=10 \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{C}=\frac{10}{2 \times 10^{4}}=5 \times 10^{-4}=500 \mu \mathrm{F} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hollow insulated conduction sphere is given a positive charge of $10 \mu \mathrm{C}$. What will be the electric field at the centre of the sphere if its radius is 2 metres?
questions.solution:
Charge resides on the outer surface of a conducting hollow sphere of radius $\mathrm{R}$. We consider a spherical surface of radius $r < R$. By Gauss theorem
$$ \begin{array}{l} \int_{\mathrm{s}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \mathrm{ds}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}} \times \operatorname{ch} a r g \mathrm{e} \quad \text { enclosed or } \\ \mathrm{E} \times 4 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}} \times 0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}=0 \end{array} $$ i.e electric field inside a hollow sphere is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The equivalent capacitance between $a$ and $b$ for the combination of capacitors shown in figure where all capacitances are in microfarad is
questions.solution:
Rearranging the circuits, we get the following circuit.
$\therefore$ equivalent capacitance between $A$ and $B$, $C_{A B}=\frac{4 \times 4}{4+4}=2 \mu F$ and equivalent capacitance between $C$ and $D$, $$ \begin{array}{l} C_{C D}=\frac{8 \times 8}{8+8}=4 \mu F \\ \therefore \quad C_{a b}=2 \mu F+4 \mu F=6 \mu F \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel plate capacitor with air between the plates has a capacitance of $8 \mathrm{pF}$. Calculate the capacitance if the distance between the plates is reduced by half and the space between them is filled with a substance of dielectric constant. (k =6)
questions.solution:
Capacity of parallel plate capacitor
$$
\mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{k} \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}} \quad\left(\text { For air } \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{r}}=1\right)
$$
So, $\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}=8 \times 10^{-12}$
If $d \rightarrow \frac{d}{2}$ and $k_r \rightarrow 6$ then new capacitance $\mathrm{C}^{\prime}=6 \times \frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d} / 2}=12 \frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}=12 \times 8 \mathrm{pF}=96 \mathrm{pF}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: There capacitors \(\mathrm{C}_1, \mathrm{C}_2\) and \(\mathrm{C}_3\) are connected as shown in the figure. A potential difference of 14 volts is applied to the input terminals. What is the charge on \(\mathrm{C}_3(\mathrm{in} \mu \mathrm{C})\) ?
questions.solution: The capacitance of parallel capacitors \(=\frac{2 \times 4}{2+4}=\frac{4}{3} \mu \mathrm{F}\) If \(\mathrm{V}_1\) and \(\mathrm{V}_2\) are the p.d. s across \(\mathrm{C}_1\) and parallel capacitors, then \(\mathrm{V}_1+\mathrm{V}_2=14\) ...(i) \(\begin{aligned} \text { and } 4 \mathrm{~V}_1 & =\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{~V}_2 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{V}_2 & =3 \mathrm{~V}_1 \quad ...(ii) \end{aligned}\) From (i) and (ii), \(\mathrm{V}_1=3.5 \mathrm{~V} \text { and } \mathrm{V}_2=10.5 \mathrm{~V}\) Thus charge of \(\mathrm{C}_3\), \(\mathrm{q}_3=\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{~V}_2=4 \times 10.5=42 \mathrm{~V} \text {. }\)

In given circuit, $\mathrm{C}_{1}=\mathrm{C}_{2}=\mathrm{C}_{3}=\mathrm{C}$ initially. Now, a dielectric slab of dielectric constant $\mathrm{K}=\frac{3}{2}$ is inserted in $\mathrm{C}_{2}$.
The equivalent capacitance become

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The equivalent capacitance of the combination of the capacitors is
questions.solution:
Equivalent capacitance of two parallel capacitors $10 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and $6 \mu \mathrm{F}=(10+6) \mu \mathrm{F}=16 \mu \mathrm{F}$ This $16 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor is in series combination with $4 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor,
$\therefore$ Equivalent capacitance of the entire combination $=\frac{16 \times 4}{16+4}=\frac{64}{20}=3.2 \mu \mathrm{F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Taking the internal resistance of the battery as negligible, the steady state current in the \(2 \Omega\) resistor shown in the figure will be
questions.solution:
No current flows through \(6 \Omega\) resistor as a capacitor offers infinite resistance to a D.C. The equivalent circuit is thus as shown below in which \(2 \Omega\) and \(3 \Omega\) are in parallel
\(\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}=\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}\) or \(\quad R^{\prime}=\frac{6}{5} \Omega=1.2 \Omega\) Current drawn from the battery \(I=\frac{E}{R}=\frac{6}{4}=1.5 \text { Ampere }\) Potential difference between \(\mathrm{A}\) and \(\mathrm{B}\) \(V=I R^{\prime}=1.5 \times 1.2=1.8 \mathrm{~V}\) \(\therefore\) Current through \(2 \Omega\) resistor \(=\frac{V}{2}=\frac{1.8}{2}=0.9 \mathrm{~A}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During charging a capacitor, variations of potential $V$ of the capacitor with time $t$ is shown as
questions.solution: For charging the capacitor, $\mathrm{q}=\mathrm{q}_{0}$ $\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{\frac{-t}{\mathrm{CR}}}\right)$ And, Potential difference $V=V_{0}\left(1-e^{-t / C R}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circuit has a self-inductance of $1 \mathrm{H}$ and carries a current of $2 \mathrm{~A}$. To prevent sparking, when the circuit is switched off, a capacitor which can withstand $400 \mathrm{~V}$ is used. The least capacitance of capacitor connected across the switch must be equal to
questions.solution:
Energy stored in capacitor $=$ energystored in inductance
i.e., $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{LI}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{LI}^{2}}{\mathrm{~V}^{2}}=\frac{1 \times(2)^{2}}{(400)^{2}}=25 \mu \mathrm{F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel plate capacitor has capacitance C. If it is equally filled the parallellayers of materials of dielectric constant $K_{1}$ and $K_{2}$ its capacity becomes $C_{1}$. The ratio of $C_{1}$ and $C$ is
questions.solution:
Capacitance, $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{1} \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{\frac{\mathrm{d}}{2}}, \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{2} \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{\frac{\mathrm{d}}{2}}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~K}_{1} \mathrm{~K}_{2}}{\mathrm{~K}_{1}+\mathrm{K}_{2}} \\
=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{~K}_{1} \mathrm{~K}_{2}}{\mathrm{~K}_{1}+\mathrm{K}_{2}}\right) \frac{\varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}\left(\because \mathrm{e}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}\right)
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor with air as dielectric is $C$. If a slab of dielectric constant $K$ and of the same thickness as the separation between the plates is introduced so as to fill $1 / 4$ th of the capacitor (shown in figure), then the new capacitance is
questions.solution:
Capacitance, $\mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}$
As one-fourth of capacitor is filled with dielectric of constant $\mathrm{K}$, then,
$\mathrm{C}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{K} \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A} / 4}{\mathrm{~d}}$
and $C_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon 3 A / 4}{d}$
Both $C_{1}$ and $C_{2}$ are in parallel.
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{K} \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{4 \mathrm{~d}}+\frac{3 \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{4 \mathrm{~d}} \\
=(\mathrm{K}+3) \frac{\varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{4 \mathrm{~d}}=(\mathrm{K}+3) \frac{\mathrm{C}}{4}
\end{array}$

If the charge on the combination is $80 \mu \mathrm{C}$, the energy stored in the capacitor of capacity $\mathrm{C}$ in $\mu \mathrm{J}$ is

questions.subject: Physics
