questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A light whose frequency is equal to $6 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$ is incident on a metal whose work function is
$2 \mathrm{eV} \cdot\left[\mathrm{h}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right]\left[1 \mathrm{eV}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}\right]$
The maximum energy of the electrons emitted will be
questions.solution:
Here,
Frequency, $v=6 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$
Work-function, $\phi=2 \mathrm{eV}=2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}$
$=3.2 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}$
Maximum energy, $\mathrm{T}_{\max }=$ ?
By Einstein's photo electric equation, we have
$\begin{aligned} & h v=\phi+T_{\max } \\ \Rightarrow & T_{\max }=h v-\phi \\=&\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 6 \times 10^{14}\right)-\left(3.2 \times 10^{-19}\right) \\=&\left(3.97 \times 10^{-19}\right)-\left(3.2 \times 10^{-19}\right) \\=& 0.77 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\=& \frac{0.77 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}=0.49 \mathrm{eV} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One electron and one proton is accelerated by equal potential. Ratio in their deBroglie wavelength is
questions.solution:
de-Broglie wavelength $(\lambda)$ of a particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and moving with a velocity $\mathrm{v}$ is given by, $\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}$, where $\mathrm{h}$ is Planck's constant.
When a particle having charge $\mathrm{q}$ is accelerated through a potential $\mathrm{V}$ then
$\mathrm{qV}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}$
or, $\mathrm{qV}=\frac{\mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{v}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~m}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{mv}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mqV}}$
$\therefore \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mqV}}}$
Hence, de-Broglie wavelength of electron,
$\lambda_{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{eV}}}$
and de-Broglie wavelength of proton, $\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{eV}}}$, where e represents the charge of electron (or proton)
$\therefore \frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{e}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The half life of a radioactive element is $3.8$ days. The fraction left after 19 days will be
questions.solution:
Given half life $\mathrm{T}=3.8$ days; $\mathrm{t}=19$ days
$\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_{0}}=?$ now $\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{19}{3.8}=5$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_{0}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{T}}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{5}=\frac{1}{32}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{0}}{32}=0.03 \mathrm{~N}_{0}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Identify the INCORRECT statement regarding a superconducting wire
options: transport current flows through its surface
transport current flows through the entire area of cross-section of the wire
it exhibits zero electrical resistivity and expels applied magnetic field
it is used to produce large magnetic field
questions.solution: The transport current flows through the surface of the superconducting wire and not through the entire area of cross-section of the wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A potential difference of $300 \mathrm{~V}$ is applied to a combination of $2.0 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and $8.0 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitors connected in series. The charge on the $2.0 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{V}=300 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{C}_{1}=2.0 \mu \mathrm{F}, \mathrm{C}_{2}=8.0 \mu \mathrm{F}$,
Net capacitance, $\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{s}}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{2}}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{\mathrm{C}_{1}+\mathrm{C}_{2}} \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{2 \times 8}{2+8}=\frac{16}{10}=1.6 \mu \mathrm{F}
\end{array}
$$
Now total charge,
$\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}} \times \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{s}}=300 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-6}=4.8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{C}$
$\because$ In series charge is same on capacitors
$\Rightarrow$ Charge on $2 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor is $4.8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two capacitors $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ in a circuit are joined as shown in figure. The potentials of points $A$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are $\mathrm{V}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{V}_{2}$ respectively. Then the potential of point D will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor \(\mathrm{C}_1\) is charged to a potential difference \(\mathrm{V}\). The charging battery is then removed and the capacitor is connected to an uncharged capacitor \(\mathrm{C}_2\). The potential difference across the combination is
questions.solution:
Charge \(\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{~V}\)
Total capacity of combination (parallel)
\(\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2\)
\(\text {P.D. }=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{~V}}{\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The series combination of two capacitors shown in figure is connected across $1000 \mathrm{~V}$. The magnitude of the charges on the capacitors will be
questions.solution:
In series combination of capacitors, charges on both capacitors will be same.
$\begin{array}{l}
V_{s}=\frac{Q}{C_{1}}+\frac{Q}{C_{2}} \\
\Rightarrow 1000=Q\left(\frac{1}{C_{1}}+\frac{1}{C_{2}}\right) \\
\Rightarrow 1000=Q\left(\frac{C_{1}+C_{2}}{C_{1} C_{2}}\right) \\
\Rightarrow Q=\frac{1000 \times C_{1} C_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}} \\
Q=1000 \times \frac{3 \times 10^{-12} \times 6 \times 10^{-12}}{(3+6) \times 10^{-12}}=\frac{18}{9} \times 10^{-9} \\
=2 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two capacitors of capacities $\mathrm{C}$ and $2 \mathrm{C}$ are connected in parallel and then connected in series with a third capacitor of capacity $3 \mathrm{C}$. The combination is charged with $\mathrm{V}$ volt. The charge on capacitor of capacity $\mathrm{C}$ is
questions.solution:
In parallel combination, the potential difference across each individual capacitor is the same as the potenital difference across the combination.
So, potential difference across capacitor of capacity $\mathrm{C}$ is $\frac{V}{2}$.
Charge on capacitor, $\mathrm{C}=\frac{Q}{V}$
$\therefore$ Charge on capacitor of capacity $\mathrm{C}=\frac{C V}{2}$

questions.q: $K_{\alpha}$ and $K_{\beta}$ X-rays are emitted when there is a transition of electron between the levels
questions.solution: When electron jumps from the level $\mathrm{n}=2$ to the level $\mathrm{n}=1, \mathrm{~K}_{\alpha} \mathrm{x}-$ rays are emitted. Similarly, $\mathrm{K}_{3}$ x-rays are emitted when there is atransition of electron from the level $\mathrm{n}=3$ to the level $\mathrm{n}=1$. X-ray spectra has been shown below.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor with air as medium is $3 \mu \mathrm{F}$. As a dielectric is introduced between the plates, the capacitance becomes 15 $\mu \mathrm{F}$. The permittivity of the medium in $\mathrm{C}^{2} \mathrm{~N}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ is
questions.solution:
Capacitance of air capacitor
$\mathrm{C}_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}=3 \mu \mathrm{F}$
When a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}$ and dielectric constant $\mathrm{K}$ is introduced between the plates, then
Capacitance, $\mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{K} \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}=15 \mu \mathrm{F}$
Dividing eq. (ii) by (i), we get
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{C}_{0}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\frac{\varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}}=\frac{15}{3} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & \mathrm{K}=5 \\
\therefore \quad & \text { permittivity of the medium } \\
& \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=\varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~K} \\
&=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 5=0.44 \times 10^{-10}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical incandescent light bulbs are connected as shown in the Figure. When the circuit is an AC voltage source of frequency $f$, which of the following observations will be correct?
options: both bulbs will glow with same brightness provided frequency $\mathrm{f}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{(1 / L C)}}$
bulb b $_{1}$ will light up initially and goes off, bulb b $_{2}$ will be ON constantly
bulb b $_{1}$ will blink and bulb $\mathrm{b}_{2}$ will be $\mathrm{ON}$ constantly
questions.solution: The circuit shown is a parallel resonant circuit. The frequency is $\mathrm{f}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}$ at resonance. Also, at resonance, the capacitative reactance is equal to the inductive reactance. Therefore, equal current will flow through both the bulbs $\mathrm{b}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{b}_{2}$. So, both will glow with same brightness.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Two masses \(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}}\) and \(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{b}}\) moving with velocities \(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{a}}\) and \(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{b}}\) in opposite direction collide elastically and after the collision \(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}}\) and \(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{b}}\) move with velocities \(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{b}}\) and \(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{a}}\) respectively. Then the ratio \(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}} / \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{b}}\) is
questions.solution:
As velocities are exchanged on perfectly elastic collision, therefore masses of two objects must be equal.
\(\therefore \frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{b}}}=1 \text { or } \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}}=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{b}} \text {. }\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: The principle of conservation of linear momentum can be strictly applied during a collision between two particles provided the time of impact
questions.solution:
In physics, collision does not means that one particle strike another particle. Infact, two particles may not even touch each other \& may still said to be colliding.
The necessary requirements of collision are
(i) A large force for a relatively short time (i.e., an impulse) acts on each colliding particle.
(ii) The motion of the particles (at least one of the particle) is changed abruptly.
(iii) The total momentum (as also the total energy) of particles remains conserved.

questions.subject: Physics

Each bullet has a mass $10 \mathrm{~g}$ with a muzzle velocity of $800 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The velocity which the rifle man attains after firing 10 shots will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A disc of mass 10 g is kept floating horizontally by throwing 10 marbles per second against it from below. If the mass of each marble is 5 g . What will be velocity with which the marble are striking the disc? Assume that, the marble strikes the disc normally and rebound downwards with the same speed.
questions.solution:
$F=\frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}=\frac{\Delta(m n v)}{\Delta t}$
Here, $m=$ mass of one marble $=5 \mathrm{~g}$ $\begin{aligned} \frac{n}{\Delta t} & =\text { number of molecules striking per second } \\ & =10 \\ \therefore M g & =m\left(\frac{n}{\Delta t}\right)|\Delta v|\end{aligned}$ Here, $M=$ mass of disc $\begin{aligned}\left(10 \times 10^{-3}\right)(9.80) & =\left(5 \times 10^{-3}\right)(10) 2 v \\ \text { or } \quad v & =0.98 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A smooth sphere of mass $\mathrm{M}$ moving with velocity u directly collides elastically with another sphere of mass $\mathrm{m}$ at rest. After collision, their final velocities are $V$ and $v$ respectively. The value of $v$ is
questions.solution:
By law of conservation of momentum, $\mathrm{Mu}=\mathrm{MV}+\mathrm{mv}$
$.$ (i)
Also e $=\frac{\left|\mathrm{v}_{1}-\mathrm{v}_{2}\right|}{\left|\mathrm{u}_{1}-\mathrm{u}_{2}\right|} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Mu}=\mathrm{Mv}-\mathrm{MV}$
(ii)
From (i) and (ii), $2 \mathrm{Mu}=(\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}) \mathrm{v}$
$$
\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\frac{2 \mathrm{uM}}{\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\frac{2 \mathrm{u}}{1+\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{M}}}
$$

questions.q: Two bodies A and B having masses in the ratio of $3: 1$ possess the same kinetic energy. The ratio of linear momentum of B to A is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Two balls of equal masses are thrown upwards along the same vertical direction at an interval of $2 \mathrm{~s}$, with the same initial velocity of $39.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The two balls will collide at a height of
questions.solution:
Let two balls collide at a height $s$ from the ground after $t$ second when second ball is thrown upwards.
$\therefore$ Time taken by first ball to reach the point of collision $=(t+2) \mathrm{s}$
$s=39.2(t+2)+\frac{1}{2}(-9.8)(t+2)^{2}$
$=39.2(\mathrm{t}+2)-4.9(\mathrm{t}+2)^{2}$
For second ball
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{s}=39.2 \mathrm{t}+\frac{1}{2}(-9.8) \mathrm{t}^{2} \\
=39.2 \mathrm{t}-4.9 \mathrm{t}^{2} \quad \text { (ii) } \\
\text { Fromeqs. (i) and (ii) } \\
39.2(\mathrm{t}+2)-4.9(\mathrm{t}+2)^{2}=(39.2) \mathrm{t}-4.9 \mathrm{t}^{2} \\
\text { On solving we get, } \mathrm{t}=3 \mathrm{~s} \\
\text { From Eq. (ii), } \\
\mathrm{s}=39.2 \times 3-4.9 \times(3)^{2}=117.6-44.1=73.5 \\
\mathrm{~m}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A body of mass $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ makes an elastic collision with another body at rest and continues to move in the original direction after collision with a velocity equal to $\frac{1}{10}$ th of its original velocity. Then the mass of the second body is
questions.solution:
Mass of the first body $m_{1}=5 \mathrm{~kg}$ and for elastic collision coefficient of restitution, $e=1$.
Let initially body $m_{1}$ moves with velocity $v$ after collision velocity becomes $\left(\frac{u}{10}\right)$. Let after collision velocity of $M$ block becomes $\left(v_{2}\right)$ By conservation of momentum $m_{1} u_{1}+m_{2} u_{2}=m_{1} v_{1}+m_{2} v_{2}$ or $\quad 5 u+M \times 0=5 \times \frac{u}{10}+M v_{2}$ or $\quad 5 u=\frac{u}{2}+M v_{2}$ Since, $v_{1}-v_{2}=-e\left(u_{1}-u_{2}\right)$ or $\frac{u}{10}-v_{2}=-1(u)$ or $\frac{u}{10}+u=v_{2}$ or $\frac{11 u}{10}=v_{2}$ Substituting value of $v_{2}$ in Eq. (i) from Eq. (ii) $5 u=\frac{u}{2}+M\left(\frac{11 u}{10}\right) \quad=\frac{9 \times 10}{2 \times 11}=\frac{45}{11}$ or $5-\frac{1}{2}=M\left(\frac{11}{10}\right) \Rightarrow M=4.09 \mathrm{~kg}$ $=4.09 \mathrm{~kg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A body of mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ moving with velocity of $6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ strikes in elastically with another body of same mass mass at rest. The amount of heat evolved during collision is
questions.solution:
Common velocity, $\mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2} \mathrm{v}_{2}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}}$
$\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{2 \times 6+2 \times 0}{2+2}=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
\text { Initial kinetic energy }\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \\
=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_{2} \mathrm{v}_{2}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times(6)^{2}=36 \mathrm{~J} \\
=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{V}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}(2+2)(3)^{2}=18 \mathrm{~J} \\
\therefore \text { Heat evolved }=(36-18) \mathrm{J}=18 \mathrm{~J}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Three resistances of $4 \Omega$ each are connected as shown in figure. If the point $\mathrm{D}$ divides the resistance into two equal halves, the resistance between point $A$ and $D$ will be
questions.solution:
Redrawing the figure, we get
The $4 \Omega$ and $2 \Omega$ resistors are in series and the same is the case with another $4 \Omega$ and $2 \Omega$ resistors. So, the four resistors are equivalent to following two resistors. Now, in fig (b) these two $6 \Omega$ resistors are in parallel. $\therefore$ Equivalent resistance, $\mathrm{R}=\frac{6 \times 6}{6+6}=\frac{36}{12}$ $=3 \Omega$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A particle of mass $4 \mathrm{~m}$ explodes into three pieces of masses $m, m$ and $2 m$. The equal masses move along $\mathrm{X}$-axis and $\mathrm{Y}$-axis with velocities $4 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ and $6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ respectively. The magnitude of the velocity of the heavier mass is
questions.solution:
Let third mass particle $(2 m)$ moves making angle $\theta$ with $X$-axis. The horizontal component of velocity of $2 m$ mass particle $=u \cos \theta$ And vertical component $=u \sin \theta$
From conservation of linear momentum in $X$-direction $m_{1} u_{1}+m_{2} u_{2}=m_{1} v_{1}+m_{2} v_{2}$ or $\quad 0=m \times 4+2 m(u \cos \theta)$ or $-4=2 u \cos \theta$ or $-2=u \cos \theta...(i)$ Again, applying law of conservation of linear momentum in $Y$-direction $$ \begin{array}{l} 0=m \times 6+2 m(u \sin \theta) \\ \Rightarrow-\frac{6}{2}=u \sin \theta \text { or }-3=u \sin \theta...(ii) \end{array} $$ Squaring Eqs. (i) and (ii) and adding, $$ \begin{aligned} (4)+(9) &=u^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta+u^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta \\ &=u^{2}\left(\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta\right) \\ \text { or } \quad 13 &=u^{2} \\ \therefore \quad u &=\sqrt{13} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A bullet of mass $0.02 \mathrm{~kg}$ travelling horizontally with velodity $250 \mathrm{~ms}^{1}$ strikes a block of wood of mass $0.23 \mathrm{~kg}$ which rests on a rough horizontal surface. After the impact, the block and bullet move together and come to rest after travelling a distance of $40 \mathrm{~m}$. The coefficient of sliding friction of the rough surface is $\left(\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}\right)$
questions.solution:
After collision the bullet and block move together and comes to rest after covering a distance of $40 \mathrm{~m}$.
By conservation of momentum $$ \begin{array}{l} m_{1} u_{1}+m_{2} u_{2}=m_{1} v_{1}+m_{2} v_{2} \\ \Rightarrow 0.02 \times 250+0.23 \times 0=0.02 v+0.23 v \\ 5+0=v(0.25) \text { or } v=20 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1} \end{array} $$ Now, by conservation of energy $$ \begin{array}{l} \text { or } \frac{1}{2} M v^{2}=\mu R \cdot d \\ \text { or } \frac{1}{2} \times 0.25 \times 400=\mu \times 0.25 \times 9.8 \times 40 \\ \Rightarrow \mu=\frac{200}{9.8 \times 40}=0.51 \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A $5000 \mathrm{~kg}$ rocket is set for vertical firing. The exhaust speed is $800 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. To give an initial upward acceleration of $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$, the amount of gas ejected per second to supply the needed thrust will be (Take $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ )
questions.solution:
Given : Mass of rocket $(\mathrm{m})=5000 \mathrm{Kg}$ Exhaust speed $(v)=800 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Acceleration of rocket $(\mathrm{a})=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$
Gravitational acceleration $(\mathrm{g})=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$
We know that upward force
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{F} &=\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{g}+\mathrm{a})=5000(10+20) \\
&=5000 \times 30 &=150000 \mathrm{~N}
\end{aligned}
$$
We also know that amount of gas ejected
$$
\left(\frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{150000}{800}=187.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{s}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A metal ball of mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ moving with a velocity of $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ has a head on collision with a stationary ball of mass $3 \mathrm{~kg}$. If after the collision, the two balls move together, the loss in kinetic energy due to collision is
questions.solution:
Apply conservation of momentum,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{1}=\left(\mathrm{m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{v} \\
\mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{1}}{\left(\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}\right)} \\
\text { Here } \mathrm{v}_{1}=36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
\mathrm{m}_{1}=2 \mathrm{~kg}, \mathrm{~m}_{2}=3 \mathrm{~kg} \\
\mathrm{v}=\frac{10 \times 2}{5}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{array}
$$
K.E. (initial) $=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times(10)^{2}=100 \mathrm{~J}$ 
K.E. (Final) $=\frac{1}{2} \times(3+2) \times(4)^{2}=40 \mathrm{~J}$
Loss in K.E. $=100-40=60 \mathrm{~J}$
Alternatively use the formula
$$
-\Delta \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{~m}_{2}}{\left(\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}\right)}\left(\mathrm{u}_{1}-\mathrm{u}_{2}\right)^{2}
$$

questions.solution: Frequency deviation is the frequency of an FM transmitter without signal input.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In frequency modulation
options: the amplitude of modulated wave varies as frequency of carrier wave
the frequency of modulated wave varies as amplitude of modulating wave
the amplitude of modulated wave varies as frequency of carrier wave
the frequency of modulated wave varies as frequency of modulating wave
questions.solution: The process of changing the frequency of a carrier wave (modulated wave) in accordance with the audio frequency signal (modulating wave) is known as frequency modulation (FM).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a radio-receiever amplifiers all the signal frequencies equally well, it is said to have high
questions.solution: If a radio receiver amplifies all the signal frequencies equally well, it is said to have high fidelity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A transmitter radiates \(10 \mathrm{~kW}\) of power with the carrier unmodulated and \(11.8 \mathrm{~kW}\) with the carrier sinusoidally modulated. The modulation factor is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& P_{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{t}}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{m}^2}{2}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow 11.8=10\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{m}^2}{2}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=0.6 \Rightarrow \% \text { modulation }=60 \% .
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Beyond which frequency, the ionosphere bands any incident electromagnetic radiation but do not reflect it back towards the earth?
questions.solution: The ionosphere can reflect electromagnetic waves of frequency less than $40 \mathrm{MHz}$ but not of frequency more than $40 \mathrm{MHz}$.

questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The maximum range, $\mathrm{d}_{\max }$ of radar is
options: proportional to the cube root of the peak transmitted power
proportional to the fourth root of the peak transmitted power
proportional to the square root of the peak transmitted power
not related to the peak transmitted power at all
questions.solution: Maximum range of radar $\mathrm{d}_{\max } \propto \sqrt{1}$ and power transmitted by antenna of length 1 is $\mathrm{p} \propto(1 / \lambda)^{2} \Rightarrow 1 \propto \sqrt{\mathrm{p}}$ and $\mathrm{d} \propto(\mathrm{p})^{1 / 4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The fundamental radio antenna is a metal rod which has a length equal to
options: $\lambda$ in free space at the frequency of operation
$\frac{\lambda}{2}$ in free space at the frequency of operation
$\frac{\lambda}{4}$ in free space at the frequency of operation
$\frac{3 \lambda}{4}$ in free space at the frequency of operation
questions.solution: The common antenna is a straight conductor of length $1=\frac{\lambda}{4}$ held vertically with its lower end touching the ground.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a Wheatstone's bridge all the four arms have equal resistance \(R\). If the resistance of the galvanometer arm is also \(R\), the equivalent resistance of the combination as seen by the battery is
questions.solution: Since, wheatstone's bridge is balanced, then resistance of galvanometer will be uneffective.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the network shown in the Fig, each resistance is \(1 \Omega\). The effective resistance between \(A\) and \(B\) is
questions.solution:
At A current is distributed and at B currents are collected. Between A and B, the distribution is symmetrical. It has been shown in the figure. It appears that current in \(\mathrm{AO}\) and \(\mathrm{OB}\) remains same. At \(\mathrm{O}\), current \(\mathrm{i}_4\) returns back without any change. If we detach \(\mathrm{O}\) from \(\mathrm{AB}\) there will not be any change in distribution. Now, CO & OD will be in series hence its total resistance \(=2 \Omega\) It is in parallel with \(\mathrm{CD}\), so, equivalent resistance \(=\frac{2 \times 1}{2+1}=\frac{2}{3} \Omega\) This equivalent resistance is in series with \(\mathrm{AC}\) & \(\mathrm{DB}\), so, total resistance \(=\frac{2}{3}+1+1=\frac{8}{3} \Omega\)
Now \(\frac{8}{3} \Omega\) is parallel to \(\mathrm{AB}\), that is, \(2 \Omega\), so total resistance \(=\frac{8 / 3 \times 2}{8 / 3+2}=\frac{16 / 3}{14 / 3}=\frac{16}{14}=\frac{8}{7} \Omega\) [Alt:
Between \(\mathrm{C}\) & D, the equivalent resistance is given by \(1 / \mathrm{r}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_3}+\frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{r}_4+\mathrm{r}_5\right)}=1+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{3}{2}\) Equivalent resistance along \(\mathrm{ACDB}=1+\frac{2}{3}+1=\frac{8}{3}\) \(\therefore\) Effective resistance between \(\mathrm{A}\) and \(\mathrm{B}\) is \(\left.\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{3}{8}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{7}{8} \text { or } \mathrm{R}=\frac{8}{7}\right]\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The maximum power rating of a $20 \Omega$ resistor is 2 kW . Then, it cannot be used with 300 V DC source because the
options: current drawn is very large and it can blow the fuse.
current drawn is very less and appliance does not work.
resistor may melt due to excessive heat.
questions.solution:
Maximum power rating of $20 \Omega$ resistor,
$P_r=2 \mathrm{~kW}$
Power dissipated through $20 \Omega$ resistor when it is connected with 300 VD ,
$\begin{aligned} P_1 & =\frac{V^2}{R}=\frac{300^2}{20} \\ \Rightarrow \quad P_1 & =\frac{300 \times 300}{20} \\ & =4500 \mathrm{~W} \\ & =4.5 \mathrm{~kW}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore P_1 \gt P_r$
Hence, resistor may be damaged due to production of excessive heat.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current in the $1 \Omega$ resistor shown in the circuit is
questions.solution:
Two $4 \Omega$ resistors are in parallel combination. Their equivalent resistance $$ =\frac{4 \times 4}{4+4}=\frac{16}{8}=2 \Omega $$ $\therefore$ Total resistance of the
$$ \text { network }=2+1=3 \Omega $$ $\therefore$ Current through $1 \Omega$ resistor, $\mathrm{i}=\frac{6}{3}=2 \mathrm{~A}$

Two batteries of emf $4 \mathrm{~V}$ and $8 \mathrm{~V}$ with internal resistance $1 \Omega$ and $2 \Omega$ are connected in a circuit with a resistance of $9 \Omega$ as shown in figure.
The current and potential difference between the points $P$ and $Q$ are

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The resistance of a wire is 'R' ohm. If it is melted and stretched to 'n' times its original length, its new resistance will be
questions.solution:
We know that, $\mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho \ell}{\mathrm{A}}$ 
or $\quad \mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho \ell^{2}}{\text { Volume }} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R} \propto \ell^{2}$
According to question $\ell_{2}=\mathrm{n} \ell_{1}$ $\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}^{2} l_{1}^{2}}{l_{1}^{2}}$ or, $\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}=\mathrm{n}^{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}=\mathrm{n}^{2} \mathrm{R}_{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Resistances $1 \Omega, 2 \Omega$ and $3 \Omega$ are connected to form a triangle. If a $1.5 \mathrm{~V}$ cell of negligible internal resistance is connected across the $3 \Omega$ resistor, the current flowing through this resistor will be
questions.solution:
Equivalent resistance between A and B= series combination of $1 \Omega$ and $2 \Omega$ in parallel with $3 \Omega$ resistor.
$\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3}=\frac{2}{3} \quad$ or $\mathrm{R}=1.5 \Omega$ $\therefore$ Current in the circuit is $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{V} / \mathrm{R}=1.5 / 1.5=$ $1 \mathrm{~A}$. Since the resistance in $\operatorname{arm} \mathrm{ACB}=$ resistance in $\operatorname{arm} \mathrm{AB}=3 \Omega$, the current divides equally in the two arms. Hence the current through the $3 \Omega$ resistor $=\mathrm{I} / 2=0.5 \mathrm{~A}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The power dissipated in the circuit shown in the figure is 30 Watts. The value of $R$ is
questions.solution:
The power dissipated in the circuit.
$$
\begin{array}{c}
P=\frac{V^{2}}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}} .....(i)\\
v=10 \mathrm{volt} \\
\frac{1}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{5}=\frac{5+R}{5 R} \\
R_{\mathrm{eq}}=\left(\frac{5 R}{5+R}\right) \\
P=30 \mathrm{~W}
\end{array}
$$
Substituting the values in equation (i)
$$
\begin{array}{c}
30=\frac{(10)^{2}}{\left(\frac{5 R}{5+R}\right)} \\
\frac{15 R}{5+R}=10 \Rightarrow 15 R=50+10 R \\
5 R=50 \Rightarrow R=10 \Omega
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A milli voltmeter of 25 milli volt range is to be converted into an ammeter of 25 ampere range. The value (in ohm) of necessary shunt will be
questions.solution:
Galvanometer is converted into ammeter, by connected a shunt, in parallel with it.
$$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{G S}{G+S}=\frac{V_{G}}{I}=\frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{25} \\ \frac{G S}{G+S}=0.001 \Omega \end{array} $$ Here $S < G$ so $S=0.001 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A potentiometer wire, $10 \mathrm{~m}$ long, has a resistance of $40 \Omega$. It is connected in series with a resistance box and a $2 \mathrm{~V}$ storage cell. If the potential gradient along the wire $0.1 \mathrm{~m}$ is $\mathrm{V} / \mathrm{cm}$, the resistance unplugged in the box is
questions.solution:
Potential gradient along wire
$$
=\frac{\text { potential difference along wire }}{\text { length of wire }}
$$
or, $0.1 \times 10^{-3}=\frac{\mathrm{I} \times 40}{1000} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{cm}$
or, Current in wire, $\mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{400} \mathrm{~A}$
or,$\frac{2}{40+\mathrm{R}}=\frac{1}{400}$ or $\mathrm{R}=800-40=760 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Wheatstone bridge, 4 resistors $\mathrm{P}=10 \Omega$, $\mathrm{Q}=5 \Omega, \mathrm{R}=4 \Omega, \mathrm{S}=4 \Omega$ are connected in cyclic order. To ensure no current through galvanometer
options: $5 \Omega$ resistance is connected in series with $\mathrm{Q}$
$10 \Omega$ resistance is connected in series with $\mathrm{P}$
$4 \Omega$ resistance is connected in series with $\mathrm{R}$
questions.solution:
For no current through the galvanometer, the wheatstone bridge should be balanced. For this, we must have $$ \frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{\mathrm{S}}{\mathrm{R}} $$ This condition is satisfied with only option (a).
When a $5 \Omega$ resistor is connected in series with $Q$, the equivalent resistance in the P-arm becomes $10 \Omega$. $$ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{10}{10}=1 $$ and $\frac{\mathrm{S}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{4}{4}=1$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{\mathrm{S}}{\mathrm{R}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In given circuit, all resistances are of $10 \Omega$. Current flowing through ammeter is
questions.solution:
An equivalent of the given network is as shown in the figure. If $R_{p}$ be the net resistance, then
$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{p}}} &=\frac{1}{10}+\left(\frac{1}{10+10}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{10+10}\right)+\frac{1}{10} \\ &=\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{20}+\frac{1}{20}+\frac{1}{10} \end{aligned}$ $\begin{array}{l} \quad=\frac{6}{20}=\frac{3}{10} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{10}{3} \Omega \end{array}$ Hence, current flowing through ammeter is $$ I=\frac{V}{R_{p}}=\frac{12}{\left(\frac{10}{3}\right)}=3.6 \mathrm{~A} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two resistances equal at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ with temperature coefficient of resistance $\alpha_{1}$ and $\alpha_{2}$ joined in series act as a single resistance in a circuit. The temperature coefficient of their single resistance will be
questions.solution:
$R_{1}=R_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{1} t\right)+R_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{2} t\right)$
$=2 R_{0}\left(1+\frac{\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}}{2} t\right)$
Comparing with $R=R_{0}(1+\alpha t)$
$\alpha=\frac{\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current density varies with radial distance $r$ as $J=a r^{2}$, in a cylindrical wire of radius $R$. The current passing through the wire between radial distance $R / 3$ and $R / 2$ is
questions.solution:
Given; $J={ar}^{2}$. 
$\begin{array}{l}i=\int_{1}^{2} J \times 2 \pi r d r=\int_{R / 3}^{R / 2} a r^{2} \times 2 \pi r d r \\ =2 \pi a \int_{R / 3}^{R / 2} r^{3} d r=2 \pi a\left|\frac{r^{4}}{4}\right|_{R / 3}^{R / 2} \\ \quad=\frac{\pi a}{2}\left[\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^{4}-\left(\frac{R}{3}\right)^{4}\right] \\ =\frac{\pi a R^{4}}{2} \times \frac{65}{81 \times 16}=\frac{65 \pi a R^{4}}{2592}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A potentiometer circuit shown in the figure is set up to measure emf of cell $\mathrm{E}$. As the point $\mathrm{P}$ moves from $X$ to $Y$, the galvanometer $G$ shows deflection always in one direction, but the deflection decreases continuously until $Y$ is reached. The balance point between $X$ and $Y$ may be obtained by
questions.solution: Decreasing R increases current in XY and there by increases the potential drop across XP and the balance point may be obtained. The current may be increased also by increasing $\mathrm{V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A30V-90W lamp is operated on a $120 \mathrm{VDC}$ line. A resistor is connected in series with the lamp in order to glow it properly. The value of resistance is
questions.solution:
Resistance of lamp
$\mathrm{R}_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{(30)^{2}}{90}=10 \Omega$
Current in the lamp
$\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}_{0}}=\frac{30}{10}=3 \mathrm{~A}$
As the lamp is operated on $120 \mathrm{~V} \mathrm{DC}$, then resistance becomes
$\mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{i}}=\frac{120}{3}=40 \Omega$
For proper glow, a resistance $\mathrm{R}$ is joined in series with the bulb
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{R}_{0} \\
\Rightarrow & \mathrm{R}^{\alpha}=\mathrm{R}^{\prime}-\mathrm{R}_{0}=40-10=30 \Omega
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Power dissipated across the \(8 \Omega\) resistor in the circuit shown here is 2 watt. The power dissipated in watt units across the \(3 \Omega\) resistor is
questions.solution:
Power \(=\mathrm{V} . \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}^2 \mathrm{R}\)
\(\mathrm{i}_2=\sqrt{\frac{\text { Power }}{\mathrm{R}}}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{8}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{1}{2}\)
Potential over \(8 \Omega=\mathrm{Ri}_2=8 \times \frac{1}{2}=4 \mathrm{~V}\) This is the potential over parallel branch. So, \(i_1=\frac{4}{4}=1\)
Power of \(3 \Omega=\mathrm{i}_1{ }^2 \mathrm{R}=1 \times 1 \times 3=3 \mathrm{~W}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: 36 cells each of internal resistance $0.5 \Omega$ and emf 1.5 V each are used to send current through an external circuit of $2 \Omega$ resistor. For getting maximum current, $n$ cells are combined in series in each row. If total number of such rows are $m$, then the value of $(n / m)$ will be
questions.solution:
Here, $E=1.5 \mathrm{~V}, r=0.5 \Omega, R=2 \Omega$
Total number of cells, $m n=36\ldots(i)$
For maximum current in the mixed grouping,
$\frac{n r}{m}=R$ or $\frac{n \times 0.5}{m}=2$ ....(ii)
Multiplying Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$0.5 n^2=72$ or $n^2=144$
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & n=12 \text { and } m=\frac{36}{12}=3 \\ \therefore & \frac{n}{m}=\frac{12}{3}=4\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The figure below shows currents in a part of electric circuit. The current $i$ is
questions.solution:
According to Kirchhoff's first law, At junction $\mathrm{A}, i_{A B}=2+2=4 \mathrm{~A}$ At junction $\mathrm{B}, i_{A B}=i_{B C}+1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{i}_{B C}=4-1=$ $3 \mathrm{~A}$
At junction $\mathrm{C}, i=i_{B C}-1.3=3-1.3=1.7 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a given network, each resistance has value of \(6 \Omega\). The point \(\mathrm{X}\) is connected to point \(\mathrm{A}\) by a copper wire of negligible resistance and point \(Y\) is connected to point B by the same wire. The effective resistance between \(\mathrm{X}\) and \(\mathrm{Y}\) will be
questions.solution:
One end of all the three resistors are connected at the point \(X\) and the other at the point \(Y\). Therefore, the equivalent circuit is given below:
The equivalent resistance is given by \(\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{3}{6}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow R_{\text {eq }}=2 \Omega \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current in the $1 \Omega$ resistor shown in the circuit is
questions.solution:
Two $4 \Omega$ resistors are in parallel combination. Their equivalent resistance $$ =\frac{4 \times 4}{4+4}=\frac{16}{8}=2 \Omega $$
$\therefore$ Total resistance of the network $=2+1=$ $3 \Omega$ $\therefore$ Current through $1 \Omega$ resistor $=\frac{6}{3}=2 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two sources of equal emf are connected to a resistance $R$. The internal resistance of these sources are $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}\left(r_{1}>r_{2}\right) .$ If the potential difference across the source having internal resistance $r_{2}$ is zero, then
questions.solution:
Let the emf of each source be E. When they are connected in series, the current in the circuit
$I=\frac{E_{\text {tot }}}{R_{\text {tot }}}=\frac{E+E}{r_{1}+r_{2}+R}=\frac{2 E}{r_{1}+r_{2}+R}$
$\therefore \quad$ potential drop across the cell of internal
resistance $r_{2},\left(\frac{2 E}{r_{1}+r_{2}+R}\right) r_{2}$
Hence, $E-\frac{2 E}{\left(r_{l}+r_{2}+R\right)} r_{2}=0$
$\begin{aligned}
& r_{1}+r_{2}+R=2 r_{2} \\
\Rightarrow & R=r_{2}-r_{1}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A potential difference across the terminals of a battery is $50 \mathrm{~V}$ when 11 A current is drawn and $60 \mathrm{~V}$,when 1 Acurrent is drawn. The emf and the internal resistance of the battery are
questions.solution:
For a closed circuit cell supplies a constant current in the circuit.
For cell $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{V}+\mathrm{Ir}$ For $\mathrm{V}=50 \mathrm{~V}$ $E=50+11 r$ Similarly, for $\mathrm{V}=60 \mathrm{~V}$ $E=60+r$ From eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $E=61 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a potentiometer experiment, the balancing length of a cell is $560 \mathrm{~cm}$. When an external resistance of $10 \Omega$ is connected in parallel to the cell, the balancing length changes by 60 $\mathrm{cm}$. The internal resistance of a cell is
questions.solution:
Let us Consider a cell of emf E and balancing length $1_{1}$
$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{kl}_{1}$
potential difference is balanced by length $\mathrm{l}_{2}$. $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{kl}_{2}$
Internal resistance of the cell
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{r}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{V}}\right) \mathrm{R}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{V}}-1\right) \mathrm{R}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{l}_{1}}{\mathrm{l}_{2}}-1\right) \mathrm{R} \\
=\left(\frac{560}{560-60}-1\right) 10=\left(\frac{56}{50}-1\right) 10 \\
=\frac{6}{5}=1.2 \Omega
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A galvanometer with a scale divided into 100 equal divisions has a current sensitivity of 10 divisions per milliampere and a voltage sensitivity of 2 divisions per millivolt. The galvanometer resistance will be
questions.solution:
Given: current sensitivity $=10 \mathrm{div} / \mathrm{mA}$ and there are 100 division on the scale.
$\therefore \quad$ Current required for full scale deflection.
$\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{1}{10} \times 100 \mathrm{~mA}=10 \mathrm{~mA}=0.01 \mathrm{~A}$
Also voltage sensitivity $=2 \mathrm{div} / \mathrm{mV}$
$\therefore \quad$ voltage required for full scale deflection
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{1}{2} \times 100 \mathrm{mV}=0.05 \mathrm{~V}$
Galvanometer resistance is given by
$\mathrm{G}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{g}}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}}=\frac{0.05}{0.01}=5 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A galvanometer has current range of $15 \mathrm{~mA}$ and voltage range $750 \mathrm{mV}$. To convert this galvanometer into an ammeter of range $25 \mathrm{~A}$, the required shunt is
questions.solution:
Given: $\mathrm{V}=750 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V}$ $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}=15 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}$ and $\mathrm{I}=25 \mathrm{~A}$
Using, $a=\frac{y}{I_{g}}$
$\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{750 \times 10^{-3}}{15 \times 10^{-3}}=50 \Omega \\
\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{\mathrm{S}}{\mathrm{S}+\mathrm{a}} \times \mathrm{I} \\
15 \times 10^{-3}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{S}}{\mathrm{S}+50}\right) \times 25 \\
\mathrm{~S}=0.03 \Omega
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The denial cell is balanced on $125 \mathrm{~cm}$ length of a potentiometer. Now, the cell is short circuited by a resistance of $2 \Omega$ and the balance is obtained at $100 \mathrm{~cm}$. The internal resistance of the denial cell is
questions.solution:
Here, $\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{R}\left(\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}-1\right)=2\left(\frac{125}{100}-1\right)$
$=2\left(\frac{5}{4}-1\right)=2 \times \frac{1}{4}=0.05 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four resistance of $10 \Omega, 60 \Omega, 100 \Omega$ and $200 \Omega$ respectively taken in order are used to form a Wheatstone's bridge. A $15 \mathrm{~V}$ battery is connected to the ends of a $200 \Omega$ resistance, the current through it will be
questions.solution:
Resistances $10 \Omega, 60 \Omega$ and $100 \Omega$ are in series and they together are in parallel to $200 \Omega$ resistance. When a potential difference of $15 \mathrm{~V}$ is applied across $200 \Omega$ then current through it
$\mathrm{I}=\frac{15}{200}=7.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circuit shown the cells $A$ and $B$ have negligibleresistances. For $V_{A}=12 \mathrm{~V}, R_{1}=500 \Omega$ and $R=100 \Omega$ the galvanometer $(G)$ shows no deflection. The value of $V_{B}$ is :
questions.solution: Since deflection in galvanometer is zero so current will flow as shown in the above diagram. current, $I=\frac{V_{A}}{R_{1}+R}=\frac{12}{500+100}=\frac{12}{600}$ So $\quad V_{B}=I R=\frac{12}{600} \times 100=2 V$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A copper wire of length $2.2 \mathrm{~m}$ and a steel wire of length $1.6 \mathrm{~m}$, both of diameter $3.0 \mathrm{~mm}$ are connected end to end. When stretched by a force, the elonation in length $0.50 \mathrm{~mm}$ is produced in the copper wire. The stretching force is $\left(\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{cu}}=1.1 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}, \mathrm{Y}_{\text {steel }}=2.0 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}\right)$
questions.solution:
For Cu wire, $1_{1}=2.2 \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{r}_{1}=1.5 \mathrm{~mm}$ $=1.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{n}$ $\mathrm{Y}_{1}=1.1 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$ For steel wire, $\mathrm{l}_{2}=1.6 \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{r}_{2}=1.5 \mathrm{~mm}$ $=1.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ $\mathrm{Y}_{2}=2.0 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$
Let $\mathrm{F}$ be the stretching force in both the wires the
For Cu wire, $\mathrm{Y}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_{1}^{2}} \times \frac{\mathrm{l}_{1}}{\Delta \mathrm{l}_{1}}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{Y}_{1} \pi \mathrm{r}_{1}^{2} \times \Delta \mathrm{l}_{1}}{1_{1}} \\
=\frac{1.1 \times 10^{-11}}{2.2} \times \frac{22}{7} \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2} \times 0.5 \times 10^{3} \\
=1.8 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{~N}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A network of resistances, cell and capacitor $\mathrm{C}(=2 \mu \mathrm{F})$ is shown in adjoining figure.In steady state condition, the charge on $2 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitor is $Q$, while $R$ is unknown resistance. Values of $Q$ and $R$ are respectively
questions.solution:
In the steady state, current through capacitor
Using Kirchhof's voltage law to the circuit ACD We have, $10-2+1 \times \mathrm{R}+1 \times 2=0$ or $\mathrm{R}=10 \Omega$ Potential difference across $\mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{D}$ $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{D}}=2 \times 1=2 \mathrm{~V}$ As $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{D}}=0 \mathrm{~V}$ So, $V_{C}=2 V$ Potential difference across capacitor $=4-2=2 \mathrm{~V}$ $\therefore$ Chargeon capacitor $\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{CV}=2 \mu \mathrm{F} \times 2=4 \mu \mathrm{C}$

questions.q: Seven resistance are connected between points $A$ and $B$ as shown in adjoining figure. The equivalent resistance between $A$ and $B$ is
questions.solution:
The equivalent circuit of the given circuit is
This is a balanced Wheatstone bridge. Therefore, the arm CD becomes in effective. Hence $5 \Omega$ and $3 \Omega$ are in series and they together are in parallel with $(5+3) \Omega$ $\therefore \text { Net resistance }=\frac{(5+3)+(5+3)}{(5+3) \times(5+3)}=4 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the Wheatstone's network given, $\mathrm{P}=10 \Omega$, $Q=20 \Omega, R=15 \Omega, S=30 \Omega$, the current passing through the battery (of negligible internal resistance) is
questions.solution:
Balanced wheatstone bridge condition
$$
\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S}
$$
No, current flows through galvanometer Now, $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ are in series, so Resistance $\mathrm{R}_{1}=\mathrm{P}+\mathrm{R}$
$$
=10+15=25 \Omega
$$
Similarly, Q and S are in series, so Resistance $\mathrm{R}_{2}=\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{S}$ $=20+30=50 \Omega$
Net resistance of the network as $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ are in parallel
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{2}} \\
\therefore \mathrm{R}=\frac{25 \times 50}{25+50}=\frac{50}{3} \Omega
\end{array}
$$
Hence, current, $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{6}{50 / 3}=0.36 \mathrm{~A}$

Given circuit is a balanced Wheatstone bridge.

questions.q: Dimensions of a block are $1 \mathrm{~cm} \times 1 \mathrm{~cm} \times 100 \mathrm{~cm}$. If specific resistance of its material is $3 \times 10^{-7} \Omega \mathrm{m}$, then the resistance between the opposite rectangular faces is
questions.solution:
Here length $l=1 \mathrm{~cm}=10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$ Area of cross-section, $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{A}=1 \mathrm{~cm} \times & 100 \mathrm{~cm} \\ &=10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}^{2} \end{aligned}$
Resistance, $\mathrm{R}=\rho \frac{\ell}{A}=3 \times 10^{-7} \times \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-2}}$ $=3 \times 10^{-7} \Omega$

The resistance of an incandescent lamp is greater when the lamp is switched off.
An ordinary $100 \mathrm{~W}$ lamp has less resistance than a $60 \mathrm{~W}$ lamp.
At constant voltage, the heat developed in a uniform wire varies inversely as the length of the wire used.

questions.q: A potentiometer is connected across A and B and a balance is obtained at $64.0 \mathrm{~cm}$. When potentiometer lead to $\mathrm{B}$ is moved to $\mathrm{C}$, a balance is found at $8.0 \mathrm{~cm}$. If the potentometer is now connected across B and C, a balance will be found at
questions.solution:
Here, $\mathrm{E}_{1} \propto 64$
$\mathrm{E}_{1}-\mathrm{E}_{2} \propto 8$
$\mathrm{E}_{2} \propto l$
$\therefore \quad 64-l=8$ or $l=64-8=56 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.solution: Shunt S $=\frac{G I_{g}}{I-I_{g}}$ $=\frac{0.81 \times 1}{10-1}=\frac{0.81}{9} \Omega=0.09 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: According to the Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom, the speed of the electron, energy and the radius of its orbit vary with the principal quantum number $\mathrm{n}$, respectively, as
questions.solution:
According to Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom,
(i) The speed of the electron in the $n$th orbit
$$
V_{n}=\frac{1}{n} \frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(h / 2 \pi)} \text { or } v_{n} \propto \frac{1}{n}
$$
(ii) The energy of the electron in the $n$th orbit
$$
E_{n}=\frac{m e^{4}}{8 n^{2} \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}}=\frac{-13.6}{n^{2}} e V \text { or } E_{n} \propto \frac{1}{n^{2}}
$$
(iii) The radius of the electron in the $n$th orbit
$$
r_{n}=\frac{n^{2} h^{2} \varepsilon_{0}}{\pi m e^{2}}=n^{2} a_{0} \text { or } r_{n} \propto n^{2}
$$
where $a_{0}=\frac{h^{2} \varepsilon_{0}}{\pi m e}=5.29 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two point charges $-\mathrm{q}$ and $+\mathrm{q}$ are located at points $(0,0,-a)$ and $(0,0$, a) respectively. The electric potential at a point $(0,0, z)$, where $z>a$ is
questions.solution:
Potential at $P$ due to $(+q)$ charge of dipole $$ V_{1}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{(z-a)} $$ Potential at $P$ due to $(-q)$ charge of dipole $V_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{-q}{(z+a)}$
Total potential at $P$ due to electric dipole $$ \begin{aligned} V &=V_{1}+V_{2} \\ &=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{(z-a)}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q}{(z+a)} \\ &=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{(z+a-z+a)}{(z-a)(z+a)} \\ \text { or } \quad V &=\frac{2 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(z^{2}-a^{2}\right)} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the adjacent shown circuit, a voltmeter of internal resistance $R$, when connected across B and $\mathrm{C}$ reads $\frac{100}{3} \mathrm{~V}$. Neglecting the internal resistance of the battery, the value of $R$ is
questions.solution:
Internal resistance of voltmeter $=R$. Effective resistance across $B$ and $C$ $$ \frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{50}=\frac{50+R}{50 R} $$ or $R^{\prime}=\left(\frac{50 R}{50+R}\right)$
According to Ohm's law, $V^{\prime}=I R^{\prime}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text { or } \frac{100}{3}=I \cdot\left(\frac{50 R}{50+R}\right) \\ \text { or } \frac{100}{3}\left(\frac{50+R}{50 R}\right)=I...(i) \end{array} $$ Now, total resistance of circuit $$ \begin{array}{l} R^{n}=50+\frac{50 R}{50+R} \\ \text { or } R^{\prime \prime}=\frac{(2500+100 R)}{(50+R)} \end{array} $$ Now, $V^{\prime \prime}=I R^{\prime \prime}$ $$ \Rightarrow 100=\frac{100}{3}\left(\frac{50+R}{50 R}\right) \frac{2500+100 R}{(50+R)} $$ $$ \Rightarrow 150 R=2500+100 R \text { or } R=50 \mathrm{k} \Omega $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ammeter and a voltmeter of resistance $R$ are connected in series to an electric cell of negligible internal resistance. Their reading are $A$ and $V$ respectively. If another resistance $R$ is connected in parallel with the voltmeter, then
questions.solution:
The effective resistance will decrease when resistance $R$ is connected in parallel with the voltmeter.
According to Ohm's law, $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{IR}$ or, $\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}}$ Here, as $R$ decreases, so $V$ decrease and $I$ should increase.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A resistor of $6 \mathrm{k} \Omega$ with tolerance $10 \%$ and another resistance of $4 \mathrm{k} \Omega$ with tolerance $10 \%$ are connected in series. The tolerance of the combination is about
questions.solution:
In series combination equivalent resistance,
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2} \\
=6+4=10 \mathrm{k} \Omega
\end{array}$
Error in combination,
$\begin{array}{l}
\Delta \mathrm{R}=\Delta \mathrm{R}_{1}+\Delta \mathrm{R}_{2} \\
=\frac{10}{100} \times 6+\frac{10}{100} \times 4 \\
=0.6+0.4=1 \\
\frac{\Delta \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{1}{10}=10 \%
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: ${ }^{234}$ U has 92 protons and 234 nucleons total in its nucleus. It decays by emitting an alpha particle. After the decay it becomes
questions.solution:
The $\alpha$ - decay in the case of ${ }^{234} \mathrm{U}$ takes place as follows :
${ }_{92}^{234} \mathrm{U} \longrightarrow{ }_{90}^{230} \mathrm{Th}+{ }_{2}^{4} \mathrm{He}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cell in secondary circuit gives null deflection for $2.5 \mathrm{~m}$ length of potentiometer having $10 \mathrm{~m}$ length of wire. If the length of the potentiometer wire is increased by $1 \mathrm{~m}$ without changing the cell in the primary, the position of the null point now is
questions.solution:
Here length of potentiometer wire, $l=10 \mathrm{~m}$ Resistance of potentiometer wire
$R=\rho \times \frac{l}{A}$ or $R=\left(\rho \times \frac{10}{A}\right)$
The value of $2.5 \mathrm{~m}$ length wire
$$
R^{\prime}=\frac{\rho \times 10}{A \times 10} \times 2.5 \Rightarrow R^{\prime}=\left(\frac{2.5 \rho}{A \times 10}\right)
$$
Potential, $V^{\prime}=I \times R^{\prime}=I\left(\frac{2.5 \rho}{A \times 10}\right)$
Again the length of potentiometer wire is increased by $1 \mathrm{~m}$.
Resistance of null position
$$
R^{n}=\left(\frac{\rho \times l}{11 \times A}\right)
$$
$\therefore V^{\prime \prime}=I R^{\prime \prime}$ and $V=V^{\prime}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \frac{I \times 2.5 \rho}{A \times 10}=\frac{\rho \times I}{11 \times A} \times I \\
\text { or } \frac{2.5 \times 11}{10}=I=2.75 \mathrm{~m}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics

options: the electrical and thermal conductivities both increase
the electrical and thermal conductivities both decrease
the electrical conductivity increases but thermal conductivity decreases
the electrical conductivity decrease but thermal conductivity increases
questions.solution:
If the number of electrons increase, their number of collision, increasing the thermal and electrical resistance.
So, electrical and thermal conductivities both decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A radio station has two channels. One is AM at $1020 \mathrm{kHz}$ and the other FM at $89.5 \mathrm{MHz}$. For good results you will use
options: longer antenna for the AM channel and shorter for the FM
shorter antenna for the AM channel and longer for the FM
same length antenna will work for both
information given is not enough to say which one to use for which
questions.solution:
Antenna length $=\frac{\lambda}{4} \propto \lambda$
$\because \lambda \propto \frac{1}{v}$ for cosntant velocity,
$\therefore$ Antenna length $\propto \frac{1}{v}$
Here, $v_{\mathrm{AM}} < v_{\mathrm{FM}}$
$\therefore$ Antenna length for AM should be longer than that of FM.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When an electrical appliance is switched on, it responds almost immediately, because
options: the electrons in the connecting wires move with the speed of light
the electrical signal is carried by electromagnetic waves moving with the speed of light
the electrons move with the speed which is close to but less than speed of light
questions.solution: When an electrical appliance is switched on, the electrons in the conducting wires move with their drift speed which is very less than the speed of light. But as soon as the switch is on, an electromagnetic wave is set up inside, the conductor and the electrical signal is carried by them. The speed of electromagnetic wave is, of course, equal to the speed of light and hence the appliance, responds almost immediately after the switch is made on.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The communication using optical fibers is based on the principle of
questions.solution: The principle of communication using optical fibers is based on the principle of total internal reflection.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three solenoid coils of same dimension, same number of turns and same number of layers of winding are taken. Coil 1 with inductance $\mathrm{L}_{1}$ was wound using a Mn wire of resistance $11 \Omega / \mathrm{m}$; Coil 2 with inductance $\mathrm{L}_{2}$ was wound using the similar wire but the direction of winding was reversed in each layer; Coil 3 with inductance $\mathrm{L}_{3}$ was wound using a superconducting wire. The self inductance of the coils $\mathrm{L}_{1}, \mathrm{~L}_{2}, \mathrm{~L}_{3}$ are
questions.solution:
Self-inductance (L) of a solenoid $=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{~N}^{2} \mathrm{~A}}{l}$ where $\mu_{o}=$ absolute permittivily of space/ $\mathrm{N}=$ number of turns in the coil
$A=$ area of cross-section of the solenoid
$l=$ length of solenoid
This expression shows that for all three solenoids, the self-inductances will be equal.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Light travels with a speed of $2 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in crown glass of refractive index $1.5$. What is the speed of light in dense flint glass of refractive index $1.8 ?$
questions.solution:
We know that the refractive index of a medium is given by
$\mu=\frac{\mathrm{c}_{0}}{\mathrm{c}}$
Where $\mathrm{c}=$ velocity of light in the medium $\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{o}}=$ velocity of light in vacuum For crown glass,
$1.5=\frac{\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{o}}}{2 \times 10^{8}}$ ...(1)
For flint glass, $1.8=\frac{\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{o}}}{\mathrm{c}}$ ...(2)
Dividing (2) by (1), we get $\frac{2 \times 10^{8}}{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1.8}{1.5}$ or $\mathrm{c}=\frac{1.5 \times 2 \times 10^{8}}{1.8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}=1.67 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Charge passing through a conductor of crosssection area $\mathrm{A}=0.3 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ is given by $\mathrm{q}=3 \mathrm{t}^{2}+5 \mathrm{t}+2$ in coulomb, where $\mathrm{t}$ is in second. What is the value of drift velocity at $t=2 s ?$ $\left(\right.$ Given, $\left.\mathrm{m}=2 \times 10^{25} / \mathrm{m}^{3}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel beam of fast moving electrons is incident normally on a narrow slit. A screen is placed at a large distance from the slit. If the speed of the electrons is increased, which of the following statement is correct?
options: diffraction pattern is not observed on the screen in the case of electrons
the angular width of the central maximum of the diffraction pattern will increase
the angular width of the central maximum will decrease
the angular width of the central maximum will remain the same
questions.solution:
Fast moving electrons create electromagnetic waves. So, the diffraction pattern will be observed. Also, the angular width of the central maximum is given by
$\mathrm{W}=\frac{2 \lambda}{\mathrm{a}}$, where $\mathrm{a}=$ width of the slit
$\lambda=$ wavelength of the light .
When the speed of electrons will increase, the frequency will increase which will result in decreasing the wavelength as the speed of light is a constant. Therefore, the angular width will decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A transformer rated at $10 \mathrm{~kW}$ is used to connect a $5 \mathrm{kV}$ transmission line to a $240 \mathrm{~V}$ circuit. The ratio of turns in the windings of the transformer is
questions.solution:
In a transformer, the ratio of turns in the windings is given by
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}}} \\
\therefore & \frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{S}}}=\frac{5 \mathrm{kV}}{240 \mathrm{~V}}=\frac{5000 \mathrm{~V}}{240 \mathrm{~V}}=20.8
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The transmission of high frequencies in a coaxial cable is determined by
options: $\frac{1}{(\mathrm{LC})^{1 / 2}}$ where $\mathrm{L}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ are inductance and capacitance
questions.solution: A coaxial cable consists of a conducting wire surrounded by a dielectric space, over which there is a sleeve of coppermesh covered with a shield of PVC insulation. The power transmission is regulated by dielectric. At high frequencies energy loss due to mesh is significant (called skin effect).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The antenna current of an AM transmitter is $8 \mathrm{~A}$ when only the carrier is sent, but it increases to $8.93 \mathrm{~A}$ when the carrier is modulated by a single sine wave. Find the percentage modulation.
questions.solution:
The rms value of carrier current is $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{c}}=8 \mathrm{~A}$. The rms value of modulated current
$=I_{c}+I_{m}=8.93 \mathrm{~A}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{t}}$
Percentage modulation $=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}} \times 100$ 
The current relation in AM wave is
$\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{t}}^{2}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{c}}^{2}}=1+\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}}^{2}}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{t}}^{2}}{\left.\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{c}}^{2}-1\right) 2}\right.}$
$\Rightarrow$ Modulation index, $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{(8.93)^{2}}{8^{2}}-1\right) 2}$
$\begin{aligned}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{79.7}{64}-1\right) 2} &=\sqrt{(1.24-1) 2} \\ &=0.701 \\ \text { Percentage modulation } &=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}} \times 100=70.1 \% \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Five resistances are connected as shown in the figure. The effective resistance between points $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ is
questions.solution:
It is a parallel combination of three resistances, each of $10 \Omega$.
$\therefore \quad$ The effective resistance between points $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$.
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{10} \\
\text { or } \quad R=\frac{10}{3} \Omega
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A voltage of peak value $283 \mathrm{~V}$ and varying frequency is applied to a series $\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{R}$ combination in which $\mathrm{R}=3 \mathrm{ohm}, \mathrm{L}=25 \mathrm{mH}$ and $\mathrm{C}=400 \mu \mathrm{F}$. The frequency (in Hz) of the source at which maximum power is dissipated in the above is
questions.solution:
Average power of an LCR circuit is $\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{V}} \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{v}} \cos \phi .$ Maximum power is dissipated at resonance when $\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}$. The resonance frequency is given by
$v=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}$
$=\frac{1}{2 \times 3.14 \times \sqrt{25 \times 10^{-3} \times 400 \times 10^{-6}}}$
$=\frac{1}{2 \times 3.14 \times 5 \times 20 \times 10^{-5} \sqrt{10}}$
$=\frac{10^{5}}{3.14 \times 200 \sqrt{10}}$
$=\frac{10^{3}}{6.28 \times \sqrt{10}}=\frac{159.2}{3.16}=50.31 \mathrm{~Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A battery is charged at a potential of \(15 \mathrm{~V}\) for 8 hours when the current flowing is \(10 \mathrm{~A}\). The battery on discharge supplies a current of \(5 \mathrm{~A}\) for 15 hour. The mean terminal voltage during discharge is 14V. The "Watt-hour" efficiency of the battery is
questions.solution:
Efficiency is given by \(\eta=\frac{\text { output }}{\text { input }}\)
\(=\frac{5 \times 15 \times 14}{10 \times 8 \times 15}=0.875 \text { or } 87.5 \%\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Following diffraction pattern was obtained using a diffraction grating using two different wavelengths $\lambda_{1}$ and $\lambda_{2} .$ With the help of the figure identify which is the longer wavelength and their ratios.
options: $\lambda_{2}$ is longer than $\lambda_{1}$ and the ratio of the longer to the shorter wavelength is $1.5$
$\lambda_{1}$ is longer than $\lambda_{2}$ and the ratio of the longer to the shorter wavelength is $1.5$
$\lambda_{1}$ and $\lambda_{2}$ are equal and their ratio is $1.0$
$\lambda_{2}$ is longer than $\lambda_{1}$ and the ratio of the longer to the shorter wavelength is $2.5$.
questions.solution:
Since position of central maximum is same, hence $\operatorname{asin} \theta=\lambda$ is same for both wavelengths. i.e, $a=$ constant, $\theta=$ same, $\Rightarrow \lambda$ is same for both waves
$\Rightarrow \lambda_{1}=\lambda_{2}$ and $\frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A 2 V battery, a $15 \Omega$ resistor and a potentiometer of $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ length, all are connected in series. If the resistance of potentiometer wire is $5 \Omega$, then the potential gradient of the potentiometer wire, is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$ is passing through a metallic wire of cross-sectional area $4 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$. If the density of the charge carriers in the wire is $5 \times$ $10^{26} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$, the drift speed of the electrons will be $\left[\mathrm{e}=1.602 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right]$
questions.solution:
In a metal, conduction current is due to electrons given by
$\begin{array}{l}
I=n A e v \\
\Rightarrow \text { drift velocity, } v=\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{nAe}} \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\frac{5}{5 \times 10^{26} \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \\
\quad=\frac{1}{4 \times 1.602 \times 10^{1}} \\
\quad=\frac{10^{-1}}{6.408}=1.56 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The resistance of a field coil measures $50 \Omega$ at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $65 \Omega$ at $70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The temperature coefficient of resistance is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three resistances of values $2 \Omega, 3 \Omega$ and $6 \Omega$ are to be connected to produce an effective resistance of $4 \Omega$. This can be done by connecting
options: $6 \Omega$ resistance in series with the parallel combination of $2 \Omega$ and $3 \Omega$
$3 \Omega$ resistance in series with the parallel combination of $2 \Omega$ and $6 \Omega$
$2 \Omega$ resistance in series with the parallel combination of $3 \Omega$ and $6 \Omega$
$2 \Omega$ resistance in parallel with the parallel combination of $3 \Omega$ and $6 \Omega$
questions.solution:
Parallel combination of $3 \Omega$ and $6 \Omega$ gives effective resistance,
$\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{3 \times 6}{3+6}=\frac{18}{9}=2 \Omega$. This in series with $2 \Omega$ gives net ressistance as $4 \Omega$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A galvanometer has a resistance of $50 \Omega$. If a resistance of $1 \Omega$ is connected across its terminals, the total current flow through the galvanometer is $\left[\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}\right.$ represents the maximum current that can be passed through the galvanometer]

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a tangent galvanometer, a current of $1 \mathrm{~A}$ produces a deflection of $30^{\circ}$. The current required to produce a deflection of $60^{\circ}$ is
questions.solution:
Current in tangent galvanometer
$\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{G}} \tan \theta$
Where $\mathrm{G}=$ galvanometer constant
$\mathrm{H}=\text { earth's horizontal field }=\text { constant }$
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}=\frac{\tan \theta_{1}}{\tan \theta_{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}=\frac{\tan 30^{\circ}}{\tan 60^{\circ}}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2}=\frac{\tan 60^{\circ}}{\tan 30^{\circ}}=\frac{1.7321}{0.5774}=2.999 Å \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2} \simeq 3 \mathrm{~A}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Ionization power and penetration range of radioactive radiation increases in the order
questions.solution: $\alpha$ particles are positively charged He nucleus, it can accept $2 \mathrm{e}^{-}, \beta$ rays are negatively charged which are similar to $\mathrm{e}^{-}$, can donate $1 \mathrm{e}^{-}, \gamma$ are radiations. Hence ionisation power of $\alpha$ is maximum. $\gamma$ are most energetic and $\propto$ is least energetic $\therefore$ Penetration power of $\gamma$ is maximum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance $100 \mathrm{pF}$ is to be constructed by using paper sheets of $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ thickness as dielectric. If the dielectric constant of paper is 4 , the number of circular metal foils of diameter $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ each required for the purpose is
questions.solution:
The capacitance (C) of a parallel plate capacitor with dielectric is given by
$\begin{array}{l}
C=\frac{\epsilon_{0} A}{d-t\left(1-\frac{1}{K}\right)} \\
C=100 p F=100 \times 10^{-12} F, \in_{0}=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \\
A=\pi \mathrm{I}^{2}=3.14 \times\left(10^{-2}\right)^{2}(\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{lcm}) \\
\mathrm{t}=10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{~d}=\mathrm{t}, \mathrm{K}=4
\end{array}$
$\mathrm{C}_{1}=\frac{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.14 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}-10^{-3}\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right)}$
$=\frac{27.79 \times 10^{-16}}{10^{-3}-0.75 \times 10^{-3}}$
$=\frac{27.79 \times 10^{-13}}{0.25}$
$=\frac{27.79 \times 10^{-13}}{0.25}=111.16 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~F}$ (for 1 set)
Required $\mathrm{C}=100 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~F}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{C}_{1}}=\frac{100 \times 10^{-12}}{111.16 \times 10^{-13}} \simeq 10$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Four independent waves are represented by equations
(1) $\mathrm{X}_{1}=\mathrm{a}_{1} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}$
(3) $\mathrm{X}_{2}=\mathrm{a}_{2} \sin 2 \omega \mathrm{t}$
(2) $\mathrm{X}_{3}=\mathrm{a}_{1} \sin \omega_{1} \mathrm{t}$
(4) $\mathrm{X}_{4}=\mathrm{a}_{1} \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}+\delta)$
Interference is possible between waves represented by equations
questions.solution: Coherent sources should have same frequency and wavelength and constant or zero phase difference. Frequency is same only for wave $X_{1}$ and $X_{4}$ and phase difference $=\delta$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment, the interference pattern is found to have an intensity ratio between bright and dark fringes is $9 .$ This implies the
options: the intensities at the screen due to two slits are 5 units and 4 units respectively
the intensities at the screen due to the two slits are 4 units and 1 units respectively
questions.solution:
For bright fringes, $\left.\right|_{\max }=(a+b)^{2}$
For dark fringes, $I_{\min }=(a-b)^{2}$
Now $\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\max }}{\mathrm{I}_{\min }}=9=\frac{(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b})^{2}}{(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b})^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}}=3$
$\Rightarrow a+b=3(a-b) \Rightarrow a+b=3 a-3 b \Rightarrow 2 a=4 b$
$\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}=2 \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{\mathrm{~b}^{2}}=4$
$\therefore$ Ratio of intensities of the two slits,
$\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{\mathrm{~b}^{2}}=\frac{4}{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The electrolyte used in Lechlanche cell is
questions.solution: In Leclanche cell a strong solution of ammonium chloride acts as an electrolyte.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a photoelectric experiment anode potential is plotted against plate current
options: A and \(\mathrm{B}\) will have different intensities while \(\mathrm{B}\) and \(\mathrm{C}\) will have different frequencies
\(\mathrm{B}\) and \(\mathrm{C}\) will have different intensities while \(\mathrm{A}\) and \(\mathrm{C}\) will have different frequencies
\(\mathrm{A}\) and \(\mathrm{B}\) will have different intensities while \(A\) and \(C\) will have equal frequencies
A and B will have equal intensities while B and \(\mathrm{C}\) will have different frequencies
questions.solution: From the graph it is clear that \(\mathrm{A}\) and \(\mathrm{B}\) have the same stopping potential and therefore the same frequency. Also B and C have the same intensity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a photoelectric effect measurement, the stopping potential for a given metal is found to be $V_{0}$ volt when radiation of wavelength $\lambda_{0}$ is used. If radiation of wavelength $2 \lambda_{0}$ is used with the same metal then the stopping potential (in volt) will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the wavelength is brought down from $6000 Å$ to $4000 Å$ in a photoelectric experiment then what will happen?
options: The work function of the metal will increase
questions.solution:
When wavelength decreases, frequency increases. Also, we know that cut-off voltage (or stopping potential) increases when frequency increases.
Hence, option (d) is correct. Note that work function and threshold frequency are constant for a given metal.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A metal surface is illuminated with photons of energies $E_1=4 \mathrm{eV}$ and $E_2=2.5 \mathrm{eV}$ respectively. The ratio of maximum speeds of the photoelectrons in two cases is 2 .
Work function of metal surface will be
questions.solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation,
As $\mathrm{KE}=h f-\phi_0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} m v_1^2=4-\phi_0$...(i)
and $\frac{1}{2} m v_2^2=2.5-\phi_0$...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$\therefore \quad \frac{v_1^2}{v_2^2}=\frac{4-\phi_0}{2.5-\phi_0}$
or $\quad 4=\frac{4-\phi_0}{2.5-\phi_0}$
$\left(\mathrm{as}, \frac{v_1}{v_2}=2\right)$
So, $\quad \phi_0=2 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Light of frequency \(5 v_0\) falls on a photosensitive surface of the threshold frequency \(v_0\). The velocity of emitted electrons is \(4 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\). If now light of frequency \(9 v_0\) is incident on the surface, then velocity of electron will become:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In photoelectric effect, initially when energy of electrons emitted is $E_{o}$, de-Broglie wavelength associated with them is $\lambda_{0}$. Now, energy is doubled then associated de-Broglie wavelength $\lambda^{\prime}$ is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the momentum of a photon is changed by an amount $p$ ' then the corresponding change in the de-Broglie wavelength is found to be $0.20 \%$. Then, the original momentum of the photon was
questions.solution:
As, we know de-Broglic wavelength,
$\begin{array}{r}
\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{p}} \\
\therefore \quad \lambda \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{p}}
\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Delta \mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{p}}=-\frac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda} \therefore\left|\frac{\Delta \mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{p}}\right|=\left|\frac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda}\right|$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{p}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{0.20}{100}=\frac{1}{500}$
or, $\quad \mathrm{p}=500 \mathrm{p}^{\prime}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose a beam of electrons with each electron having energy $E_{0}$ incident on a metal surface kept in an evacuated chamber. Then,
options: electrons can be emitted with any energy with a maximum of $E_{0}$
no electrons will be emitted as only photons can emit electrons
electrons can be emitted but all with an energy $E_{0}$
electrons can be emitted with any energy with a maximum of $E_{0}-\phi$, where $\phi$ being work function
questions.solution:
The emitted electrons may lie near the surface and can have a maximum amount of energy $\mathrm{E}_{0}$.
If they are from deep inside, then energy is less than $\mathrm{E}_{0}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metallic surface ejects electrons. When exposed to green light of intensity I but no photoelectrons are emitted,when exposed to yellow light of intensity I.It is possible to eject electron from the same surface by
options: yellow light of same intensity which is more than I
questions.solution:
$\lambda_{\text {red }}>\lambda_{\text {violet }}$
As wavelength $\lambda$ less than that of yellow colour and hence can initiate photoelectric effect irrespective of intensity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When sunlight is scattered by atmospheric atoms and molecules the amount of scattering of light of wavelength $880 \mathrm{~nm}$ is $A$. Then, the amount of scattering of light of wavelength $330 \mathrm{~nm}$ is approximately
questions.solution:
From Rayleigh's law of scattering, intensity
$\begin{aligned}
& I \propto \frac{1}{\lambda^{4}} \\
\therefore \quad & \frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\left(\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}\right)^{4} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & \frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\left(\frac{330}{880}\right)^{4}=\left(\frac{3}{8}\right)^{4}=\frac{81}{4096} \\
& I_{2}=\frac{4096}{81} Å=(50.557) Å
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An eye can detect $5 \times 10^{4}$ photons per square meter per sec of green light $(\lambda \alpha=5000 Å)$ while the ear can detect $10^{-13} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The factor by which the eye is more sensitive as a power detector then ear is close to
questions.solution:
Energy $=\frac{12375}{5000}=2.475 \mathrm{eV}=4 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}$
Minimum intensity to which the eye can respond.
$\begin{array}{l}
1_{\text {eye }}=(\text { photon flux }) \times \text { energy of a photon } \\
1_{\text {eye }}^{\text {fort }}=\left(5 \times 10^{4}\right) \times 4 \times 10^{-19} \\
=2 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}
\end{array}$
Now, lesser the intensity required by a detector for detection more sensitive it will
$\mathrm{be}=\frac{\mathrm{l}_{\mathrm{ear}}}{\mathrm{l}_{\text {eye }}}=\frac{10^{-13}}{2 \times 10^{-14}}=5$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Silver has a work function of $4.7 \mathrm{eV}$. When ultraviolet light of wavelength $100 \mathrm{~nm}$ is incident on it a potential of $7.7 \mathrm{~V}$ is required to stop the photoelectrons from reaching the collector plate. How much potential will be required to stop photoelectrons, when light of wavelength 200 nm is incident on it?
questions.solution:
From Einstein's Photoelectric equation,
$\mathrm{ev}_{0}+\phi=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}$
and $\mathrm{ev}_{0}+\phi_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda^{\prime}}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{\mathrm{ev}_{0}^{\prime}+\phi}{\mathrm{eV}_{0}+\phi}=\frac{\lambda}{\lambda^{\prime}}=\frac{100}{200}=\frac{1}{2} \\
2 \mathrm{ev}_{0}^{\prime}+2 \phi=\mathrm{ev}_{0}+\phi \\
\mathrm{ev}_{0}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{ev}_{0}-\phi}{2}=\frac{7.7-4.7}{2}=1.5 \mathrm{~V}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The threshold frequency for a photo-sensitine metal is $3.3 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$. If light of frequency $8.2 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$ is incident on this metal, the cut-off voltage for the photo-electric emission is nearly

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $\mathrm{~A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are two metals with threshold frequencies $1.8 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$ and $2.2 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~Hz}$. Two identical photons of energy $0.825 \mathrm{eV}$ each are incident on them. Then photoelectrons are emitted by $\left(\right.$ Take $\left.\mathrm{h}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}-\mathrm{s}\right)$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \text { Threshold energy of A E }_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{hv}_{\mathrm{A}} \\=& 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 1.8 \times 10^{14} \\=& 11.88 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{~J} \\=& \frac{11.88 \times 10^{-20}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}=0.74 \mathrm{eV} \end{aligned}$
Similarly, $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=0.91 \mathrm{eV}$
As the incident photons have energy greater than $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}}$ but less than $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}$ So, photoelectrons will be emitted from metal A only.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a hydrogen discharged tube it is observed that through a given cross-section $3.13 \times 10^{15}$ electrons are moving from right to left and $3.12 \times 10^{15}$ protons are moving from left to right. What is the electric current in the discharge tube and what is its direction?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{e}}+\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{E}}=1 \mathrm{~mA}$ (towardsright)

questions.q: In photoelectric emission process from a metal of work function $1.8 \mathrm{eV}$, the kinetic energy of most energetic electrons is $0.5 \mathrm{eV}$. The corresponding stopping potential is
questions.solution:

$$

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron accelerated under a potential difference $\mathrm{V}$ volt has a certain wavelength $\lambda$. Mass of proton is some 2000 times of the mass of the electron.
