If the proton has to have the same wavelength $\lambda$, then it will have to be accelerated under a potential difference of
questions.solution:
According to question $\lambda_{e}=\lambda_{P}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { or } \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{e} Q_{e} V}}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{p} Q_{p} V_{p}}} \\
\therefore \quad & m_{e} Q_{e} V=m_{p} Q_{p} V_{p} \\
\therefore \quad & V_{p}=\left(\frac{m_{e}}{m_{p}}\right)\left(\frac{Q_{e}}{Q_{p}}\right) V \\
&=\left(\frac{1}{2000}\right)(1) V \\
&=\frac{V}{2000} v o l t
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: There area $25 W-220 \mathrm{~V}$ bulb and a $100 \mathrm{~W}-220 \mathrm{~V}$ line. Which eletric bulb will glow more brightly?
options: Neither 25 W nor $100 \mathrm{~W}$ bulb will give light
questions.solution:
Power, $P=\frac{V^{2}}{R}, \Rightarrow R=\frac{V^{2}}{P}$
For the first bulb,
$$
R_{1}=\left(\frac{V^{2}}{P_{1}}\right)=\left(\frac{(220)^{2}}{25}\right)=1936 \Omega
$$
For the second bulb,
$$
R_{2}=\left(\frac{V^{2}}{P_{2}}\right)=\left(\frac{(220)^{2}}{100}\right)=484 \Omega
$$
Current in series combination is the same in the two bulbs,
$i=\frac{V}{R_{1}+R_{2}}=\frac{220}{1936+484}=\frac{220}{2420}=\frac{1}{11} A$
If the actual powers in the two bulbs be $P_{1}$ and $P_{2}$ then
$P_{1}^{\prime}=i^{2} R_{1}=\left(\frac{1}{11}\right)^{2} \times 1936=16 \mathrm{~W}$
and $P_{2}^{\prime}=i^{2} R_{2}=\left(\frac{1}{11}\right)^{2} \times 484=4 \mathrm{~W}$
Since $P_{1}^{\prime}>P_{2}^{\prime}$, so, $25 \mathrm{~W}$ bulb will glow more brightly.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Meter bridge or Wheatstone bridge for measurement of resistance, the known and the unknown resistances are interchanged. The error so removed is
questions.solution: To remove the error, resistance box and the unknown resistance must be interchanged and then the mean reading must be taken.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work function of a certain metal is $3.31 \times 10^{-19}$ J. Then, the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons emitted by incident radiation of wavelength $5000 Å$ is (Given, $\mathrm{h}=6.62 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}$ $\left.\mathrm{s}, \mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}, e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)$
questions.solution:
Work function $W_{0}=3.31 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}$ Wavelength of incident radiation $\lambda=5000 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation $E=W_{0}+K E$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \frac{h c}{\lambda} &=3.31 \times 10^{-19}+\mathrm{KE} \\ \mathrm{KE} &=-3.31 \times 10^{-19}+\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{5000 \times 10^{-10}} \\ &=-3.31 \times 10^{-19}+\frac{6.62 \times 3}{5} \times 10^{-19} \\ &=(-3.31 \times 1.324 \times 3) \times 10^{-19} \end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
&=(3.972-3.31) \times 10^{-19} \\
&=0.662 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\
\text { or } E &=\frac{0.662 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=0.41 \mathrm{eV}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Photoelectric work function of a metal is $1 \mathrm{eV}$. Light of wavelength $\lambda=3000 Å$ falls on it. The photo electrons come out with a maximum velocity of :

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the kinetic energy of a moving particle is $\mathrm{E}$, then the de-Broglie wavelength is
questions.solution:
$\quad \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}$ or $\mathrm{mv}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{E}}$ 
so $\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{E}}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The surface of a metal is illuminated with the light of $400 \mathrm{~nm}$. The kinetic energy of the ejected photoelectrons was found to be $1.68$ $\mathrm{eV}$. The work function of the metal is $(\mathrm{hc}=$ 1240 eV.nm)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Let the energy of an emitted photoelectron be $\mathrm{E}$ and thewave-length of incident light be $\lambda$. What will be the change in $\mathrm{E}$ if $\lambda$ is doubled?
questions.solution:
We have $\mathrm{h} v=\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{o}}+\mathrm{E}$, where $\mathrm{E}$ is the energy of emitted photoelectron
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{o}}+\mathrm{E}$
As he and $\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{o}}$ are constant,
$\mathrm{E} \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}$
Therefore, as $\lambda$ is doubled, $E$ will become half.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A photon of energy $E$ ejects a photoelectron from a metal surface whose work function is $W_{0}$. If this electron enters into a uniform magnetic field of induction B in a direction perpendicular to the field and describes a circular path of radius $\mathrm{r}$, then the radius $\mathrm{r}$ is given by, (in the usual notation)
questions.solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation
$E=W_{0}+\frac{1}{2} m v^{2} \quad \text { or } \quad \sqrt{\frac{2\left(E-W_{0}\right)}{m}}=v$
A charged particle placed in uniform magnetic field experience a force
$F=e v B=\frac{m v^{2}}{r}$
or $r=\frac{m v}{e B}$
or $r=\frac{m \sqrt{\frac{2\left(E-W_{0}\right)}{m}}}{e B} \Rightarrow r=\frac{\sqrt{2 m\left(E-W_{0}\right)}}{e B}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the force exerted by an electric dipole on a charge $\mathrm{q}$ at a distance of $1 \mathrm{~m}$ is $\mathrm{F}$, the force at a point $2 \mathrm{~m}$ away in the same direction will be
questions.solution:
We know that the electric field at any point due to an electric dipole varies inversely with the cube of the distance of the point from the centre of the dipole, that is, $\mathrm{E} \propto \frac{1}{r^{3}}$
Also, force on a charge (q) is given by $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qE} \Rightarrow \mathrm{F} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{r}^{3}}$ When the distance of the charge becomes $2 \mathrm{~m}$, i.e. double of its initial value, then new force $\left(\mathrm{F}^{\prime}\right)$ will become $\mathrm{F}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{(2)^{3}} \cdot \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{8}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circuit shown above, an input of $1 \mathrm{~V}$ is fed into the inverting input of an ideal Op-amp A. The output signal $\mathrm{V}_{\text {out }}$ will be
questions.solution: For the Op-amp shown above, we have $\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{o}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{I}}}=-\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{f}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{I}}}$ Comparing this circuit with the given one, We get $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{I}}=1 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{f}}=10 \mathrm{k} \Omega=10 \times 10^{3} \Omega$ $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{I}}=1 \mathrm{k} \Omega=1 \times 10^{3} \Omega$ $\therefore \frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{o}}}{1}=-\frac{10 \times 10^{3}}{1 \times 10^{3}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{o}}=-10 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid sphere of radius $R_{1}$ and volume charge density $\rho=\frac{\rho_{0}}{\mathrm{r}}$ is enclosed by a hollow sphere of radius $R_{2}$ with negative surface charge density $\sigma$, such that the total charge in the system is zero. $\rho_{0}$ is a positive constant and $r$ is the distance from the centre of the sphere. The ratio $\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}$ is
questions.solution:
Let us first calculate the total charge on the soild sphere. Let us consider a concentric sphere of radius $r$ and thickness dr. Then volume of thesphere, $\mathrm{d} \mathrm{V}=4 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \mathrm{dr}$ Given, the volume charge density of the sphere $=\rho=\frac{\rho_{0}}{\mathrm{r}}$
$\therefore$ Charge on this sphere, $\mathrm{dQ}=\rho \cdot \mathrm{dV}=\frac{\rho_{0}}{\mathrm{r}} \cdot 4 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2} \mathrm{dr}=4 \pi \rho \cdot \mathrm{rdr}$ $\therefore$ Total charge on the whole solid sphere, $\begin{array}{l} \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{S}} &=\int_{0}^{\mathrm{R}_{1}} \mathrm{~d} \mathrm{Q}=4 \pi \rho_{0} \int_{0}^{\mathrm{R}_{1}} \mathrm{rdr} \\ &=4 \pi \rho_{0} \frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}}{2}=2 \pi \rho_{0} \mathrm{R}_{1}^{2} \end{aligned} \\ \therefore \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{S}}=2 \pi \rho_{0} \mathrm{R}_{1}^{2} ...(1) \end{array}$ Now, the total charge on the hollow sphere, $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{h}}=-\left(4 \pi \mathrm{R}_{2}^{2}\right) \sigma$ ...(2) By question, $Q_{s}+Q_{h}=0$ $\begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow 2 \pi \rho_{0} \mathrm{R}_{1}^{2}=4 \pi \mathrm{R}_{2}^{2}{ }^{\mathrm{h}} \\ \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}\right)^{2}=\frac{\rho_{0}}{2 \sigma} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}=\sqrt{\frac{\rho_{0}}{2 \sigma}} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A photo-sensitive material would emit electrons if excited by photons beyond a threshold. To overcome the threshold, one would increase
options: the voltage applied to the light source
questions.solution: By Einstein's equation, photoemission occurs when $h v>\phi_{0}$ or $h v>h v_{0}$ i.e., frequency of incident photon is greater than threshold frequency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the distance of $100 \mathrm{~W}$ lamp is increased from a photocell, the saturation current $\mathrm{i}$ in the photocell varies with the distance $d$ as
questions.solution:
As we know, Photoelectric current depends on the intensity of incident radiation i.e., $i \propto I$
But, intensity of radiation $I \propto \frac{1}{d^{2}}$ so, $i \propto \frac{1}{d^{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The kinetic energy of an electron get tripled then the de-Broglie wavelength associated with it changes by a factor

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Light of two different frequencies whose photons have energies $1 \mathrm{eV}$ and $2.5 \mathrm{eV}$, respectively, successively illum inate a metal whose work function is $0.5 \mathrm{eV}$. The ratio of the maximum speed of the emitted electrons will be
questions.solution:
If $\mathrm{E}$ is the energy of incident photon and $\mathrm{W}_{0}$ the work function, then
$\text { Available energy }=\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{W}_{0}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{W}_{0}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2} \\
\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{2\left(E-W_{0}\right)}{m}} \\
\frac{\mathrm{v}_{1}}{\mathrm{v}_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{1-0.5}{2.5-0.5}}=\frac{1}{2}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which phenomenon best supports the theory that matter has a wave nature?
questions.solution: Davison and Germer performed an experiment to prove that matter has a wave nature. The experiment was based on electron diffraction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The deBroglie wavelength associated with a steel ball of mass $1000 \mathrm{gm}$ moving at a speed of $1 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ is $\left[\mathrm{h}=6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right]$
questions.solution:
de Broglie wavelength is given by
$\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{1000 \times 10^{-3} \times 1}=6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature coefficient of a zener mechanism is
questions.solution: Zener diode is a semiconductor device and for semiconductors, temperature coefficient is negative

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil of 40 henry inductance is connected in series with a resistance of \(8 \mathrm{ohm}\) and the combination is joined to the terminals of a 2 volt battery. The time constant of the circuit is
questions.solution:
Time constant is \(\mathrm{L} / \mathrm{R}\)
Given, \(\mathrm{L}=40 \mathrm{H} ~\&~ \mathrm{R}=8 \Omega\)
\(\therefore \tau=40 / 8=5 \mathrm{sec}\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current in a coil of $\mathrm{L}=40 \mathrm{mH}$ is to be increased uniformly from 1 A to $11 \mathrm{~A}$ in 4 milli sec. The induced e.m.f. will be
questions.solution: $\mathrm{e}=\frac{\mathrm{LdI}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{40 \times 10^{-3}(11-1)}{4 \times 10^{-3}}=100 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A varying current in a coil changes from \(10 \mathrm{~A}\) to zero in \(0.5 \mathrm{sec}\). If the average e.m. \(f\) induced in the coil is \(220 \mathrm{~V}\), the self-inductance of the coil is
questions.solution:
Initial current \(\left(\mathrm{I}_1\right)=10 \mathrm{~A}\); Final current \(\left(I_2\right)=0\); Time \((t)=0.5 \mathrm{sec}\) and induced e.m.f. \((\varepsilon)=\) \(220 \mathrm{~V}\).
Induced e.m.f. (\(\varepsilon)\)
\(=-\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{dI}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{L} \frac{\left(\mathrm{I}_2-\mathrm{I}_1\right)}{\mathrm{t}}=-\mathrm{L} \frac{(0-10)}{0.5}=20 \mathrm{~L}\)
or, \(\mathrm{L}=\frac{220}{20}=11 \mathrm{H}\)
[where \(\mathrm{L}=\) Self inductance of coil]

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Lenz's law gives
options: the magnitude of the induced e.m.f.
the direction of the induced current
both the magnitude and direction of the induced current
the magnitude of the induced current
questions.solution: Lenz's law helps to identify the direction of induced current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil of 40 henry inductance is connected in series with a resistance of $8 \mathrm{ohm}$ and the combination is joined to the terminals of a 2 volt battery. The time constant of the circuit is
questions.solution:
Time constant is $\mathrm{L} / \mathrm{R}$ 
Given, $L=40 \mathrm{H} \& \mathrm{R}=8 \Omega$ 
$\therefore \tau=40 / 8=5 \mathrm{sec} .$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a current carrying inductor, emf associated is $20 \mathrm{mV}$. Now, current through it changes from $6 \mathrm{~A}$ to $2 \mathrm{~A}$ in $2 \mathrm{~s}$. The coefficient of mutual inductance is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
|\mathrm{e}|=\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{dI}}{\mathrm{dt}} \\
\text { Here, } \mathrm{e}=20 \mathrm{~m} V=20 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V} \\
\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{6-2}{2}=2 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{s} \\
\mathrm{L}=? \\
\Rightarrow \quad 20 \times 10^{-3}=\mathrm{L} \times 2 \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{L}=10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H}=10 \mathrm{mH} .
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In shown fig, the circular loop of wire is moved with velocity towards the infinite current carrying wire. Then
options: no current is induced in loop
current is induced in loop clockwise
current is induced in loop anticlockwise
extra charges are induced on the wire loop
questions.solution: Mganetic field at the centre of the circular loop increases as it moves towards the current carrying wire as we know it varies inversely with the distance. Therefore, induced current in the loop should be such that it can reduce the increased magnetic field. This is possible only when the induced current is clockwise.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square current carrying loop is changed to a circular loop in time $\mathrm{t}_{1}$. Then
options: emf is induced in loop for time $t < t_{1}$
emf is induced in loop for time $t>t_{1}$
no emf is induced in loop during whole process
emf is induced due to change in magnetic field
questions.solution: For the time $\mathrm{t} < \mathrm{t}_{1}$, area of loop changes. Hence, magnetic flux linked with it changes during this time and emf is induced thereby.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic flux in a closed circuit of resistance $10 \Omega$ varies with time as $\phi=\left(2 \mathrm{t}-4 \mathrm{t}^{2}+1\right)$. The current in the loop will change its direction after a time of
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{e} &=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=[-2+8 \mathrm{t}+0] \\ & \Rightarrow 8 \mathrm{t}=2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{sec}=0.25 \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An induced emf has
options: a direction same as field direction
a direction opposite to the field direction
questions.solution:
From Lentz's law, the direction of induced emf in a circuit is such that it opposes the magnetic flux that produces it. So, if the magnetic flux linked with a closed circuit increases the induced current flows in a direction so as to develop a magnetic flux in the opposite direction of original flux.
If the magnetic flux linked with a closed circuit decreases then the induced current flows in the same direction of original flux. So, the induced emf has not direction of its own.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil of area $5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ having 20 turns is placed in a uniform magnetic field of $10^{3}$ gauss. The normal to the plane of coil makes an angle $30^{\circ}$ with the magnetic field. The flux through the coil is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solenoid $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ long is made by winding 2000 loops of wire on an iron rod whose cross-section is $1.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$.If the relative permeability of the iron is $6000 .$ What is the self-inductance of the solenoid?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two coils have a mutual inductance $0.55 \mathrm{H}$. The current changes in the first coil according to equation $I=I_{0} \sin \omega t$.
where, $\mathrm{I}_{0}=10 \mathrm{~A}$ and $\omega=100 \pi \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ The maximum value of emf in the second coil is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\text { E.M.F. } \mathrm{e}=\mathrm{M} \frac{\mathrm{di}}{\mathrm{dt}}=0.005 \times \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left(\mathrm{i}_{0} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}\right) \\
=0.005 \times \mathrm{i}_{0} \cos \omega \mathrm{t} \\
\mathrm{e}_{\max }=0.005 \times 10 \times 100 \pi=5 \pi
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The natural frequency of the circuit shown in adjoining figure is
questions.solution:
In the given circuit, two condensors and the inductor are in series.
$\therefore \mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{s}}=\mathrm{L}+\mathrm{L}=2 \mathrm{~L}$
and $\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{s}}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{C}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{s}}=\frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}$
$\therefore$ Natural frequency of the circuit
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{v}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{s}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{s}}}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{2 \mathrm{~L}} \times \sqrt{\mathrm{C} / 2}} \\
=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In $\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}$ solution when electric current equal to $2.5$ faraday is passed, the gm equivalent deposited on the cathode is
questions.solution: 1 faraday deposited $1 \mathrm{~g}$ equivalent

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil of resistance $10 \Omega$ and an inductance $5 \mathrm{H}$ is connected to a $100 \mathrm{~V}$ battery. The energy stored in the coil is
questions.solution:
$\quad$ Current $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{100}{10}=10 \mathrm{~A}$
Energy, $\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{LI}^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times(10)^{2}=250 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current $\mathrm{i}$ in a coil varies with time as shown in the figure. The variation of induced emfwith time would be
questions.solution:
We know, inducedemf
$$
\mathrm{e}=-\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{di}}{\mathrm{dt}}
$$
During 0 to $\frac{\mathrm{T}}{4}, \frac{\mathrm{di}}{\mathrm{dt}}=$ constant
$\mathrm{So}, \mathrm{e}=-\mathrm{ve}$
For $\frac{T}{4}$ to $\frac{T}{2}, \frac{d i}{d t}=0$ i.e., $\mathrm{e}=0$
For $\frac{T}{4}$ to $\frac{3 \mathrm{~T}}{4}, \frac{\mathrm{di}}{\mathrm{dt}}=$ constant i.e., $\mathrm{e}=+\mathrm{ve}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Ina magnetic field of $0.05 \mathrm{~T}$, area of a coil changes from $101 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ to $100 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ without changing the resistance which is $2 \Omega$. The amount of charge that flow during this period is
questions.solution:
Magnetic Flux, $\phi=B \cdot A$
Change in flux, $\mathrm{d} \phi=B \cdot d A$
$=0.05(101-100) \times 10^{-4}$
$=5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~Wb}$
Charge dQ $=\frac{d \phi}{R}$ $=\frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{2}=2.5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solenoid has 2000 turns wound over a length of $0.30 \mathrm{~m}$. The area of its cross-section is $1.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$. Around its central section, a coil of 300 turn is wound. If an initial current of $2 \mathrm{~A}$ in the solenoid is reversed in $0.25 \mathrm{~s}$, then the emf induced in the coil is
questions.solution:
Induced emfe $=M \frac{d i}{d t}$
$=\frac{\mu_{0} N_{1} N_{2} A}{l} \cdot \frac{d i}{d t}$
$=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2000 \times 300 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.30}$
$=48.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V}=48 \mathrm{mV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Figure shows a straight wire length $l$ carrying current $i$. The magnitude of magnetic field produced by the current at point $\mathrm{P}$ is
questions.solution:
Magnetic field due to finite length of a wire, $$ B=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{i}{r}\left(\sin \phi_{1}+\sin \phi_{2}\right) $$
Here, $\phi_{1}=0^{\circ}, \phi=45^{\circ}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} B=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{i}{r}\left(\sin 0^{\circ}+\sin 45^{\circ}\right)=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{i}{\sqrt{2 l}} \\ \Rightarrow B=\frac{\sqrt{2} \mu_{0} i}{8 \pi l} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a region of uniform magnetic induction \(\mathrm{B}=10^{-2}\) tesla, a circular coil of radius \(30 \mathrm{~cm}\) and resistance \(\pi^2 \mathrm{ohm}\) is rotated about an axis which is perpendicular to the direction of \(B\) and which forms a diameter of the coil. If the coil rotates at \(200 \mathrm{rpm}\) the amplitude of the alternating current induced in the coil is
questions.solution:
\(I_0=\frac{E_0}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mathrm{nBA} \omega}{\mathrm{R}}\)
Given, \(\mathrm{n}=1, \mathrm{~B}=10^{-2} \mathrm{~T}\),
\(\mathrm{A}=\pi(0.3)^2 \mathrm{~m}^2, \mathrm{R}=\pi^2\)
\(\mathrm{f}=(200 / 60)\) and \(\omega=2 \pi(200 / 60)\)
Substituting these values and solving, we get \(\mathrm{I}_0=6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}=6 \mathrm{~mA}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an inductor of self-inductance \(\mathrm{L}=2 \mathrm{mH}\), current changes with time according to relation \(\mathrm{i}=\mathrm{t}^2 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}}\). At what time emf is zero?
questions.solution:
\(\mathrm{L}=2 \mathrm{mH}, \mathrm{i}=\mathrm{t}^2 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}}\)
\(\mathrm{E}=-\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{di}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{L}\left[-\mathrm{t}^2 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}}+2 \mathrm{te} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}}\right]\)
when \(\mathrm{E}=0\),
\(\begin{aligned}
& -\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{t}^2+2 \mathrm{te}-\mathrm{t}=0 \\
& \text {or, } 2 \mathrm{t} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{t}^2 \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{sec}.
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rectangular coil ABCD which is rotated at a constant angular velocity about an horizontal as shown in the figure. The axis of rotation of the coil as well as the magnetic field $\mathrm{B}$ are horizontal. Maximum current will flow in the circuit when the plane of the coil is
options: inclined at 30 degrees to the magnetic field
inclined at 45 degrees to the magnetic field
questions.solution:
Torque on the coil is $\tau=\mathrm{nIB} \mathrm{A} \cos \theta$. If coil is set with its plane parallel to direction of magnetic field B, then $\theta=0^{\circ}, \cos \theta=1$
$\Rightarrow \tau=n I B A .1=n I B A=m a x i m u m$
Hence, $\mathrm{I}=$ maximum $($ as $\mathrm{n}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{A}$ are constant $)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal disc of radius $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ is rotated at a constant angular speed of $60 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ in a plane at right angles to an external field of magnetic induction $0.05 \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$. The emfinduced between the centre and a point on the rim will be
questions.solution:
Induced emf produced between the centre and a point on the disc is given by $e=\frac{1}{2} \omega B R^{2}$
Putting the values,
$\omega=60 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{B}=0.05 \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$ and $\mathrm{R}=100 \mathrm{~cm}=1 \mathrm{~m}$
We get $\quad e=\frac{1}{2} \times 60 \times 0.05 \times(1)^{2}=1.5 V$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current in an $L-R$ circuit builds up to $(3 / 4)^{\mathrm{th}}$ of its steady state value in 4 seconds. The time constant of this circuit is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave in free space travelling along \(\mathrm{x}\)-direction is given by : \(E_z=60 \sin (k x+\omega t) \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}\).
The magnetic field component will be given as :
Here c represents the speed of light in free space.
questions.solution:
Given \(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{z}}=60 \sin (\mathrm{kx}+\omega \mathrm{t}) \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m}\). The magnetic field component must be perpendicular to \(E_z\) and \(x\).
Also \(B_0=\frac{E_0}{c}=\frac{60}{c}\). Thus
\(\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{y}}=\frac{60}{\mathrm{c}} \sin (\mathrm{kx}+\omega \mathrm{t}) \text {. }\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic field in a travelling electromagnetic wave has a peak value of $20 \mathrm{nT}$. The peak value of electric field strength is
questions.solution:
From question,
$\mathrm{B}_{0}=20 \mathrm{nT}=20 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~T}$ 
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{0}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{0} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{C}}$ 
$\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{0}\right|=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{0}|.| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{C}} \mid=20 \times 10^{-9} \times 3 \times 10^{8}=6 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m} .$ 
$\left(\because\right.$ velocity of light in vacuum $\left.\mathrm{C}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The magnetic field in a electromagnetic wave is given by
$B_y=2 \times 10^{-7} \sin \left(0.5+10^3 x+1.5 \times 10^{11} t\right) \mathrm{T}$
The frequency of the wave is (in GHz )
questions.solution:
Comparing the given equation with
 $B_y=B_0 \sin \left[2 \pi\left(\frac{x}{\lambda}+\frac{t}{T}\right)\right]$
 We get, $\quad \lambda=\frac{2 \pi}{0.5 \times 10^3}=1.26 \mathrm{~cm}$
 and $\quad \frac{1}{T}=v=\frac{\left(1.5 \times 10^{11}\right)}{2 \pi}=23.9 \mathrm{GHz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an electromagnetic wave
options: power is transmitted along the magnetic field
power is transmitted along the electric field
power is equally transferred along the electric and magnetic fields
power is transmitted in a direction perpendicular to both the fields
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an electric circuit, a capacitor of reactance $55 \Omega$ is connected across the source of 220 V . The value of displacement current is
questions.solution:
We know that, displacement current = conduction current
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{V}{X_c} \\ & =\frac{220}{55} \\ & =4 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electromagnetic wave propagating along north lies its electric field vertically upward. The magnetic field vector points towards
questions.solution: As the electromagnetic wave is the crossed field of electric and magnetic waves, so the direction of propagation of EM wave is the direction of vector $E \times B$. Here $E$ is upward and $(\mathrm{E} \times \mathrm{B})$ is towards north. So, from right hand thumb rule B will be along east.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Pick out the wrong statement.
options: Gauss's law of magnetism is given by $\alpha \oint \mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{d s}=0$
An EM wave is a wave radiated by a charge at rest and propagates through electric field only
A time varying electric field is a source of changing magnetic field

Faraday's law of EM induction is
$\oint \mathbf{E . d l}=-\frac{d \phi_{B}}{d t}$
questions.solution: An electromagnetic wave is the wave radiated by an accelerated charge and propagates through space as coupled electric and magnetic field. These fields are oscillating perpendicular to each other.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The amplitude of an electromagnetic wave in vaccum is doubled with no other changes made to the wave. As a result of this doubling of the amplitude, which of the following statement is correct?
options: The frequency of the wave changes only
The wave length of the wave changes only
The speed of the wave propagation changes only
Alone of the above is correct
questions.solution:
As we know, velocity of electromagnetic wave, $\mathrm{c}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
It is independent of amplitude of electromagnetic wave, frequency and wavelength of electromagnetic wave.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $\varepsilon_{0}$ and $\mu_{0}$ represent the permittivity and permeability of vaccum and $\varepsilon$ and $\mu$ represent the permittivity and permeability of medium, then refractive index of the medium is given by
questions.solution: Refractive index of a medium $\mu=\sqrt{\frac{\mu \varepsilon}{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The graph between the square root of the frequency of a specific line of characterstic spectrum of X-ray and the atomic number of the ttarget will be
questions.solution: $\sqrt{\mathrm{v}} \propto(\mathrm{Z}-\mathrm{b})$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an electromagnetic wave, the average energy density associated with magnetic field is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{mag}}=\frac{B^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
An electromagnetic wave going through vacuum is described by
$$
E=E_{0} \sin (k x-\omega t)
$$
Which of the following is/are independent of the wavelength?
questions.solution: $\frac{k}{\omega}$ is independent of $\lambda$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The power of a thin convex lens $\left(\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{g}}=1.5\right)$ is $5.0 \mathrm{D} .$ When it is placed in a liquid of refractive index $_{a} n_{b}$, then it behaves as a concave lens of focal length $100 \mathrm{~cm}$. The refractive index of the liquid $\mathrm{a}_{l}$ will be
questions.solution:
By using lens maker's formula,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right) \\
\Rightarrow 5=(1.5-1)\left(\frac{2}{R}\right)...(i)
\end{array}
$$
If a lens of refractive index $\mu_{g}$ is immersed in a liquid of refractive index $\mu_{1}$, then its focal length in liquid
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{f_{l}}=\left(_{\ell} \mu_{g}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right) \\
\Rightarrow-1=\left(\frac{1.5}{n}-1\right)\left(\frac{2}{R}\right) ...(ii)\\
\text { Dividing, (i) by (ii) }-5=\frac{0.5 n}{1.5-n} \\
\Rightarrow-7.5+5 n=0.5 n \Rightarrow-7.5=-4.5 n \\
\Rightarrow n=\frac{75}{45}=\frac{5}{3}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two particles X and Y having equal charges, after being accelerated through the same potential difference, enter a region of uniform magnetic field and describe circular paths of radii $\mathrm{R}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{2}$, Respectively. The ratio of masses of $\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y}$ is
questions.solution:
As we know, $\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=q V$ or $v=\sqrt{\frac{2 q V}{m}}$
Centripetal force $\frac{m v^{2}}{R}=q \times B \times v$
$\therefore v=\frac{q B R}{m}$
Hence, $\sqrt{\frac{2 q V}{m}}=\frac{q B R}{m}$ or $R=\left(\frac{2 m V}{q}\right)^{1 / 2} \times \frac{1}{B}$
Here, $V, q$ and $B$ are constants.
$\therefore R \propto m$
And, $\frac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}=\left(\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}\right)^{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two light sources are said to be coherent if they are obtained from
options: two independent point sources emitting light of the same wavelength
two ordinary bulbs emitting light of different wavelengths
questions.solution: If two independent sources emitting light of the same wavelength are said to be coherent.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An observer 'A' sees an asteroid with a radioactive element moving by at a speed $=0.3 \mathrm{c}$ and measures the radioactivity decay time to be $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{A}^{\prime}}$. Another observer ' $\mathrm{B}$ ' is moving with the asteroid and measures its decay time as $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{B}}$. Then $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{B}}$ are related as below
options: Either (A) or (C) depending on whether the asteroid is approaching or moving away from A
questions.solution:
Due to time dilation the interval between two events at the same point in a moving frame appears to be longer by a factor
$\gamma\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\frac{v^{2}}{c^{2}}}}\right)$ to an observer in a stationary frame.
Time dilation is independent of the direction of velocity and depends only on its magnitude.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the speed of a wave doubles as it passes from shallow water into deeper water, its wavelength will be
questions.solution:
We have, speed $=$ frequency $\times$ wavelength
Also, when a wave passes from one medium to the other, its frequency remains constant.
$\therefore$ speed $\propto$ wavelength
Therefore, when the speed of a wave doubles, its wavelength also doubles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The velocity of a particle at which the kinetic energy is equal to its rest energy is
questions.solution:
Here, Kinetic energy = Rest energy we know that 
Kinetic energy (Relativistic) $=\left(\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{m}_{0}\right) \mathrm{c}^{2}$
and Rest energy $=m_{0} c^{2}$
where $m_{0}=$ rest mass, $\mathrm{c}=$ velocity of light
Also, mass (m) of a particle moving with velocity $v$ is given by
$\mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{0}}{\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}\right)}}$
$\therefore\left(\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{m}_{0}\right) \mathrm{c}^{2}=\mathrm{m}_{0} \mathrm{c}^{2}$ provides
$\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}_{0}}{\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}\right)}}-\mathrm{m}_{0}\right) \mathrm{c}^{2}=\mathrm{m}_{0} \mathrm{c}^{2}$
or, $\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}\right)}}-1=1$
or, $\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{v^{2}}{c^{2}}\right)}}=2 \quad$ or, $\quad 1-\frac{v^{2}}{c^{2}}=\frac{1}{4}$ or, $\frac{v^{2}}{c^{2}}=1-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{3}{4}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \mathrm{v}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{c} \\
\Rightarrow 1-\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}=\frac{1}{4} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}=1-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}^{2}=\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{c}^{2} \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} \mathrm{c}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electromagnetic wave with poynting vector $6 \mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$ is absorbed by a surface area $12 \mathrm{~m}^2$. The force on the surface is
questions.solution:
As $F=p A$, but $p=\frac{1}{c}$
Force on the surface is given as So, $F=\frac{I A}{C}=\frac{6 \times 12}{3 \times 10^8}=2.4 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The proton of energy $1 \mathrm{MeV}$ describes a circular path in plane at right angles to a uniform magnetic field of $6.28 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~T}$. The mass of the proton is $1.7 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{Kg}$. The cyclotron frequency of the proton is very nearly equal to

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Radio carbon dating is done by estimating in specimen
options: the amount of ordinary carbon still present
the amount of radio carbon still present
the ratio of amount of ${ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_{6}$ to ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_{6}$ still present
the ratio of amount of ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_{6}$ to ${ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_{6}$ still present
questions.solution: Radio carbon dating is done by measuring ratio of ${ }^{14} \mathrm{C}$ present in the sample, since proportion of $^{14} \mathrm{C}$ and $^{12} \mathrm{C}$ in a body is same; but after death ${ }^{14} \mathrm{C}$ decays. Hence knowing the present ratio of $^{14} \mathrm{C}$ to ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}$, sample can be dated.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following are not electromagnetic waves?
questions.solution: Cosmic rays are coming from outer space, having high energy charged particles, like a-particle, proton etc. $\beta$-rays are stream of high energy electrons, coming from the nucleus of radioactive atoms.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Our eyes respond to wavelengths ranging from
questions.solution: Our eyes respond to visible range from 400 $\mathrm{nm}$ to $700 \mathrm{~nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The glancing angle in a X-rays diffraction experiment is \(30^{\circ}\) and the wavelength of the \(\mathrm{X}\) rays used is \(20 \mathrm{~mm}\). The interplanar spacing of the crystal diffracting these X-rays will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A new system of units is evolved in which the values of $\mu_{0}$ and $\varepsilon_{0}$ are 2 and 8 respectively. Then the speed of light in this system will be
questions.solution: Velocity of electromagnatic wave in space is $\mathrm{c}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \in_{0}}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \times 8}}$ $=\frac{1}{\sqrt{16}}=\frac{1}{4}=0.25$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which is the incorrect statement of the following?
options: Photon is a particle with zero rest mass
Photon is a particle with zero momentum
Photons travel with velocity of light in vacuum
Photons even feel the pull of gravity
questions.solution:
Photon moves with speed of light ie, $v=c$ and rest mass of a particle is
$\mathrm{m}_{0}=\mathrm{m} \sqrt{1-\mathrm{v}^{2} / \mathrm{c}^{2}}$
hence $m_{0}$ (photon) $=0$
$\therefore$ photon has zero rest mass.
Momentum of photon $=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\lambda}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An element with atomic number $Z=11$ emits $K_{\alpha}-$ X-ray of wavelength $\lambda$. The atomic number which emits $K_{\alpha}$-X-ray of wavelength $4 \lambda$ is
questions.solution:
According to Moseley's law $\sqrt{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{a}(\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{b})$ or $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{a}^{2}(\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{b})^{2}$
or $\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\lambda}=\mathrm{a}^{2}(\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{b})^{2}$
$\therefore \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{z}_{2}-1\right)^{2}}{\left(\mathrm{z}_{1}-1\right)^{2}}$
Here $\lambda_{1}=\lambda, \lambda_{2}=4 \lambda, \mathrm{z}_{1}=11$ and $\mathrm{z}_{2}=$ ?
$\therefore \frac{1}{4 \lambda}=\frac{\left(z_{2}-1\right)^{2}}{(11-1)^{2}}$
or $\left(\mathrm{z}_{2}-1\right)^{2}=25$ or $\mathrm{z}_{2}=6$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A direct X-ray photograph of the intenstines is not generally taken by radiologists because
options: intenstines would burst an exposure to X-rays
the X-rays would be not pass through the intenstines
the X-rays will pass through the intenstines without causing a good shadow for any useful diagnosis
a very small exposure of $X$-rays causes cancer in the intenstines,
questions.solution: As X-rays pass through the intestine without casting a clear shadow.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work done in carrying a charge \(\mathrm{q}\) once around a circle or radius \(r\) with a charge \(Q\) placed at the centre will be
questions.solution: In a round trip, displacement is zero. Hence, work done is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two charged spherical conductors (each of radius \(\mathrm{R})\) are at a distance \(d\) part \((\mathrm{d} > 2 \mathrm{R})\). The charge on one is \(+q\) and that on the other is \(-q\). The magnitude of the force between them will be :
questions.solution: Because of induction, the effective force between them becomes equal to greater than \(\mathrm{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\mathrm{q}^2}{\mathrm{~d}^2}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Eight dipoles of charges of magnitude $e$ each are placed insidÃƒÂ© a cube. The total electric flux coming out of the cube will be
questions.solution:
Net charges of one dipole $=-e+e=0$
Net charge of 8 dipoles $=8 \times 0=0$
$\Rightarrow$ Net charge inside cube $=0=q$
By Gauss' law,
Total flux emerging from surface $=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{0}{\varepsilon_0}=0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a region of space having a uniform electric field \(\mathrm{E}\), a hemispherical bowl of radius \(r\) is placed. The electric flux \(\phi\) through the bowl is
questions.solution: \(\phi=\mathrm{E}\) (ds) \(\cos \theta=\mathrm{E}\left(\pi \mathrm{r}^2\right) \cos 0^{\circ}=\pi \mathrm{r}^2 \mathrm{E}\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two point charges $+3 \mu C$ and $+8 \mu C$ repel each other with a force of $40 \mathrm{~N}$. If a charge of $-5 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is added to each of them, then the force between them will become
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three charges, each of $+4 \mu \mathrm{C}$, are placed at the corners $A, B, C$ of a square $A B C D$ of side 1 m . What is the magnitude (in $\mathrm{NC}^{-1}$ ) of electric field at point $O$ towards point $D ?$
questions.solution: $E_{\mathrm{A}}$ cancels $E_{\mathrm{C}}$. At $\begin{aligned} O ; E_B & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(\frac{d}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 q}{d^2} \\ & =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{1^2} \\ & =72 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}, \text { towards } D \\ & =72000 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electric dipole is kept in a uniform electric field. It experiences
options: a torque but no net force
neither a force nor a torque,
questions.solution: In a uniform electric field, net force $=0$, but torque $\neq 0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metallic sphere is placed in a uniform electric field. The line of force follow the path (s) shown in the figure as
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A (+)vely charged particle is placed near an infinitely long straight conductor where there is zero gravity. Then
options: the charged particle will not move
it will move parallel to the straight conductor
it will move perpendicular to the straight conductor
it will move with constant acceleration
questions.solution: As the charged particle is at rest, no force will act on it due to the magnetic field produced by the conductor at the site of the charge. Hence, it will remain at rest.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid sphere of radius $R$ carries a uniform volume charge density $\rho$. The magnitude of electric field inside the sphere at a distance $\mathrm{r}$ from the centre is
questions.solution: $E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q r}{R^{3}}=\frac{q}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}} \times \frac{r}{3 \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{\rho \times r}{3 \varepsilon_{0}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four point charges $-Q,-q, 2 q$ and $2 Q$ are placed, one at each corner of the square. The relation between $Q$ and $q$ for which the potential at the centre of the square is zero is
questions.solution:
Let the side length of square be ' $a$ ' then potential at centre $O$ is $V=\frac{k(-Q)}{\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}+\frac{k(-q)}{\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}}+\frac{k(2 q)}{\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}}+\frac{k(2 Q)}{\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}}=0$
$=-Q-q+2 q+2 Q=0 \Rightarrow Q+q=0$ $\Rightarrow Q=-q$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge $+\mathrm{q}$ is at a distance $\mathrm{L} / 2$ above a square of side $\mathrm{L}$. Then what is the flux linked with the surface?
questions.solution: The given square of side $\mathrm{L}$ may be considered as one of the faces of a cube with edge $\mathrm{L}$. Then given charge $\mathrm{q}$ will be considered to be placed at the centre of the cube. Then according to Gauss's theorem, the magnitude of the electric flux through the faces (six) of the cube is given by $$ \phi=\mathrm{q} / \varepsilon_{0} $$ Hence, electric flux through one face of the cube for the given square will be $$ \phi^{\prime}=\frac{1}{6} \phi=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{6 \varepsilon_{0}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Equal charges $q$ each are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side $r$. The magnitude of electric field intensity at any vertex is
questions.solution:
Due to charge at $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ magnitude of intensity of electric field at point C $\mathrm{E}_{1}=\mathrm{E}_{2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}}$
Net intensity at point $\mathrm{C}$ is $\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{R}}=\sqrt{\mathrm{E}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{E}_{2}^{2}+2 \mathrm{E}_{1} \mathrm{E}_{2} \cos 60^{\circ}} \\ =\sqrt{\mathrm{E}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{E}_{1}^{2}+2 \mathrm{E}_{1}^{2} \times \frac{1}{2}}=\sqrt{3} \mathrm{E}_{1}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{r}^{2}} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two point dipoles $p \hat{k}$ and $\frac{p}{2} \hat{k}$ are located at $(0,0,0)$ and $(1 \mathrm{~m}, 0,2 \mathrm{~m})$ respectively. The resultant electric field due to the two dipoles at the point $(1 \mathrm{~m}, 0,0)$ is
questions.solution:
The given point is on axis of $\frac{\vec{p}}{2}$ dipole and at equatorial line of $\vec{p}$ dipole so that field at given point is $\left(\vec{E}_{1}+\vec{E}_{2}\right)$
$\begin{array}{c} \vec{E}_{1}=\frac{2 K(p / 2)}{2^{3}}=\frac{K p}{8}(+\hat{k}) \\ \vec{E}_{2}=\frac{K p}{1}(-\hat{k}) \\ \vec{E}_{1}+\vec{E}_{2}=-\frac{7}{8} \mathrm{~K} p(-\hat{k})=-\frac{7 p}{32 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \hat{k} \\ 250 \times 9.8 \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two points masses, $m$ each carrying charges $-q$ and $+q$ are attached to the ends of a massless rigid non-conducting wire of length ' $L$ '. When this arrangement is placed in a uniform electric field, then it deflects through an angle $i$. The minimum time needed by rod to align itself along the field is
questions.solution:
Torque when the wire is brought in a uniform field $\mathrm{E}$. $\begin{array}{l} \tau=\mathrm{qEL} \sin \theta \\ =\mathrm{qEL} \theta \end{array} \quad[\because \theta \text { is very small }]$ Moment of inertia of rod AB about O $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2}\right)^{2}+\mathrm{m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2}\right)^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{mL}^{2}}{2}$
$\begin{array}{l} \tau=\mathrm{I} \alpha . \\ \therefore \quad \alpha=\frac{\tau}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{\mathrm{qEL} \theta}{\frac{\mathrm{mL}^{2}}{2}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \omega^{2} \theta=\frac{2 \mathrm{qEL} \theta}{\mathrm{mL}^{2}} \quad\left[\because \theta=\omega^{2} \theta\right] \\ \Rightarrow \quad \omega^{2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{q} \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{mL}} \end{array}$ Time period of the wire $\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{mL}}{2 \mathrm{q} \mathrm{E}}}$ The rod will become parallel to the field in time $\frac{T}{4}$. $\therefore \quad \mathrm{t}=\frac{\mathrm{T}}{4}=\frac{\pi}{2} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{mL}}{2 \mathrm{q} \mathrm{E}}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If 1000 droplets each of potential $1 \mathrm{~V}$ and radius $r$ are mixed to form a big drop. Then, the potential of the drop as compared to small droplets, will be
questions.solution: Potential of big drop $=\mathrm{q} \times \mathrm{n}^{2 / 3}=100 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential of a large liquid drop when eight liquid drops are combined is $20 \mathrm{~V}$. Then the potential of each single drop was
questions.solution:
Volume of 8 small drops $=$ Volume of big drop
$\therefore\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}\right) \times 8=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}$ $\Rightarrow 2 r=R$ $\ldots$ (i)
According to charge conservation
(.) (ii)
$$
8 \mathrm{q}=\mathrm{Q}
$$
Potential of one small drop $\left(\mathrm{V}^{\prime}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{r}}$
Similarly, potential of big drop $(\mathrm{V})=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R}$
Now, $\frac{\mathrm{V}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{Q}} \times \frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{r}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{V}^{\prime}}{20}=\frac{9}{8 \mathrm{q}} \times \frac{2 \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}}$ $\therefore \mathrm{V}^{\prime}=5 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hollow conducting sphere is placed in an electric field produced by a point charg placed at $\mathrm{P}$ as shown in figure. Let $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}, \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}, \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}$ be the potentials at points $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ respectively. Then
questions.solution: Conducting surface behaves as equipotential surface.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Charge $q$ is uniformly spread on a thin ring of radius $\mathrm{R}$. The ring rotates about its axis with a uniform frequency $f \mathrm{~Hz}$. The magnitude of magnetic induction at the centre of the ring is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three charges, each $+\mathrm{q}$, are placed at the comers of an isosceles triagle $A B C$ of sides $B C$ and $A C$, 2a. D and E are the mid-points of BC and CA. The work done in taking a charge $Q$ from $D$ to $E$ is
questions.solution:
Here, $\mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{BC}$
$$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{D}}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{E}}=\mathrm{V} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{Q}\left[\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{E}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{D}}\right] \\ \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{Q}[\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{V}] \\ \mathrm{W}=0 \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A glass rod rubbed with silk is used to change a gold leaf electroscope and the leaves are observed to diverge. The electroscope thus charged is exposed to X-rays for a short period. Then
options: the divergence of leave will not affected
questions.solution: As charge on glass $\operatorname{rod}$ is $+$ (ve) so charge on gold leaves will also be $+($ ve $)$. Due to X-rays more electrons from leaves will be emitted, so leaves becomes more positive and diverge further.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cube of side $\ell$ is placed in a uniform field $\mathrm{E}$, where $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{i}}$. The net electric flux through the cube is
questions.solution: Here, net flux is zero, as there is no charge residing inside the cube.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Figure shows three points \(\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}\) and \(\mathrm{C}\) in a region of uniform electric field \(\vec{E}\). The line \(A B\) is perpendicular and \(\mathrm{BC}\) is parallel to the field lines. Then which of the following holds good?
Where \(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}, \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}\) and \(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}\) represent the electric potentials at the points \(\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}\) and \(\mathrm{C}\) respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two point charges of $+3 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-3 \mu \mathrm{C}$ are placed at a certain distance apart from each other. Electric potential at a distance 60 cm from the mid-point of dipole on axial line is 150 V , then the value of dipole length is
questions.solution:
Given, $V=150 \mathrm{~V}$
$\begin{aligned} r & =60 \mathrm{~cm}=0.6 \mathrm{~m} \\ \therefore \quad V & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{p \cos \theta}{r^2}\end{aligned}$
For axial line, $\theta=0^{\circ}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{p \cos 0^{\circ}}{r^2} \\ 150 & =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times p \times 1}{(0.6)^2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad p & =\frac{150 \times 0.36}{9 \times 10^9}\end{aligned}$
$p=6 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~cm}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & & 2 a \cdot q & =6 \times 10^{-9} \\ & \Rightarrow & 2 a & =\frac{6 \times 10^{-9}}{q}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{6 \times 10^{-9}}{3 \times 10^{-6}} \\ & =2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An infinite number of charge, each of charge $1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ are placed on the $\mathrm{x}$-ax is with coord inates $\mathrm{x}=1,2$, $4,8, \ldots \ldots \infty$. If a charge of $1 \mathrm{C}$ is kept at the origin, then what is the net force acting on $1 \mathrm{C}$ charge?
questions.solution:
In figure there is the schematic diagram of distribution of charges on $x$-axis

Using principle of superposition total force acting on 1 C charge
$$
\begin{aligned}
F &=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{1 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{(1)^{2}}+\frac{1 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{(2)^{2}}\right.\\
&\left.+\frac{1 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{(4)^{2}}+\frac{1 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{(8)^{2}}+\ldots \infty\right] \\
=& \frac{10^{-6}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{16}+\frac{1}{64}+\ldots \infty\right] \\
=& 9 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{9}\left(\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{4}}\right) \\
=& 9 \times 10^{9} \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{4}{3}=12000 \mathrm{~N}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An object A has a charge of \(+2 \mu \mathrm{C}\) and the object \(B\) has charge of \(+6 \mu \mathrm{C}\). Which statement is true?
questions.solution:
The magnitude of force on \(A\) due to \(B\) and on \(B\) due to \(A\) is same but both will act in opposite directions as the electrostatic force between them is repulsive.
\(\therefore \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{AB}}=-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{\mathrm{BA}}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point charge $\mathrm{q}$ is rotated along a circle in the electric field generated by another point charge Q. The work done by the electric field on the rotating charge in one complete revolution is
options: zero if the charge $Q$ is at the centre and nonzero otherwise.
questions.solution:
Work done in displacement of charge, $\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{q} \Delta \mathrm{V}$
where $\Delta \mathrm{V}=$ potential difference between the displacement of charge Hence, work done in complete rotation is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In the figure, a proton moves a distance \(d\) in a uniform electric field \(\vec{E}\) as shown in the figure. Does the electric field do a positive or negative work on the proton? Does the electric potential energy of the proton increase or decrease?
Here, distance increases so, potential energy increases.
questions.solution:
Force \(=\mathrm{eE}\)
Workdone \(=\) force \(\times\) distance
Force and distance are in opposite direction, so work is negative.
\(\mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{e} \mathrm{E} \times \mathrm{d}\)
Here, distance increases so, potential energy increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The radii of two concentric spherical conducting shells are \(r_1\) and \(r_2\left( > r_1\right)\). The charge on the outer shell is \(q\). What will be the charge on the inner shell which is connected to the earth?
questions.solution: Let \(\mathrm{q}_1\) be the charge on the inner shell. On being earth its potential will be zero. or \(\mathrm{V}=0=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{\mathrm{q}_1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{\mathrm{q}_2}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right]\) \(\therefore \mathrm{q}^{\prime}=-\frac{\mathrm{r}_1}{\mathrm{r}_2} \mathrm{q}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a Millikan's oil drop experiment the charge on an oil drop is calculated to be $6.35 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$. The number of excess electrons on the drop is
questions.solution:
As we know, $Q=$ ne
Number of electron $\mathrm{n}=\frac{Q}{e}$
$\frac{6.35 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=3.9=4$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A thin metallic spherical shell contains a charge Q on it. Apoint charge $q$ is placed with the centre of the shell and another charge $\mathrm{q}_{1} \mathrm{~s}$ placed outside it as shown in the figure. All the three charges are positive.
The force on the charge at the centre is
questions.solution: Field inside the metallic shell is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two light rays having the same wavelength $\lambda$ in vacuum are in phase initially. Then the first ray travels a path $l_{1}$ through a medium of refractive index $n_{1}$ while the second ray travels a path of length $l_{2}$ through a medium of refractive index $n_{2}$. The two waves are then combined to observe interference. The phase difference between the two waves is
questions.solution:
Optical path for ray $1=n_{1} l_{1}$ Optical path for ray $2=n_{2} l_{2}$ Phase difference,
$$
\Delta \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Delta x=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\left(n_{1} l_{1}-n_{2} l_{2}\right)
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential of the electric field produced by point charge at any point $(x, y, z)$ is given by $\mathrm{V}=3 \mathrm{x}^{2}+5$, where $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}$ are in metres and $\mathrm{V}$ is in volts. The intensity of the electric field at $(-2,1,0)$ is
questions.solution:
Intensity of the electric field, $E=\frac{d V}{d x}=6 x$
Potential $(\mathrm{v})=3 \mathrm{x}^{2}+5$
$\therefore$ E at $x=-2$
$$
=6(-2)=-12 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: On moving a charge of $20 \mathrm{C}$ by $2 \mathrm{~cm}, 2 \mathrm{~J}$ of work is done, then the potential difference between the points is
questions.solution:
Potential difference between two points in an electric field
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{W}{q_{0}}$
where $\mathrm{W}$ is work done by moving charge $\mathrm{q}_{0}$ from point $\mathrm{A}$ to $\mathrm{B}$
Here, $\mathrm{W}=2 \mathrm{~J}, \mathrm{q}_{0}=20 \mathrm{c}$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{2}{20}=0.1 \text { volt }$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bar magnet is $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ long is kept with its north (N)-pole pointing north. A neutral point is formed at a distance of $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ from each pole. Given the horizontal component of earth's field is $0.4$ Gauss, the pole strength of the magnet is
questions.solution:
Here, length ofmagnet $=10 \mathrm{~cm}=10 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$, $r=15 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$
Since, at the neutral point, magnetic field due to the magnet is equal to $B_{H}$, $$ \begin{array}{l} B_{H}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{M}{\left(O P^{2}+A O^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \\ 0.4 \times 10^{-4}=10^{-7} \times \frac{M}{\left(200 \times 10^{-4}+25 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{3 / 2}} \\ \Rightarrow \frac{0.4 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-7}} \times\left(225 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{3 / 2}=M \\ \Rightarrow 0.4 \times 10^{3} \times 10^{-6}(225)^{3 / 2}=M \\ \Rightarrow M=1.35 \mathrm{~A}-\mathrm{m} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An infinitely long thin straight wire has un iform linear charge density of $\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$. Then, the magnitude of the electric intensity at a point $18 \mathrm{~cm}$ away is $\left(\right.$ given $\left.\varepsilon_{0}=8.8 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^{2} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sample of $\mathrm{HCl}$ gas is placed in an electric field $3 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. The dipole moment of each $\mathrm{HCl}$ molecule is $6 \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{~cm}$. The maximum torque that can act on a molecule is
questions.solution:
Here
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{E}=3 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{NC}^{-1} \\
\mathrm{p}=6 \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{Cm} \\
\Gamma_{\max }=?
\end{array}$
We have,
torque acting on a dipole $\Gamma=\mathrm{pE} \sin \theta$
The maximum value of $\Gamma$, i.e. $\Gamma_{\max }=\mathrm{pE}$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore \Gamma_{\max } &=\left(3 \times 10^{4} \times 6 \times 10^{-30}\right) \mathrm{Nm} \\
&=18 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{Nm}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A certain radioactive material $_{Z} X^{\Lambda}$ starts emitting $\alpha$ and $\beta$ particles successively such that the end product is $\mathrm{Z}-3 \mathrm{Y}^{\mathrm{A}-8}$. The number of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ particles emitted are
questions.solution:
Let thenumber of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ particles emitted be $\mathrm{m}$ and $\mathrm{n}$ respectively. Then $\mathrm{A}-4 \mathrm{~m}=\mathrm{A}-8 \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}=2$
$[\because$ the mass number of a radioactive nuclide decreases by 4 due to emission of one $\alpha$ particle]
Again,
$(Z-2 m)+n=Z-3$
$[\because$ the atomic number decreases by 2 due to emission of $1 \alpha$-particle but increases by 1 due to emission of $1 \beta$-particle]
or $\quad-2 \mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{n}=-3$
or $\quad 2 \mathrm{~m}-\mathrm{n}=3$
or $\quad(2 \times 2)-\mathrm{n}=3 \quad(\because \mathrm{m}=2)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{n}=1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a metallic plate swings between the poles of a magnet
options: eddy currents are set up inside the plate and the direction of the current is along the motion of the plate
eddy currents are set up inside the plate and the direction of the current oppose the motion of the plate
eddy currents are set up inside the plate
questions.solution: When a metallic plate swings between the poles of a magnet, eddy currents are set up inside the plate. These currents set up their own magnetic field which opposes the magnetic field of the poles. Thus, the direction of the current opposes the motion of the plate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two point charges $4 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ are separated by a distance of $1 \mathrm{~m}$ in air. Then the distance of the point on the line joining the charges, where the resultant electric field is zero, is (in metre)
questions.solution: Let the point $\mathrm{P}$ where resultant field is zero be $x \mathrm{~m}$ from $4 \mu \mathrm{C}$ charge and $(1-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{m}$ distance apart from $-2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ charge. Since field is zero at this point then, $\begin{array}{l} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{1}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{2}=0 \\ \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}|=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \frac{\mathrm{q}_{1}}{\mathrm{r}_{1}^{2}}+\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \frac{\mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}_{2}^{2}} \\ \Rightarrow 0=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{4 \mu \mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}+\frac{(-2 \mu \mathrm{C})}{(1-\mathrm{x})^{2}}\right] \\ \Rightarrow \frac{4 \mu \mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}=\frac{2 \mu \mathrm{C}}{(1-\mathrm{x})^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{2}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}=\frac{1}{(1-\mathrm{x})^{2}} \\ \Rightarrow 2(1-\mathrm{x})^{2}=\mathrm{x}^{2} \end{array}$ Taking root $\sqrt{2}(1-x)=x$ $\begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow 1.414(1-x)=x \Rightarrow 1.414-1.414 x=x \\ \Rightarrow 1.414=(1+1.414) x \Rightarrow x=\frac{1.414}{2.414} \\ \Rightarrow x=0.58 \mathrm{~m} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform copper wire of length $1 \mathrm{~m}$ and crosssectional area $5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ carries a current of $1 \mathrm{~A}$. Assuming that there are $8 \times 10^{28}$ free electrons/ $\mathrm{m}^{3}$ in copper, how long will an electron take to drift from one end of the wire to the other
questions.solution:
Time taken by free electrons to cross the conductor
$t=\frac{\ell}{v_{d}}$ where drift velocity $v_{d}=\frac{I}{n e A}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{1}{8 \times 10^{28} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 5 \times 10^{-7}}$
$=\frac{10^{-2}}{64} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{d}}}=\frac{64}{10^{-2}}=64 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{~s}=6.4 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{sec}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Figure shows a triangular array of three point charges. The electric potential $\mathrm{V}$ of these source charges at the midpoint $\mathrm{P}$ of the base of the triangle is $\left[\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} \mathrm{C}^{-2}\right]$
questions.solution:
The net electric potential is algebraic sum of potential due to individual point charges.
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{V} &=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_{0}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{q}_{1}}{\mathrm{r}_{1}}+\frac{\mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}_{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{q}_{3}}{\mathrm{r}_{3}}\right] \\
\mathrm{V} &=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{0.2}-\frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.2}+\frac{3 \times 10^{-6}}{0.3}\right] \\
&=9 \times 10^{9}[5-10+10] \times 10^{-6} \\
&=9 \times 10^{3}[5]=45 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~V}=45 \mathrm{kV}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four equal charges $Q$ each are placed at four corners of a square of side a each. Work done in carrying a charge $-\mathrm{q}$ from its centre to infinity is
questions.solution:
At the centre $\mathrm{O}$ of the square due to four equal charges $\mathrm{q}$ at the corners, potential
$\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{V}=\frac{4 \mathrm{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(\mathrm{a} \sqrt{2}) / 2}=\frac{\sqrt{2} \mathrm{q}}{\pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{a}} \\ \therefore \mathrm{W}_{0 \infty}=-\mathrm{W}_{\infty 00} \\ =-(-\mathrm{q}) \mathrm{V}=\frac{\sqrt{2} \mathrm{q}^{2}}{\pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{a}} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical light sources $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ emit light of same wavelength $\lambda .$ These light rays will exhibit interference if
questions.solution: For inter ference phase difference must be constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, charges $+\mathrm{q}$ and $-\mathrm{q}$ are placed at the vertices $\mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ of an isosceles triangle. The potential at the vertex $\mathrm{A}$ is
questions.solution:
Potential at A
$=+\frac{q}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}}-\frac{q}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}}=0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A proton of mass $1.67 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$ enters a uniform magnetic field $1 \mathrm{~T}$ of at point $\mathrm{A}$ shown in figure with a speed of $10^{7} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$.
The magnetic field is directed normal to the plane of paper downwards. The proton emerges out of the magnetic field at point $C$, then the distance $\mathrm{AC}$ and the value of angle $\theta$ will respectively be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical P-N junctions are connected in series in three different ways as shown below to a battery. The potential drop across the P-N junctions are equal in
questions.solution: If the p-n junctions are identical, then their resistances would be same. In circuit 1 , the first p-n function is forward biased and second is reverse biased. Hence the voltages across them would be different. In circuit 2 both are reverse biased. So potential drop would be same. In circuit 3 both are forward biased; again voltages would be same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The insulation property of air breaks down at $3 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$. The maximum charge that can be given to a sphere of diameter $5 \mathrm{~m}$ is nearly

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A planet revolves in an elliptical orbit around the sun. The semi-major and semi-minor axes are a and $b$. Then the square of time period, $T$ is directly proportional to
questions.solution:
The square of the period of planet is directly proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of the elliptical orbit.
$$
\Rightarrow T^{2} \propto a^{3}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two particles of equal mass $m$ go round a circle of radius $\mathrm{R}$ under the action of their mutual gravitational attraction. The speed of each particle is
questions.solution:
From given condition
$\frac{\mathrm{Gmm}}{(2 \mathrm{R})^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}, \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{Gm}}{4 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{Gm}}{\mathrm{R}}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The distance of the centres of moon and earth is D. The mass of earth is 81 times the mass of the moon. At what distance from the centre of the earth, the gravitational force will be zero?
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { ( } \frac{G m_e}{x^2}=\frac{G m_m}{(D-x)^2} \\ & \text { or } \frac{G(81 m)}{x^2}=\frac{m}{(D-x)^2} \\ & \therefore \quad x=\frac{9 D}{10} . \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If gravitational attraction between two points masses be given by $F=G \frac{m_1 m_2}{r^n}$, then the period of a satellite in a circular orbit will be proportional to
questions.solution:
$\frac{m v^2}{r}=\frac{G m_1 m_2}{r^n}$ or $v \propto r^{\left(\frac{1-n}{2}\right)}$
Now, $T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}$
or $T \propto \frac{r}{V}$
$\therefore \quad T \propto \frac{r}{r^{\left(\frac{1-n}{2}\right)}}$
$T \propto r^{\frac{1+n}{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The gravitational potential at a place varies inversely with $x^{2}\left(\right.$ i.e., $\left.V=k / x^{2}\right)$, the gravitational field at that place is
questions.solution:
Gravitational intensity,
$I=-\frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{k}{x^{2}}\right)=\frac{2 k}{x^{3}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The distance of the centres of Moon and the Earth is $D$. The mass of the Earth is 81 times the mass of the Moon. At what distance from the centre of the Earth, the gravitational force on a particle will be zero?
questions.solution:
Force will be zero at the point of zero intensity.
$\begin{aligned} & x=\frac{\sqrt{m_1}}{\sqrt{m_1}+\sqrt{m_2}} d \\ & \text { = } \frac{\sqrt{81 M}}{\sqrt{81 M}+\sqrt{M}} D=\frac{9}{10} D\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body is projected vertically upwards from the surface of the earth with a velocity equal to half the escape velocity. If $R$ is the radius of the earth, maximum height attained by the body from the surface of the earth is
questions.solution:
Here, maximum height attained by a projectile
$$
h=\frac{v^{2} R}{2 g R-v^{2}}...(i)
$$
Velocity of body $=$ half the escape velocity
i.e., $v=\frac{v_{e}}{2}$
or $v=\frac{\sqrt{2 g R}}{2} \Rightarrow v^{2}=\frac{2 g R}{4} \Rightarrow v^{2}=\left(\frac{g R}{2}\right)$
Now, putting value of $v^{2}$ in Eq. (i), we get
Height, $h=\frac{\frac{g R}{2} \cdot R}{2 g R-\frac{g R}{2}}=\frac{\frac{g R^{2}}{2}}{\frac{3 g R}{2}}=\frac{R}{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Escape velocity when a body of mass $\mathrm{m}$ is thrown vertically from the surface of the earth is $\mathrm{v}$, what will be the escape velocity of another body of mass $4 \mathrm{~m}$ is thrown vertically
questions.solution: Escape velocity does not depend upon the mass of the body.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $\bar{v}, v_{\mathrm{rms}}$ and $v_{p}$ represent the mean speed, root mean square and most probable speed of the molecules in an ideal monoatomic gas at temperature $T$ and if $m$ is mass of the molecule, then
options: no molecule can have a speed greater than $\sqrt{2} v_{r m s}$
no molecule can have a speed less than $v_{p} / \sqrt{2}$
questions.solution:
Mean speed, $\overline{\mathrm{v}}=\sqrt{\frac{8 \mathrm{kT}}{\pi \mathrm{m}}}=0.92 \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}}$
$\mathrm{rms}$ speed, $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{kT}}{\mathrm{m}}}$
Most probable speed $v_{p}$
$=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{kT}}{\mathrm{m}}}=0.816 \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}}$
i.e., $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{p}} < \overline{\mathrm{v}} < \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $50 \Omega$ and $100 \Omega$ resistors are connected in series. This connection is connected with a battery of $2.4 \mathrm{~V}$. When a voltmeter of $100 \Omega$ resistance is connected across $100 \Omega$ resistor, then the reading of the voltmeter will be
questions.solution:
Equivalent resistance of the circuit
$$
R_{e q}=100 \Omega
$$
Current through the circuit, $i=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{2.4}{100} \mathrm{~A}$
Potential difference across combination of voltmeter and $100 \Omega$ resistance
$$
=\frac{2.4}{100} \times 50=1.2 \mathrm{~V}
$$
Since the voltmeter and $100 \Omega$ resistance are in parallel, the voltmeter reads the same value i.e., $1.2 \mathrm{~V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The molar specific heats of an ideal gas at constant pressure and volume are denoted by $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}$, respectively. If $\gamma=\frac{C_{p}}{C_{v}}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ is the universal gas constant, then $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}$ is equal to

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Variation of internal energy with density of one mole of monoatomic gas is depicted in the below figure, corresponding variation of pressure with volume can be depicted as (assuming the curve is rectangular hyperbola)
questions.solution:
$U \propto \frac{1}{\rho}$ or $T \propto \frac{1}{(1 / V)}$
$\therefore T \propto V$
Hence, $p=$ constant
Further $\rho$ is increasing, therefore $V$ should decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a gas mixture contains 2 moles of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ and 4 moles of Ar at temperature $T$, then what will be the total energy of the system (neglecting all vibrational modes)
questions.solution:
Total energy
$\mathrm{U}=2\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}_{1}}{2} \mathrm{RT}\right)+4\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}_{2}}{2} \mathrm{RT}\right)$
For $\mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{n}=5$ and for $\mathrm{Ar}, \mathrm{n}_{2}=3$
$\therefore \mathrm{U}=2\left(\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{RT}\right)+4\left(\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{RT}\right)=11 \mathrm{RT}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Graph of specific heat at constant volume for a monatomic gas is
questions.solution: For a monatomic gas $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{R}$ So correct graph is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The root mean square velocity of hydrogen molecules at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ is $1930 \mathrm{metre} / \mathrm{sec}$. Then the r.m.s velocity of oxygen molecules at $1200 \mathrm{~K}$ will be
questions.solution:
Root-mean square-velocity is given by
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}} \text { i.e., } \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}} \propto \sqrt{\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{M}}\right)} \\
& \frac{\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}}\right) \mathrm{O}_2}{\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}}\right) \mathrm{H}_2}=\sqrt{\left[\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{\mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{H}_2}} \times \frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{H}_2}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}\right]} \\
& =\sqrt{\left[\left(\frac{1200}{300}\right) \times\left(\frac{2}{32}\right)\right]}=\frac{1}{2} \\
& \therefore \quad\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}}\right) \mathrm{O}_2=\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}}\right) \mathrm{H}_2 \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1930}{2}=
\end{aligned}
$$
965m/s

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ resting on a horizontal surface is connected by a cord, passing over a light frictionless pulley to a hanging block of mass $5 \mathrm{~kg}$. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the surface is $0.5$. Tension in the cord is : $\left(\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}^{2}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle tied to a string describes a vertical circular motion of radius \(r\) continually. If it has a velocity \(\sqrt{3 \mathrm{gr}}\) at the highest point, then the ratio of the respective tensions in the string holding it at the highest and lowest points is
questions.solution:
Tension at the highest point
\(\mathrm{T}_{\text {top }}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}}-\mathrm{mg}=2 \mathrm{mg}\left(\therefore \mathrm{v}_{\text {top }}=\sqrt{3 \mathrm{gr}}\right)\)
Tension at the lowest point
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{T}_{\text {bottom }}=2 \mathrm{mg}+6 \mathrm{mg}=8 \mathrm{mg} \\
& \therefore \frac{\mathrm{T}_{\text {top }}}{\mathrm{T}_{\text {bottom }}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{mg}}{8 \mathrm{mg}}=\frac{1}{4} .
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following is a self adjusting force?
questions.solution: Static friction is a self adjusting force in magnitude and direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the adjoining figure, the position time graph of a particle of mass $0.1 \mathrm{~kg}$ is shown. The impulse at $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is
questions.solution:
From the graph we can say, upto $\mathrm{t}=2.0 \mathrm{~s}$, the body moves with a constant velocity
Slope of position-time graph $=\frac{4}{2}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
After $\mathrm{t}=2.0 \mathrm{~s}$, position-time graph is parallel to time axis i.e., body comes to rest.
$\therefore$ Change in velocity $=\mathrm{dx}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Impulse $=$ Change in momentum
$=\mathrm{mdv}=0.1 \times 2=0.2 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: On a smooth plane surface (figure) two block A and B are accelerated up by applying a force 15 $\mathrm{N}$ on A. If mass of B is twice that of A, the force on B is
questions.solution:
The acceleration of both the blocks =
$$
\frac{15}{3 x}=\frac{5}{x}
$$
$\therefore$ Force on $\mathrm{B}=\frac{5}{\mathrm{x}} \times 2 \mathrm{x}=10 \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which figure shows the correct force acting on the body sliding down an inclined plane? (\(\mathrm{m} \rightarrow\) mass, \(\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}} \rightarrow\) force of friction)
questions.solution: If a body slides down, then the force of friction acts upwards along the plane weight(\(\mathrm{mg})\) act vertically downwards.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If \(\mu_{\mathrm{s}}, \mu_{\mathrm{k}}\) and \(\mu_{\mathrm{r}}\) are coefficients of static friction, sliding friction and rolling friction, then
questions.solution: \(\mu_{\mathrm{s}} > \mu_{\mathrm{k}} > \mu_{\mathrm{r}}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Identify the correct statement.
options: Static friction depends on the area of contact
Kinetic friction depends on the area of contact
Coefficient of kinetic friction is more than the coefficient of static friction
Coefficient of kinetic friction is less than the coefficient of static friction
questions.solution: $\mu_{\mathrm{k}} < \mu_{\mathrm{s}}$ coefficient of static friction is always greater than kinetic friction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The resistance of a metal increases with increasing temperature because
options: the collisions of the conducting electrons with the electrons increase
the collisions of the conducting electrons with the lattice consisting of the ions of the metal increase
the number of conduction electrons decreases
the number of conduction electrons increases
questions.solution: In a conductor, the charge carriers are electrons. As the temperature is increased, the collisions of these conduction electrons with the fixed ions of the lattice of the metal increases and hence the resistance of the conductor also increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A marble block of mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ lying on ice when given a velocity of $6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ is stopped by friction in $10 \mathrm{~s}$. Then the coefficient of friction is (Take $\mathrm{g}=$ $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ )
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{a}=\mu \mathrm{g}=\frac{6}{10} \quad[$ using $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{u}+\mathrm{at}]$ 
$\Rightarrow \mu=\frac{6}{10 \times \mathrm{g}}=\frac{6}{10 \times 10}=0.06$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two persons $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are located in $\mathrm{X}$ - $\mathrm{Y}$ plane at the points $(0,0)$ and $(0,10)$ respectively. (The distances are measured in MKS unit). At a time $\mathrm{t}=0$, they start moving simultaneously with velocities $\quad \vec{v}_{a}=2 \hat{j} m s^{-1} \quad$ and $\quad \vec{v}_{b}=2 \hat{i} m s^{-1}$ respectively. The time after which $A$ and $B$ are at their closest distance is
questions.solution:
Let after the time $(t)$ the position of $A$ is $\left(0, v_{A} t\right)$ and position of $B=\left(v_{B} t, 10\right)$. Distance between them
$y=\sqrt{\left(0-v_{B} t\right)^{2}+\left(v_{A} t-10\right)^{2}}$ or $y^{2}=(2 t)^{2}+(2 t-10)^{2}$ or $y^{2}=l=4 t^{2}+4 t^{2}+100-40 t$ $\Rightarrow l=8 t^{2}+100-40 t$ Now, $\frac{d l}{d t}=(16 t-40)=0$
or
$$
t=\frac{40}{16}=2.5 \mathrm{~s} \quad \because \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} l}{d t^{2}}=16=(+\mathrm{ve})
$$
So, $l$ will be minimum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A positively charged particle moving due east enters a region of uniform magnetic field directed vertically upwards. The particle will
options: move in a circular orbit with its speed unchanged
move in a circular orbit with its speed increased
questions.solution: In a perpendicular magnetic field, the path of a charged particle is a circle, and the magnetic field does not cause any change in energy.

questions.subject: Physics
