questions.q: Which of the following graph represents the variation of magnetic flux density $B$ with distance $r$ for a straight long wire carrying an electric current?
questions.solution:
Magnetic field density $B$ due to long straight current carrying conductor at a distance $r$ is given as
$B=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{i}{r}$
i.e.
$B \propto \frac{1}{r}$
Hence, option (3) is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The figure shows three situations when an electron with velocity \(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}\) travels through a uniform magnetic field \(\vec{B}\). In each case, what is the direction of magnetic force on the electron?
questions.solution:
According to Fleming's Right hand Rule In (1) force is in \(-z\) axis In (2) force is in - \(x\)-axis
In (3) force is zero because
\(\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{q}(\overline{\mathrm{v}} \times \overline{\mathrm{B}})=\mathrm{qvB} \sin 180\)
i.e. \(F=0\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work done by an uniform magnetic field, on a moving charge is
questions.solution:
Force on moving charge while moving in magnetic field is; $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=\mathrm{q}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})$ where $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}$ is perpendicular to $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}}$.
Work done/sec $=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{Fv} \cos 90^{\circ}=0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic field at a distance $r$ from a long wire carrying current i is $0.4$ tesla. The magnetic field at a distance $2 \mathrm{r}$ is
questions.solution:
Magnetic field due to long wire,
$$
B=\frac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi r} \text { or } B \propto \frac{1}{r}
$$
When $r$ is doubled, the magnetic field becomes half, i.e., now the magnetic field will be $0.2 \mathrm{~T}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A straight wire of diameter \(0.5 \mathrm{~mm}\) carrying a current of \(1 \mathrm{~A}\) is replaced by another wire of \(1 \mathrm{~mm}\) diameter carrying same current. The strength of magnetic field far away is
questions.solution: The current is same in both the wire, hence magnetic field induced will be same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A current carrying conductor experiences maximum force in magnetic field when the angle between current element and direction of magnetic field is
questions.solution:
Force on a current carrying conductor is given as
$F=BiL \sin \theta$
For maximum force,
$\begin{array}{rlrl} & & \sin \theta & =1=\sin 90^{\circ} \\ \therefore \quad & \theta & =90^{\circ}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge $\mathrm{q}$ is moving with a velocity $\mathrm{v}$ parallel to a magnetic field $B$. Force on the charge due to magnetic field is
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current sensitivity of a moving coil galvanometer depends on
options: the number of turns in the coil
moment of inertia of the coil
questions.solution:
Current sensitivity $=\frac{\mathrm{nBA}}{\mathrm{K}}$ 
where $\mathrm{K}$ is constant of torsional rigidity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A square loop, carrying a steady current $I$, is placed in a horizontal plane near a long straight $\quad$ conductor carrying a steady current $\mathrm{I}_{1}$ at a distance d from the conductor as shown in figure.
The loop will experience
options: a net repulsive force away from the conductor
a net torque acting upward perpendicular to the horizontal plane
a net torque acting downward normal to the horizontal plane
a net attractive force towards the conductor
questions.solution: $\mathrm{F}_{1}>\mathrm{F}_{2}$ as $\mathrm{F} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{~d}}$, and $\mathrm{F}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{F}_{4}$ are equal and opposite. Hence, the net attraction force will be towards the conductor. Heat gain $=$ heat lost

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steel wire of length $\ell$ has a magnetic moment M. It is then bent into a semicircular arc. The new magnetic moment is
questions.solution:
Let pole strength $=\mathrm{m}$
So, $\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{m} \ell$
When wire is in form of arc, then the distance between poles $=\frac{2 \ell}{\pi}$
So, $M^{\prime}=\frac{m 2 \ell}{\pi}=\frac{2 M}{\pi}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting circular loop of radius $r$ carries a constant current $i .$ It is placed in a uniform magnetic field $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{0}$ such that $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{0}$ is perpendicular to the plane of the loop. The magnetic force acting on the loop is
questions.solution:
The magnetic field is perpendicular to the plane of the paper. Let us consider two diametrically opposite elements. By Fleming's Left hand rule on element AB the direction of force will be Leftwards and the magnitude will be $d F=\mid d l B \sin 90^{\circ}=I d l B$
On element $\mathrm{CD}$, the direction of force will be towards right on the plane of the papper and the magnitude will be $\mathrm{dF}=\mathrm{Id} / \mathrm{B}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A current I flows in the anticlockwise direction through a square loop of side a lying in the xoy plane with its center at the origin. The magnetic induction at the center of the square loop is
questions.solution:
Field due to one side of loop at $O$ $$ =\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)}\left(2 \sin 45^{\circ}\right) $$ Field at $O$ due to all four sides is along unit vector $\hat{e}_{z}$
$\therefore$ Total field $=4 \cdot \frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)}\left(2 \sin 45^{\circ}\right)=\frac{2 \sqrt{2} \mu_{0} I}{\pi a}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of charge $\mathrm{q}$ and mass $\mathrm{m}$ moves in a circular orbit of radius $r$ with angular speed $\omega$. The ratio of the magnitude of its magnetic moment to that of its angular momentum depends on
questions.solution:
The angular momentum $\mathrm{L}$ of the particle is given by
$L=m r^{2} \omega$ where $\omega=2 \pi n$
$\therefore$ Frequency $n=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}$;
Further $i=q \times n=\frac{\omega q}{2 \pi}$
Magnetic moment, $M=i A=\frac{\omega q}{2 \pi} \times \pi r^{2}$;
$\therefore M=\frac{\omega q r^{2}}{2}$
So, $\frac{M}{L}=\frac{\omega q r^{2}}{2 m r^{2} \omega}=\frac{q}{2 m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the magnetic field at $\mathrm{P}$ due to the arrangement shown
questions.solution:
$B=2\left[\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{i}{d \cos 45^{\circ}}\left\{\sin \left(-45^{\circ}\right)+\sin 90^{\circ}\right\}\right]$
$=\frac{\mu_{0} i}{\sqrt{2} \pi d}\left[-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+1\right] . \otimes$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A long straight wire of radius $R$ carries current $i$. The magnetic field inside the wire at distance $r$ from its centre is expressed as :
questions.solution:
Using Ampere's law, we have
$\begin{array}{l} \oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{\ell}=\mu_{0} i_{i n} \\ \text { or } \quad B \times 2 \pi r=\mu_{0} \frac{i}{\pi R^{2}} \pi r^{2} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{i r}{R^{2}} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron of mass $9.0 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$ under the action of a magnetic field moves in a circle of radius $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ at a speed of $3 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If a proton of mass $1.8 \times 10^{27} \mathrm{~kg}$ was to move in a circle of same radius in the same magnetic field, then its speed will become

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A current of $2 \mathrm{~A}$ is flowing in the sides of an equilateral triangle of side $9 \mathrm{~cm}$. The magnetic field at the centroid of the triangle is
questions.solution:
Due to current through side AB Magnetic field at the centre $\mathrm{O}$ $\mathrm{B}_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{a}}\left[\sin \theta_{1}+\sin \theta_{2}\right]$
As the magnetic field due to each of the three sides is the same in magnitude and direction. $\therefore \quad$ Total magnetic field at $\mathrm{O}$ is sum of all the fields. i.e. $\mathrm{B}=3 \mathrm{~B}_{1}=\frac{3 \mu_{0} \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{a}}\left[\sin \theta_{1}+\sin \theta_{2}\right]$ Here, $\tan \theta_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{AD}}{\mathrm{OD}} \Rightarrow \tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{\frac{\ell}{2}}{\mathrm{a}}$ $\Rightarrow \quad a=\frac{\ell}{2 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \sqrt{3}}$ Now B $\begin{aligned} &=3 \times \frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{4 \pi \times \frac{9 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \sqrt{3}}}\left[\sin 60^{\circ}+\sin 60^{\circ}\right] \\ =& \frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{9} \times 10^{-5}\left[\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right] \\ =& 1.33 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The direction of magnetic field $\mathbf{d B}$ due to current element $\mathbf{d l}$ at a distance $\mathbf{r}$ is the direction of
questions.solution: The direction of $\mathrm{dB}$ is the direction of vector $\mathrm{dl} \times \mathrm{r}$. From right hand screw rule, if we place a right handed screw at the point where the magnetic field is needed to be determined and turn its handle from dl to $\mathrm{r}$, then the direction in which the screw advances gives the direction of field $\mathrm{dB} .$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron moves at right angle to a magnetic field of $5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~T}$ with a speed of $6 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If the specific charge of the electron is $1.7 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{kg}$. The radius of the circular path will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular current carrying coil has a radius $\mathrm{R}$. The distance from the centre of the coil on the axis, where the magnetic induction will be $\frac{1}{8}$ th to its value at the centre of the coil is
questions.solution:
Here the ratio, $\frac{\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{Centre}}}{\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{axis}}}=\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2}$
Also, $B_{\text {axis }}=\frac{1}{8} B_{\text {centre }}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{8}{1}=\left(1+\frac{x^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)^{3 / 2} \Rightarrow 4=1+\frac{x^{2}}{R^{2}} \\
3=\frac{x^{2}}{R^{2}} \Rightarrow X^{2}=3 R^{2} \\
\text { or, } x=\sqrt{3} R
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic field at \(P\) on the axis of a solenoid having 100 turns/metre and carrying a current of \(5 \mathrm{~A}\) is:
questions.solution:
Given: \(\mathrm{n}=100\) turns \(/ \mathrm{m}\) \(\mathrm{I}=5 \mathrm{~A}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{B} & =\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{nI}}{2}\left(\sin \phi_1+\sin \phi_2\right) \\
\mathrm{B} & =\frac{\mu_0 \times 100 \times 5}{2}\left(\sin 45^{\circ}+\sin 45^{\circ}\right) \\
& =\frac{\mu_0 \times 500}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \\
& =\frac{\mu_0 \times 500}{2} \times \frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}=\mu_0 250 \sqrt{2} \\
& =250 \sqrt{2} \mu_0
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a current I is flowing in a loop of radius $\mathrm{r}$ as shown in adjoining figure, then the magnetic field induction at the centre $\mathrm{O}$ will be
questions.solution:
Magnetic field $\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{r}}$
Here, $2 \pi=\theta$
$\therefore \mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{\theta I}{I}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic field at a point due to a current carrying conductor is directly proportional to
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A straight section $P Q$ of a circuit lies along the $X$-axis from $x=-\frac{a}{2}$ to $x=\frac{a}{2}$ and carries a steady current $i$. The magnetic field due to the section $P Q$ at a point $X=+a$ will be
questions.solution: Magnetic field at a point on the axis of a current carrying wire is always zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solenoid of length 0.4 m and having 500 turns of wire carries a current of $3 \mathrm{~A}$. A thin coil having 10 , turns of wire and of radius 0.01 m carries a current of 0.4 A. The torque required to hold the coil in the middle of the solenoid with its axis perpendicular to the axis of solenoid.
questions.solution:
Magnetic field inside the solenoid,
$\begin{aligned} B & =\mu_0 n l \\ & =\mu_0 \frac{N}{1} \cdot 1 \\ \Rightarrow B & =4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \frac{500}{0.4} \times 3\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Torque,
$\begin{aligned} \tau= & I A B N \\ = & 3 \times\left(\pi r^2\right) \times\left(\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 500 \times 3}{0.4}\right) \times 500 \\ = & 3 \times \frac{22}{7} \times\left(0.01 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 \\ & \times \frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 500 \times 3}{0.4} \times 500 \\ = & 5.92 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{m}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square loop, carrying a steady current $\mathrm{I}$, is placed in horizontal plane near a long straight conductor carrying a steady current $I_{1}$ at a distance of $\mathrm{d}$ from the conductor as shown in figure. The loop will experience
options: a net repulsive force away from the conductor
a net torque acting upward perpencicular to the hor izontal plane
a net torque acting downward normal to the hor izontal plane
a net attractive force towards the conductor
questions.solution: $$ \begin{array}{l} \mathrm{F}_{2}=-\mathrm{F}_{4} \\ \mathrm{~F}_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{I} l}{2 \pi \mathrm{d} l} \\ \mathrm{~F}_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}_{1} \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi(\mathrm{d}+\mathrm{l})} \\ \mathrm{F}_{1}>\mathrm{F}_{3} \\ \mathrm{~F}_{\text {net }}=\mathrm{F}_{1}-\mathrm{F}_{3} \end{array} $$ So, wire attract loop.

questions.q: In producing chlorine through electrolysis $100 \mathrm{~W}$ power at $125 \mathrm{~V}$ is being consumed. How much chlor ine per minute is leberated? (ECE of chlor ine is $0.367 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{C}$ )

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle carrying a charge to 100 times the charge on an electron is rotating per second in a circular path of radius $0.8 \mathrm{~m}$. The value of the magnetic field produced at the centre will be $\left(\mu_{0}=\right.$ permeabil ity for vacuum $)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The earth is considered as a short magnet with its centre coinciding with the geometric centre of earth. The angle of $\operatorname{dip} \phi$ related to the magnetic latitude $\alpha \lambda$ as
questions.solution:
For a dipole at position $(\mathrm{R}, \mathrm{Q})$ $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \mathrm{M} \cos \theta}{\mathrm{R}^{3}}$ ...(i) and $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{M} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{R}^{3}}$ ...(ii) Also $\tan \phi=\frac{\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{V}}}{\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{H}}}=-\frac{\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{R}}}{\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{Q}}}$ ...(iii)
Dividing eq. (i) by (ii) $\frac{\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{R}}}{\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{Q}}}=\frac{2 \cos \theta}{\sin \theta}=2 \cot \theta$ From eq. (iii) and (iv) $\tan \phi=-2 \cot \theta$ From figure, $\theta=90^{\circ}+\lambda$ $\therefore \quad \tan \phi=-2 \cot (90+\lambda)$ $\tan \phi=2 \tan \lambda$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field is $3.6 \times 10^{-5}$ tesla where the dip angle is $60^{\circ} .$ The magnitude of the earth's magnetic field is
questions.solution:
Horizontal component of earth's field, $\mathrm{H}=\mathrm{B} \cos \theta$, since,$\theta=60^{\circ}$
$3.6 \times 10^{-5}=\mathrm{B} \times \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{B}=7.2 \times 10^{-5} \text { Tesla }$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a magnet is suspended at angle $30^{\circ}$ to the magnet meridian, the dip of needle makes angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal, the real dip is
questions.solution: Here, $\tan \delta^{\prime}=\frac{\tan \delta}{\cos \theta}=\frac{\tan 45^{\circ}}{\cos 30^{\circ}}$ $\tan \delta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3} / 2}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$ or $\delta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The dipole moment of the short bar magnet is $12.5 \mathrm{~A}-\mathrm{m}^{2} .$ The magnetic field on its axis at a distance of $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ from the centre of the magnet is
questions.solution:
The magnetic field, $\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{~d}^{3}}$
$$
=10^{-7} \times \frac{2 \times 1.25}{(0.5)^{3}}=2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{A}-\mathrm{m}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bar magnet of magnetic moment $\mathrm{M}$, is placed in a magnetic field of induction B. The torque exerted on it is
questions.solution: $\tau=\mathrm{MB} \sin \theta=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{M}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A magnetic needle lying parallel to the magnetic field requires $W$ units of work to turn it through an angle $45^{\circ} .$ The torque required to maintain the needle in this position will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The incorrect statement regarding the lines of force of the magnetic field $\mathrm{B}$ is
options: magnetic intensity is a measure of lines of force passing through unit area held normal to it
magnetic lines of force forms a close curve
inside a magnet, its magnetic lines of force move from north pole of a magnetic towards its south pole
due to a magnetic lines of force never cut each other
questions.solution: Inside a magnet, magnetic lines of force move from south pole to north pole.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A short bar magnet of magnetic moment of $0.15 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{T}$ is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.4 T . The potential energy of the magnet in unstable equilibrium is
questions.solution:
Magnetic potential of a bar magnet in external magnetic field is given by
$U=-M B \cos \theta$
Here, $M=0.15 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{T}, B=0.4 \mathrm{~T}$,
and $\theta=180^{\circ}$ as magnet is in unstable equilibrium.
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad U & =-0.15 \times 0.4 \times \cos 180^{\circ} \\ & =-0.15 \times 0.4 \times(-1) \\ & =6 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two indentical magnetic dipoles of magnetic moment $1.0 \mathrm{Am}^{2}$ each,placed at a separation of $2 \mathrm{~m}$ with their axes perpendicular to each other. The resultant magnetic field at a point midway between the dipoles is
questions.solution:
Since axes are perpendicular so mid point lies on axial line of one magnet and on equitorial line of other magnet
$\therefore \mathrm{B}_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{d}^{3}}=\frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times 1}{1^{3}}=2 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T}$ and $\mathrm{B}_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{d}^{3}}=\frac{10^{-7} \times 1}{1^{3}}=10^{-7} \mathrm{~T}$
As $\mathrm{B}_{1} \perp \mathrm{B}_{2}$
$\therefore$ Resultant magnetic field
$=\sqrt{\mathrm{B}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{B}_{2}^{2}}=\sqrt{5} \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment, the spacing between the slits is $d$ and wavelength of light used is $6000 Å$. If the angular width of a fringe formed on a distance screen is $1^{\circ}$, then value of $d$. is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the Bohr model of a hydrogen atom, the centripetal force is furnished by the coulomb attraction between the proton and the electron. If $a_{0}$ is the radius of the ground state orbit, $m$ is the mass and e is charge on the electron and $\varepsilon_{0}$ is the vacuum permittivity, the speed of the electron is
questions.solution:
Centripetal force $=$ force of attraction of nucleus on electron.
$$
\frac{m v^{2}}{a_{0}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{e^{2}}{a_{0}^{2}} \Rightarrow v=\frac{e}{\sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} m a_{0}}}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The two lenses of an achromatic doublet should have
options: equal ratio of their power and dispersive power
sum of the product of their powers and dispersive power equal to zero
questions.solution: The two lenses of an achromatic doublet should have, sum of the product of their powers and dispersive power $=$ zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two bar magnets A and B are placed one over the other and are allowed to vibrate in a vibration magnetometer. They make 20 oscillations per minute when the similar poles of A and B are on the same side, while they make 15 oscillations per minute when their opposite poles lie on the same side. If $M_{A}$ and $M_{B}$ are the magnetic moments of $A$ and $B$ and if $M_{A}>M_{B}$, the ratio of $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}$ is
questions.solution:
Ratio of magnetic moments
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{M_{A}}{M_{B}} &=\frac{T_{d}^{2}+T_{s}^{2}}{T_{d}^{2}-T_{s}^{2}}=\frac{v_{s}^{2}+v_{d}^{2}}{v_{s}^{2}-v_{d}^{2}} \\
&=\frac{\left(\frac{1}{20}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{15}\right)^{2}}{\left(\frac{1}{15}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{20}\right)^{2}}=\frac{400+225}{400-225} \\
M_{A}: & M_{B}=25: 7
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Radar waves are sent towards a moving airplane and the reflected waves are received. When the airplane is moving towards the radar, the wavelength of the wave
questions.solution:
By Dopplers effect, if source moves towards the observer, frequency received will increase.
As $v \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}$
$\therefore$ wavelength will decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A neutral water molecule $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$ in its vapour state has an electric dipole moment of magnitude $6.4 \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{m}$. How far apart are the molecules centres of positive and negative charges?
questions.solution:
In a neutral water molecule, there are 10 electrons and 10 protons.
So, its dipole moment $p=q(2 l)=10 e(2 l)$ Hence length of the dipole $=$ distance between centres of positive and negative charges
$$
2 l=\frac{p}{10 e}=\frac{6.4 \times 10^{-30}}{10 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=4 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature coefficient of resistance of a wire is $0.00125 / \mathrm{K}$. At $300 \mathrm{~K}$ its resistance is $1 \Omega$. The resistance of the wire will be $2 \Omega$ at
questions.solution:
Temperature coefficient, $\alpha=\frac{R_{2}-R_{1}}{R_{1}\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right)}$
$\Rightarrow\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right)=\frac{R_{2}-R_{1}}{\alpha R_{1}}$
$\Rightarrow t_{2}-t_{1}=\frac{2-1}{1 \times 0.00125}$
$t_{2}=\frac{1}{0.00125}+t_{1}=800+300=1100 \mathrm{~K}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A Zener diode has a contact potential of $1 \mathrm{~V}$ in the absence of biasing. It undergoes Zener breakdown for an electric field of $10^{6} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$ at the depletion region of $\mathrm{p}-\mathrm{n}$ junction. If the width of the depletion region is $2.5 \mu \mathrm{m}$, what should be the reverse biased potential for the Zener breakdown to occur ?
questions.solution:
Here, electricfield, $E=10^{6} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$ width of depletion region,
$\mathrm{d}=2.5 \mu \mathrm{m}=2.5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore$ Potential required for breakdown $(\mathrm{V})=\mathrm{Ed}=\left(10^{6} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6}\right) \mathrm{V}=2.5 \mathrm{~V}$ Contact Potential $=1.0 \mathrm{~V}$
$\therefore$ Reverse biased potential for zener breakdown $=(2.5-1.0) \mathrm{V}=1.5 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal ring is held horizontally and bar magnet is dropped through the ring with its length along the axis of the ring. The acceleration of the falling magnet
options: depends on the diameter of ring and length of magnet
questions.solution: As the magnet falls, the magnetic flux linked with the ring increases. This induces emf in the ring which opposes the motion of the falling magnet, so $\mathrm{a} < \mathrm{g}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the work done in turning a magnet of magnetic moment $M$ by an angle of $90^{\circ}$ from the magnetic meridian is n times the corresponding work done to turn it through an angle of $60^{\circ}$, then the value of $n$ is
questions.solution:
Wehave, $\mathrm{W}=-\mathrm{MB}\left(\cos \theta_{2}-\cos \theta_{1}\right)$
So, $\mathrm{W}_{1}=-\mathrm{MB}\left(\cos 90^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right)=\mathrm{MB}$
and $\mathrm{W}_{2}=-\mathrm{MB}\left(\cos 60^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MB}$
As $W_{1}=n W_{2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{W}_{1}}{\mathrm{~W}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{MB}}{\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MB}}=2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following statement related to hysteresis loop is incorrect?
options: The curve of $\mathrm{B}$ against $\mathrm{H}$ for $\mathrm{a}$ ferromagnetic material is called hysteresis loop
The area of $B$ - $H$ curve is a measure of power dissipated per cycle per unit area of the specimen
Coercitivity is a measure of the magnetic field required to destroy the residual magnetism of ferromagnetic material
The retentivity of a specimen is the measure of magnetic field remaining in the specimen when the magnetising field is removed
questions.solution: The hysteresis loop i.e. area of B-H curve is a measure of energy dissipated per cycle per unit volume of the specimen. It depends on the nature of magnetic material.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $\mathrm{ABCD}$ is a rectangle. At corners $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{D}$ of the rectangle are placed charges $+10 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}$, $-20 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}$ and $10 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}$ respectively. Calculate the potential at the fourth corner. The side $\mathrm{AB}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $\mathrm{BC}=3 \mathrm{~cm}$
questions.solution: $A C=\sqrt{4^{2}+3^{2}}=\sqrt{25}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ $\therefore$ Potential at $\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}+\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}+\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{D}}$ $\therefore \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_{0}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{q}_{1}}{\mathrm{AB}}+\frac{\mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{AC}}+\frac{\mathrm{q}_{3}}{\mathrm{AD}}\right)$ $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=9 \times 10^{9}\left(\frac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{4 \times 10^{-2}}+\frac{-20 \times 10^{-12}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}+\frac{10 \times 10^{-12}}{3 \times 10^{-2}}\right)$ $=9 \times 10^{9} \times 10^{-10}\left(\frac{10}{4}+\frac{-20}{5}+\frac{10}{3}\right)$ $=0.9\left(\frac{150-240+200}{60}\right)$ $=\frac{0.9 \times 110}{60}=1.65 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The resultant of $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ is $\mathbf{R}_1$. On reversing the vector $\mathbf{B}$, the resultant becomes $\mathbf{R}_2$. What is the value of $R_1^2+R_2^2$ ?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & R_1^2=A^2+B^2+2 A B \cos \theta \\ & R_2^2=A^2+B^2-2 A B \cos \theta \\ & \therefore R_1^2+R_2^2=2\left(A^2+B^2\right)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The pressure on a square plate is measured by measuring the force on the plate. If the maximum error in the measurement of force and length are respectively $4 \%$ and $2 \%$, then the maximum error in the measurement of pressure is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\text { Pressure }=\frac{\text { force }}{\text { area }}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{L}^{2}} \\
\therefore \frac{\Delta \mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{F}}+\frac{2 \Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}=4 \%+2(2 \%)
\end{array}$
or percentage error, $=8 \%$\

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a car lift, compressed air exerts a force $F_1$ on a small piston having a radius of 5.0 cm . This pressure is transmitted to a second piston of radius 10.0 cm . If the mass of the car to be lifted is 1350 kg . The force on a small piston is
questions.solution:
Here, $r_1=\frac{5}{100} \mathrm{~m}$,
$F_2=1350 \mathrm{~kg}=1350 \times 9.8 \mathrm{~N}, r_2=\frac{10}{100} \mathrm{~m}$
According to Pascal law, As, $\frac{F_1}{A_1}=\frac{F_2}{A_2}$ or $F_1=\frac{A_1}{A_2} F_2=\frac{\pi r_1^2}{\pi r_2^2} F_2$
or $F_1=\frac{r_1^2}{r_2^2} F_2=\frac{\left(\frac{5}{100}\right)^2}{\left(\frac{10}{100}\right)^2} \times 1350 \times 9.8=1470 \mathrm{~N}$

It is independent of the size of orifice.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The velocity of water in a river is $18 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$ near the surface. If the river is $5 \mathrm{~m}$ deep, find the shearing stress between the horizontal layers of water. The co-efficient of viscosity of water $=10^{-2}$ poise.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A large number of liquid drops each of radius $\mathrm{r}$ coalesce to from a single drop of radius $\mathrm{R}$. The energy released in the process is converted into kinetic energy of the big drop so formed. The speed of the big drop is (given, surface tension of liquid $\mathrm{T}$, density $\rho$ )
questions.solution:
When drops combine to form a single drop of radius $\mathrm{R}$.
Then energy released,
$$
\mathrm{E}=4 \pi \mathrm{TR}^{3}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}\right]
$$
If this energy is converted into kinetic energy then
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}=4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{3} \mathrm{~T}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}\right] \\
\frac{1}{2} \times\left[\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}^{3} \rho\right] \mathrm{v}^{2}=4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{3} \mathrm{~T}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}\right] \\
\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{6 \mathrm{~T}}{\rho}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}\right]}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Bernoulli's theorem which of the following is conserved?
questions.solution: In Bernoulli's theorem only law of conservation of energy is obeyed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A lead shot of $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ diameter falls through a long column of glycerine. The variation of the velocity with distance covered (s) is correctly represented by
questions.solution: In the beginning due to gravity pull, the lead shot will be accelerated and hence will move, with increasing velocity for some time When the viscous force balance the gravity pull, then the shot will move with constant velocity. As in the beginning, the velocity of shot is not fully linear with the effective distance covered by the shot.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A long glass capillary tube is dipped in water. It is known that water wets glass. The water level rises by $h$ in the tube. The tube is now pushed down so that only a length $h / 2$ is outside the water surface. The angle of contact at the water surface at the upper end of the tube will be
questions.solution:
Here, $\mathrm{h}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~s} \cos 0^{\circ}}{\mathrm{r} \rho \mathrm{g}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~s}}{\mathrm{r} \rho \mathrm{g}}$
According to question,
$\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~s} \cos \theta^{\prime}}{\mathrm{rpg}}$
Dividing eq. (ii) by (i) we get,
$\frac{1}{2}=\cos \theta^{\prime}$
or $\theta^{\prime}=60^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Zener diode is used for
questions.solution: Zener diode is suitable for voltage regulating purpose. It is used as voltage stabilizer in many applications in electronics.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The maximum velocity of electrons emitted from. a metal surface is $\mathrm{v}$, when frequency of light falling on it is $f$. The maximum velocity when frequency becomes $4 \mathrm{f}$ is
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation,
$$
E=W_{0}+\frac{1}{2} m v_{\max }^{2} \Rightarrow v_{\max }=\sqrt{\frac{2\left(h f-W_{0}\right)}{m}}
$$
If frequency becomes $4 f$ then
$$
\begin{array}{l}
v^{\prime}=\sqrt{\frac{2\left(h \times 4 f-W_{0}\right)}{m}}=2 \sqrt{\frac{2\left(h f-\frac{W_{0}}{4}\right)}{m}} \\
\Rightarrow v^{\prime}>2 v
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A soap bubble of radius $r$ is blown up to form a bubble of radius $2 r$ under isothermal conditions. If $\mathrm{T}$ is the surface tension of soap solution, the energy spent in the blowing
questions.solution:
Initially area of soap bubble, $A_{1}=4 \pi r^{2}$ Under isothermal condition radius becomes $2 r$.
$\therefore$ Area $A_{2}=4 \pi(2 r)^{2}=16 \pi r^{2}$
Increase in surface area
$$
\Delta A=2\left(A_{2}-A_{1}\right)=2\left(16 \pi r^{2}-4 \pi r^{2}\right)=24 \pi r^{2}
$$
Energy spent,
$$
W=T \times \Delta A=T \cdot 24 \pi r^{2}=24 \pi T r^{2} \mathrm{~J}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two beams of light will not give rise to an interference patern, if
options: they are linearly polarized perpendicular to each other
questions.solution:
Two beams of light give rise to an interference pattern if they are coherent, they have same wavelength/frequency in same phase or having a constant phase difference and same state of polarisation.
Interference pattern can not be obtained if the two beams are not mono chromatic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Indicate which one of the following statements is NOT CORRECT?
options: Intensities of reflections from different crystallographic planes are equal
According to Bragg's law higher order of reflections have high $\theta$ values for a given wavelength of radiation
For a given wavelength of radiation there is a smallest distance between the crystallographic planes which can be determined
Bragg's law may predict a reflection from a crystallographic plane to be present but it may be absent due to the crystal symmetry
questions.solution: Bragg's law gives $2 \mathrm{dsin} \theta=\mathrm{n} \lambda, \mathrm{n}=$ order of reflection, $\mathrm{d}=$ distance between planes. For same $\lambda$ and $\mathrm{d}, \mathrm{n} \propto \theta ;$ for a given $\lambda$, smallest $\mathrm{d}$ for least $\mathrm{n}, \theta$ can be found. If crystal is symmetric reflections from different planes may cancel out.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the total emf in a thermocouple is a parabolic function expressed as $\mathrm{E}=$ at $+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{bt}^{2}$, which of the following relations does not hold good
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{at}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{bt}^{2}$ is the parabolic equation for thermo emf.
The thermoelectric power is
$\mathrm{S}=\frac{\mathrm{dE}}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{S}=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{bt}$. The graph between $\mathrm{S}\left(=\frac{\mathrm{dE}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)$ and straight line.
When $\mathrm{t}=0, \mathrm{~S}=\mathrm{a}$ (intercept).
At neutral temperature $\frac{\mathrm{dE}}{\mathrm{dt}}=0$ and 
$\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{n}} \Rightarrow 0=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{bt}_{\mathrm{n}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{-\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}$ and at cold junction $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{i}}=2 \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{-2 \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{b}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Kerosene oil rises up in a wick of a lantern because of
options: diffusion of the oil through the wick
the gravitational pull of the wick
questions.solution: Kerosene oil rises up in wick of a lantern because of capillary action. If the surface tension of oil is zero, then it will not rise, so oil rises up in a wick of a lantern due to surface tension.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two mercury drops (each of radius $\mathrm{r}$ ) merge to form a bigger drop. The surface energy of the bigger drop, if $\mathrm{T}$ is the surface tension, is
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{R}$ be the radius of the bigger drop, then Volume of bigger drop $=2 \times$ volume of small drop
$$
\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=2 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=2^{1 / 3} r
$$
Surface energy of bigger drop,
$$
\mathrm{E}=4 \pi R^{2} T=4 \times 2^{2 / 3} \pi r^{2} T=2^{8 / 3} \pi r^{2} T
$$

questions.q: Two liquids of densities \(d_1\) and \(d_2\) are flowing in identical capillary tubes uder the same pressure difference. If \(t_1\) and \(t_2\) are time taken for the flow of equal quantities (mass) of liquids, then the ratio of coefficient of viscosity of liquids must be
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the terminal speed of a sphere of gold (density $=19.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ ) is $0.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in a viscous liquid (density $=1.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ ), find the terminal speed of a sphere of silver (density $=10.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ ) of the same size in the same liquid

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Eight spherical rain drops of the same mass and radius are falling down with a terminal speed of $6 \mathrm{~cm}-\mathrm{s}^{-1}$. If they coalesce to form one big drop, what will be the terminal speed of bigger drop? (Neglect the buoyancy of the air)
questions.solution:
Let radius of big drop $=R$.
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=\frac{4}{3} \times \pi r^{3} \cdot 8 \\
R=2 r
\end{array}
$$
Here $r=$ radius of small drops.
Now, terminal velocity of drop in liquid
$$
v_{T}=\frac{2}{9} \times \frac{r^{2}}{\eta}(\rho-\sigma) g
$$
where $\eta$ iscoefficient of viscosity and $\rho$ is density of drop $\sigma$ is density of liquid. Term inal speed drop is $6 \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
$$
\therefore 6=\frac{2}{9} \times \frac{r^{2}}{\eta}(\rho-\sigma) g...(i)
$$
Let terminal velocity becomes $v^{\prime}$ after coalesce, then
$$
v^{\prime}=\frac{2}{9} \frac{R^{2}}{\eta}(\rho-\sigma) g...(ii)
$$
Dividing Eq. (i) by (ii), we get
$$
\frac{6}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{\frac{2}{9} \frac{r^{2}}{\eta}(\rho-\sigma) g}{\frac{2}{9} \frac{R^{2}}{\eta}(\rho-\sigma) g} \Rightarrow \frac{6}{v^{\prime}}=\frac{r^{2}}{(2 r)^{2}}
$$
$$
\text { or } v^{\prime}=24 \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A liquid is allowed to flow into a tube of truncated cone shape. Identify the correct statement from the following.
options: The speed is high at the wider end and low at the narrow end.
The speed is low at the wider end and high at the narrow end.
The speed is same at both ends in a stream line flow.
The liquid flows with uniform velocity in the tube.
questions.solution:
The theorem of continuity is valid. $$ \therefore \mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{1} \rho=\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{v}_{2} \rho $$ As the density of the liquid can be taken as uniform.
$\therefore \mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{1}=\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{v}_{2}$ $\Rightarrow$ Smaller the area, greater the velocity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The Young's modulus of a perfectly rigid body is
questions.solution: For a perfectly rigid body strain produced is zero for the given force applied, so $Y=$ stress $/$ strain $=$ Infinite $(\infty)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two wires $A$ and $B$ are of the same material. Their lengths are in the ratio of $1: 2$ and the diameter are in the ratio $2: 1$. If they are pulled by the same force, then increase in length will be in the ratio of
questions.solution:
We know that Young's modulus
$$
\mathrm{Y}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\pi \mathrm{r}^2} \times \frac{\mathrm{L}}{\ell}
$$
Since Y, F are same for both the wires, we have,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_1^2} \frac{\mathrm{L}_1}{\ell_1}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_2^2} \frac{\mathrm{L}_2}{\ell_2} \quad \text { or, } \frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2}=\frac{\mathrm{r}_2^2 \times \mathrm{L}_1}{\mathrm{r}_1^2 \times \mathrm{L}_2}= \\
& \frac{\left(\mathrm{D}_2 / 2\right)^2 \times \mathrm{L}_1}{\left(\mathrm{D}_1 / 2\right)^2 \times \mathrm{L}_2} \\
& \text { or, } \frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2}=\frac{\mathrm{D}_2^2 \times \mathrm{L}_1}{\mathrm{D}_1^2 \times \mathrm{L}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{D}_2^2}{\left(2 \mathrm{D}_2\right)^2} \times \frac{\mathrm{L}_2}{2 \mathrm{~L}_2}=\frac{1}{8} \\
& \text { So, } \ell_1: \ell_2=1: 8
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.solution:
Stress $=\frac{\text { Force }}{\text { Area }} \therefore$ Stress $\propto \frac{1}{\pi r^{2}}$
$$
\frac{S_{B}}{S_{A}}=\left(\frac{r_{A}}{r_{B}}\right)^{2}=(2)^{2} \Rightarrow S_{B}=4 S_{A}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An iron rod of length $2 \mathrm{~m}$ and cross-sectional area of $50 \mathrm{~mm}^{2}$ stretched by $0.5 \mathrm{~mm}$, when a mass of $250 \mathrm{~kg}$ is hung from its lower end. Young's modulus of iron rod is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the ratio of lengths, radii and Young's modulus of steel and brass wires shown in the figure are a, b and c respectively, the ratio between the increase in lengths of brass and steel wires would be
questions.solution:
Given, $\frac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}=a, \frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=b, \frac{Y_{1}}{Y_{2}}=c$ Free body diagram of the two blocks brass and steel are
Let Young's modulus of steel is $Y_{1}$ and of brass is $Y_{2}$. $\therefore Y_{1}=\frac{F_{1} \cdot l_{1}}{A_{1} \cdot \Delta l_{1}}...(i)$ and $Y_{2}=\frac{F_{2} \cdot l_{2}}{A_{2} \cdot \Delta l_{2}}...(ii)$ Dividing Eq. (i) by(ii), $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{Y_{1}}{Y_{2}}=\frac{\frac{F_{1} \cdot l_{1}}{A_{1} \cdot \Delta l_{1}}}{\frac{F_{2} \cdot l_{2}}{A_{2} \cdot \Delta l_{2}}} \\ \text { or } \frac{Y_{1}}{Y_{2}}=\frac{F_{1} \cdot A_{2} \cdot l_{1} \cdot \Delta l_{2}}{F_{2} \cdot A_{1} \cdot l_{2} \cdot \Delta l_{1}}...(ii) \end{array} $$ Force on steel wire from free body diagram $T=F_{1}=(2 g)$ newton Force on brass wire from free body diagram $F_{2}=T^{\prime}=T+2 g=(4 g)$ newton Now, putting the value of $F_{1}, F_{2}$, in Eq. (iii), we get $$ \frac{Y_{1}}{Y_{2}}=\left(\frac{2 g}{4 g}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{\pi r_{2}^{2}}{\pi r_{1}^{2}}\right) \cdot\left[\frac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}\right] \cdot\left(\frac{\Delta l_{2}}{\Delta l_{1}}\right) $$ or $c=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{b^{2}}\right) \cdot a\left(\frac{\Delta l_{2}}{\Delta l_{1}}\right)$ or $\frac{\Delta l_{1}}{\Delta l_{2}}=\left(\frac{a}{2 b^{2} c}\right)$

Distance $=$ half of circumference of circle $=$ $\pi$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The velocity-time graph of a body moving in a straight line is shown in fig. Find the displacement and distance travelled by the body in 10 seconds.
questions.solution: Total distance covered in $10 \mathrm{~s}=$ Area $1+$ Area $2+$ Area 3 $$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 20+\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 20+\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10=90 \mathrm{~m} $$ Total displacement in $10 \mathrm{~s}=$ Area $1-$ Area 2 + Area 3 $$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 20-\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 20+\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10=50 \mathrm{~m} $$

questions.q: The horizontal range of a projectile is $4 \sqrt{3}$ times of its maximum height. The angle of projection will be
questions.solution:
$R=4 \sqrt{3}$
$\begin{aligned} \frac{2 u^2 \sin \theta \cos \theta}{g} & =4 \sqrt{3}\left(\frac{u^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 g}\right) \\ \tan \theta & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\ \therefore \quad \theta & =30^{\circ}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person swims in a river aiming to reach exactly on the opposite point on the bank of a river. His speed of swimming is $0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at an angle of $120^{\circ}$ with the direction of flow of water. The speed of water is
questions.solution: Let the speed of water $=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}}$ Speed of swimmer $=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$ Angle between $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}}$ is $120^{\circ} .$ Then $\sin \theta=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}}}{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{u}}{0.5}=\frac{1}{2}$ or $\mathrm{u}=0.25 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body is projected vertically upwards at time $t=0$ and it is seen at a height $H$ at time $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$ second during its flight. The maximum height attained is ( $g$ is acceleration due to gravity)
questions.solution:
Let $t^{\prime}$ be the time taken by the body to fall from point $C$ to $B$. Then $t_{1}+2 t^{\prime}=t_{2} \Rightarrow t^{\prime}=\left(\frac{t_{2}-t_{1}}{2}\right)$...(i)
Total time taken to reach point $C$ $$ \begin{aligned} T &=t_{1}+t^{\prime}=t_{1}+\frac{t_{2}-t_{1}}{2} \\ &=\frac{2 t_{1}+t_{2}-t_{1}}{2}=\left(\frac{t_{1}+t_{2}}{2}\right) \end{aligned} $$ Maximum height attained $$ \begin{array}{c} H_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} g(T)^{2}=\frac{1}{2} g\left(\frac{t_{1}+t_{2}}{2}\right)^{2} \\ =\frac{1}{2} g \cdot \frac{\left(t_{1}+t_{2}\right)^{2}}{4} \\ \text { Or, } H_{\max }=\frac{1}{8} g \cdot\left(t_{1}+t_{2}\right)^{2} m \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two bodies of same mass are projected with the same velocity at an angle $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ respectively. The ratio of their horizontal ranges will be
questions.solution: Horizontal range is same when angle of projection is $\theta$ or $\left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Half-lives of two radioactive substances \(\mathrm{A}\) and \(B\) are respectively 20 minutes and 40 minutes. Initially, the sample of A and \(B\) have equal number of nuclei. After 80 minutes, the ratio of remaining number of \(A\) and \(B\) nuclei is
questions.solution:
No. of half lives \(\mathrm{n}_1=\frac{80}{20}=4\);
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{n}_2=\frac{80}{40}=2 \\
& \frac{\mathrm{N}_1}{\mathrm{~N}_{0 \mathrm{~A}}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}_1}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^4=\frac{1}{16} \Rightarrow \text { for } \mathrm{A} \\
& \frac{\mathrm{N}_2}{\mathrm{~N}_{0 \mathrm{~B}}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}_2}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \text { for } \mathrm{B} \\
& \frac{\mathrm{N}_1}{\mathrm{~N}_2}=\frac{1}{16} \times \frac{4}{1}=\frac{1}{4} \quad\left(\because \mathrm{N}_{0 \mathrm{~A}}=\mathrm{N}_{0 \mathrm{~B}}\right)
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The binding energy of deuteron \(\left({ }_1^2 \mathrm{H}\right)\) is 1.15 \(\mathrm{MeV}\) per nucleon and an alpha particle \(\left({ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}\right)\) has a binding energy of \(7.1 \mathrm{MeV}\) per nucleon. Then in the reaction
\({ }_1^2 \mathrm{H}+{ }_1^2 \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow{ }_2^2 \mathrm{He}+\mathrm{Q}\)
the energy released \(Q\) is:
questions.solution:
Given, \({ }_1 \mathrm{H}^2+{ }_1 \mathrm{H}^2 \rightarrow{ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4+\mathrm{Q}\)
The total binding energy of the deutrons
\(=4 \times 1.15=4.60 \mathrm{MeV}\)
The total binding energy of alpha particle
\(=4 \times 7.1=28.4 \mathrm{MeV}\)
The energy released in the process
\(=28.4-4.60=23.8 \mathrm{MeV} \text {. }\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The half life for $\alpha$-decay of uranium ${ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{228}$ is $4.47 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{yr}$. If a rock contains $60 \%$ of original ${ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{228}$ atoms, then its age is [take $\log 6=0.778, \log 2=0.3$ ]
questions.solution:
Given: $\mathrm{T}_{1 / 2}=4.47 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{yr}$
$\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_{0}}=\frac{60}{100}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}} \Rightarrow 2^{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{10}{6}$
Apply logarithm on both sides
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{n} \log 2=\log 10-\log 6 \\
\Rightarrow & \mathrm{n} \times 0.3=1-0.778=0.22 \\
\Rightarrow & \mathrm{n}=\frac{0.222}{0.3}=0.74
\end{aligned}$
So, $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{nT}_{1 / 2}=0.74 \times 4.47 \times 10^{8}$ or, $\mathrm{t}=3.3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{yr}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the nuclear radius of ${ }^{27} \mathrm{Al}$ is $3.6$ Fermi, the approximate nuclear radius of ${ }^{64} \mathrm{Cu}$ in Fermi is
questions.solution:
Nuclear radius, $r \propto A^{1 / 3}$ where $A$ is mass number
$$
\frac{r_{\mathrm{Cu}}}{r_{\mathrm{Al}}}=\left(\frac{A_{\mathrm{Cu}}}{A_{\mathrm{Al}}}\right)^{1 / 3}=\left(\frac{64}{27}\right)^{1 / 3}
$$
$$
\Rightarrow r_{\mathrm{Cu}}=3.6\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)=4.8 \mathrm{Fermi}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of volume occupied by an atom to the volume of the nucleus is
questions.solution:
As we knows, radius of an atom,
$\gamma_{\mathrm{A}} \approx 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$
radius of nucleus, $\gamma_{\mathrm{B}} \approx 10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}$
So, ratio of their volumes
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{N}}}=\frac{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{A}}^{3}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{N}}^{3}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{N}}}\right)^{3}=\left(\frac{10^{-10}}{10^{-15}}\right)^{3} \\
\therefore \quad & \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}: \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{N}}=10^{15}: 1
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A nuclear transformation is given by $Y(n, \alpha) \rightarrow{ }_{3} \mathrm{Li}^{7}$. The nucleus of element $Y$ is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Y}(\mathrm{n}, \alpha)$ the nucleus splits into $\alpha$-particle and neutrons
i.e. $\mathrm{Z}^{\mathrm{Y}}{ }^{\mathrm{A}}+{ }_{0} \mathrm{n}^{1} \longrightarrow{ }_{3} \mathrm{~L}_{\mathrm{i}}^{7}+{ }_{2} \mathrm{He}^{4}$
So $\mathrm{A}+1=7+4 \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}=10$
and $Z+0=3+2$ or $Z=5$
Hence, the nucleus of element $Y$ is boron ${ }_{5} \mathrm{Y}^{10}={ }_{5} \mathrm{~B}^{10}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The activity of a radioactive sample is measured as $N_{0}$ counts per minute at $t=0$ and $N_{0} / \mathrm{C}$ counts per minute at $t=5 \mathrm{~min}$. The time, (in minute) at which the activity reduces to half its value, is
questions.solution:
After $\mathrm{n}$ half-lives
$\begin{array}{c}
\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_{0}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{t} / 7} \\
\mathrm{~N}=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{0}}{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{\mathrm{c} \mathrm{N}_{0}}{\mathrm{cN}_{0}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{5 / 7} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{e}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{5 / 7}
\end{array}$
Taking log on both sides, we get
$\log 1-\log \mathrm{e}=\frac{5}{7} \log \frac{1}{2}$
$\begin{array}{l}
-1=\frac{5}{7}(-\log 2) \\
\mathrm{T}=5 \log _{\mathrm{e}} 2
\end{array}$
Now, let $\mathrm{t}$ ' be the time after which activity reduces to half
$\begin{array}{l}
\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{1 / 5 \log _{\mathrm{e}} 2} \\
\mathrm{t}^{\prime}=5 \mathrm{olog}_{\mathrm{e}} 2
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A neutron is moving with velocity $u$. It collides head on and elastically with an atom of mass number $A$. If the initial kinetic energy of the neutron is $\mathrm{E}$, then how much kinetic energy will be retained by the neutron after reflection?
questions.solution:
Fraction retained by nucleus
$\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{k}}\right)_{\text {retained }}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}_{2}-\mathrm{m}_{1}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}-1}{\mathrm{~A}+1}\right)^{2}$ After collision kinetic energy retained by neutron $\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}-1}{\mathrm{~A}+1}\right)^{2} \mathrm{E}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical conductor of radius $R$ carries a current i. The value of magnetic field at a point which is $\frac{\mathrm{R}}{4}$ distance inside from the surface is $10 \mathrm{~T}$. The value of magnetic field at point which is $4 \mathrm{R}$ distance outside from the surface
questions.solution:
Magnetic field inside the cyclindrical conductor $B_{\text {in }}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 i r}{R^{2}}$
$(R=$ radius of cylinder and $r=$ distance of observation point from axis of cylinder) Magnetic field outside the cylinder at a
distance $r^{\prime}$ from its axis, $B_{\text {out }}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 i}{r^{\prime}}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \frac{B_{\mathrm{in}}}{B_{\mathrm{out}}}=\frac{r r^{\prime}}{R^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{10}{B_{\mathrm{out}}}=\frac{\left(R-\frac{R}{4}\right)(R+4 R)}{R^{2}} \\
\Rightarrow B_{\mathrm{out}}=\frac{8}{3} T
\end{array}
$$

questions.q: Silver has a work function of $4.7 \mathrm{eV}$. When ultraviolet light of wavelength $100 \mathrm{~nm}$ is incident upon it, potential of $7.7 \mathrm{~V}$ is required to stop photoelectrons from reaching the collector plate. The potential required to stop electrons when light of wavelength $200 \mathrm{~nm}$ is incident upon silver is
questions.solution:
Given: $\lambda=100 \mathrm{~nm}=1000 Å$
Energy corresponding to $1000 Å$
$=\frac{12375}{1000}=12.375 \mathrm{eV}$ Now, 7.7 $=12.375-\phi_{0}$
or $\phi_{0}=12.375-7.7=4.675 \mathrm{eV}$
In the second case,
Energy corresponding to $2000 Å$
$$
=\frac{12375}{2000} \mathrm{eV}=6.1875 \mathrm{eV}
$$
Now, $4.7=6.1875-\phi_{0}^{\prime}$
or $\phi_{0}^{\prime}=6.1875-4.7=1.4875 \simeq 1.5 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.q:
The Binding energy per nucleon of ${ }_{3}^{7} \mathrm{Li}$ and ${ }_{2}^{4}$ He nuclei are $5.60 \mathrm{MeV}$ and $7.06 \mathrm{MeV}$, respectively.
In the nuclear reaction ${ }_{3}^{7} \mathrm{Li}+{ }_{1}^{1} \mathrm{H} \rightarrow 2{ }_{2}^{4} \mathrm{He}+\mathrm{Q}$, the value of energy $\mathrm{Q}$ released is :

BE of ${ }_{2} \mathrm{He}^{4}=4 \times 7.06=28.24 \mathrm{MeV}$
$\mathrm{BE}$ of $_{3}^{7} \mathrm{Li}=7 \times 5.60=39.20 \mathrm{MeV}$
${ }_{3}^{7} \mathrm{Li}+{ }_{1}^{1} \mathrm{H} \rightarrow{ }_{2} \mathrm{He}^{4}+{ }_{2} \mathrm{He}^{4}+\mathrm{Q}$
Therefore, $\mathrm{Q}=56.48-39.20=17.28 \mathrm{MeV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The radioactivity of a certain material drops to $\frac{1}{16}$ of the initial value in 2 hours. The half life of this radionuclide is
questions.solution:
We have, $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{t}}=\mathrm{N}_{0}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{T}_{1 / 2}}}$,
When
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{t}}=\text { number of atoms present after time t } \\
\mathrm{N}_{0}^{\mathrm{t}}=\text { initial number of atoms } \\
\mathrm{T}_{1 / 2}=\text { half life of the nuclide } \\
\therefore \frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{t}}}{\mathrm{N}_{0}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{T}_{1 / 2}}} \quad \text { or } \frac{1}{16}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{2}{\mathrm{~T}_{1 / 2}}} \\
\text { or }\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{4}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{2}{\mathrm{~T}_{1 / 2}}} \Rightarrow 4=\frac{2}{\mathrm{~T}_{1 / 2}} \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_{1 / 2}=\frac{2}{4} \mathrm{Hr}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Hr} \quad \therefore \mathrm{T}_{1 / 2}=30 \mathrm{~min}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The velocity $\mathrm{v}$, at which the mass of a particle is double its rest mass is
questions.solution:
Let the velocity of a particle be v where mass $\mathrm{m}$ is double the rest mass i.e., $\mathrm{m}=2 \mathrm{~m}_{0}$ then
$\mathrm{m}_{0}=\mathrm{m} \sqrt{1-\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{m}_{0}=2 \mathrm{~m}_{0} \sqrt{1-\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}}$
$\frac{1}{2}=\sqrt{1-\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4}=1-\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}=1-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{3}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{3}{4}} \mathrm{c}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assuming $\mathrm{f}$ to be the frequency of first line in Balmer series, the frequency of the immediate next (i.e. second) line is
questions.solution:
Balmer series is given for $\mathrm{n}_{1}=2$ and $\mathrm{n}_{2}=3,4, \ldots$ $v=\operatorname{Rc}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{2}^{2}}\right]$
For Ist line in spectrum $n_{2}=3$
$\begin{array}{l}
v_{1}=\operatorname{Rc}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right]=\operatorname{Rc}\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right]=\mathrm{f} \\
\Rightarrow f=\frac{5}{36} R c \Rightarrow R c=\frac{36}{5} \mathrm{f}
\end{array}$
For second line $\mathrm{n}_{2}=4$
$\begin{aligned}
v_{2}=\operatorname{Rc}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right] &=\operatorname{Rc}\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{16}\right] \\
&=\operatorname{Rc}\left[\frac{4-1}{16}\right]
\end{aligned}$
$v_{2}=\frac{3}{16} \mathrm{Rc}=\frac{3}{16} \times \frac{36}{5} \mathrm{f}=1.35 \mathrm{f}$

\frac{R}{R_{\text {He }}}=\left(\frac{A}{4}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}
\end{array}
$$
$$
(14)^{1 / 3}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{4}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}=56
$$
So, $=56-30=26$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is the age of an ancient wooden piece if it is known that the specific activity of $C^{14}$ nuclide in its amounts is $3 / 5$ of that in freshly grown trees? Given the half of C nuclide is $5570 \mathrm{yr}$.
questions.solution: Here,$$\mathrm{N}_{0}=\frac{3}{5} N_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$ $$ \begin{array}{l}e \lambda^{t}=\frac{5}{3} \\ \therefore \quad \log e^{\lambda t}=\log _{e} \frac{5}{3} \text { or } \lambda t=\log _{e} \frac{5}{3} \\ \text { or } \mathrm{t}=\frac{1}{\lambda} \log _{e} \frac{5}{3} \\ =\frac{T}{0.693} \times 0.5 \quad\left(\because T=\frac{0.693}{\lambda}\right) \\ =\frac{5570 \times 0.5}{0.693} y r=4018.7 y r s\end{array}

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin film of soap solution $(\mathrm{n}=1.4)$ lies on the top of a glass plate $(\mathrm{n}=1.5)$. When visible light is incident almost normal to the plate, two adjacent reflection maxima are observed at two wavelengths 420 and $630 \mathrm{~nm}$. The minimum thickness of the soap solution is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{R}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{2}$ are the two rays considered for interference. $R_{1}$ is the result of reflection at denser medium; hence it suffers an additional path difference of $\frac{\lambda}{2}$. Ray $R_{2}$ originates after reflection at narer medium. Net path difference $=2 \mu \mathrm{t}+\frac{\lambda}{2}$ where $t$ is the thickness of the soap solution. For constructive interference, $2 \mu \mathrm{t}+\frac{\lambda}{2}=\mathrm{m} \lambda$, where $\mathrm{m}=0,1,2 \ldots . .$ Let the two adjacent reflection maxima be observed at $\mathrm{m}$ and $\mathrm{m}-1$. Then $2 \mu \mathrm{t}+\frac{\lambda_{1}}{2}=\mathrm{m} \lambda_{1}$ or $2 \mu \mathrm{t}=\left(\mathrm{m}-\frac{1}{2}\right) \lambda_{1}$ ...(1) Similarly, $2 \mu \mathrm{t}=\left(\mathrm{m}-\frac{3}{2}\right) \lambda_{2}$ ...(2) Solving (1) \& (2), we get
$2 \mu \mathrm{t}=\left(\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}-1}\right)$ Putting, $\lambda_{1}=420 \mathrm{~nm}, \lambda_{2}=630 \mathrm{~nm}, \mu=1.4$ We get, $t=450 \mathrm{~nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two point like charges Q1 and Q2 of whose strength are equal in absolute value are placed at a certain distance from each other. Assuming the field strength to be posiive in the positive direction of $x$-axis the signs of the charges $Q 1$ and Q2 for the graphs (field strength versus distance) shown in Figures $1,2,3$ and 4 are
options: Q1 positive, Q2 negative; both positive; Q1 negative, Q2 positive; both negative
Q1 negative, Q2 positive;Q1 positive, Q2 negative; both positive; both negative
Q1 positive, Q2 negative; both negative; Q1 negative, Q2 positive; both positive
Both positive; Q1 positive, Q2 negative; Q1 negative, Q2 positive; both negative
questions.solution: For a point charge, $\mathrm{E} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}$. For positive charges, electric field will decrease in positive direction as distance increases, (case 1 ), for negative charge, as distance increases field will increase (case 4). As we move from positive charge to negative charge, field will keep on decreasing (case 2 ), As we move from negative to positive charge, field will keep on increasing (case 3 )

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential energy of a particle of mass 0.1 kg moving along the $X$-axis, is given by $U=5 x(x-4) \mathrm{J}$, where $x$ is in metres. Choose the wrong option.
options: The speed of the particle is maximum at $x=2 \mathrm{~m}$
The particle executes simple harmonic motion
The period of oscillation of the particle is $\frac{\pi}{5} \mathrm{~s}$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & U=5 x^2-20 x, \frac{d U}{d x}=10 x-20 \\ & F=-\frac{d U}{d x}=(-10 x+20)\end{aligned}$
Let us suppose, $x=(X+2)$. Then, $F=-10 X$, $F=0$ at $X=0$ or $x=2$, i.e. $x=2 \mathrm{~m}$ is the mean position about which particle is in SHM. $k=10$ $\therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{0.1}{10}}=\frac{\pi}{5} \mathrm{~s}$
At mean position kinetic energy is maximum and force acting on particle in SHM is variable.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The displacement of a particle executing SHM is given by
$y=5 \sin \left(4 t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$
If $T$ is the time period and the mass of the particle is $2 \mathrm{~g}$, the kinetic energy of the particle when $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{T} / 4$ is given by
questions.solution:
Particle executing SHM.
Displacement $y=5 \sin \left(4 t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)...(i)$
Velocity of particle
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left(\frac{d y}{d t}\right)=\frac{5 d}{d t} \sin \left(4 t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \\
=5 \cos \left(4 t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \cdot 4=20 \cos \left(4 t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)
\end{array}
$$
Velocity at $t=\left(\frac{T}{4}\right)$
$$
\left(\frac{d y}{d t}\right)_{t=\frac{T}{4}}=20 \cos \left(4 \times \frac{T}{4}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)
$$
or $\quad u=20 \cos \left(T+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)...(ii)$
Comparing the given equation with standard equation of SHM.
$y=a \sin (\omega t+\phi)$
We get, $\omega=4$
As $\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T} \Rightarrow T=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}$
or $T=\frac{2 \pi}{4}=\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
Now, putting value of $T$ in Eq. (ii), we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
u &=20 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=-20 \sin \frac{\pi}{3} \\
&=-10 \times \sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}
$$
The kinetic energy of particle,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{KE} &=\frac{1}{2} m u^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times(-10 \sqrt{3})^{2} \\
&=10^{-3} \times 100 \times 3=0.3 \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a particle executing S.H.M. the displacement $x$ is given by $x=A \cos \omega t$. Identify the graph which represents the variation of potential energy (P.E.) as a function of time $t$ and displacement $x$.
questions.solution: In $x=A \cos \omega t$, the particle starts oscillating from extreme position. So at $t=0$, its potential energy is maximum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the differential equation for a simple harmonic motion is $\frac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}+2 y=0$, the time-period of the motion is
questions.solution:
The differential equation of simple harmonic motion is
$$
\frac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}+2 y=0 \text { or } \frac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}=-2 y......(i)
$$
Standard equation of simple harmonic motion is
$$
\frac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}=-\omega^{2} y......(ii)
$$
Comparing eq. (i) and (ii), $\omega^{2}=2$ or $\omega=\sqrt{2}$
As we know, $\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}$
$\therefore$ Time period, $T=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=\frac{2 \pi}{\sqrt{2}}=\pi \sqrt{2} \mathrm{sec}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A light ray of wavelength 546 nm is incident on an air-glass interface. If the speed of light in air is $3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Then, wavelength of light ray in glass will be
[Take, refractive index of glass, $\mu=1.5$ ]
questions.solution:
Given, wavelength of light,
$\lambda=546 \mathrm{~nm}$
Refractive ve index, $\mu=1.5$
Speed of light, $c=3 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
Refractive index,
$\begin{aligned} \mu & =\frac{\text { Speed of light in vacuum (c) }}{\text { Speed of light in glass (v) }} \\ & =\frac{c}{v} \\ & =\frac{v \lambda}{v \lambda_g}\left[\lambda_g=\text { wavelength of light in glass }\right] \\ \mu & =\frac{\lambda}{\lambda_g} \Rightarrow \lambda_g=\frac{\lambda}{\mu} \\ & =\frac{546}{1.5}=364 \mathrm{~nm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A given ray of light suffers minimum deviation in an equilateral prism P. Additional prisms Q and R of identical shape and of same material as that of $\mathrm{P}$ are now combined as shown in figure. The ray will now suffer
questions.solution: When the ray suffers minimum deviation, it becomes parallel to the base of prism P. As prisms $\mathrm{Q}$ and $\mathrm{R}$ are of same material and have identical shape, therefore, the ray continues to be parallel to base of $Q$ and $R$. Hence final deviation of the ray remains the same as before.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A vessel of depth $2 \mathrm{~d} \mathrm{~cm}$ is half filled with a liquid of refractive index $\mu_{1}$ and the upper half with a liquid of refractive index $\mu_{2} .$ The apparent depth of the vessel seen perpendicularly is
questions.solution: Apparent depth $=\mathrm{d} / \mu_{1}+\mathrm{d} / \mu_{2}$.

questions.solution: $\mathrm{P}_{2}=\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{P}_{1}=\frac{100}{80}-\frac{100}{20}=-3.75 \mathrm{D}$

questions.solution: $P=2\left[\frac{100}{10}+\frac{100}{-20}+0\right]$ $P=10$ dioptre.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A fish looking up through the water sees the outside world contained in a circular horizon. If the refractive index of water is $4 / 3$ and the fish is $12 \mathrm{~cm}$ below the surface, the radius of this circle (in $\mathrm{cm}$ ) is
questions.solution:
$\sin i_{c}=\frac{1}{\mu}=\frac{r}{\sqrt{r^{2}+h^{2}}}$ Using $h=12 \mathrm{~cm}, \mu=4 / 3$
We get $\mathrm{r}=\frac{36}{\sqrt{7}} \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ray of light is incident on a plane mirror at an angle of $30^{\circ}$. At what angle with the horizontal must a plane mirror be placed so that the reflected ray becomes vertically upwards?
questions.solution:
When a light ray falls on a mirror at an angle $30^{\circ}$, then the reflected ray will make the same angle with the plane as shown in Fig. (i)
In order to make the reflected ray vertical, the mirror should be rotated at an angle of $60^{\circ}$. So, the mirror should be tilted by $\frac{60^{\circ}}{2}=30^{\circ}$ Fig. (ii)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A compound microscope having magnifying power 35 with its eye-piece of focal length 10 $\mathrm{cm}$. Assume that the final image is at least distance of distinct vision then the magnification produced by the objective is
questions.solution:
For a compound microscope, magnifying power
$\mathrm{MP}=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}} \times \mathrm{m}_{0}$
When the final image is at least distance of distance vision then
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{e}}=1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}} \\
\therefore \quad & \mathrm{MP}=\mathrm{m}_{0}\left[1+\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right] \\
\Rightarrow \quad &-35=\mathrm{m}_{0}\left[1+\frac{25}{10}\right] \\
\Rightarrow \quad &-35=\mathrm{m}_{0} \times 35 \\
\Rightarrow \quad & \mathrm{m}_{0}=-10
\end{aligned}$
The negative sign shows that the image formed by objective is inverted.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The refractive index for a prism is given as $\mu=\cot \frac{A}{2}$. Then, angle of minimum deviation in terms of angle of prism is
questions.solution:
Using prism formula,
$\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}+\delta_{\mathrm{m}}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)}$ ...(i)
where, $A=$ angle of prism
$\delta_{\mathrm{m}}=$ angle of minimum deviation
Given, $\mu=\cot \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)=\frac{\cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)}$
So, from Eq. (i)
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\cos \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}+\delta_{\mathrm{m}}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)} \\
\Rightarrow & \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)=\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}+\frac{\delta_{\mathrm{m}}}{2}\right) \\
\Rightarrow & \delta_{\mathrm{m}}=\pi-2 \mathrm{~A}=180^{\circ}-2 \mathrm{~A}
\end{aligned}$

questions.solution:
Lens formula, $\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}$
or $\frac{\mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{v}}-1=\frac{\mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{f}}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}}-1=\frac{\mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{f}}$ or $\frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}}=\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}\right) \mathrm{u}+1$
this is the equation of a straight line whose slope
$\frac{1}{f}-\frac{b}{c} \therefore 1-\frac{c}{b}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Four light sources produce the following four waves:
(i) $ y_{1}=a \sin \left(\omega t+\phi_{1}\right)$
(ii) $y_{2}=a \sin 2 \omega t$
(iii) $y_{3}=a \sin \left(\omega t+\phi_{2}\right)$
(iv) $y_{4}=a \sin (3 \omega t+\phi)$
Superposition of which two waves give rise to interference?
questions.solution:
Inteference takes place between two waves having equal frequency and propagate in same direction.
Hence, $y_{1}=a \sin \left(\omega t+\phi_{1}\right)$
$$
y_{3}=a^{\prime} \sin \left(\omega t+\phi_{2}\right)
$$ same frequency $\omega$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $\mathrm{A}$ wire $\mathrm{AB}$, in the shape of two semicircular segments of radius $R$ each and carrying a current I, is placed in a uniform magnetic field B directed into the page (see figure). The magnitude of the force due to the field $B$ on the wire $A B$ is
questions.solution:
Force on a conductor of length $\ell$ due to a magnetic field of strength $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$ is given by
$\begin{array}{l}
\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=\mathrm{I}(\vec{\ell} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}) \\
\therefore|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}|=\mathrm{I} \ell \mathrm{B} \sin \theta
\end{array}$
Here, $\theta=90^{\circ}$
$\therefore \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{I} \ell \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{I} \times \pi \mathrm{R} \times \mathrm{B}=\pi \mathrm{RIB}$
Therefore, a force of magnitude $(\pi R I B)$ will act on each wire. The direction of the forces on each wire will be same. Thus, total force on the wire $\mathrm{AB}=\pi \mathrm{RIB}+\pi \mathrm{RIB}$ $=2 \pi \mathrm{RIB}$

questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ray PQ incident on the refracting face BA is refracted in the prism BAC as shown in the figure and emerges from the other refracting face $\mathrm{AC}$ as $\mathrm{RS}$ such that $\mathrm{AQ}=\mathrm{AR}$. If the angle of prism $\mathrm{A}=60^{\circ}$ and the refractive index of the material of prism is $\sqrt{3}$, then the angle of deviation of the ray is
questions.solution: Given $A Q=A R$ and $\angle A=60^{\circ}$ $\therefore \quad \angle A Q R=\angle A R Q=60^{\circ}$ $\therefore \quad r_{1}=r_{2}=30^{\circ}$ Applying Snell's law on face $\mathrm{AB}$. $$ \begin{array}{l} \sin \mathrm{i}_{1}=\mu \sin \mathrm{r}_{1} \\ \Rightarrow \sin \mathrm{i}_{1}=\sqrt{3} \sin 30^{\circ}=\sqrt{3} \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{i}_{1}=60^{\circ} \end{array} $$ Similarly, $\mathrm{i}_{2}=60^{\circ}$ In a prism, deviation $\delta=\mathrm{i}_{1}+\mathrm{i}_{2}-\mathrm{A}=60^{\circ}+60^{\circ}-60^{\circ}=60^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If \(\varepsilon_0\) and \(\mu_0\) are the electric permittivity and magnetic permeability in vacuum, \(\varepsilon\) and \(\mu\) are corresponding quantities in medium, then refractive index of the medium is
questions.solution:
We know that velocity of electromagnetic wave in vacuum \(\left(\mathrm{v}_0\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}}\) and velocity of electromagnetic wave in medium is
\(\text {(v) }=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}} \text {. }\)
Therefore refractive index of the medium
\(\begin{aligned}
(\mu) & =\frac{\text { Vel. of E.M. wave in vacuum }\left(\mathrm{v}_0\right)}{\text { Vel. of E.M. wave in medium(v) }} \\
& =\frac{1 / \sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}}{1 / \sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mu \varepsilon}{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A meter bridge is used to determine the resistance of an unknown wire by measuring the balance point length $l$. If the wire is replaced by another wire of same material but with double the length and half the thickness, the balancing point is expected to be
questions.solution:
If the wire is replaced by another wire of same material but with double the length and half the thickness, the resistance of the wire as a whole will change. Let us calculate this change.
Initial resistance, $\mathrm{R}=\rho \frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{A}}=\rho \frac{\mathrm{L}}{\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}$
Fubak resistance, $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\rho \frac{2 \mathrm{~L}}{\mathrm{~A}^{\prime}}=\rho \frac{2 \mathrm{~L}}{\pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2}\right)^{2}}$
$=\rho \frac{8 \mathrm{~L}}{\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}=8\left(\rho \frac{\mathrm{L}}{\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}\right)=8 \mathrm{R}$
Therefore, the resistance will increase by eight times.
In a meter-bridge, we have
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{S}}=\frac{l}{100-l} \\
\text { where } \quad \mathrm{R}=\text { unknown resistor } \\
\quad \mathrm{S}=\text { known resistor } \\
l=\text { balancing length } \\
\therefore l=\frac{100 \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{S}+\mathrm{R}}
\end{array}$
When the resistance of the new wire is $8 \mathrm{R}$ then the new balancing length will be
$\begin{aligned}
l &=\frac{100 \times 8 \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{S}+8 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{100 \times 8 \mathrm{R}}{(\mathrm{S}+\mathrm{R})+7 \mathrm{R}} \\
&=\frac{100 \times 8 \mathrm{R}}{\left(\frac{100 \mathrm{R}}{l}\right)+7 \mathrm{R}} \quad \therefore l^{\prime}=\frac{800 l}{100+7 l}
\end{aligned}$
No option is correct.

questions.subject: Physics

questions.solution:
Output of upper AND gate $=\overline{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{B}$
Output of lower AND gate $=A \bar{B}$
$\therefore$ Output of OR gate, $\mathrm{Y}=\mathrm{A} \overline{\mathrm{B}}+\mathrm{B} \overline{\mathrm{A}}$
This is boolean expression for XOR gate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A transistor with \(\alpha=0.98\) is operated as an amplifier in a common base circuit with a load resistance of \(5 \mathrm{k} \Omega\). If the dynamic resistance of the emitter base junction is 70 , the voltage gain and power gain of the circuit will be:
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \alpha=0.95 ; R_L=5 \mathrm{k} \Omega \\
& \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{i}}=70 \Omega \\
& \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{V}}=\alpha \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{L}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{i}}}=0.98 \times \frac{5 \times 10^3}{70}=70
\end{aligned}\)
Power gain \(=\alpha^2 \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_i}=0.98 \times 70=68.6\)

questions.q: In a p-n junction diode acting as a half-waverectifier, which of the following statements is not true?

The drift current depends on biasing
The depletion zone decreases in forward biasing
The diffusion current increases due to forward biasing
questions.solution: In forward biasing, the value of diffusion current decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A p type semiconductor has acceptor levels 57 meV above the valance band. The maximum wavelength of light required to create hole is (Planck constant \(\mathrm{h}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\) )
questions.solution:
Given:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}=57 \mathrm{meV}=57 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\
& \mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda} \\
& \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{57 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-22}} \\
& \lambda=\frac{6.6 \times 3 \times 10^{-26}}{57 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-22}} \\
& \lambda=0.2171052 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m} \\
& \lambda=217105.2 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m} \\
& \lambda=217105 Å \text { (Approx.) }
\end{aligned}\)

questions.q: Intrinsic semiconductor is electrically neutral, extrinsic semiconductor having large number of current carriers would be:
options: positively charged or negatively charged depending upon the type of impurity that has been added
questions.solution: Extrinsic semiconductor is due to the type of impurity atom's valency. If impurity atoms are trivalent, then carriers are hole. And if impurity atoms are pentavalent then carriers are electron.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a common emitter transistor amplifier $\beta=60$, $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{o}}=5000 \Omega$ and internal resistance of a transistor is $500 \Omega$. The voltage amplification of amplifier will be
questions.solution:
Voltage amplification
$$
A_{\mathrm{V}}=\beta \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{o}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{i}}}=60 \times \frac{5000}{500}=600
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a common base mode of a transistor, the collector current is $5.488 \mathrm{~mA}$ for an emitter current of $5.60 \mathrm{~mA}$. The value of the base current amplification factor $(\beta)$ will be
questions.solution:
$$
\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{c}}=5.488 \mathrm{~mA}, \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{e}}=5.6 \mathrm{~mA}
$$
$$
\alpha=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{e}}}
$$
$$
\alpha=\frac{5.488}{5.6}
$$
$$
\beta=\frac{\alpha}{(1-\alpha)}=49
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For what value of $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{C}$, the output $\mathrm{Y}=1$
questions.solution:
Clearly,
$$
\mathrm{Y}=(\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}) \cdot \mathrm{C}=(\mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{C})+(\mathrm{B} \cdot \mathrm{C})
$$
For
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{A}=0, \mathrm{~B}=0 \& \mathrm{C}=1, & \mathrm{Y}=(0.1)+(0.1)=0 \\
\mathrm{~A}=1, \mathrm{~B}=0 \& \mathrm{C}=1, & \mathrm{Y}=(1.1)+(0.1)=1 \\
\mathrm{~A}=1, \mathrm{~B}=0 \& \mathrm{C}=0, & \mathrm{Y}=(0.1)+(0.0)=0 \\
\mathrm{~A}=0, \mathrm{~B}=1 \& \mathrm{C}=0, & \mathrm{Y}=(0.0)+(1.0)=0
\end{array}
$$
So, option (b) is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An $n$-type semiconductor is
options: negatively or positively charged depending on the amount of impurity added
questions.solution: The n-type semiconductor has excess of free electrons for conduction. The total number of electrons in an atom is equal to the total number of protons in the nucleus. So, $\mathrm{n}$-type semiconductor is neutral.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The input resistance of a common emitter amplifier is $330 \Omega$ and the load resistance is 5 $\mathrm{k} \Omega$. A change of base current is $15 \mu \mathrm{A}$ results in the change of collector current by $1 \mathrm{~mA}$. The voltage gain of amplifier is
questions.solution:
Given: $\Delta \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}}=1 \mathrm{~mA}=10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}$ $\Delta \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{b}}=15 \mu \mathrm{A}=15 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~A}$ $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{L}}=5 \mathrm{k} \Omega=5 \times 10^{3} \Omega$ $\mathrm{Ri}=330 \Omega$ The voltage gain of an amnl
The voltage gain of an amplifier
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}} \times \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{L}}}{\Delta \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{b}} \times \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{i}}} \\
=\frac{10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{3}}{15 \times 10^{-6} \times 330} \approx 1010
\end{array}$

questions.solution:
As we know, output of OR gate $\mathrm{Y}=\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}$
Output of AND gate
$\begin{aligned}
& Y^{\prime}=Y . C \\
\Rightarrow & Y^{\prime}=(A+B) \cdot C
\end{aligned}$
If $\mathrm{C}=0$ irrespective of $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$, then output $Y$ must be zero.

questions.subject: Physics

questions.solution:

questions.solution: For $\mathrm{R}=1 \mathrm{k} \Omega$ $\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{i}_{1}=\frac{15}{1} \mathrm{~mA}=15 \mathrm{~mA} \\ \mathrm{R}=250 \Omega \\ \mathrm{i}_{250}=\frac{20-15}{250}=\frac{5}{250}=20 \mathrm{~mA} \\ \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{Zener}}=20-15=5 \mathrm{~mA} \end{array}$

Arsenic is pentavalent while indium is trivalent.

Which of the following gates will have an output of 1 ? (a)
(b)
(c)
(d)
questions.solution:
(A) is a NAND gate so output is $\overline{1 \times 1}=\overline{1}=0$
(B) is a NOR gate so output is $\overline{0+1}=\overline{1}=0$
(C) is a NAND gate so output is $\overline{0 \times 1}=\overline{0}=1$
(D) is a XOR gate so output is $0 \oplus 0=0$
