questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical capacitor has charge $\mathrm{Q}$ and length L. If both the charge and length of the capacitor are doubled, by keeping other parameters fixed, the energy stored in the capacitor
questions.solution:
The energy stored in a capacitor is given by
$E=\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}$
Here, $C=\frac{2 \pi \in_{0} L}{\ln \left(\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}\right)}$, where
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{L}=\text { length of the cylindrical conductor } \\
\mathrm{R}_{1}=\text { inner radius } \\
\mathrm{R}_{2}=\text { outer radius } \\
\therefore \mathrm{C} \propto \mathrm{L} \Rightarrow \mathrm{E} \propto \frac{\mathrm{Q}^{2}}{\mathrm{~L}}
\end{array}$
When both $Q$ and $L$ are doubled, by keeping other parameters fixed, the energy stored (E') becomes
$\begin{array}{l}
E^{\prime} \propto \frac{(2 Q)^{2}}{2 L} \\
\propto \frac{2 Q^{2}}{L} \\
\therefore E^{\prime}=2 E
\end{array}$

the ammeter connected to the conductor will register higher current than the ammeter connected to the semiconductor
the ammeters connected to both semiconductor and conductor will register the same current
the ammeter connected to both semiconductor and conductor will register no change in the current
questions.solution: Since conductor and semiconductor are connected in parallel hence voltage across them is same. If ammeters show same reading hence their resistances $\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}}$ are same. If voltage is increased by small value then following the same relation $\mathrm{V} \propto \mathrm{I}$ for constant $R$, both conductor and semiconductor show same current.

questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell}=\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T} &=10^{-5} \times 100=10^{-3} \\
\frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell} \times 100 \% &=10^{-3} \times 100 \\
&=10^{-1}=0.1 \%
\end{aligned}$

questions.q: A body cools from $50.0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $49.9^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in $5 \mathrm{~s}$. How long will it take to cool from $40.0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $39.9^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ? Assume the temperature of surroundings to be $30.0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and Newton's law of cooling to be valid
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{50-49.9}{5}=K\left(\frac{50+49.9}{2}-30\right)....(i) \\
& \frac{40-39.9}{t}=K\left[\frac{40+39.9}{2}-30\right]....(ii)
\end{aligned}
$$
From equations (i) and (ii), we get $\mathrm{t} \approx 10 \mathrm{~s}$.

questions.solution:
The equivalent electrical circuit of the arrangement is shown in figure.
Temperature difference between the end points $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{D}=200-20=180^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ As the resistances for the three parts are equal, the temperature difference must be distribuited equally in the three parts $(=180 /$ ) $3=60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) $\therefore$ Temperature of $\mathrm{B}=200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-60^{\circ}=140^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A clock pendulum made of invar has a period of $0.5 \mathrm{~s}$, at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the clock is used in a climate where the temperature averages to $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, how much time does the clock lose in each oscillation? (For invar, $\alpha=9 \times 10^{-7} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{g}=$ constan $\left.\mathrm{t}\right)$
questions.solution:
Time period of oscillation,
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}} \Rightarrow \frac{d T}{T}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{d l}{l}$
$\begin{aligned} \text { As, } \frac{d l}{l} &=\alpha d t \\ \Rightarrow \frac{d T}{T} &=\frac{1}{2} \alpha d t=\frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times 10^{-7} \times(30-20) \\ &=4.5 \times 10^{-6} \end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Loss in time } &=4.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.5 \\ &=2.25 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~s} \end{aligned}$

questions.q:
An ideal gas is subjected to a cyclic process $\mathrm{ABCD}$ as depicted in the $\mathrm{p}-\mathrm{V}$ diagram given below:
Which of the following curves represents the equivalent cyclic process?
questions.solution:
Process $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow A$ is clockwise. During $A \rightarrow B$, pressure is constant and $B \rightarrow C$, process follows $p \propto \frac{1}{V}$ ie., $T$ is constant. During process $C \rightarrow D$, both $p$ and $V$ changes and process $D \rightarrow A$ follows $p \propto \frac{1}{V}$ i.e., $T$ is constant.
Hence, equivalent cyclic process is has follows.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
An ideal gas is subjected to cyclic process involving four thermodynamic states, the amounts of heat (Q) and work (W) involved in each of these states are,
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{Q}_{1}=6000 \mathrm{~J}, \mathrm{Q}_{2}=-5500 \mathrm{~J} ; \mathrm{Q}_{3}=-3000 \mathrm{~J} \\
\mathrm{Q}_{4}=3500 \mathrm{~J} \\
\mathrm{~W}_{1}=2500 \mathrm{~J} ; \mathrm{W}_{2}=-1000 \mathrm{~J} ; \mathrm{W}_{3}=-1200 \mathrm{~J} \\
\mathrm{~W}_{4}=\mathrm{x} \mathrm{J}
\end{array}$
The ratio of the net work done by the gas to the total heat absorbed by the gas is $\eta$ ). The values of $x$ and $\eta$ respectively are
questions.solution:
From first law of thermodynamics
$$
\begin{array}{l}
Q=\Delta U+W \text { or } \Delta U=Q-W \\
\therefore \Delta U_{1}=Q_{1}-W_{1}=6000-2500=3500 \mathrm{~J} \\
\Delta U_{2}=Q_{2}-W_{2}=-5500+1000=-4500 \mathrm{~J} \\
\Delta U_{3}=Q_{3}-W_{3}=-3000+1200=-1800 \mathrm{~J} \\
\Delta U_{4}=Q_{4}-W_{4}=3500-x
\end{array}
$$
For cyclic process $\Delta U=0$
$\therefore 3500-4500-1800+3500-x=0$
or $x=700 \mathrm{~J}$
Efficiency, $\eta=\frac{\text { output }}{\text { input }} \times 100$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
=\frac{W_{1}+W_{2}+W_{3}+W_{4}}{Q_{1}+Q_{4}} \times 100 \\
=\frac{(2500-1000-1200+700)}{6000+3500} \times 100 \\
=\frac{1000}{9500} \times 100=10.5 \%
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the $V-T$ diagram shown in adjoining figure, what is the relation between $p_{1}$ and $p_{2}$ ?
questions.solution:
In an isobaric process, $\mathrm{p}=$ constant Hence, $\mathrm{V} \propto \mathrm{T}$
i.e., $\mathrm{V}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{nR}}{\mathrm{P}}\right) \mathrm{T}$
$\therefore$ V-T graph is astraight linewith slope $\propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{p}}$
(slope) $_{2}>$ (slope) $_{1}$ $\therefore \mathrm{p}_{2} < \mathrm{p}_{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two different ideal diatomic gases $A$ and $B$ are initially in the same state. $A$ and $B$ are then expanded to same final volume through adiabatic and isothermal process, respectively. If $p_A, p_B$ and $T_A, T_B$ represent the final pressures and temperatures at $A$ and $B$ respectively, then
questions.solution: In adiabatic process, pressure will be less. Further temperature is the product of $p$ and $V$, hence $p_A \lt p_B$ and $T_A \lt T_B$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cyclic process for 1 mole of an ideal is shown in the $V-T$ diagram. The work done in $A B, B C$ and $C A$ respectively is
questions.solution:
Process $A B$ is isochoric, hence $W_{A B}=0$
Process $B C$ is isothermal, hence
$W_{B C}=n R T_2 \ln \left(\frac{V_f}{V_i}\right)$
or $\quad W_{B C}=R T_2 \ln \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)$
Process $C A$ is isobaric (as, $V \propto T)$
$\therefore W_{C A}=p\left(V_f-V_i\right)=n R\left(T_t-T_i\right)=R\left(T_1-T_2\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two cylinders A and B fitted with pistons contain equal number of moles of an ideal monoatomic gas at $400 \mathrm{~K}$. The piston of A is free to move while that of $B$ is held fixed. Same amount of heat energy is given to the gas in each cylinder. If the rise in temperature of the gas in $\mathrm{A}$ is $42 \mathrm{~K}$, the rise in temperature of the gas in $B$ is
questions.solution:
From first law of thermodynamics
$$
Q=\Delta U+W
$$
For cylinder $A$ pressure remains constant.
$\therefore$ Work done by a system
$$
W=\frac{\mu R}{\gamma-1}\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right)
$$
For monoatomic gases, $\mu=1 ; \gamma=\frac{5}{3}$
$$
\therefore W=\frac{1 \times R}{\frac{5}{3}-1}(442-400)=\frac{3}{2} R \times 42
$$
or $W=63 R$
But $\Delta U=0$, for cylinder $A$
$$
\therefore Q=0+63 R=63 R
$$
For cylinder $B$ volume is constant, $\therefore W=0$ and $Q=\mu C_{V} \Delta T$
For monoatomic gas
$$
C_{V}=\frac{3}{2} R \Rightarrow Q=1 \times \frac{3}{2} R \Delta T
$$
As heat given on both cylinder is same,
$$
\therefore 63 R=\frac{3}{2} R \Delta T \Rightarrow \Delta T=42 \mathrm{~K}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Imagine a system of unit in which the unit of mass is 10 kg , length is 1 km and time is 1 min . Then, 1 J in this system is equal to
questions.solution:
As joule is the SI unit of energy and dimensional formula for energy is $\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]$. In this question, we have to convert one unit into another, therefore
$\begin{aligned} \eta_1\left[M_1 L_1^2 T_1^{-2}\right] & =\eta_2\left[M_2 L_2^2 T_2^{-2}\right] \\ \Rightarrow \quad \eta_2 & =\eta_1\left[\frac{M_1}{M_2}\right]^1\left[\frac{L_1}{L_2}\right]^2\left[\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right]^{-2} \\ & =1 \times\left[\frac{1 \mathrm{~kg}}{10 \mathrm{~kg}}\right]^1\left[\frac{1 \mathrm{~m}}{1 \mathrm{~km}}\right]^2\left[\frac{1 \mathrm{~s}}{1 \mathrm{~min}}\right]^{-2} \\ & =1 \times\left[\frac{1 \mathrm{~kg}}{10 \mathrm{~kg}}\right]^1\left[\frac{1 \mathrm{~m}}{10^3 \mathrm{~m}}\right]^2\left[\frac{1 \mathrm{~s}}{60 \mathrm{~s}}\right]^{-2} \\ & =1 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-6} \times 36 \times 10^2 \\ \eta_2 & =36 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The time dependence of a physical quantity $P$ is given by $P=P_{0} e_{\alpha}\left(-\alpha t^{2}\right)$, where $\alpha$ is a constant and $t$ is time. The constant $\alpha$
questions.solution:
Given, $\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{P}_{0} \exp \left(-\alpha \mathrm{t}^{2}\right)$
As $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{0}$ have the same units, therefore $\alpha t^{2}$ must be dimensionless for which
$\alpha=\frac{1}{T^{2}}=T^{-2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $x=a t+b t^2$, where $x$ is the distance travelled by the body in kilometers while $t$ is the time in seconds, then the unit of $b$ is
questions.solution: $[\mathrm{x}]=\left[\mathrm{bt}^2\right]$. Hence $[\mathrm{b}]=\left[\mathrm{x} / \mathrm{t}^2\right]=\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{s}^2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a wave traverses a medium the displacement of a particle located at $x$ at a time $t$ is given by $y=a \sin (b t-c x)$. Where $a, b$ and $c$ are constants of the wave. Which of the following is a quantity with dimensions?
questions.solution:
Here, $y=a \sin (b t-c x)$
Compar ing this equation with geneal wave equation
$$
y=a \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi t}{T}-\frac{2 \pi x}{\lambda}\right)
$$
we get $b=\frac{2 \pi}{T}, c=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}$
(a) Dimensions of $\frac{y}{a}=\frac{[L]}{[L]}=$ Dimensionless
(b) Dimensions of $b t=\frac{2 \pi}{T} \cdot t=\frac{[T]}{[T]}$
$=$ Dimensionless
(c) Dimensions of $c x=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot x=\frac{[L]}{[L]}$
$=$ Dimensionless
(d) Dimensions of $\frac{b}{c}=\frac{\frac{2 \pi}{T}}{\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}}=\frac{\lambda}{T}=\left[L T^{-1}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The dimensional formula of magnetic flux is
questions.solution:
Magnetic flux, $\phi=\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{A}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{I} \lambda} \mathbf{A}$
$=\frac{\left[M^{1} L^{+1} T^{-2}\right] L^{2}}{[A . L]}=\left[M^{1} L^{2} T^{-2} A^{-1}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment, the central point on the screen is
questions.solution: In the normal adjustment of YDSE, Path difference between the waves at central location is always zero. So maxima is obtained at central position. Central point on the screen is bright.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment the two slits are illuminated by light of wavelenght $5890 Å$ and the distance between the fringes obtained on the screen is $0.2^{\circ} .$ If the whole apparatus is immersed in water then the angular fringe width will be, if the refractive index of water is $4 / 3$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel monochromatic beam of light is incident normally on a narrow slit. A diffraction pattern is formed on a screen placed perpendicular to the direction of the incident beam. At the first minimum of the diffraction pattern, the phase difference between the rays coming from the two edges of slit is
questions.solution:
In a single slit diffraction pattern, the path difference \((\Delta \mathrm{x})\) for \(n^{\text {th }}\) minima is given by \(\Delta \mathrm{x}=n \lambda\), where \(n= \pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3, \ldots\)
or phase difference, \(\Delta \phi=n(2 \pi)\)
For \(1^{\text {st }}\) minimum, \(n=1\).
\(\therefore \Delta \phi=2 \pi\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the Bragg scattering of a beam of electrons each of mass \(m\) and velocity \(v\) by a nickel crystal, the first maximum is observed at \(\theta=30^{\circ}\) (\(\theta\) being the angle the beam makes with the crystal plane). What is the inter-planar distance \(d\) for the crystal?
questions.solution:
For Bragg's scattering of a beam of electron, the inter-planar distance ' \(\mathrm{d}\) ' is given by \(2 \mathrm{~d} \sin \theta=\mathrm{n} \lambda\)
Here \(\theta=30^{\circ}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \sin 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{2} ; \mathrm{n}=1 \\
& \therefore \mathrm{d}=\frac{\mathrm{n} \lambda}{2 \sin \theta}=\frac{1 \times \lambda}{2 \times \frac{1}{2}}=\lambda
\end{aligned}\)
or \(\mathrm{d}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}\)
(According to de-Broglie, wavelength associated with an electron \(\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}\))

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following does not support the wave nature of light?
questions.solution: Photoelectric effect does not support the wave nature of light.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In young's double-slit experiment, the intensity of light at a point on the screen where the path difference is $\lambda$ is $\mathrm{I}, \lambda$ being the wavelength of light used. The intensity at a point where the path difference is $\frac{\lambda}{4}$ will be
questions.solution:
For path difference $\lambda$, phase
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { difference }=2 \pi\left(\mathrm{Q}=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \mathrm{x}=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \lambda=2 \pi\right) \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{0}+\mathrm{I}_{0}+2 \mathrm{I}_{0} \cos 2 \pi \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=4 \mathrm{I}_{0}(\therefore \cos 2 \pi=1)
\end{array}
$$
For $x=\frac{\lambda}{4}$, phase difference $=\frac{\pi}{2}$
$$
\therefore I^{\prime}=I_{1}+I_{2}+2 \sqrt{I_{1}} \sqrt{I_{2}} \cos \frac{\pi}{2}
$$
If $I_{1}=I_{2}=I_{0}$ then $I^{\prime}=2 I_{0}=2 \cdot \frac{I}{4}=\frac{I}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Hologram is based on phenomenon of
questions.solution: Hologram is based on the phenomenon of interference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two light rays having the same wavelength in vacuum are in phase initially. Then, the first ray travels a path $L_{I}$ through a medium of refractive index $\mu_{1}$ while the second ray travels a path $L_{2}$ through a medium of refractive index $\mu_{2}$. The two waves are then combined to observe interference. The phase difference between the two waves is
questions.solution:
First ray optical path $=\mu_{1} \mathrm{~L}_{1}$ second ray optical path $=\mu_{2} \mathrm{~L}_{2}$
So, phase difference
$\begin{aligned}
\Delta \phi &=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \text { path difference }=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \Delta \mathrm{x} \\
\therefore \quad \Delta \phi &=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\left(\mu_{1} \mathrm{~L}_{1}-\mu_{2} \mathrm{~L}_{2}\right)
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two polaroids are kept crossed to each other. If one of them is rotated an angle $60^{\circ}$, the percentage of incident light now transmitted through the system is
questions.solution:
Let the intensity of unpolarised light be $\mathrm{I}_{0}$, so the intensity of first polaroid is $\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{2}$.
On rotating through $60^{\circ}$, the intensity of light from second polaroid
$\mathrm{I}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{2}\right)(\cos 60)^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{2} \frac{1}{4}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{0}}{8}=0.125 \mathrm{I}_{0}$
$\therefore \quad$ percentage of incident light transmitted through the system $=12.5 \%$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Polarising angle for water is $53^{\circ} 4^{\prime}$. If light is incident at this angle on the surface of water and reflected the angle of refraction is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { Here, } \theta p+r=90^{\circ} \\
\Rightarrow \quad r=90^{\circ}-\theta p \\
\text { or } \quad r=90^{\circ}-53^{\circ} 4^{\prime}=36^{\circ} 56
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the Young's double slit experiment, the intensities at two points $\mathrm{P}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{2}$ on the screen are respectively $\mathrm{I}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{I}_{2} .$ If $\mathrm{P}_{1}$ is located at the centre of a bright fringe and $\mathrm{P}_{2}$ is located at a distance equal to a quarter of fringe width from $\mathrm{P}_{1}$, then $\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}$ is
questions.solution:
Fringe width $\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}$ Let be the amplitude of the place where constructive inference takes place. The position of fringe at $p_{2}$. $$ \Rightarrow x=\frac{n \lambda D}{d} $$ Given, $\beta^{\prime}=\left(\frac{\beta}{4}\right)$
$\therefore \frac{\lambda D}{4 d}=\frac{n \lambda D}{d}$ or $n=\frac{1}{4}$ $\therefore \frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\frac{a^{2}}{\left(\frac{a}{4}\right)^{2}}=16: 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The Young's double slit experiment is performed with blue and with green light of wavelengths \(4360 Å\) and \(5460 Å\) respectively. If \(x\) is the distance of 4 th maxima from the central one, then
questions.solution:
Distance of \(\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}\) maxima, \(\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{n} \lambda \frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}} \propto \lambda\)
As \(\lambda_{\mathrm{b}}  < \lambda_{\mathrm{g}}\)
\(\therefore \mathrm{x}_{\text {blue }} < \mathrm{x}_{\text {green }}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experimental setup, if the wavelength alone is doubled, the bandwidth $\beta$ becomes
questions.solution:
Fringe width, $\beta=\frac{\lambda \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}$
When $\lambda$ alone is doubled then fringe width becomes
$$
\beta^{\prime}=\frac{(2 \lambda) \mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}=2 \beta
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an experiment on Newton's rings, the diameter of the $20^{\text {th }}$ dark ring was found to be $5.82 \mathrm{~mm}$ and that of the $10^{\text {th }}$ ring $3.36 \mathrm{~mm}$. If the radius of the plano-convex lens is $1 \mathrm{~m}$, the wavelength of light used is
questions.solution:
Newton's ring arrangement is used for determining the wavelength of monochromatic light. For this the diameter of $n^{\text {th }}$ dark ring $\left(D_{n}\right)$ and $(n+p)^{\text {th }}$ dark ring $\left(\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{n}+\mathrm{p}}\right)$ are measured then
$D_{(\mathrm{n}+\mathrm{p})}^{2}=4(\mathrm{n}+\mathrm{p}) \lambda \mathrm{R}$ and $\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{n}}^{2}=4 \mathrm{n} \lambda \mathrm{R}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{D_{n+p}^{2}-D_{n}^{2}}{4 p R}$
Here, $n=10, n+p=20$
$\therefore \quad p=10 ; R=1 m, D_{10}=3.96 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$,
$D_{20}=5.82 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore \lambda=\frac{D_{20}^{2}-D_{10}^{2}}{4 \mathrm{pR}}$
$\quad=\frac{\left(5.82 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}-\left(3.36 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{4 \times 10 \times 1}$
$\quad=5646 Å$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment intensity at a point is $(1 / 4)$ of the maximum intensity. Angular position of this point is (separation between slits is $d$ )
questions.solution:
If $a$ is the amplitude of the wave then
$\frac{I_{\max }}{4}=a^{2}=a^{2}+a^{2}+2 a a \cos \phi$
or $\quad \cos \phi=-\frac{1}{2}$
or $\quad \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{3}$.
Corresponding path difference,
$\Delta x=\frac{\phi \times \lambda}{2 \pi}=\frac{(2 \pi / 3) \times \lambda}{2 \pi}=\frac{\lambda}{3}$
So $\quad d \sin \theta=\frac{\lambda}{3}$
or
$\theta=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\lambda}{3 d}\right)$

The ratio $\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}_{1}}{\mathrm{y}_{2}}\right)$ is

questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ray of light strikes a piece of glass at an angle of incidence of $60^{\circ}$ and the reflected beam is completely plane polarised. The refractive index of glass is
questions.solution:
According to Brewster's law, reflected light is plane polarised if unpolarised light falls at the interface of air and medium at an angle $\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{p}}$ called polarising angle then
$\mu=\tan \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{p}} \Rightarrow \mu(\text { glass })=\tan 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the light is polarised by reflection, then the angle between reflected and refracted light is
questions.solution: On polarisation by reflection, the reflected and refracted waves are at $90^{\circ}$ to each other.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a plastic thin film of refractive index $1.45$ is placed in the path of one of the interfering waves then the central fringe is displaced through width of five fringes. The thickness of the film, if the wavelength of light is $5890 Å$, will be
questions.solution:
$X_{0}=\frac{\beta}{\lambda}(\mu-1) t \Rightarrow 5 \beta=\frac{\beta(0.45) t}{5890 \times 10^{-10}}$
$\therefore t=\frac{5 \times 5890 \times 10^{-10}}{0.45}=6.544 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The attenuation in optical fibre is mainly due to
questions.solution: From an optical fibre due to absorption or light leaving the fibre area resulting scattering of light sideways by impurities in the glass fibre. And due to this reason a very small part of light energy is lost.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A string of $7 \mathrm{~m}$ length has a mass of $0.035 \mathrm{~kg}$. If tension in the string is $60.5 \mathrm{~N}$, then speed of a wave on the string is
questions.solution:
Given : Length $(l)=7 \mathrm{~m}$
$\operatorname{Mass}(\mathrm{M})=0.035 \mathrm{~kg}$ and tension $(\mathrm{T})=60.5$
$\mathrm{N}$. We know that mass of string per unit length $(\mathrm{m})$
$$
=\frac{0.035}{7}=0.005 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}
$$
and speed of
$$
\text { wave }=\sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}=\sqrt{\frac{60.5}{0.005}}=110 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} .
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two waves $y_{1}=A_{1} \sin \left(\omega t-\beta_{1}\right)$ and $y_{2}=A_{2} \sin \left(\omega t-\beta_{2}\right)$ superimpose to form a resultant wave whose amplitude is
questions.solution:
Amplitudes $\mathrm{A}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{A}_{2}$ are added as vectors. Angle between the two vectors is the phase difference $\left(\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}\right)$ between them.
$\therefore$ Resultant wave,
$\mathrm{R}=\sqrt{\mathrm{A}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{A}_{2}^{2}+2 \mathrm{~A}_{1} \mathrm{~A}_{2} \cos \left(\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}\right)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A source producing sound of frequency $170 \mathrm{~Hz}$ is approaching a stationary observer with a velocity $17 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The apparent change in the wavelength of sound heard by the observer is (speed of sound in air $=340 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ )

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a progressive wave is represented as $y=2 \sin \pi\left(\frac{t}{2}-\frac{x}{4}\right)$ where $\mathrm{x}$ is in metre and $\mathrm{t}$ is in second, then the distance travelled by the wave in $5 \mathrm{~s}$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\text { Given, } y=3 \sin \pi\left(\frac{t}{2}-\frac{x}{4}\right) \\
=3 \sin \left(\frac{\pi t}{2}-\frac{\pi x}{4}\right)
\end{array}$
Comparing it with standard equation
$\begin{array}{l}
y=r \sin \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}(v t-x) \\
=r \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi v t}{\lambda}-\frac{2 \pi x}{\lambda}\right)
\end{array}$
We have, $\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{v}}{\lambda}=\frac{\pi}{2}$ or $\mathrm{v}=\frac{\lambda}{4}$
and $\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=\frac{\pi}{4}$ or $\lambda=8 \mathrm{~m} \therefore \mathrm{v}=\frac{8}{4}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
So, the distance travelled by wave in $t$ second $=\mathrm{vt}=2 \times 5=10 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When two waves of almost equal frequency $\mathrm{n}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{n}_{2}$ are produced simultaneously, then the time interval between succesive maxima is
questions.solution: Time interval between two suecessive maxima $=$ time interval between two successive beats $=\frac{1}{n_{1}-n_{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the equation for a stationary wave given by $y=5 \cos \frac{\pi x}{25} \sin 100 \pi t$. Here, $x$ is in cm and $t$ in second. A node will not occur at distance $x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Node will occur, where $\frac{\pi x}{25}=\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3 \pi}{2}, \frac{5 \pi}{2}$, etc.
or $x=12.5 \mathrm{~cm}, 37.5 \mathrm{~cm}, 62.5 \mathrm{~cm}$, etc.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the adjoining figure, two pulses in a stretched string are shown. If initially their centres are $8 \mathrm{~cm}$ apart and they are moving towards each other, with speed of $2 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$, then total energy of the pulses after $2 \mathrm{~s}$ will be
questions.solution:
Given, speed of each pulse $=2 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ Therefore distance travelled by both pulses in $2 \mathrm{~s}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$ toward each other. On their superposition, the resultant displacement at every point will be zero.
Hence, total energy will be purely kinetic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In space charge limited region, the plate current ina diode is $10 \mathrm{~mA}$ for plate voltage $150 \mathrm{~V}$. If the plate voltagte is increased to $600 \mathrm{~V}$, then the plate current will be
questions.solution:
In space charge limited region, the plate current is given by Child's law $i_{p}=K V_{p}^{3 / 2}$ Thus,
$$
\frac{i_{p 2}}{i_{p 1}}=\left(\frac{V_{p 2}}{V_{p 1}}\right)^{3 / 2}=\left(\frac{600}{150}\right)^{3 / 2}=(4)^{3 / 2}=8
$$
or, $i_{p 2}=i_{p 1} \times 8=10 \times 8 \mathrm{~mA}=80 \mathrm{~mA}$...(i)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical piano wires have a fundamental frequency of 600 cycle per second when kept under the same tension. What fractional increase in the tension of one wires will lead to the occurrence of 6 beats per second when both wires vibrate simultaneously?
questions.solution:
When both the wires vibrate simultaneously, beats per second,
$$
\begin{array}{c}
n_{1} \pm n_{2}=6 \\
\text { or } \frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T}{m}} \pm \frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T^{\prime}}{m}}=6 \\
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T^{\prime}}{m}}-\frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}=6 \\
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T^{\prime}}{m}}-600=6 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T^{\prime}}{m}}=606...(i)
\end{array}
$$
Given that fundamental frequency
$$
\frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}=600...(ii)
$$
Dividing Eq. (i) by(ii),
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{\frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T^{\prime}}{m}}}{\frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}}=\frac{606}{600} \\
\Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{T^{\prime}}{T}}=(1.01) \Rightarrow \frac{T^{\prime}}{T}=(1.02) \% \\
\Rightarrow T^{\prime}=T(1.02)
\end{array}
$$
Increase in tension
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Delta T^{\prime}=T \times 1.02-T=(0.02 T) \\
\therefore \Delta T=0.02
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: There are two solenoids of same length and inductance $\mathrm{L}$ but their diameters differ to the extent that one can just fit into the other. They are connected in three different ways in series. 1) They are connected in series but separated by large distance 2 ) they connected in series with one inside the other and senses of the turns coinciding 3 ) both are connected in series with one inside the other with senses of the turns opposite as depicted in figures 1,2 and 3 respectively. The total inductance of the solenoids in each of the case 1,2 and 3 are respectively
questions.solution:
In first case, the two inductances are in series hence total inductance $=\mathrm{L}_{0}+\mathrm{L}_{0}$
$=2 \mathrm{~L}_{0}$
In second case, the current is same, but no. of turns has doubled since the sense of turning is same.
Thus $\mathrm{L} \propto \mathrm{n}^{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{L} \propto(2 \mathrm{n})^{2}$
hence inductance $\mathrm{L}=4 \mathrm{~L}_{0}$
In third case, the sense of turns is opposite.
So net inductance cancel each other
$\therefore \mathrm{L}=0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Tube $A$ has both ends open while tube $B$ has one end closed, otherwise they are identical. The ratio of fundamental frequency of tube $A$ and $B$ is
questions.solution:
Fundamental freq. for,
Tube $\mathrm{A} \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2 \mathrm{~L}}$
Tube $\mathrm{B} \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{4 \mathrm{~L}}$
Now,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2 \mathrm{~L}} \times \frac{4 \mathrm{~L}}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{2}{1} \\
\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{A}}: \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{B}}=2: 1
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two sources A and B are sending notes of frequency $680 \mathrm{~Hz}$. A listener moves from $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ with a constant velocity $\mathrm{u}$. If the speed of sound in air is $340 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, what must be the value of $\mathrm{u}$ so that he hears 10 beats per second?
questions.solution:
Let listener go from $A \rightarrow B$ with velocity $(\mu) .$
And the apparent frequency of sound from source $A$ by listener using Doppler's effect, $$ n^{\prime}=n\left(\frac{v-v_{o}}{v+v_{s}}\right) \Rightarrow n^{\prime}=680\left(\frac{340-u}{340+0}\right) $$ The apparent frequency of sound from source $B$ by listener $$ n^{\prime \prime}=n\left(\frac{v+v_{o}}{v-v_{s}}\right)=680\left(\frac{340+u}{340-0}\right) $$ Listener hear 10 beats per second. $$ \begin{array}{l} \text { Hence, } n^{\prime \prime}-n^{\prime}=10 \\ \Rightarrow 680\left(\frac{340+u}{340}\right)-680\left(\frac{340-u}{340}\right)=10 \\ \Rightarrow 2(340+u-340+u)=10 \\ \Rightarrow u=2.5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Light of wavelength $\lambda$ strikes a photo-sensitive surface and electrons are ejected with kinetic energy E. If the kinetic energy is to be increased to $2 \mathrm{E}$, the wavelength must be changed to $\lambda^{\prime}$. where
questions.solution:
Here, $E=\frac{h c}{2}-W_{0}$ and $2 E=\frac{h c}{\lambda^{\prime}}-W_{0}$
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda}=\frac{E+W}{2 E+W_{0}} \Rightarrow \lambda^{\prime}=\lambda\left(\frac{1+W_{0} / E}{2+W_{0} / E}\right)
$$
Since $\frac{\left(1+W_{0} / E\right)}{\left(2+W_{0} / E\right)}>\frac{1}{2}$ So $\lambda^{\prime}>\frac{\lambda}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An installation consisting of an electric motor driving a water pump left $75 \mathrm{~L}$ of water per second to a height of $4.7 \mathrm{~m}$.If the motor consumes a power of $5 \mathrm{~kW}$, then the efficiency of the installation is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A motor of power $P_{0}$ is used to deliver water at a certain rate through a given horizontal pipe. To increase the rate of flow of water through the same pipe $n$ times, the power of the motor is increased to $P_{1}$. The ratio of $P_{1}$ to $P_{0}$ is
questions.solution:
Power of motor initially $=P_{0}$
Let, rate of flow of motor $=(x)$
Since, power,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
P_{0}=\frac{\text { work }}{\text { time }}=\frac{m g y}{t}=m g\left(\frac{y}{t}\right) \\
\frac{y}{t}=x=\text { rate of flow of water }=m g x...(i)
\end{array}
$$
If rate of flow of water is increased by $n$ times, i.e., $(n x)$.
Increased power,
$$
P_{1}=\frac{m g y^{\prime}}{t}=m g\left(\frac{y^{\prime}}{t}\right)=n m g x...(ii)
$$
The ratio of power,
$$
\frac{P_{1}}{P_{0}}=\frac{n m g x}{m g x} \quad ; P_{1}: P_{0}=n: 1
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential energy of a system increases if work is done
options: upon the system by a non conservative force
by the system against a conservative force
by the system against a non conservative force
upon the system by a conservative force
questions.solution: When work is done upon a system by a conservative force then its potential energy increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A running man has half the kinetic energy of that of a boy of half of his mass. The man speeds up by $1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ so as to have same K.E. as that of the boy. The original speed of the man will be
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{m}=$ mass of boy, $\mathrm{M}=$ mass of man $\mathrm{v}=$ velocity of boy, $\mathrm{V}=$ velocity of man
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{2} M V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}\right]....(i) \\
\frac{1}{2} M(V+1)^{2}=1\left[\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}\right]....(ii)
\end{array}
$$
Putting $\mathrm{m}=\frac{M}{2}$ and solving $\mathrm{V}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rod of length $l$ is held vertically stationary with its lower end located at a point $\mathrm{P}$, on the horizontal plane. When the rod is released to topple about $\mathrm{P}$, the velocity of the upper end of the rod with which it hits the ground is
questions.solution:
Here, potential energy of the metre stick will be converted into rotational kinetic energy.
Because centre of gravity of stick lies at the middle of the rod, PE of metre stick $=\frac{m g l}{2}$ Rotational kinetic energy $E=\frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ $I$ about point $A=\frac{m l^{2}}{3}$. By law of conservation of energy $$ m g\left(\frac{l}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{m l^{2}}{3}\left(\frac{v_{B}}{l}\right)^{2} $$ By solving, we get $v_{B}=\sqrt{3 g l}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the potential energy of a gas molecule is $U=\frac{M}{r^{6}}-\frac{N}{r^{12}}, M$ and $N$ being positive constants, then the potential energy at equilibrium must be
questions.solution:
Given, $U=\frac{M}{r^{6}}-\frac{N}{r^{12}}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \mathrm{F}=\frac{-\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dr}}=\frac{-\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dr}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{r}^{6}}-\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{r}^{12}}\right) \\
=-\left(\frac{-6 \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{r}^{7}}+\frac{12 \mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{r}^{13}}\right)=\left(\frac{6 \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{r}^{7}}-\frac{12 \mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{r}^{13}}\right)
\end{array}$
For equilibrium position, $\mathrm{F}=0$
$\therefore \frac{6 \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{r}^{7}}=\frac{12 \mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{r}^{13}} \text { or } \mathrm{r}^{6}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{M}}$
Hence, $U=\frac{M}{(2 N / M)}-\frac{N}{(2 N / M)^{2}}=\frac{M^{2}}{4 N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work done in moving a charge particle of charge $2 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$ between two points having potential difference of 36 V is
questions.solution:
Given, $q=2 \times 10^{-8}, \Delta V=36 \mathrm{~V}$
 $\begin{aligned}\textrm{Work done,} W & =q \cdot \Delta V \\ & =2 \times 10^{-8} \times 36 \\ & =72 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~V} \\ & =0.72 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~V} \\ & =0.72 \mu \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is acted by a force \(\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{kx}\), where \(\mathrm{k}\) is a + ve constant. Its potential energy at \(x=0\) is zero. Which curve correctly represents the variation of potential energy of the block with respect to \(\mathrm{x}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential energy of a system increases if work is done
options: upon the system by a non conservative force
by the system against a conservative force
by the system against a non conservative force
upon the system by a conservative force
questions.solution: When work is done upon a system by a conservative force then its potential energy increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is projected up from a point at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal direction. At any time $t$, if $p$ is the linear momentum, $y$ is the vertical displacement, $x$ is horizontal displacement, the graph among the following which does not represent the variation of kinetic energy KE of the particle is
questions.solution:
Momentum, $p=m \cdot v \Rightarrow v=\left(\frac{p}{m}\right)$
Kinetic energy, KE $$ \begin{array}{l} =\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{p^{2}}{m^{2}}\right)=\frac{1}{2 m} p^{2} \\ \text { or, } \mathrm{KE} \propto p^{2} \quad\left(\because \frac{1}{2 m}=\text { constant }\right) \end{array} $$ Hence, the graph between $\mathrm{KE}$ and $p^{2}$ will be linear. Now, kinetic energy $\mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ The velocity component at point $P$, $v_{y}=(u \sin \theta-g t)$ and $v_{x}=u \cos \theta$ Resultant velocity at point $P$ $$ \begin{aligned} \vec{v} &=v_{y} \hat{j}+v_{x} \hat{i} \\ &=(u \sin \theta-g t) \hat{j}+u \cos \theta \hat{i} \\ &|\vec{v}|=\sqrt{(u \cos \theta)^{2}+(u \sin \theta-g t)^{2}} \\ =\sqrt{u^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta+u^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta+g^{2} t^{2}-2 u g t \sin \theta} \\ &=\sqrt{u^{2}\left(\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta\right)+g^{2} t^{2}-2 u g t \sin \theta} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \therefore \mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m\left(u^{2}+g^{2} t^{2}-2 u g t \sin \theta\right)ii/ $$ i. e., $\mathrm{KE} \propto t^{2}$ Hence, graph will be parabolic intercept on $y$-axis. Hence, the graph between $\mathrm{KE}$ and $t$. Now, in case of height $$ \begin{array}{l} \mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m\left(v^{2}\right) \text { and } v^{2}=\left(u^{2}-2 g y\right) \\ \therefore \mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m\left(u^{2}-2 g y\right) \\ \mathrm{KE}=-m g y+\frac{1}{2} m u^{2} \end{array} $$ Intercept on $y$-axis $=\frac{1}{2} m u^{2}$
$$ \mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{x}{t}\right)^{2} $$ i.e., $\mathrm{KE} \propto x^{2}$. Thus graph between $\mathrm{KE}$ and $x$ will be parabolic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the absence of applied potential, the electric current flowing through a metallic wire is zero because
options: the electrons are drifted in random direction with a speed of the order of $10^{-2} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$
the electrons move in random direction with a speed of the order close to that of velocity of light
electrons and ions move in opposite direction
questions.solution: If there is no potential difference through a metallic wire, the current is zero because the electrons drift in a random direction with a speed of the order of $10^{-2} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ so that the net charge crossing a particular cross-section in a given time is zero.
